title: HierBias: Context-Conditioned Hierarchical Media Bias Detection with Multi-Task Type Classification
summary: Media bias detection is a critical task for ensuring fair and balanced information dissemination, yet existing sentence-level approaches classify each sentence independently, ignoring inter-sentence contextual signals that human annotators naturally exploit. We present \textbf{HierBias}, a hierarchical context-conditioned media bias detector that formally models document context in bias prediction. We introduce the \emph{context-conditioned bias probability} and prove theoretically that leveraging document context strictly reduces the Bayes error of sentence-level classification when inter-sentence mutual information is non-zero. A multi-task generalization bound further establishes that jointly training binary bias detection and fine-grained bias type classification improves sample efficiency on small annotated corpora. Architecturally, HierBias pairs a sentence-level RoBERTa encoder with a cross-sentence Transformer aggregator and dual output heads for binary detection and four-class type classification. Evaluated on BABE and BASIL, HierBias achieves 0.853 F1 and 0.723 MCC, surpassing the state-of-the-art bias-detector by $+2.6\%$ F1 and $+4.3\%$ MCC (McNemar's test, $p < 0.05$). Ablation experiments confirm that each theoretical component contributes independently and consistently.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes HierBias, a hierarchical context-conditioned model for sentence-level media bias detection that leverages document context and multi-task learning to significantly outperform prior methods on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing media bias detection models typically classify each sentence independently, neglecting the inter-sentence context that humans naturally use when judging bias. This leads to suboptimal performance, especially on small annotated corpora. The authors aim to formally model and exploit document-level context and related bias-type information to improve the accuracy and sample efficiency of sentence-level bias classification.
AI.method: The authors define a context-conditioned bias probability and provide a theoretical analysis showing that using document context reduces Bayes error when there is non-zero mutual information between sentences. They further derive a multi-task generalization bound indicating benefits from jointly learning binary bias detection and fine-grained bias-type classification. Practically, they implement HierBias, which uses a RoBERTa encoder at the sentence level, a cross-sentence Transformer to aggregate contextual information across sentences in a document, and dual output heads to perform both binary bias detection and four-class bias-type classification in a multi-task setting.
AI.result: On two benchmark datasets, BABE and BASIL, HierBias achieves an F1 score of 0.853 and MCC of 0.723, outperforming the previous state-of-the-art by 2.6 F1 points and 4.3 MCC points, with the improvements statistically validated using McNemar's test (p < 0.05). Ablation studies show that incorporating document context and multi-task learning each provide independent and consistent gains.
AI.conclusion: Modeling document-level context and jointly learning binary and fine-grained bias labels substantially improves sentence-level media bias detection. HierBias both theoretically and empirically demonstrates that context-conditioned predictions and multi-task learning reduce error and improve sample efficiency, setting a new state-of-the-art on standard bias detection benchmarks.

title: Know2Guess: A Contamination-Aware Multi-Zone Benchmark for Knowledge-Boundary Evaluation in Large Language Models
summary: Reliable evaluation of large language models should separate supported answering from unsupported guessing without conflating either with data contamination, prompt idiosyncrasy, or generic refusal behavior. We present a contamination-aware, multi-zone benchmark for measuring the transition from answerable knowledge to abstention-expected unknowns under frozen build-time labels. The benchmark contains 1,200 items across five domains, explicit abstention expectations, contamination-risk metadata, and dual parsing with an official strict parser plus a normalized robustness parser. We evaluate FLAN-T5, Qwen2.5-Instruct, and Llama-3-Instruct models under locked answer-or-abstain prompts, answer-only controls, and prompt-template variants. The benchmark is not solved by generic non-answer behavior: FLAN baselines remain weak on productive abstention, while stronger instruction-tuned models expose a selective but incomplete transition from answering to abstaining. Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct achieves the best overall reliability, but answer-expected zones remain difficult, calibration remains poor, and benign-item refusal persists. Prompt and parser robustness analyses preserve the main ranking and qualitative conclusions. The benchmark therefore provides a reproducible protocol for auditing answerability, abstention, refusal, and contamination as distinct but interacting dimensions of LLM reliability.The dataset is publicly available at https://github.com/renweimeng/Know2Guess-A-Contamination-Aware-Multi-Zone-Benchmark.

AI.tldr: They introduce Know2Guess, a benchmark that tests when LLMs should answer versus abstain, explicitly accounting for data contamination and prompt/refusal effects, and show current models are still unreliable and poorly calibrated.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluations often mix up three things: (1) whether a model actually knows the answer, (2) whether it is just guessing, and (3) whether data contamination, weird prompts, or over-cautious refusals distort behavior. There is no standard way to cleanly measure the point where models should stop answering and start abstaining, or to separate abstention from generic refusal. The authors want a principled, reproducible benchmark to audit these distinct aspects of reliability.
AI.method: They build a contamination-aware, "multi‑zone" benchmark of 1,200 items in five domains. Each item is labeled with whether the model is expected to answer or abstain, and with metadata about contamination risk. They define zones from answer-expected to abstention-expected unknowns, include explicit abstention expectations, and provide two parsers (strict and robustness-normalized) to score outputs. Using frozen labels and locked prompts (answer-or-abstain and answer-only) plus prompt-template variants, they evaluate several instruction-tuned models (FLAN‑T5, Qwen2.5‑Instruct, Llama‑3‑Instruct) on their ability to give correct answers where expected and to abstain where expected, while also analyzing robustness to prompts and parsing choices.
AI.result: They find that mere non-answer behavior does not solve the task: FLAN baselines are still poor at productive (selective) abstention. Stronger instruction-tuned models show a partial but incomplete shift from answering to abstaining across zones. Among tested models, Qwen2.5‑3B‑Instruct is the most reliable overall. However, answer-expected zones remain challenging, models are poorly calibrated, and they often refuse benign items. Prompt and parser robustness checks leave the ranking and main qualitative findings unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Know2Guess provides a reproducible evaluation protocol that disentangles and jointly audits four interacting dimensions of LLM reliability: answerability, abstention, refusal, and contamination. Existing models do not yet show robust, well-calibrated transitions from answering to abstaining and still mis-handle benign cases, so the benchmark can be used to systematically track and improve these behaviors.

title: Helpfulness Hurts: Domain-Dependent Degradation of Mid-Trained Compassion Values Under Post-Training
summary: Standard post-training pipelines apply supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL) to make language models helpful, but these processes may inadvertently degrade values instilled during pre-training. We investigate whether the domain of post-training data differentially affects the retention of animal compassion values in a Llama 3.1 8B model mid-trained on compassion-oriented synthetic data, using both SFT (helpfulness via Dolly-15k vs. coding via Magicoder-110K) and GRPO (helpfulness via RLHFlow vs. coding via Magicoder), evaluated on the Animal Harm Benchmark (AHB 2.2) and MORU benchmark (Moral Reasoning Under Uncertainty). Helpfulness training significantly degrades animal compassion relative to coding training on AHB (SFT: 35.7% vs. 65.2%; GRPO: 18.7% vs. 32.0%), replicating across two independent helpfulness datasets and two training paradigms. On English MORU items, helpfulness training degrades general moral reasoning by 25.5 percentage points (46.4% vs. 71.9%), a striking gap that rivals the compassion effect in magnitude. However, this effect does not transfer cross-lingually: on the multilingual MORU benchmark, the domain effect disappears (SFT: 52.3% vs. 51.2%). In contrast, the animal compassion effect transfers consistently across languages, with Magicoder's AHB percentage-point gain over the base model 4.5 times larger on non-English items than English items. This divergence suggests that values instilled through mid-training are encoded more deeply and cross-lingually than reasoning improvements from domain-specific post-training. These results suggest that, for labs building on value-laden mid-training, coding-domain post-training may better preserve mid-trained values than helpfulness post-training without harming general reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: Post-training on 'helpfulness' data can erode compassion and moral reasoning values instilled during mid-training in Llama 3.1 8B, while coding-domain post-training preserves them better.
AI.motivation: To understand how different domains of post-training data (helpfulness vs. coding) affect the retention of compassion-related values and general moral reasoning that were instilled during mid-training of a language model.
AI.method: Mid-train a Llama 3.1 8B model on compassion-oriented synthetic data, then apply two types of post-training—SFT and GRPO—with either helpfulness-focused datasets (Dolly-15k, RLHFlow) or coding-focused datasets (Magicoder-110K, Magicoder RL). Evaluate the resulting models on the Animal Harm Benchmark (AHB 2.2) for animal compassion and on the MORU benchmark for moral reasoning in both English and multilingual settings, comparing performance changes across training regimes.
AI.result: Helpfulness-oriented SFT and GRPO substantially reduce the model's animal compassion scores on AHB compared with coding-oriented post-training (e.g., SFT: 35.7% vs. 65.2%; GRPO: 18.7% vs. 32.0%). Helpfulness training also lowers English MORU moral reasoning performance by 25.5 percentage points (46.4% vs. 71.9%), but this degradation does not appear in the multilingual MORU benchmark. The animal compassion benefits from mid-training transfer robustly across languages, with coding-domain post-training yielding a much larger improvement on non-English AHB items than on English ones.
AI.conclusion: Values imparted during mid-training (like animal compassion) are encoded more robustly and cross-lingually than moral reasoning improvements derived from domain-specific post-training. Helpfulness-focused post-training can erode both compassion and English moral reasoning, whereas coding-focused post-training better preserves mid-trained values without sacrificing general reasoning. For labs building on value-laden mid-training, coding-domain post-training may be a safer choice than generic helpfulness optimization.

title: Investigating LLM's Problem Solving Capability -- a Study on Statics Questions
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have rapidly influenced many aspects of society, particularly education, due to their demonstrated ability to complete assignments and examinations across a wide range of subjects. Although prior studies have examined the educational impact of LLMs, much of the existing work relies on public or open problem datasets and lacks topic-specific analysis. In engineering education, especially within mechanical engineering, systematic investigations of LLM performance on specific problem types remain limited. Instead of using traditional methods that directly ask textbook questions to an LLM tool, our study adopts a model distillation process to evaluate LLM capabilities in solving statics problems. By distilling ChatGPT, we extracted 25 text-only statics questions and further constructed two additional datasets by adding diagrams and modifying their numerical values. Experimental results show that while LLMs perform well on text-only statics problems, their accuracy decreases when diagrams are introduced and the problems require multi-step reasoning. Further analysis suggests that this performance drop is not primarily caused by limitations in image recognition, but rather by difficulties in multi-step reasoning and in consistently applying extracted visual information across successive solution stages.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well large language models solve mechanical engineering statics problems, showing strong performance on text-only questions but reduced accuracy when diagrams and multi-step reasoning are involved.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of LLMs in education mostly use generic, open datasets and do not focus on specific engineering domains or detailed problem types. In mechanical engineering statics, there is little systematic analysis of how LLMs handle the types of problems students actually solve, particularly those involving diagrams and multi-step reasoning. The authors seek to fill this gap to better understand the practical capabilities and limitations of LLMs in engineering education contexts.
AI.method: Instead of directly feeding textbook questions to an LLM, the authors use a model distillation approach: they first interact with ChatGPT to extract 25 representative text-only statics questions. They then create two additional datasets from these base questions: one that includes associated diagrams and another that modifies numerical values while preserving structure. They evaluate multiple LLMs on these datasets, comparing performance across text-only, text-plus-diagram, and numerical-variant problems, and analyze error patterns with respect to reasoning steps and use of visual information.
AI.result: LLMs achieve relatively high accuracy on the purely text-based statics questions. However, accuracy drops noticeably when diagrams are added and when the problems require multiple reasoning steps. The decline is not strongly correlated with raw image interpretation errors; instead, it is associated with failures to correctly integrate and reuse visual information across sequential steps of the solution. This indicates a specific weakness in chained, visually grounded reasoning rather than in basic recognition.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current LLMs are competent at solving straightforward, text-only statics problems but struggle with diagram-based, multi-step engineering tasks that require consistent use of visual information over several reasoning stages. The bottleneck lies less in image recognition and more in multi-step, visually grounded reasoning. This highlights the need for improved architectures or training methods that better support chained reasoning and integration of diagrammatic information for effective use of LLMs in engineering education.

title: Detecting and Controlling Sycophancy with Cascading Linear Features
summary: Interpreting and controlling model behaviors through activation steering methods requires many pairs of contrastive samples that clearly exhibit desired or undesired behavior. These data pairs determine the degree to which interpretability frameworks can reliably detect model features responsible for a behavior, and therefore the ability to steer models toward or away from such behavior. In this work, we present an iterative data generation pipeline that isolates cascading linear features responsible for a behavior. Specifically, we show how moving beyond simple binary pairs of samples, and instead isolating samples that show degrees of features that scale linearly with behavior, allows for better disentanglement of features. We focus on detecting and steering away from sycophancy -- the tendency of language models to prioritize user validation. We demonstrate that sycophancy features discovered through cascading samples form linearly separable subspaces, and allow for selection of model activations that more clearly correspond to the desired behavior than baseline approaches. We also evaluate their ability to enable detection, deterministic scoring, and robust steering, and see that they either match or outperform LLM-as-a-judge and system prompting baselines while providing lower computational demand and more interpretability guarantees. Code & Data: https://cascading-feats.github.io/

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new iterative data-generation pipeline for activation steering that uses graded, cascading examples instead of simple binary contrastive pairs, enabling more precise interpretation and control of language model behaviors, especially sycophancy.
AI.motivation: Existing activation steering and interpretability methods rely heavily on binary contrastive data pairs (e.g., one example with an undesired behavior and one without). These pairs often fail to capture the nuanced, graded nature of model behaviors, making it harder to reliably identify the underlying features and to steer models effectively. There is a need for a more systematic, data-driven approach that can uncover linearly structured features responsible for complex behaviors like sycophancy, while also providing better interpretability and computational efficiency than current techniques such as LLM-as-a-judge or heavy prompt engineering.
AI.method: The authors propose an iterative data generation pipeline that constructs cascading sets of samples exhibiting progressively stronger or weaker degrees of a target behavior. Instead of only using binary contrastive examples, the pipeline curates examples that vary along a spectrum where the strength of the behavior scales approximately linearly. By analyzing model activations across these cascades, they identify linear features and subspaces associated with the behavior. They then perform activation steering in these learned subspaces to adjust the model’s outputs, and compare this approach against baseline methods, including standard activation steering with binary pairs, LLM-as-a-judge scoring, and system-level prompting strategies.
AI.result: The study finds that features derived from cascading samples define linearly separable subspaces that are more cleanly aligned with the target behavior than those obtained from standard binary contrastive approaches. In the case of sycophancy, the extracted activation directions better isolate the model’s tendency to prioritize user validation. Using these directions for detection, scoring, and steering achieves performance that matches or exceeds LLM-as-a-judge and system prompting baselines while being more computationally efficient. The method also delivers clearer interpretability guarantees because the behavior is tied to well-characterized linear activation patterns.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging cascading, graded examples rather than simple binary contrastive pairs yields more disentangled, linearly structured features underlying specific language model behaviors. These features support more robust and interpretable activation steering, as demonstrated for reducing sycophancy. The approach offers competitive or superior performance to common baselines at lower computational cost and with stronger interpretability, suggesting a promising direction for systematically understanding and controlling complex behaviors in large language models.

title: Assert, don't describe: Linguistic features that shift LLM reasoning about animal welfare
summary: Animal-welfare advocates produce a lot of writing, and increasingly that writing trains the language models that millions of people then ask about animal welfare. Using vocabulary-matched stance-contrast probes on a held-out animal-welfare benchmark, we measure how each of ten linguistic features changes Llama-3.2-1B's preference for pro-animal-welfare reasoning when used as fine-tuning data. Eight of the ten features produce statistically significant shifts. Seven move the model toward stronger pro-animal-welfare reasoning: assertive certainty, explicit moral vocabulary, emotion words, evaluative claims, narrative structure, depicted harm severity, and immediate temporal framing. Two move it the other way: hedged language and concrete sensory description both dilute the pro-animal-welfare stance. First-person perspective has no statistically significant effect. The practical recommendation for anyone writing animal-welfare text that may end up in LLM training corpora: assert a position rather than describe a scene neutrally. The features that shift the model are the ones that make the writer's position explicit; the features that dilute it hold animal-welfare content but withhold stance.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how specific linguistic features in animal-welfare texts, when used to fine-tune Llama-3.2-1B, change the model’s tendency to produce pro-animal-welfare reasoning.
AI.motivation: Animal-welfare advocacy generates substantial text that increasingly enters LLM training corpora, implicitly shaping how models reason about animals. Advocates and AI practitioners lack clear, empirical guidance on which writing styles most effectively push models toward stronger pro-animal-welfare reasoning. The authors want to understand which specific linguistic features in training data increase or decrease an LLM’s pro-animal-welfare stance so advocates can strategically craft text that, if scraped, nudges future models toward more welfare-supportive outputs.
AI.method: The authors construct a held-out animal-welfare benchmark with vocabulary-matched stance-contrast probes—pairs of prompts differing in stance but similar in wording. They fine-tune Llama-3.2-1B multiple times, each run enriching the fine-tuning data with one of ten targeted linguistic features (e.g., assertive certainty, moral vocabulary, hedging, narrative structure). For each feature-specific model, they measure how often it prefers pro-animal-welfare reasoning on the benchmark, then apply statistical tests to see whether each feature systematically increases or decreases pro-welfare preferences relative to a baseline model.
AI.result: Eight of ten linguistic features cause statistically significant shifts in the model’s stance. Seven features push the model toward more pro-animal-welfare reasoning: (1) assertive certainty, (2) explicit moral vocabulary, (3) emotion words, (4) evaluative claims, (5) narrative structure, (6) depicted harm severity, and (7) immediate temporal framing. Two features—hedged language and concrete sensory description—significantly weaken the model’s pro-animal-welfare stance. First-person perspective shows no statistically significant effect. Overall, the stance-shifting features are those that make the author’s normative position explicit, whereas stance-diluting features describe animal-welfare content without clearly endorsing a position.
AI.conclusion: Linguistic style in training data measurably shapes an LLM’s moral and normative leanings on animal welfare. Explicit, assertive, evaluative, emotionally colored, and narratively structured texts that highlight harm and immediacy tend to strengthen an LLM’s pro-animal-welfare reasoning. In contrast, hedged or purely descriptive language, even when thematically about animal welfare, dilutes that stance. For advocates whose texts may enter training corpora, the recommended strategy is to state clear, confident positions on animal welfare rather than relying on neutral or heavily hedged descriptions: models appear to learn values from explicit stance, not from content alone.

title: Life After Benchmark Saturation: A Case Study of CORE-Bench
summary: When a benchmark's accuracy saturates, it is often retired and replaced with a more challenging version. We show that this approach privileges accuracy and misses the opportunity to study six other key dimensions of agent performance: construct validity issues such as shortcuts, out-of-distribution generalizability, efficiency, reliability, the relative importance of the model versus the scaffold, and uplift from human-agent collaboration. We use CORE-Bench Hard, a benchmark for computational reproducibility of scientific code, as a case study to demonstrate that measuring agents along these dimensions yields meaningful insights into agent performance even after accuracy saturates. First, we surface threats to construct validity in CORE-Bench Hard that are difficult to anticipate with less capable agents. We introduce an improved benchmark, CORE-Bench v1.1, and an out-of-distribution task suite, CORE-Bench OOD. Second, we find that despite accuracy saturation, CORE-Bench v1.1 remains useful for measuring efficiency, reliability, model performance, and scaffold performance. Finally, we conduct a small-scale randomized experiment to measure uplift from human-agent collaboration on real-world computational reproducibility tasks. We find a statistically significant speedup by about a factor of two -- likely underestimated due to one-fifth of human-only reproductions reaching the time limit before completing -- and describe various other findings. Together, our contributions present a more rigorous alternative to the dominant accuracy-centric evaluation paradigm.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that benchmarks should not be retired once accuracy saturates, but instead used to evaluate additional dimensions of agent performance beyond accuracy, using CORE-Bench (for code reproducibility) as a case study.
AI.motivation: Benchmark culture in AI typically replaces a benchmark once models reach high accuracy, focusing almost exclusively on accuracy as the primary metric. This misses other important aspects of agent behavior and evaluation, such as robustness, efficiency, and human collaboration. The authors are motivated to show that “saturated” benchmarks can still yield rich insights if we broaden what we measure.
AI.method: Use CORE-Bench Hard, a benchmark for computational reproducibility of scientific code, as an empirical testbed. (1) Analyze construct validity issues, revealing shortcuts and other threats; propose and build an improved benchmark CORE-Bench v1.1 and an out-of-distribution task suite CORE-Bench OOD. (2) Evaluate agents on CORE-Bench v1.1 along non-accuracy dimensions: efficiency, reliability, model versus scaffold contributions. (3) Run a randomized controlled experiment comparing human-only vs human+agent collaboration on real computational reproducibility tasks to quantify performance uplift, especially speed.
AI.result: They uncover previously unseen construct validity problems and shortcuts in CORE-Bench Hard when using more capable agents, and address these with CORE-Bench v1.1 and CORE-Bench OOD. Even when accuracy is saturated, the new benchmark variants still differentiate systems on efficiency, reliability, and model vs scaffold performance. In the human-subject experiment, human-agent collaboration yields roughly a 2× speedup on reproducibility tasks, although this effect is likely underestimated because many human-only attempts time out before finishing.
AI.conclusion: Benchmarks remain valuable beyond the point of accuracy saturation if evaluated along richer dimensions than accuracy alone. By analyzing construct validity, generalization, efficiency, reliability, scaffold vs model contributions, and human-agent collaboration, researchers can gain deeper and more practical insights about agent performance. The authors propose their updated CORE-Bench suite as an example of this more rigorous, multi-dimensional evaluation paradigm, advocating a shift away from purely accuracy-centric benchmark lifecycles.

title: Context Recycling for Long-Horizon LLM Inference
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit strong capabilities in short-context reasoning but degrade in performance over long conversational horizons due to context window limitations and inefficient token usage. We introduce ContextForge, a system for context recycling that maintains task-relevant information across turns by combining structured query generation, external memory retrieval, and controlled synthesis. The system enables efficient reuse of prior computation without relying on full context replay, reducing token overhead while preserving answer quality. We evaluate ContextForge using a 15-turn conversational benchmark that tests multi-turn reasoning, back-references, and domain shifts across structured healthcare queries. Compared to a baseline agent using identical underlying models, ContextForge demonstrates improved consistency and reduced token consumption, while maintaining comparable response accuracy. These results suggest that context recycling provides a practical approach for extending LLM capabilities in long-horizon tasks without requiring larger context windows or model retraining. Code and evaluation artifacts are available at https://github.com/Betanu701/ContextForge.

AI.tldr: ContextForge is a context-recycling system that lets LLMs handle long, multi-turn conversations more efficiently by storing and reusing only the task-relevant information instead of replaying full histories.
AI.motivation: LLMs handle short contexts well but struggle with long conversations because of limited context windows and wasted tokens from repeatedly sending long histories. There is a need for a way to preserve important prior information and computation across turns without expanding model size or context length.
AI.method: ContextForge combines three key components: (1) structured query generation to extract and formalize what is important from each turn, (2) external memory retrieval to store and later fetch this task-relevant state, and (3) controlled synthesis to integrate retrieved information with the current query so the model can reason over prior turns without replaying the full context. The system is tested on a 15-turn conversational benchmark in healthcare involving multi-turn reasoning, back-references, and domain shifts.
AI.result: On the healthcare conversational benchmark, ContextForge is compared to a baseline agent using the same LLMs but with standard context usage. ContextForge yields better consistency across turns, significantly lowers token consumption, and keeps response accuracy on par with the baseline.
AI.conclusion: Context recycling via structured state extraction, external memory, and controlled synthesis can effectively extend LLMs to long-horizon conversational tasks without needing larger context windows or model retraining, offering a practical and more token-efficient deployment strategy.

title: Refusal Lives Downstream of Persona in Chat Models
comment: Accepted to the ICML 2026 Mechanistic Interpretability workshop
summary: Linear directions in activation space have been identified for both refusal and persona traits in instruction-tuned chat models, but the two have been studied as separate mechanisms. We show they interact: a compliant persona gates refusal. In Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, we extract a compliant model-persona direction and a refusal direction and intervene on both. Compliant persona steering suppresses refusal -- in Llama, the refusal rate falls from 97% to 2%. Reintroducing the refusal direction partially restores refusal at late layers but not at early ones. Projecting out the persona direction in a late-layer window restores it to baseline; projecting out a random direction does not. Refusal is therefore gated at the late-layer expression stage, downstream of where it is computed. Treating refusal as a single isolated direction misses its dependence on persona.

AI.tldr: The paper finds that in instruction-tuned chat models, refusal behavior is not governed by a single isolated direction in activation space: it is gated by a “compliant persona” direction at late layers, which suppresses refusal even when refusal features are present earlier.
AI.motivation: Previous work identified linear directions for both refusal and persona traits in model activation space, but treated them as independent. Safety and alignment methods often assume refusal corresponds to a single, separable mechanism. The authors aim to test whether refusal is in fact independent, or whether it depends on persona-related representations, and to pinpoint where in the network this interaction occurs.
AI.method: Using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, the authors (1) extract an activation-space direction corresponding to a “compliant model persona” and a direction corresponding to refusal, (2) perform targeted activation steering along these directions, and (3) conduct projection/intervention experiments at different layers and layer windows. They measure refusal rates under these manipulations and compare effects of removing persona directions vs random directions on refusal behavior.
AI.result: Steering the model along the compliant persona direction strongly suppresses refusal, with Llama’s refusal rate dropping from 97% to 2%. Adding back the refusal direction at late layers partially restores refusal, but attempting to restore it at early layers does not. When the persona direction is projected out in a late-layer window, refusal reverts to baseline, whereas projecting out a random direction has no such effect. This shows causal, layer-localized gating of refusal by persona.
AI.conclusion: Refusal behavior in these chat models is not purely controlled by a single refusal direction. Instead, a compliant persona direction at late layers acts as a gate on refusal expression: refusal features can be computed earlier but blocked from surfacing in the output. Consequently, treating refusal as an isolated, linearly-addressable feature is misleading; safety and interpretability work must account for its dependence on persona representations and the specific layer stages where this interaction occurs.

title: Reducing Conversational Escalation in Large Language Model Dialogue with Nonviolent Communication Constraints
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in emotionally charged situations involving interpersonal conflict, frustration, and distress. While prior safety research has focused on preventing explicit harms such as toxic or policy-violating content, less attention has been paid to conversational behaviors that may unintentionally escalate conflict. In this paper, we investigate whether LLMs can be guided toward more de-escalating dialogue behavior through lightweight prompt-level constraints derived from Nonviolent Communication (NVC). We reformulate NVC principles as process-oriented guidelines that discourage blame attribution, emphasize attention to users' emotional experiences, and encourage clarification before advice. Using a dual-agent simulation framework across multiple instruction-tuned models and user resistance levels, we show that NVC-constrained prompting consistently reduces conversational escalation and stabilizes interactions with highly resistant users. These results suggest that simple communication constraints can meaningfully improve the trustworthiness of LLM dialogue in conflict-prone settings.

AI.tldr: The paper explores whether adding simple Nonviolent Communication (NVC)-based instructions to prompts can make LLMs handle emotionally charged, conflict-heavy conversations in a more de-escalating, stable way.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in emotionally charged, conflict-prone interactions, most safety work has focused on blocking obvious harms (toxicity, policy violations). However, even when content is non-toxic, models can still respond in ways that escalate conflict or user distress. There is a need to understand and improve the subtle conversational dynamics of LLMs, especially in the presence of user resistance, using approaches that are lightweight and easy to apply.
AI.method: The authors translate core principles of Nonviolent Communication (NVC) into explicit, process-level prompt guidelines for LLMs. These guidelines discourage blame and judgment, focus on acknowledging users’ emotions, and encourage asking clarifying questions before giving advice. They then run dual-agent simulations, where LLMs interact with simulated users exhibiting varying levels of resistance, across multiple instruction-tuned models. They compare standard prompting with NVC-constrained prompting and measure conversational escalation and stability.
AI.result: Across different models and resistance scenarios, prompts augmented with NVC-style constraints lead to conversations with less escalation and more stable trajectories, especially with highly resistant users. The NVC-constrained LLMs are less likely to respond in ways that amplify conflict or tension and maintain more constructive dialogues over time.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight, prompt-level constraints inspired by Nonviolent Communication can substantially improve the de-escalatory behavior and robustness of LLMs in conflict-prone interactions. This suggests a practical path to making LLM dialogue more trustworthy and safer in emotionally charged settings without requiring major architectural or training changes.

title: AlgoEvolve: LLM-driven Meta-evolution of Algorithmic Trading Programs
summary: Recent work shows that Large Language Models (LLMs) can act as semantic mutation operators for the evolutionary discovery of programs and proofs. Most current applications focus on static coding benchmarks. We extend this paradigm to algorithmic trading. This domain is uniquely challenging because it is noisy, non-stationary, and highly discontinuous. We present AlgoEvolve, an LLM-driven evolutionary framework that generates, evaluates, and iteratively improves executable trading strategies. These strategies are expressed as Python code and evaluated through a rigorous testing protocol. Across multiple experiments, the system exhibits emergent regime-adaptive strategy logic, including autonomous shifts in trading rules. We further introduce a meta-evolutionary outer loop that evolves the prompts guiding program synthesis in the inner loop. This outer loop discovers improved search heuristics. These heuristics balance exploration and exploitation while reducing zero-trade failures. They consistently outperform initial human-designed instructions. The results demonstrate that LLM-based semantic evolution provides a viable approach for continual program synthesis in complex environments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AlgoEvolve, an LLM-driven evolutionary framework that automatically generates and improves Python-based trading strategies, showing that LLM semantic evolution can adapt strategies to changing market regimes and outperform human-designed prompting heuristics.
AI.motivation: Prior work has shown that LLMs can serve as semantic mutation operators for evolving programs and proofs, but most studies focus on static coding benchmarks. The authors want to explore whether this paradigm can be extended to a more complex, dynamic, and noisy real-world environment: algorithmic trading, where market conditions are non-stationary and discontinuous. They aim to test if LLM-driven evolution can continually synthesize and adapt trading strategies that remain effective across changing regimes, and whether meta-optimization of prompts can further enhance the search over programs.
AI.method: The authors propose AlgoEvolve, an evolutionary framework in which an LLM generates, mutates, and refines executable trading strategies written in Python. The system evaluates these strategies via a rigorous backtesting and testing protocol. The evolutionary process includes an inner loop where the LLM performs semantic mutations on code (trading strategies) based on performance feedback, and an outer meta-evolutionary loop that evolves the prompts used to guide the LLM’s program synthesis. This outer loop searches over different prompting strategies to discover heuristics that better balance exploration versus exploitation and reduce failure cases (e.g., strategies that place no trades).
AI.result: In a series of experiments on algorithmic trading tasks, AlgoEvolve yields trading strategies that exhibit emergent regime-adaptive behavior, such as autonomously changing trading rules in response to market conditions. The meta-evolutionary outer loop discovers improved prompting heuristics that lead to more effective search, fewer zero-trade strategies, and overall performance that consistently surpasses that achieved with initial human-designed prompts. These findings suggest that the coupled inner/outer evolutionary process can effectively navigate the complex search space of trading algorithms.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLM-based semantic evolution is a practical and effective approach for continual program synthesis in complex, non-stationary environments like financial markets. By combining an LLM-driven inner evolutionary loop that refines trading strategies with a meta-evolutionary outer loop that optimizes prompts, the framework can autonomously discover and adapt trading logic over time, outperforming manually designed search heuristics and demonstrating strong potential for broader applications beyond static benchmarks.

title: Low Resource Multimodal Translation of Nepali Spoken Words into Emotion-Conditioned Sign Language Avatars
summary: Sign language communication systems, that integrate emotional expression remain underexplored, particularly for low-resource languages. This pilot study presents NEST-V1 (Nepali Emotion and Speech Transformer - Version 1), a proof-of-concept multimodal framework that demonstrates the feasibility of generating emotion-conditioned Nepali Sign Language avatars from spoken input. As a preliminary investigation, we focus on four common Nepali words ("thank you", "hello", "house", "me") across three emotional states (happy, neutral, sad) to validate our core technical approach. Our lightweight architecture employs a shared acoustic encoder for simultaneous Automatic Speech Recognition and emotion classification, achieving 81.1% ASR accuracy and 79.21% emotion recognition accuracy on a dataset of 600 labeled audio samples from 50 speakers. The system demonstrates 37% parameter efficiency compared to separate model architectures while maintaining a lightweight footprint with only 22.1M parameters suitable for edge deployment. This pilot work establishes the technical foundation for emotion-aware sign language translation in low-resource settings and provides a scalable framework for future expansion to larger vocabularies and more diverse emotional expressions. Our preliminary results indicate the viability of real-time, emotionally expressive sign language communication systems for the hearing-impaired community, with clear pathways for enhancement in subsequent development phases.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NEST-V1, a pilot multimodal framework that generates emotion-aware Nepali Sign Language avatars from spoken input using a shared acoustic encoder for both ASR and emotion recognition.
AI.motivation: Existing sign language communication systems rarely model emotional expression, and low-resource languages like Nepali are especially underrepresented. The authors aim to explore whether it is technically feasible to build an emotion-conditioned sign language avatar system for Nepali using limited data and lightweight models suitable for edge devices.
AI.method: They design NEST-V1, a multimodal framework that takes spoken Nepali as input and outputs Nepali Sign Language avatar animations conditioned on emotion. The core model uses a shared acoustic encoder for joint Automatic Speech Recognition and emotion classification. The pilot setup restricts the vocabulary to four Nepali words and three emotional states. They train and evaluate on 600 labeled audio samples from 50 speakers, measuring ASR and emotion recognition accuracy, model parameter count, and parameter efficiency relative to using separate models.
AI.result: The shared-encoder model achieves 81.1% ASR accuracy and 79.21% emotion recognition accuracy on the small pilot dataset. It uses 22.1M parameters and is 37% more parameter-efficient than maintaining separate ASR and emotion models, making it feasible for edge deployment. The system successfully produces emotion-conditioned sign language avatar outputs for the limited vocabulary and emotion set.
AI.conclusion: NEST-V1 demonstrates the technical viability of an emotion-aware sign language translation pipeline for a low-resource language. Despite being limited in vocabulary and emotional diversity, the pilot confirms that a shared acoustic encoder can provide accurate ASR and emotion recognition in a compact model. This establishes a foundation for scaling to larger vocabularies, richer emotional categories, and more robust real-time sign language communication tools for the hearing-impaired community in low-resource settings.

title: Agentic Analysis for Agentic Infrastructure: An LLM-Powered Pipeline for Comparative Governance of DAO and Corporate AI Protocols
summary: As AI agent protocols proliferate, the governance structures shaping their interoperability standards remain empirically underexamined. We introduce an LLM-powered comparative pipeline for large-scale governance discourse analysis, integrating automated annotation, neural topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis to study socio-technical power structures at scale. We validate it on two contrasting standards for agent interoperability: ERC-8004 (permissionless, on-chain) and Google A2A (corporate-led). Analyzing 4,323 governance participation records, we combine LLM-assisted coding, topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis to examine how institutional design shapes thematic priorities and community structure. We find that while governance form influences substantive focus, both regimes exhibit comparable levels of participation inequality and community fragmentation. Discourse alignment is denser in the permissionless setting, suggesting that open governance may foster greater thematic convergence despite decentralized participation. These findings illustrate how LLM-assisted methods can advance the empirical study of technology governance, with implications for designing more equitable agentic AI standards. All data and code are openly available.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an LLM-powered pipeline to empirically analyze governance discourses around AI agent interoperability standards, comparing a permissionless blockchain standard (ERC-8004) and a corporate-led standard (Google A2A).
AI.motivation: Governance structures for AI agent interoperability standards are multiplying, but there is little empirical analysis of how different governance forms (e.g., open/permissionless vs. corporate-led) shape discourse, power, and participation. Traditional qualitative methods do not easily scale to thousands of governance records, so there is a need for computational, scalable, yet interpretable methods. The authors aim to fill this gap and better understand socio-technical power structures in emerging AI standards.
AI.method: They build an LLM-powered comparative analysis pipeline that combines: (1) automated text annotation and coding via large language models, (2) neural topic modeling to extract thematic structures, and (3) multi-layer network analysis to map relationships among participants, topics, and institutions. They apply this pipeline to 4,323 governance participation records from two contrasting interoperability standards: ERC-8004 (permissionless, on-chain) and Google A2A (corporate-led). The pipeline is used to compare thematic emphases, participation patterns, and community structure across the two governance regimes.
AI.result: They find that the form of governance (permissionless vs. corporate-led) does shape the substantive thematic focus of discussions. However, both regimes show similar levels of participation inequality and community fragmentation, indicating that neither model inherently solves concentration of influence. Interestingly, discourse alignment (the degree to which discussions converge thematically) is denser in the permissionless ERC-8004 setting, suggesting that open governance can still produce strong thematic convergence despite being more decentralized and open to broad participation.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that LLM-assisted pipelines can effectively support large-scale, empirical analysis of technology governance discourse. The findings indicate that governance form affects what is talked about but not necessarily how power and participation are distributed, and that open, permissionless models may foster stronger thematic convergence than expected. These insights can inform the design of more equitable governance mechanisms for agentic AI standards. The authors also contribute to transparency and reproducibility by releasing all data and code.

title: Where Larger Models Excel: The Primacy of Constraint-Guided Reasoning
summary: Larger language models consistently outperform smaller ones on reasoning benchmarks, yet the reasoning differences underlying this gap remain underexplored. Across benchmarks in mathematics, physics, chemistry, and programming, we observe stable performance gaps: averaged over datasets, Qwen3-32B outperforms Qwen3-8B by 6.43%, while GPT-OSS-120B exceeds GPT-OSS-20B by 7.38%. To study the reasoning differences behind these gains, we develop AdvCluster, an automated framework that identifies questions where the larger model shows a stable advantage, extracts fine-grained advantage descriptions from paired reasoning traces produced by larger and smaller models, and organizes them through semantic clustering with quantitative evaluation and selection guided by a reviewer model. Our analysis yields a systematic taxonomy of larger model reasoning advantages, spanning both common advantages that recur across domains and specialized advantages associated with particular domains. Across these patterns, a recurring theme is Constraint-Guided Reasoning: larger models are better at identifying explicit and implicit constraints, organizing them into structured reasoning, and using them to rule out infeasible paths and verify intermediate steps.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why larger language models outperform smaller ones on reasoning tasks, finding that larger models are systematically better at constraint-guided reasoning across domains.
AI.motivation: Although larger language models consistently outperform smaller ones on reasoning benchmarks, the specific reasoning behaviors and qualitative differences responsible for this performance gap are not well understood. The authors want to move beyond aggregate accuracy metrics to uncover what kinds of reasoning advantages larger models actually exhibit, and whether these advantages are shared across domains or domain-specific.
AI.method: The authors introduce AdvCluster, an automated analysis framework. First, it identifies questions where a larger model has a stable advantage over a smaller one. Second, it generates paired reasoning traces from both models and extracts fine-grained descriptions of where and how the larger model is better. Third, it uses semantic clustering to group similar advantage descriptions and employs a reviewer model to quantitatively evaluate and select the most meaningful clusters. This pipeline yields an interpretable taxonomy of reasoning advantages. They apply this framework across benchmarks in mathematics, physics, chemistry, and programming, comparing pairs of models of different sizes (e.g., Qwen3-32B vs Qwen3-8B, GPT-OSS-120B vs GPT-OSS-20B).
AI.result: Empirically, they confirm stable performance gaps across domains: on average, the 32B Qwen model outperforms the 8B by 6.43%, and the 120B GPT-OSS outperforms the 20B by 7.38%. Using AdvCluster, they derive a structured taxonomy of the reasoning advantages displayed by larger models. These include common, cross-domain patterns as well as domain-specific advantages. A prominent recurring pattern is that larger models more reliably identify problem constraints (both explicit and implicit), maintain these constraints in a structured way throughout their reasoning, and use them to guide search, eliminate invalid solution paths, and check intermediate results.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the superior performance of larger language models on reasoning tasks is largely driven by stronger constraint-guided reasoning. Larger models are better at recognizing and organizing constraints and using them to systematically guide and verify multi-step reasoning. AdvCluster provides a scalable way to uncover such qualitative reasoning differences, suggesting a concrete target for improving smaller models and for designing training or prompting strategies that emulate the constraint-handling behavior of larger models.

title: Knowledge-augmented Agentic AI for Mental Health Medication Information Seeking
summary: Patients increasingly seek medication information online, yet safety knowledge for psychiatric drugs is split between regulatory adverse-event records, which are authoritative but abstract, and patient narratives, which are experience-near but unvalidated. Integrating them without conflating evidence and anecdote is especially consequential in psychiatry, where poorly contextualised information can amplify fear, nocebo responses, and non-adherence. Here we develop a provenance-aware, knowledge-graph-based multi-agent framework unifying 466,525 Reddit posts, 60,782 WebMD reviews, and twenty years of U.S. FDA Adverse Event Reporting System records for nine antidepressants. A large-language-model entity-recognition pipeline benchmarked against physician annotations reached highest F1 scores of 0.969 for medications and 0.973 for conditions. The two community platforms were far more concordant with each other (overlap up to a Jaccard similarity of 0.905) than with regulatory reports, indicating that patient-generated data form a partly independent safety signal. For sertraline, many adverse events appeared in community sources hundreds of days before the corresponding FDA date. A Neo4j knowledge graph grounded in ATC-N, ICD-10, and MedDRA vocabularies preserves provenance, keeping every claim traceable and regulatory facts distinct from patient experience. These results establish source-aware integration as a route to more auditable psychiatric medication information, with usefulness and patient benefit to be tested prospectively.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a provenance-aware knowledge graph that integrates patient-generated online data and FDA regulatory adverse event reports for antidepressants, showing that community data offer an earlier, partly independent drug safety signal while keeping anecdotal and regulatory evidence clearly distinguished.
AI.motivation: Patients look up psychiatric medication information online, but current safety knowledge is fragmented between abstract but authoritative regulatory data and concrete but anecdotal patient narratives. In psychiatry, misinterpretation of such information can worsen fear, nocebo effects, and non-adherence, so there is a need for a system that unifies these sources while preserving their distinct evidential status.
AI.method: The authors collected large-scale data from 466k Reddit posts, 60k WebMD reviews, and 20 years of FDA Adverse Event Reporting System records for nine antidepressants. They built a multi-agent, large-language-model-based entity recognition pipeline to extract medications and conditions, benchmarking it against physician annotations and achieving high F1 scores. They then constructed a Neo4j knowledge graph grounded in standard clinical vocabularies (ATC-N, ICD-10, MedDRA) that encodes provenance so each claim remains traceable and patient accounts remain distinct from regulatory facts. They compared overlap and timing of adverse-event mentions across sources.
AI.result: The LLM-based entity recognition system achieved high performance (F1 up to 0.969 for medications and 0.973 for conditions). Patient community platforms (Reddit and WebMD) showed high concordance with each other, with Jaccard similarity up to 0.905, but were less overlapping with FDA reports, indicating patient narratives provide a partly independent safety signal. For sertraline, several adverse events emerged in community data hundreds of days before appearing in FDA reports. The resulting knowledge graph successfully preserved source provenance while integrating heterogeneous data.
AI.conclusion: A provenance-aware, knowledge-graph-based integration of online patient narratives with regulatory adverse event data is feasible and accurate, and it reveals that community-generated content can serve as an earlier, partially independent signal of antidepressant safety issues. Maintaining clear source provenance allows regulatory evidence and patient experience to remain distinguishable, providing a more auditable foundation for psychiatric medication information whose practical benefits should be validated in future prospective studies.

title: From Lexicon to AI: A Structured-Data Pipeline for Specialized Conversational Systems in Low-Resource Languages
summary: Low-resource languages face a critical challenge in AI development: creating specialized conversational systems without access to massive training corpora. We present a systematic methodology for transforming structured linguistic resources into specialized AI systems, demonstrating that expert-curated lexical databases can serve as effective foundations for conversational AI development. Our approach converts Hindi WordNet into 1.25 million diverse instruction-response pairs, fine-tunes a 12B-parameter language model using resource-efficient LoRA with 4-bit quantization. Evaluation through a Hindi language learning chatbot demonstrates that structured-knowledge-based systems achieve superior pedagogical effectiveness (91.0 vs. 79.4-83.6 for general-purpose models) while maintaining competitive semantic performance and exceptional consistency. The complete pipeline demonstrates a proof-of-concept methodology using Hindi for developing specialized AI systems for any languages with WordNet resources. This work addresses the critical gap in AI accessibility for low-resource languages, offering a practical alternative to corpus-intensive approaches and potentially enabling specialized AI development for the hundreds of languages with existing WordNet resources.

AI.tldr: They show how to build an effective, specialized Hindi conversational AI from WordNet instead of massive corpora, via automated instruction generation and efficient fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Low-resource languages lack massive text corpora for training specialized conversational systems, creating an AI accessibility gap. However, many such languages do have structured lexical resources like WordNet that are underutilized for building AI systems. The paper aims to leverage these existing expert-curated resources to enable practical, high-quality conversational AI without corpus-heavy requirements.
AI.method: 1) Start from Hindi WordNet as a structured lexical database. 2) Automatically transform WordNet entries and relations into 1.25M diverse instruction–response pairs designed for conversational use, including pedagogical scenarios. 3) Fine-tune a 12B parameter large language model using parameter-efficient LoRA and 4-bit quantization to reduce compute and memory needs. 4) Deploy and test the resulting model in a Hindi language learning chatbot setting. 5) Compare performance against general-purpose models on semantic and pedagogical metrics.
AI.result: The WordNet-based, fine-tuned Hindi model shows higher pedagogical effectiveness in a language learning chatbot (score 91.0) compared to general-purpose models (79.4–83.6), while maintaining competitive semantic performance and showing strong consistency in responses. The pipeline successfully generates 1.25M instruction–response pairs from WordNet and trains a specialized 12B model with modest resources.
AI.conclusion: Expert-curated structured lexical resources like WordNet can substitute for massive text corpora to build specialized conversational AI systems in low-resource languages. The demonstrated Hindi pipeline serves as a proof of concept that can be adapted to other languages with WordNet, offering a practical and scalable route to specialized AI and narrowing the AI accessibility gap for hundreds of under-resourced languages.

title: Accelerating Skill Assessment in Chess: A Drift-Diffusion-Enhanced Elo Rating System
comment: Accepted at the IEEE Conference on Games (IEEE CoG) 2026
summary: Rating systems such as Elo serve as the gold standard for matchmaking in competitive chess. However, they inherently suffer from response lag due to their exclusive reliance on match outcomes, neglecting the granular quality of gameplay. Nevertheless, incorporating move-by-move information into rating adjustments presents a significant challenge given the substantial noise and the vastness of the game-state space. To address this, we propose the Drift-Diffusion-Enhanced Elo Rating System (DD-Elo), a novel skill assessment framework inspired by the drift diffusion model (DDM) from cognitive neuroscience. By modeling skill expression as a decision-making process, our model integrates move-level data to capture rapid skill fluctuations. We provide a rigorous mathematical derivation proving that DD-Elo maintains a bounded deviation from the traditional Elo system, ensuring theoretical alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DD-Elo adapts to skill changes faster than Elo. Our findings suggest that DD-Elo offers an explainable, highly responsive, and backward-compatible solution for chess rating ecosystems. The implementation code is publicly available at https://github.com/Aquila-zhou1/DD-Elo .

AI.tldr: Introduces DD-Elo, a chess rating system that augments Elo with move-level data via a drift-diffusion decision model to react faster to skill changes while staying close to standard Elo.
AI.motivation: Classical Elo ratings only use game outcomes, causing slow adaptation to rapid or short-term skill changes and ignoring the rich information in move sequences. Directly using move-level data is hard because chess states are huge and move quality is noisy. The paper aims to build a rating system that uses fine-grained move data, handles noise, adapts quickly, and remains compatible with existing Elo ratings.
AI.method: Model players’ skill expression during a game as a drift-diffusion decision process derived from the DDM in cognitive neuroscience. Use the drift parameters inferred from move-level behavior to update player ratings within an Elo-like framework. Provide a mathematical analysis proving that the resulting DD-Elo ratings stay within a bounded deviation from the ratings produced by standard Elo under the same data. Implement and empirically evaluate DD-Elo on chess datasets by comparing its responsiveness and stability against Elo.
AI.result: Theoretically, DD-Elo is shown to stay within a provable bound of standard Elo ratings, ensuring compatibility. Empirically, on chess data, DD-Elo tracks players’ performance and skill changes more quickly than Elo while remaining stable, demonstrating that move-level modeling via drift-diffusion improves the responsiveness of rating updates without diverging drastically from existing ratings.
AI.conclusion: DD-Elo is an explainable, backward-compatible enhancement of Elo that incorporates move-by-move decision information via a drift-diffusion model. It alleviates response lag and captures rapid skill fluctuations better than classical Elo while maintaining close alignment with it. This makes DD-Elo a practical, drop-in improvement for existing chess rating ecosystems, and an open-source implementation is provided for adoption and further research.

title: Dynamic-dLLM: Dynamic Cache-Budget and Adaptive Parallel Decoding for Training-Free Acceleration of Diffusion LLM
summary: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) offer a promising alternative to autoregressive models, excelling in text generation tasks due to their bidirectional attention mechanisms. However, their computational complexity scales on the order of L cubed with the sequence length L. This poses significant challenges for long-sequence and real-time applications, primarily due to the lack of compatibility with key-value caching and the non-autoregressive nature of denoising steps. Existing acceleration methods rely on static caching or parallel decoding strategies, which fail to account for the dynamic behavior of token properties across layers and decoding steps. We propose Dynamic-dLLM, a training-free framework that enhances dLLM inference efficiency through two components: Dynamic Cache Updating (DCU), which adaptively allocates cache-update budgets based on layer-wise token dynamics, and Adaptive Parallel Decoding (APD), which dynamically calibrates decoding thresholds to balance generation quality and efficiency. Extensive experiments on models like LLaDA-8B-Instruct, LLaDA-1.5, and Dream-v0-7B-Instruct across benchmarks such as MMLU, GSM8K, and HumanEval demonstrate that Dynamic-dLLM significantly improves inference speed. It attains an average speedup exceeding 3 times while maintaining performance. Dynamic-dLLM outperforms state-of-the-art acceleration methods and provides a plug-and-play solution for efficient dLLM deployment without compromising performance. The code is available at https://github.com/TianyiWu233/DYNAMIC-DLLM.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Dynamic-dLLM, a training-free framework that accelerates diffusion LLMs by dynamically updating caches and adaptively controlling parallel decoding, achieving over 3× speedup while preserving task performance.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs have attractive bidirectional attention and strong generation quality but suffer from O(L^3) complexity and incompatibility with standard KV caching, making them inefficient for long or real-time applications. Existing acceleration approaches are static and ignore how token importance and noise levels evolve across layers and denoising steps, leading to suboptimal speed–quality trade-offs.
AI.method: The authors propose Dynamic-dLLM, which has two main components: (1) Dynamic Cache Updating (DCU), which monitors token dynamics across layers and selectively updates key-value caches only for tokens or layers that require it, under a constrained cache-update budget; and (2) Adaptive Parallel Decoding (APD), which runs multiple denoising steps in parallel but dynamically adjusts the acceptance thresholds or step size based on model confidence or token stability, thereby maintaining output quality while exploiting non-autoregressive parallelism. The framework is training-free and plug-and-play, applied at inference on existing diffusion LLMs such as LLaDA and Dream without modifying their weights.
AI.result: On several diffusion LLMs (LLaDA-8B-Instruct, LLaDA-1.5, Dream-v0-7B-Instruct) and benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval), Dynamic-dLLM achieves more than 3× average inference speedup over standard dLLM decoding while retaining nearly the same accuracy and generation quality. It consistently outperforms prior acceleration baselines based on static caching or naive parallel decoding in both speed and performance retention.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic-dLLM shows that dynamically allocating cache updates and adaptively controlling parallel denoising can substantially mitigate the cubic-time bottleneck of diffusion LLMs without retraining. The approach serves as a practical, plug-and-play inference-time optimization that makes dLLMs more viable for long-sequence and latency-sensitive applications, setting a new state of the art in dLLM acceleration.

title: Governing Actions, Not Agents: Institutional Attestation as a Governance Model for Autonomous AI Systems
summary: Autonomous AI agents may begin to perform consequential, irreversible actions such as clinical prescribing and production software deployment. This paper observes that human institutions have governed powerful autonomous actors not by monitoring their reasoning but by requiring independently attested evidence at the point of consequential action. We formalise this institutional pattern as a computational governance model for AI agent systems. Under the proposed model, an agent retains full autonomy over planning and reasoning but holds no execution authority over designated high-risk actions. Execution is conditional on preconditions that are each independently attested by a separate authoritative source, cryptographically bound to a declared intent, and evaluated by a deterministic policy. Decisions are recorded in a tamper-evident log amenable to independent re-verification. We present a proof-of-concept implementation and illustrate the model with examples from software deployment and clinical prescribing.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a governance model for autonomous AI agents that restricts their ability to directly execute high-risk actions, instead requiring independently attested preconditions and tamper-evident logging.
AI.motivation: As AI agents gain the ability to autonomously perform consequential and irreversible actions (e.g., clinical prescribing, production software deployment), existing oversight mechanisms that focus on internal reasoning become impractical or insufficient. Human institutions historically have governed powerful autonomous actors (like professionals or organizations) by controlling and auditing evidence at the point of action. The paper is motivated by the need to adapt this proven institutional pattern to AI agent systems to ensure safety, accountability, and trust without micromanaging the agents’ internal processes.
AI.method: The authors formalize an institutional pattern of governance as a computational model for AI agent systems. In this model, agents keep full autonomy over planning and reasoning but are stripped of direct execution authority over designated high-risk actions. Instead, these actions can only be executed if pre-defined preconditions are met. Each precondition must be independently attested by an authoritative source, cryptographically bound to the agent’s declared intent, and then checked by a deterministic policy engine. All decisions and evidence are stored in a tamper-evident log that allows later independent re-verification. They implement a proof-of-concept system and demonstrate it on scenarios involving production software deployment and clinical prescribing.
AI.result: The result is a formal computational governance framework and a proof-of-concept implementation that shows the feasibility of separating autonomous reasoning from execution authority for high-risk actions. The examples in software deployment and clinical prescribing demonstrate how independent attestations, cryptographic binding, deterministic policies, and tamper-evident logging can constrain AI agents’ ability to perform dangerous actions while preserving their planning autonomy.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that governance of autonomous AI agents can effectively mirror existing institutional approaches to overseeing powerful human actors by controlling evidence and authority at the point of action rather than surveilling internal reasoning. Their proposed model provides a practical, auditable, and technically implementable way to restrict high-risk actions through independent attestations and verifiable logs, enabling safer deployment of autonomous AI agents in critical domains such as software engineering and healthcare.

title: Thinking Like a Scientist? A Structural Study of LLM-Generated Research Methods
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to guide research methodology, yet their default methodological tendencies under minimal prompting remain unclear. Here, we prompt GPT-5.1, Gemini 3 Pro, and DeepSeek-V3.2 with an LLM-extracted research question from each of 1,000 recent arXiv computer-science papers and compare the resulting methodology suggestions against a paper-derived experimental inventory. Since we provide only the research question, the differences we measure reflect initial suggestions and not how optimal those suggestions are. We extract structured method features from both sources, map them into a shared taxonomy, and quantify divergence across multiple taxonomy dimensions including model provider, dataset task type, and evaluation metric type. The strongest imbalance appears in provider choice, with Jensen-Shannon divergence about 3-5x larger than any other taxonomy dimension. Other/Academic single-occurrence models are underrepresented by 23-24 percentage points, while reused academic/community models are slightly overrepresented (4-6pp). LLMs also suggest a much narrower range of methods overall: the effective number of model entities contracts from 1,232 to 59-96, and inter-LLM rank correlations (0.55-0.68) generally exceed LLM-to-paper correlations (0.33-0.56), so the distortions are largely shared across models. Popularity baselines, BM25 retrieval calibration, and paper-level similarity tests confirm that the outputs are query-specific responses, but filtered through a narrower set of options. Researchers who rely on LLM suggestions without cross-checking therefore risk narrowing their methodological search space toward a more concentrated default.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how large language models, under minimal prompting, propose research methods compared to real-world methods used in recent computer science papers, finding that LLMs systematically narrow and bias the methodological search space.
AI.motivation: As researchers increasingly ask LLMs for help designing experiments or methods, it is important to understand the LLMs’ default methodological biases when given only a research question. These defaults could shape scientific practice, potentially narrowing or skewing the methods that researchers consider, even when many other viable options exist. The authors aim to empirically quantify these tendencies at scale and compare them to the diversity of methods actually used in current computer science research.
AI.method: The authors collect 1,000 recent arXiv computer-science papers and automatically extract a research question from each using an LLM. For each extracted question, they then prompt three state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-5.1, Gemini 3 Pro, DeepSeek-V3.2) to suggest an appropriate research methodology. In parallel, they construct an experimental inventory from the original papers, extracting structured method features (e.g., model choices, dataset/task types, evaluation metrics). Both LLM-generated and paper-derived methods are mapped into a shared taxonomy of methodological elements. They then compute divergences (e.g., Jensen–Shannon divergence) and correlations across taxonomy dimensions—such as model provider type, dataset task type, and evaluation metric type—and compare LLM–LLM similarity to LLM–paper similarity. They also use popularity baselines, BM25 retrieval calibration, and paper-level similarity tests to check that LLM outputs are specific to the research questions rather than generic or popularity-only responses.
AI.result: The largest divergence between LLM-suggested and paper-derived methods is in model provider choice, where Jensen–Shannon divergence is 3–5 times larger than for other taxonomy dimensions. LLMs substantially under-suggest Other/Academic single-occurrence models (by 23–24 percentage points) and modestly over-suggest reused academic/community models (by 4–6 percentage points). Overall, LLMs propose a far narrower set of methods than those found in the papers: the effective number of distinct model entities falls from 1,232 in the paper inventory to only 59–96 in LLM suggestions. Rank correlations among LLMs (0.55–0.68) are higher than correlations between each LLM and the paper inventory (0.33–0.56), indicating that the distortions are largely shared across models. Auxiliary analyses using popularity baselines, BM25 calibration, and similarity tests show that LLM outputs are tailored to each research question but filtered through a restricted model set rather than simply mirroring popularity statistics.
AI.conclusion: LLMs, when asked to propose methods from just a research question, generate query-specific but methodologically constrained suggestions that are more similar to one another than to the diverse practices found in actual research papers. They systematically underrepresent less common academic models and compress the range of model choices, effectively narrowing the methodological search space. As a result, researchers who rely heavily on uncritical LLM guidance may converge on a more concentrated and less diverse set of methods than current practice supports. The paper underscores the need for users to cross-check LLM-generated methodological advice and for future LLM design to explicitly address diversity and coverage in research-method recommendations.

title: COrigami: An AI Pipeline for Co-Designing Flat-Foldable Visually Recognisable Origami
summary: While generative AI has achieved remarkable success in solving problems with verifiable solutions, generating physical art that satisfies both strict geometric constraints and subjective visual aesthetics remains a challenge. This paper presents an approach to tackle these difficulties in the domain of computational origami, a mathematically rigid environment that grounds artistic design within the equations of flat foldability. We present COrigami, an end-to-end AI-driven pipeline that assists the design cycle by generating crease patterns from natural language. Our pipeline involves generating a semantic stick figure, computing a base packing, solving for a flat-foldable crease pattern, shaping the flat-folded crease pattern, and refining the generated model using reinforcement learning driven by an autonomous aesthetic evaluation loop. Our system acts as a highly effective collaborative assistant, generating structural starting points that human artists can further expand and shape. By integrating algorithmic optimisation with autonomous aesthetic critique, this work demonstrates how AI systems can satisfy multi-objective physical constraints to enable reliable, mathematically grounded co-creativity.

AI.tldr: COrigami is an AI pipeline that turns natural language descriptions into origami crease patterns that satisfy both mathematical foldability and aesthetic preferences.
AI.motivation: Generative AI excels at tasks with clear, verifiable solutions but struggles when outputs must meet both strict physical/geometric constraints and subjective artistic quality. Computational origami is a domain where designs must obey rigorous mathematical rules of flat foldability while also achieving appealing artistic forms.
