There is a need for tools that help artists co-create within these constraints by automating complex geometric reasoning while still accounting for aesthetics.
AI.method: The authors build an end-to-end pipeline, COrigami, that maps natural language prompts to origami crease patterns. The pipeline: (1) generates a semantic stick figure representation of the desired shape; (2) computes a base packing that encodes how paper regions correspond to semantic parts; (3) solves for a flat-foldable crease pattern consistent with origami mathematics; (4) shapes the resulting flat-folded pattern into an intended 3D form; and (5) refines the model using reinforcement learning guided by an autonomous aesthetic evaluation loop. The system combines algorithmic optimization for geometric/physical constraints with a learned aesthetic critic that scores and improves designs.
AI.result: COrigami can automatically generate crease patterns from natural language that are both flat-foldable and visually appealing enough to serve as strong starting points for human origami artists. The system reliably respects mathematical constraints while producing structurally meaningful and artistically useful designs, functioning effectively as a collaborative assistant rather than a fully autonomous artist.
AI.conclusion: By integrating constraint-based geometric optimization with reinforcement-learning-based aesthetic refinement, COrigami demonstrates that AI systems can simultaneously satisfy hard physical constraints and soft artistic objectives. This approach enables reliable, mathematically grounded co-creativity in computational origami, and suggests a general recipe for AI tools that support artistic design in other physically constrained domains.

title: From Structure to Synergy: A Survey of Vision-Language Perception Paradigm Evolution in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have recently made remarkable progress in unifying vision-language understanding and reasoning, especially following the introduction of models such as OpenAI's O-series and DeepSeek's R-series, which have driven a paradigm shift toward perception-centric intelligence. However, there remains a lack of systematic surveys that examine perception from a truly unified vision-language perspective -- one that treats vision and language as an inseparable modality. Existing reviews are often fragmented, focusing separately on either vision or language, and thus rarely capture the cross-modal evolution of perception as an integrated capability. To bridge this gap, we present the first systematic survey of unified vision-language perception in MLLMs. Specifically, we (1) formalize MLLM perception as an intrinsic, unified vision-language capability analogous to human innate perception, (2) introduce a five-stage taxonomy tracing the paradigm evolution of MLLM perception and survey representative methods and milestones at each phase, and (3) identify open challenges and outline promising research directions toward truly general, unified multimodal intelligence. We hope our study will provide both a foundational understanding and an actionable roadmap to foster further innovation on the path toward artificial general intelligence (AGI).

AI.tldr: A survey paper that systematically reviews unified vision-language perception in multimodal large language models (MLLMs), proposing a taxonomy, summarizing methods, and outlining challenges and future directions.
AI.motivation: Despite rapid advances in MLLMs like OpenAI's O-series and DeepSeek's R-series, there is no systematic, unified survey of perception that treats vision and language as a single inseparable modality. Existing reviews are fragmented, typically handling vision and language separately, and thus fail to capture how perception emerges and evolves as an integrated cross-modal capability. The paper aims to fill this gap to better understand and guide research toward perception-centric multimodal intelligence and, ultimately, AGI.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic literature survey of MLLMs with a focus on unified vision-language perception. They (1) propose a formal definition of MLLM perception as an intrinsic, human-analogous capability spanning both vision and language; (2) design a five-stage taxonomy describing the historical and technical evolution of perception paradigms in MLLMs and categorize representative models and methods within this framework; and (3) analyze open problems and synthesize forward-looking research directions for building more general multimodal intelligence.
AI.result: The survey delivers: (1) a formal conceptualization of unified vision-language perception in MLLMs; (2) a five-stage evolutionary taxonomy that organizes prior work and highlights key milestones in the development of perception-centric MLLMs; and (3) a structured overview of remaining challenges and future research themes required for progressing toward truly general, unified multimodal intelligence.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that perception in MLLMs should be understood as a unified, intrinsic vision-language capability, rather than as separate vision or language components. By organizing the field into a five-stage taxonomy and summarizing representative methods and open challenges, the survey provides both a conceptual foundation and a practical roadmap to guide future research. The authors argue that advancing unified vision-language perception is critical for moving MLLMs closer to robust, general multimodal intelligence and, ultimately, AGI.

title: The Verification Horizon: No Silver Bullet for Coding Agent Rewards
comment: Authors are listed alphabetically by their first names
summary: A classical intuition holds that verifying a solution is easier than producing one. For today's coding agents, this intuition is being inverted: as foundation models develop stronger reasoning capabilities and engineering harnesses grow more sophisticated, generating complex candidate solutions is no longer difficult -- reliably verifying them has become the harder problem. Every verifier we can build is only a proxy for human intent, never the intent itself. This makes verification subject to a twofold difficulty: first, intent is underspecified by nature, making it inherently hard to faithfully check whether it has been fulfilled; second, during model training, optimization widens the gap between proxy and intent -- manifesting as reward hacking or signal saturation. To address this, we characterize the quality of verification signals along three dimensions -- scalability, faithfulness, and robustness -- and argue that achieving all three simultaneously is the central challenge. We further study four reward constructions: a test verifier for general coding tasks, a rubric verifier for frontend tasks, the user as verifier for real-world agent tasks, and an automated agent verifier for long-horizon tasks. Across different task types and policy capability levels, we conduct in-depth analysis and experiments on the core challenges of reward design and how to more effectively leverage reward signals. Experiments show that targeted verification design can effectively suppress reward hacking, improve task completion quality, and achieve significant gains across multiple internal and public benchmarks. These experiences collectively point to a core observation: no fixed reward function can remain effective as policy capability continues to grow; and verification must co-evolve with the generator.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that for advanced coding agents, generating complex solutions has become easier than reliably verifying them, and that reward/verification mechanisms must be carefully designed and continually adapted as models improve.
AI.motivation: Traditional intuition and complexity theory suggest that checking a solution is easier than finding one, but with powerful foundation models, generating many plausible solutions is cheap while systematically verifying alignment with human intent is hard. Existing reward signals are only proxies for intent and are vulnerable to underspecification and reward hacking. The paper aims to understand what makes verification good and how to design better reward mechanisms for real coding and agent tasks.
AI.method: The authors conceptually decompose verification quality into three axes—scalability (can it cover many tasks and samples cheaply), faithfulness (does it actually reflect human intent), and robustness (is it resistant to exploitation and reward hacking). They then instantiate and study four concrete reward/verification constructions: (1) a test-based verifier for general coding tasks, (2) a rubric-based verifier for frontend tasks, (3) using real users as verifiers for deployed agent tasks, and (4) an automated agent-as-verifier for long-horizon tasks. Through experiments across internal and public benchmarks and with policies of varying capabilities, they analyze how different reward designs behave, how they fail, and how to better leverage the feedback signals.
AI.result: Empirical results show that carefully targeted verification design can suppress reward hacking, increase true task success rates, and significantly boost benchmark performance in coding and agent settings. Different verifier constructions exhibit distinct strengths and weaknesses along scalability, faithfulness, and robustness, and naive or static rewards degrade as models get stronger.
AI.conclusion: The central challenge in training and controlling advanced coding/agent models is to build verification signals that are simultaneously scalable, faithful to human intent, and robust to exploitation. No single, static reward function remains adequate as model capability grows; instead, verification mechanisms must be iteratively redesigned and co-evolve with the underlying generative policies to maintain alignment and performance.

title: Phonetic and semantic analyses of spoken corpora of Beijing and Taiwan Mandarin indicate that the neutral tone is a lexical tone
summary: The neutral, or floating, tone of Mandarin Chinese is a tone with an enigmatic set of properties. It has been described as a reduced tone, or as a tone that sometimes is lexically fixed but that can also be toneless. In two-syllable words, it is found only on the second syllable, but single-syllable words can also have the neutral tone. We present a corpus-based study of the phonetic realization of the neutral tone in spontaneous conversational speech corpora of Beijing Mandarin and Taiwan Mandarin. We show that the neutral tone has its own tonal target, just as the four lexical tones of Mandarin. We also show that disyllabic words with a neutral tone have pitch contours that have a pitch component that depends on the tone on the first syllable, just as has been observed for two-syllable words with a lexical tone on the second syllable (Chuang et al., 2026). Furthermore, words with a floating tone have word-specific pitch signatures, which have also been documented for single-syllable words (Jin et al., 2026) as well as two-syllable words (Lu et al., 2026b). These word-specific pitch signatures are shown to be predictable to some extent from words' contextualized embeddings, as previously reported for lexical tones (Chuang et al., 2026; Lu et al., 2026b). As there is also considerable variability in the realization of lexical tones, we propose that the neutral tone is, in fact, a lexical tone in both Taiwan Mandarin and Beijing Mandarin. We document both similarities and differences in the realization of the floating tone in these two varieties and provide evidence, using contextualized embeddings, that some of the observed differences may arise from differences in the meanings of the words as used in the two corpora.

AI.tldr: Corpus-based study argues Mandarin neutral tone is a full lexical tone with its own target and word-specific pitch patterns in Beijing and Taiwan Mandarin.
AI.motivation: The Mandarin neutral (floating) tone has long been puzzling: described as reduced, sometimes toneless, restricted to certain positions, and inconsistently analyzed across dialects. The authors aim to clarify its phonological and phonetic status by examining how it is actually realized in natural speech and whether it patterns like the canonical four lexical tones.
AI.method: They conduct a corpus-based phonetic analysis of spontaneous conversational speech from Beijing Mandarin and Taiwan Mandarin. They measure pitch contours of words containing a neutral tone, compare them to words with lexical tones, and analyze how the second-syllable neutral tone’s pitch depends on the first-syllable tone. They also extract word-specific pitch signatures and relate them to contextualized word embeddings (distributional semantic representations) to see how well meaning and context predict tonal realizations.
AI.result: The neutral tone shows a consistent tonal target, analogous to the four lexical tones. In disyllabic words, its pitch contour includes a component conditioned by the tone of the first syllable, mirroring known cross-syllabic tonal interactions for fully lexical tones. Words containing a neutral tone exhibit stable, word-specific pitch signatures similar to those previously found for mono- and disyllabic lexical-tone words. These signatures are partly predictable from contextualized embeddings, indicating that semantic and contextual factors help explain variation. There are systematic similarities but also dialect-specific differences between Beijing and Taiwan Mandarin realizations.
AI.conclusion: Given its stable tonal target, word-specific pitch signatures, and context-sensitive variation comparable to lexical tones, the authors argue that the neutral tone should be analyzed as a full-fledged lexical tone in both Beijing and Taiwan Mandarin rather than as merely reduced or toneless. Cross-variety differences in its realization can be partly attributed to differences in word meaning and usage patterns, as captured by contextualized embeddings.

title: How Do Tool-Augmented LLM Agents Perform on Real-World Energy Analytics Tasks?
summary:
Agentic benchmarks have emerged across general-purpose and domain-specific settings, including finance, coding, law, and drug discovery, yet energy-domain evaluations remain largely limited to static knowledge recall. This is a critical gap for a sector that requires live data retrieval, specialized regulatory and market knowledge, and multi-step quantitative reasoning under real-world constraints.
  We present an empirical study of tool-augmented LLM agents on real-world energy market analytics tasks. Our evaluation environment includes 243 expert-curated problems across three categories: (1) Market Data Retrieval and Analysis, (2) Knowledge Retrieval and Interpretation, and (3) Advanced Quantitative Modeling and Decision Analytics. Tasks include price and demand analysis, tariff impact modeling, asset revenue and returns estimation, hedging strategy analysis, and optimization modeling, with problems spanning multiple difficulty levels.
  Agents are equipped with a configurable suite of domain tools, including live electricity market APIs for major U.S. ISOs, regulatory docket search, utility tariff databases, asset optimization models, and retrieval-augmented generation over energy market documents. We assess agent responses using a multi-dimensional evaluation protocol that scores approach correctness, answer accuracy, attribute alignment, and source validity, with category-aware routing to match scoring criteria to question type. We evaluate both closed-source and open-source LLMs, providing a comparative analysis of how model capability and domain tooling interact in a high-stakes professional domain. Key artifacts are publicly released to support reproducibility and future research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an empirical benchmark of tool-augmented LLM agents for real-world energy market analytics tasks, going beyond static knowledge recall to evaluate live data access, domain reasoning, and decision-making.
AI.motivation: Existing agentic benchmarks cover domains like finance and coding, but the energy sector lacks realistic evaluations even though it critically depends on live data, regulatory and market complexity, and complex quantitative reasoning. This gap hinders reliable deployment of LLM agents in high-stakes energy applications.
AI.method: The authors build an evaluation environment of 243 expert-curated tasks across three categories: (1) Market Data Retrieval and Analysis, (2) Knowledge Retrieval and Interpretation, and (3) Advanced Quantitative Modeling and Decision Analytics. They equip LLM agents with a configurable set of domain tools—such as live ISO market APIs, regulatory search, tariff databases, asset optimization models, and RAG over energy documents—and design a multi-dimensional evaluation protocol that scores approach correctness, answer accuracy, attribute alignment, and source validity, using category-aware routing to apply appropriate criteria per task type. They then test both closed-source and open-source LLMs under this framework.
AI.result: The study yields comparative performance data showing how different LLMs, combined with specialized tools, handle real-world energy market analytics tasks of varying difficulty. It highlights the interaction between model capability and domain tooling, and produces a set of public artifacts (tasks, tools, and evaluation protocol) that others can reuse.
AI.conclusion: Tool-augmented LLM agents can be systematically evaluated on realistic, high-stakes energy market tasks using the proposed benchmark and protocol. The work fills an important gap in agentic benchmarking for the energy domain and provides reusable infrastructure to drive future research and more trustworthy deployment of LLM agents in energy markets.

title: Charting the Growth of Social-Physical HRI (spHRI): A Systematic Review Pipeline Augmented by Small Language Models
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables, Companion Proceedings of the 21st ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction
summary: Social-physical human-robot interaction (spHRI) has grown rapidly across robotics, human-computer interaction, human-robot interaction, and haptics. Yet, fragmented terminology and inconsistent methodologies make systematic synthesis difficult. To support scalable review practices, we evaluated the extent to which small language models (SLMs; < 1.5B parameters) can assist with title and abstract screening for a large spHRI systematic review. While no SLMs matched human reviewers' performance, the models operated locally and screened papers orders of magnitude faster. The combined SLM ensemble identified 39 papers reviewers missed, representing 10.29% of the final relevant dataset. These results demonstrate that SLMs can augment, rather than replace, expert reviewers and make large-scale literature reviews accessible and sustainable.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether small language models can effectively help screen titles and abstracts in a large systematic review of social-physical human-robot interaction literature.
AI.motivation: Systematic reviews in social-physical human-robot interaction are hard to conduct at scale because of fragmented terminology, inconsistent methods, and the sheer volume of papers to screen. There is a need for scalable, efficient tools that can support, but not replace, expert reviewers during the early screening stages.
AI.method: The authors applied several small language models (each with fewer than 1.5B parameters) to assist with title and abstract screening in a large spHRI systematic review. They compared the performance of these SLMs and their ensemble against human reviewers in terms of accuracy and speed, and analyzed which relevant papers were found by models versus humans.
AI.result: No individual SLM matched the performance of human reviewers. However, all SLMs ran locally and screened papers orders of magnitude faster than humans. An ensemble of SLMs identified 39 relevant papers that human reviewers missed, accounting for 10.29% of the final set of relevant studies.
AI.conclusion: Small language models are not yet adequate replacements for human reviewers in systematic review screening, but they can substantially augment experts. By operating quickly and locally and by identifying additional relevant papers, SLMs can help make large-scale, complex literature reviews in spHRI more feasible and sustainable.

title: What We are Missing in Multimodal LLM Evaluation?
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can process diverse inputs, e.g., text, images, audio, and video, and generate textual responses. While their capabilities have advanced rapidly, evaluation of such models has not kept pace. Most existing evaluation benchmarks are limited to isolated tasks and reveal little about whether a model integrates information across modalities. We examine current means for evaluating MLLMs and review the existing benchmark taxonomy to identify gaps, including temporal-spatial coherence, physical world understanding, multimodal consistency, and selective attention. Addressing these gaps is essential for measuring real progress in multimodal intelligence and exposing capability boundaries.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques current evaluation benchmarks for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and highlights key missing dimensions needed to truly assess multimodal intelligence.
AI.motivation: MLLMs can now handle text, images, audio, and video, but their evaluation lags behind, focusing mostly on narrow, single-task benchmarks. This mismatch risks overestimating progress because we lack tools to see whether models genuinely integrate information across modalities. The authors want to systematically reveal where current benchmarks fall short.
AI.method: The authors survey and analyze existing evaluation approaches and benchmark taxonomies for MLLMs. Through this review, they categorize what is currently measured and then identify unaddressed aspects of multimodal reasoning and integration, such as temporal-spatial coherence, physical world understanding, multimodal consistency, and selective attention.
AI.result: They show that current benchmarks largely ignore crucial dimensions of multimodal intelligence. Specifically, there are few or no systematic tests for a model’s ability to maintain coherent understanding over time and space, reason about real-world physical constraints, remain consistent across modalities, or focus attention selectively on relevant multimodal cues.
AI.conclusion: To accurately track progress and reveal limitations of MLLMs, future benchmarks must incorporate tasks that test temporal-spatial coherence, physical world understanding, multimodal consistency, and selective attention. Filling these gaps is necessary to measure real advances in multimodal intelligence rather than improvements on narrow, isolated tasks.

title: ProfileFoundry: A Synthetic Person-Object Substrate for Privacy, Memory, and Tool-Use Evaluation in LLM Agent
summary: Foundation-model research increasingly needs data about people: user state, personal histories, relationships, contact-like fields, documents, and longitudinal updates. Real user data is difficult to share, perturb, audit, or redistribute responsibly, while independently generated fake fields rarely preserve the cross-field and temporal consistency needed for controlled evaluation. We present PROFILEFOUNDRY, a deterministic generator and fixed reference release of 100,000 adult synthetic Person Objects across eight locales. Each object combines a typed current snapshot, household, family, and employer links, snapshot-aligned events, normalized relational views, and generation provenance. The release contains 709,228 events, 40,338 households, 52,491 employers, and 518,564 directed relationship edges. We report evidence in separate categories: selected population-marginal comparisons, per-object invariant checks, release-wide referential and temporal closure, and coincidence/provenance screens. PROFILEFOUNDRY is not a population-fidelity model, a rendered-text corpus, or a formal privacy mechanism. Instead, it is a responsible synthetic source layer for constructing downstream foundation-model evaluations involving memory, privacy, document understanding, record linkage, and agent state while keeping the synthetic person behind each artifact inspectable

AI.tldr: PROFILEFOUNDRY is a deterministic generator and public release of 100,000 richly structured synthetic adult profiles for safely evaluating foundation models on people-centric tasks.
AI.motivation: Foundation-model research increasingly needs realistic, people-centric data (states, histories, relationships, documents, longitudinal records), but real user data is hard to share and protect, and naive synthetic data often lacks cross-field and temporal consistency needed for rigorous evaluation.
AI.method: The authors build PROFILEFOUNDRY, a deterministic system that generates synthetic "Person Objects" across eight locales. Each object includes typed current state, family/household/employer links, time-aligned events, normalized relational representations, and detailed generation provenance. They then validate the dataset using population-level marginal comparisons, per-object invariant checks, global referential and temporal consistency checks, and coincidence/provenance screening.
AI.result: PROFILEFOUNDRY yields a fixed reference dataset of 100,000 adult synthetic persons with 709,228 events, 40,338 households, 52,491 employers, and 518,564 directed relationship edges. Empirical checks show good internal consistency (referential and temporal closure, invariants) and plausible external characteristics without claiming full population fidelity or formal privacy guarantees.
AI.conclusion: PROFILEFOUNDRY should be viewed not as a population-fidelity simulator or privacy guarantee mechanism, but as a responsibly designed synthetic base layer for constructing downstream evaluation tasks for foundation models involving memory, privacy, document understanding, record linkage, and agent state, while keeping each underlying synthetic person fully inspectable.

title: OpenFinGym: A Verifiable Multi-Task Gym Environment for Evaluating Quant Agents
summary: Although large language model agents are increasingly applied to quantitative-finance workflows, their evaluation remains fragmented across isolated tasks, while the financial relevance of benchmark tasks is often overlooked. Yet financial workflows are inherently multi-stage, spanning interdependent tasks such as forecasting, strategy construction, risk management, and trading. Existing platforms typically focus on a single task, and can therefore overstate agent competence and fail to reveal weaknesses in generalization, real-market interaction, and financially meaningful decision-making. We introduce OpenFinGym, a unified gym environment for quantitative-finance agent development that covers forecasting, market generation, real-time trading, and fraud detection under a single execution and verification interface. OpenFinGym additionally provides an automated task-construction pipeline that turns quantitative finance publications into executable task packages; a containerised runtime with a host-side verifier service that supports scalable agent rollouts and prevents runtime train-test leakage; a paper trading engine with a low-latency data-stream design; deferred-resolution support for long-horizon and event-market forecasts; and integration for SFT and RL post-training

AI.tldr: The paper presents OpenFinGym, a unified environment for developing and evaluating LLM-based agents in realistic, multi-stage quantitative finance workflows.
AI.motivation: Evaluation of LLM agents in quantitative finance is currently fragmented and often not aligned with real financial workflows. Existing benchmarks focus on isolated, single tasks (e.g., only forecasting or only trading), which can overestimate agent capabilities and miss weaknesses in generalization, market interaction, and financially meaningful decision-making. Since real quantitative-finance processes are multi-stage and interdependent—forecasting, strategy construction, risk management, trading—there is a need for a comprehensive, realistic, and technically robust evaluation platform.
AI.method: The authors design OpenFinGym, a unified gym-style environment that encapsulates multiple core quantitative-finance tasks within a single execution and verification interface. They implement: (1) a framework that covers forecasting, market generation, real-time trading, and fraud detection; (2) an automated pipeline that transforms quantitative finance research papers into executable task packages; (3) a containerized runtime with a host-side verifier to scale agent rollouts and prevent train-test leakage; (4) a low-latency paper-trading engine for streaming data; (5) deferred-resolution support for long-horizon and event-driven forecasts; and (6) integration hooks for supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL) post-training on the provided tasks.
AI.result: OpenFinGym provides a practical environment where LLM-based and other agents can be evaluated end-to-end across realistic, multi-stage quantitative finance workflows. It enables scalable, leakage-safe experimentation, supports a rich variety of tasks derived automatically from existing literature, and allows agents to interact with a low-latency, paper-trading setup and event-driven markets. The result is a more holistic and finance-relevant assessment of agent performance than existing single-task benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: OpenFinGym addresses the gap between fragmented, narrow benchmarks and the complex, multi-stage reality of quantitative finance. By offering an integrated, secure, and extensible environment that spans forecasting, trading, risk, and fraud detection—plus automatic task construction from research papers and support for SFT/RL—the platform aims to standardize and elevate the development and evaluation of quantitative-finance agents, leading to more robust and practically useful models.

title: ConflictScore: Identifying and Measuring How Language Models Handle Conflicting Evidence
summary: Existing metrics for factuality and faithfulness evaluate whether an answer is supported or contradicted by its grounding documents, but they fail to capture when both supporting and contradicting evidence coexist. We introduce ConflictScore, a novel metric that quantifies how well a model's response acknowledges conflicting evidence in its grounding documents. Our framework decomposes responses into atomic claims, labels each claim against each grounding document, and then aggregates these labels into two complementary measures: ConflictScore-Count (CS-C), the proportion of claims exhibiting conflicts, and ConflictScore-Ratio (CS-R), the balance between supporting and contradicting evidence. We develop ConflictBench, a benchmark covering diverse forms of conflicts such as ambiguity, contradiction, and divergent opinions, to systematically evaluate our metric. Experiments show that ConflictScore effectively detects overconfident claims across domains and can serve as a corrective feedback mechanism that improves truthfulness on TruthfulQA.

AI.tldr: Introduces ConflictScore, a metric that evaluates how well model responses acknowledge conflicting evidence in source documents.
AI.motivation: Current factuality and faithfulness metrics only check if answers are supported or contradicted by sources, but cannot characterize situations where both supporting and contradicting evidence exist simultaneously. This limitation means overconfident or one-sided answers are not properly penalized when the evidence itself is mixed or conflicting.
AI.method: They decompose model responses into atomic claims and, for each claim, label its relation (supporting, contradicting, etc.) to each grounding document. These labels are aggregated into two measures: ConflictScore-Count (CS-C), which is the proportion of claims that have conflicting evidence across documents, and ConflictScore-Ratio (CS-R), which quantifies the balance between supporting and contradicting evidence. They also build ConflictBench, a benchmark with various conflict types (ambiguity, contradiction, divergent opinions) to test the metric.
AI.result: On ConflictBench and other domains, ConflictScore can reliably identify overconfident claims when evidence is mixed, better than prior factuality metrics. The metric is shown to correlate with conflict-awareness in answers and to be usable as a signal for detecting problematic responses.
AI.conclusion: ConflictScore captures nuanced evidence conflicts that traditional factuality metrics miss and can be used as feedback to steer models toward more calibrated and truthful behavior, as demonstrated by improvements on TruthfulQA when ConflictScore-based feedback is applied.

title: Instruction Bleed: Cross-Module Interference in Prompt-Composed Agentic Systems
comment: 8 pages, 2 tables. Accepted to the ICML 2026 Workshop on Failure Modes in Agentic AI (FAGEN), Seoul, South Korea
summary: Practitioners of prompt-composed agentic systems report a recurring failure mode: editing one prompt module silently shifts the behavior of others despite no shared variable or executable dependency. We formalize this as compositional behavioral leakage (CBL): interference between modules sharing a context window. CBL is enabled by architectural non-isolation: transformer self-attention provides no formal boundary between concatenated modules. We probe CBL on a deployed job-evaluation agent (Claude Sonnet 4.6, 144 trials) through a reusable three-channel protocol that perturbs non-focal modules along volume, content, and form. Only the content channel produces a detectable paired effect (Cohen's d = 0.63, bootstrap 95% CI excluding zero); no recommendation flipped -- a sub-threshold regime invisible to standard QA but compounding across the thousands of decisions a deployed agent makes. CBL is orthogonal to known agent-failure axes (adversarial injection, cognitive degradation, multi-agent fault propagation, privacy leakage). We contribute an operational definition, a reusable protocol, a falsifiable prediction set, and a system-class characterization, establishing cross-module interference measurement as a requirement for prompt-composed agent evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper defines and empirically studies how different prompt modules in an agent can unintentionally affect each other's behavior when concatenated in a shared context window.
AI.motivation: Practitioners observe that changing one prompt segment in a modular, prompt-composed agent sometimes unexpectedly alters the behavior of other, supposedly independent modules, even when there is no explicit shared variable or code dependency. This unpredictable interference undermines reliability and complicates maintenance of deployed LLM agents.
AI.method: The authors formalize this interference as compositional behavioral leakage (CBL) arising from the non-isolated nature of transformer self-attention over a shared context. They design a three-channel experimental protocol (varying volume, content, and form of non-focal modules) and apply it to a real job-evaluation agent built on Claude Sonnet 4.6. In 144 trials, they carefully perturb non-focal modules along each channel while measuring the behavioral changes of a focal module, using effect size statistics and bootstrap confidence intervals.
AI.result: Perturbations in content of non-focal modules cause a statistically significant change in the focal module's behavior (Cohen's d = 0.63 with a 95% bootstrap CI excluding zero), while changes in volume and form do not show detectable paired effects. No job recommendation flips occur, meaning the leakage is sub-threshold at the single-decision level but could accumulate across many decisions. The results show that CBL is a distinct phenomenon from other known failure modes like prompt injection, cognitive degradation, multi-agent faults, or privacy leakage.
AI.conclusion: The study provides an operational definition of compositional behavioral leakage, an experimental protocol for detecting it, a set of testable predictions, and a classification of which system classes are susceptible. The authors argue that measuring cross-module interference should be treated as a standard requirement when evaluating prompt-composed LLM agents, since unmeasured CBL can subtly but systematically bias large numbers of model decisions.

title: ProvenAI: Provenance-Native Traces of Evidence in Generated Answers
summary: Retrieval-augmented systems routinely present citations alongside generated answers, yet a citation does not confirm that the corresponding source meaningfully shaped the output. This paper introduces ProvenAI, a framework that decomposes transparency in multi-hop question answering into three independently measurable layers: answer correctness, citation fidelity against benchmark supporting evidence, and per-document influence under leave-one-resource-out intervention. Targeting the HotpotQA distractor benchmark through a seven-stage pipeline covering data normalisation, retrieval indexing, citation-aware answer generation, attribution auditing, ablation-based influence estimation, batch evaluation, and interactive inspection, ProvenAI evaluates 7,405 validation examples drawn from a canonical corpus of 509,300 passages. The system achieves 53.53% answer accuracy alongside a mean citation-fidelity score of 71.55%, and a worked example surfaces what we call the citation-influence gap: a clean citation audit co-occurring with a profile in which one cited source registers only weak influence while seven uncited sources demonstrably shift the output. We formalise the relationship between the implemented surface proxy and a token-level KL-divergence target through a stated faithfulness condition, ground the framework in causal-mediation analysis and database-provenance theory, and discuss how the three measurement layers compose with cryptographic provenance architectures emerging in autonomous scientific discovery. ProvenAI establishes that meaningful transparency in retrieval-grounded QA requires traceable links across retrieved, cited, and behaviourally influential evidence as three distinct, independently measured layers.

AI.tldr: ProvenAI is a framework to measure and analyze transparency in retrieval-augmented multi-hop QA systems by separating answer correctness, citation fidelity, and true document influence, revealing gaps between what is cited and what actually influences model outputs.
AI.motivation: Existing retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems show citations next to answers, but these citations do not guarantee that the cited sources truly shaped the generated text. There is a need for a principled, quantitative way to assess how transparent and faithful these systems are, especially in complex multi-hop QA where multiple documents may contribute differently to the final answer.
AI.method: The authors design ProvenAI, a seven-stage pipeline that operates on the HotpotQA distractor benchmark: (1) data normalization, (2) retrieval indexing, (3) citation-aware answer generation, (4) attribution auditing against benchmark supporting evidence, (5) ablation-based influence estimation using leave-one-resource-out interventions, (6) batch evaluation over thousands of examples, and (7) interactive inspection. They decomposed transparency into three measurable layers—answer correctness, citation fidelity, and per-document influence—and formalized their relationship using a faithfulness condition that connects a surface proxy to a token-level KL divergence objective. The framework is grounded in causal mediation analysis and database provenance theory.
AI.result: Applied to 7,405 validation questions over a corpus of 509,300 passages, ProvenAI yields 53.53% answer accuracy and an average citation-fidelity score of 71.55%. Through detailed case analysis, the authors identify a citation-influence gap: examples where the citations look correct by standard audits but the true behavioral influence profile shows that some cited documents weakly affect the answer while several uncited documents substantially change the model’s output when ablated.
AI.conclusion: The work shows that robust transparency in retrieval-grounded QA cannot rely solely on answer correctness or citation audits. Instead, it requires tracking three distinct but interacting layers: retrieved and benchmark-supporting evidence, citations actually shown to users, and the behaviorally influential sources identified via causal-style interventions. ProvenAI provides a concrete, scalable framework for measuring these layers and suggests how they may integrate with emerging cryptographic provenance mechanisms in future autonomous scientific discovery systems.

title: Accelerating Returns and the Qualitative Engine for Science
summary: Ray Kurzweil described a thesis of accelerating returns, which is the most influential narratives in discussions of technological progress. Its central claim is that advances in multiple technological fields, especially compute, artificial intelligence, brain science, and biotechnology, interact in such a way that progress becomes self-amplifying and approximately exponential. This paper gives a simple mathematical interpretation of that claim and then argues that, even if such acceleration is real, it does not by itself resolve the central problem of scientific discovery. The reason is that accelerating returns apply most naturally to executional and infrastructural capability, whereas genuine discovery often depends on a different capacity: qualitative reasoning about when a current framework is structurally inadequate and what conceptual move is needed next. Recent ARC-AGI-3 results sharpen this distinction: humans solve the benchmark at ceiling, whereas frontier AI systems remain below 1%, indicating that the gap between current AI and human flexible reasoning is still very large. At the same time, Demis Hassabis has emphasized that humans must retain their sense of meaning and what they choose to focus their lives on, a reminder that the future of AI is not only a technical forecast but also a question of what forms of human understanding are worth preserving and transmitting. This paper positions the Qualitative Engine for Science (QES) [3] as a response to that missing capacity. In this view, the Kurzweil theory helps explain why quantitative capability may accelerate, while QES addresses the central problem in scientific discovery that acceleration alone does not solve. Its value does not depend on when AGI arrives, but on the fact that the processes of scientific discovery themselves constitute a form of human wisdom worth preserving, organizing, and making accessible.

AI.tldr: Paper critiques Kurzweil’s ‘accelerating returns’ thesis by arguing that even exponential growth in computational and infrastructural capabilities doesn’t solve the core challenge of scientific discovery, which requires qualitative, structural reasoning. It introduces the Qualitative Engine for Science (QES) as a framework to capture and support that kind of human-like scientific wisdom.
AI.motivation: Kurzweil’s influential narrative suggests that compounding technological advances will naturally lead to transformative intelligence and rapid scientific progress. However, there is a concern that this focus on acceleration ignores the distinct, harder problem of qualitative scientific reasoning—recognizing when theories are structurally inadequate and inventing new conceptual frameworks. Recent benchmarks (ARC-AGI-3) show humans far outperform current AI on such flexible reasoning tasks, underscoring this gap. The paper is motivated by the need to characterize and preserve the processes of human scientific understanding and to design AI systems that support, rather than replace, this capacity.
AI.method: The paper first formalizes Kurzweil’s accelerating returns thesis via a simple mathematical model that captures self-amplifying technological progress, particularly in computation and AI. It then conceptually distinguishes between acceleration in executional/infrastructural capabilities and the qualitatively different capacity for structural, conceptual shifts in science. Using empirical evidence from ARC-AGI-3 benchmarks and philosophical reflection on scientific practice, it argues for the need of a new kind of system—the Qualitative Engine for Science (QES)—and outlines its role and design principles at a high level.
AI.result: The main result is not an empirical performance metric but a theoretical and conceptual clarification: (1) accelerating returns are most applicable to quantitative and executional aspects of technology, not to the core qualitative leaps in scientific theory change; (2) current AI systems dramatically underperform humans on tasks requiring such qualitative reasoning (as measured by ARC-AGI-3). The paper positions QES as a proposed architecture or framework that specifically targets this missing capacity, though detailed implementation results are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Exponential growth in compute and related technologies, as envisioned by Kurzweil, does not automatically solve the deepest problems of scientific discovery because these hinge on qualitative, structural reasoning rather than sheer quantitative capacity. The persistent gap between human and AI performance on flexible reasoning tasks highlights this limitation. The Qualitative Engine for Science (QES) is proposed as a way to model, preserve, and support the human processes of conceptual innovation in science. The paper concludes that such qualitative scientific wisdom is intrinsically valuable and should be organized and made accessible, independently of timelines for AGI or continued acceleration in technological progress.

title: AnySimLite: A Lightweight Few-Shot Similarity Encoder for On-Device Speech-Adjacent Classification
summary: To minimize privacy concerns and inference latency on edge devices like smartphones, lightweight on-device models remain important for end-user applications. Many of these applications involve natural language classification, but deploying multiple specialized models creates a memory footprint challenge. We investigate: Can a single lightweight architecture solve multiple Speech-Adjacent (SA) classification tasks through reduction to a nuanced text similarity formulation? We propose AnySimLite, a lightweight similarity encoder that combines word-level and character-level channels. Together with a dataset transformation strategy, we evaluate AnySimLite across multiple SA classification tasks and show that it consistently achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) or SOTA-competitive performance in few-shot settings while maintaining a low memory footprint. Even in the worst case, the performance drop remains below 7% while using $<\frac{1}{250}^{\mathrm{th}}$ of the model size of the SOTA qLLaMA_LoRA-7B baseline.

AI.tldr: A single small on-device similarity-based model, AnySimLite, can handle many speech-adjacent text classification tasks with very low memory and near-SOTA accuracy via a task-reduction and dataset transformation strategy.
AI.motivation: On-device language models for smartphones must be small for latency, privacy, and memory reasons, but real apps need many different classifiers (intent detection, sentiment, etc.). Deploying a separate specialized model for each task wastes memory. The authors ask whether all these different classification tasks can be reframed as nuanced text similarity problems so that a single compact model can replace many larger specialized ones.
AI.method: They design AnySimLite, a lightweight similarity encoder that processes text through both word-level and character-level channels to capture semantic and subword information. They then apply a dataset transformation strategy that recasts multiple speech-adjacent classification tasks into a unified text similarity format. This single architecture is trained/evaluated in few-shot settings across these tasks, always operating as a similarity model rather than a direct classifier.
AI.result: Across multiple speech-adjacent classification benchmarks, AnySimLite achieves state-of-the-art or nearly state-of-the-art performance in few-shot regimes while being extremely compact. Compared with a strong baseline (qLLaMA_LoRA-7B), its worst-case accuracy drop is under 7%, yet it uses less than 1/250 of that model’s size, dramatically reducing the memory footprint for on-device use.
AI.conclusion: Reframing diverse speech-adjacent classification problems as text similarity tasks enables a single lightweight model to replace many specialized classifiers on edge devices. AnySimLite demonstrates that combining word- and character-level channels within a compact similarity encoder, plus an appropriate dataset transformation, can deliver near-SOTA performance with a fraction of the memory, making it well-suited for privacy-preserving, low-latency on-device applications.

title: Narration-of-Thought: Inference-Time Scaffolding for Defeasible Ethical Reasoning in Large Language Models
comment: 24 pages, 8 figures, 16 tables. To appear at ACL 2026 (submitted via ARR)
summary: Standard chain-of-thought on moral dilemmas exhibits two failure modes: stakeholder collapse (the trace names at most one party with a stake in the outcome) and uncertainty suppression (no explicit unknowns or hedges before committing to an action). We introduce narration-of-thought (NoT), a system prompt that structures chain-of-thought into five sections: protagonist, stakeholders, two-step consequences, uncertainty, then commitment. NoT adds no training, parameters, or fine-tuning. On 100 DailyDilemmas scenarios across four generators from three vendors, NoT cuts stakeholder collapse from up to 31% to under 1% and uncertainty suppression from up to 72% to 1-24% on every model. A matched-budget verbose-CoT control rules out token spend as the active ingredient; NoT retains Cliff's delta advantages of +0.79 to +0.90 on stakeholder count and +0.65 to +0.93 on uncertainty score for three of four generators, and a section ablation attributes each shift to its specific sub-instruction. Textual-gradient descent initialised at NoT improves the scaffold further; a cross-family training judge (different vendor from the generator) dominates an in-family one on every measured axis. Extended to a five-round multi-stakeholder debate protocol, the scaffold converts a 6% standoff into 95% full consensus on a calibration set and 100% combined convergence on a DailyDilemmas replication. The resulting traces externalise the stakeholders, consequences, and uncertainty grounding each commitment, providing an auditable substrate for dependable agentic deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a prompting scaffold (Narration-of-Thought, NoT) that restructures chain-of-thought for moral dilemmas to systematically list stakeholders, consequences, and uncertainties, sharply reducing two common reasoning failures and improving consensus in multi-agent debates without extra training or parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing chain-of-thought reasoning on moral dilemmas often omits key affected parties (stakeholder collapse) and glosses over unknowns or doubts (uncertainty suppression), which undermines transparency, auditability, and reliability of model decisions for high-stakes, multi-party scenarios. The authors want a lightweight, prompt-only intervention that makes moral reasoning more complete, explicit, and auditable, suitable for dependable deployment of agentic systems.
AI.method: They design a specific system prompt, Narration-of-Thought (NoT), which breaks reasoning into five ordered sections: (1) identify the protagonist; (2) list all stakeholders; (3) reason through two-step consequences; (4) explicitly articulate uncertainties; and only then (5) commit to an action. They evaluate NoT on 100 DailyDilemmas moral scenarios across four language model generators from three vendors, comparing it against standard chain-of-thought and a verbose-CoT control matched for token budget. They measure stakeholder count and explicit uncertainty markers, compute effect sizes (Cliff's delta), and perform section ablation to see which instruction drives which improvement. They further apply textual-gradient descent starting from NoT to optimize the scaffold, and test a multi-round, multi-stakeholder debate protocol using NoT, judged by either cross-family or in-family models to compare judging strategies.
AI.result: NoT dramatically reduces stakeholder collapse from up to 31% of traces to below 1%, and reduces uncertainty suppression from as high as 72% down to 1–24% across all models tested. Against a matched-length verbose-CoT baseline, NoT maintains large effect size advantages in stakeholder count (+0.79 to +0.90) and uncertainty score (+0.65 to +0.93) for three of four generators, indicating the structure—not just verbosity—is responsible. Section ablations show that each sub-instruction contributes specifically to its corresponding improvement. Optimizing the prompt via textual-gradient descent further enhances these metrics. In multi-agent debate settings, using NoT transforms a 6% rate of consensus (“standoff”) into 95% full consensus on a calibration set and 100% convergence on a DailyDilemmas replication, especially when using a cross-family judge model which outperforms in-family judges on all axes measured.
AI.conclusion: A simple, structured prompting scheme—Narration-of-Thought—substantially improves the completeness and explicitness of LLM moral reasoning without any model modifications, notably reducing stakeholder omissions and hidden uncertainty. The scaffold also scales to multi-round debates, leading to much higher consensus and producing traces that clearly externalize stakeholders, consequences, and uncertainties. This yields more transparent and auditable reasoning, making NoT a promising, low-cost tool for more dependable deployment of agentic systems in morally-loaded or multi-stakeholder contexts.

title: Soft Token Alignment for Cross-Lingual Reasoning
summary: Multilingual large language models often produce inconsistent reasoning and answers for semantically equivalent prompts in different languages. Prior work suggests that intermediate representations can be relatively language-agnostic, but generation becomes increasingly language-specific as models commit to discrete output tokens. This is problematic because language-specific lexical choices can cause semantically equivalent reasoning paths to diverge across languages. These divergences motivate searching for a cross-lingual alignment signal that is less tied to any single vocabulary item or script. We propose SOLAR, an auxiliary objective for supervised fine-tuning that aligns soft-token representations across languages, using English as a pivot. Soft tokens are probability-weighted mixtures over the vocabulary embeddings, yielding continuous representations that can aggregate information from semantically related tokens across languages. We then align each non-English soft-token summary to its English counterpart in the shared embedding space. Across four multilingual reasoning benchmarks, SOLAR improves accuracy by up to +17.7 points over the base model and +3.8 over standard supervised fine-tuning, with the largest gains on low-resource languages. SOLAR also strengthens final-layer cross-lingual similarity and substantially reduces language-cluster separability, suggesting that aligning soft-token representations helps preserve shared semantic structure during multilingual reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SOLAR, an auxiliary training objective that aligns multilingual model representations by matching language-agnostic soft-token distributions to English, improving cross-lingual consistency and reasoning accuracy, especially in low-resource languages.
AI.motivation: Multilingual LLMs often give inconsistent reasoning and answers for semantically equivalent prompts across languages. While intermediate internal representations can be relatively language-agnostic, the final token-level generation becomes language-specific, causing semantically equivalent reasoning paths to diverge due to lexical and script differences. This inconsistency harms reliability and transfer to low-resource languages, motivating a method to align model behavior across languages using a signal that is less dependent on discrete vocabulary choices.
AI.method: The authors propose SOLAR, an auxiliary objective added during supervised fine-tuning. Instead of aligning discrete tokens, SOLAR works with soft tokens: probability-weighted mixtures over the vocabulary embeddings that form continuous representations capturing distributions over semantically related words. Using English as a pivot, the method computes a soft-token summary in both English and a non-English language for corresponding positions and then aligns non-English representations to their English counterparts in the model’s shared embedding space. This auxiliary alignment loss is combined with standard supervised fine-tuning to encourage language-agnostic internal representations during multilingual reasoning.
AI.result: Across four multilingual reasoning benchmarks, integrating SOLAR into supervised fine-tuning yields accuracy improvements of up to +17.7 percentage points over the underlying base model and up to +3.8 points over standard supervised fine-tuning without SOLAR. The gains are particularly strong for low-resource languages, where cross-lingual transfer is typically weakest.
AI.conclusion: Aligning soft-token representations across languages during fine-tuning helps maintain shared semantic structure and improves multilingual reasoning consistency. SOLAR both boosts task accuracy and measurably increases final-layer cross-lingual similarity while reducing separability between language clusters in representation space, indicating more unified, language-agnostic reasoning behavior in multilingual LLMs.

title: Geometry-Aware MCTS for Extremal Problems in Combinatorial Geometry
summary: We study certain extremal problems in combinatorial geometry that ask about configurations of points in an $n \times n$ grid that satisfy strict, global geometric constraints. Classical exact solvers suffer from combinatorial explosion for these types of problems, and standard reinforcement learning and transformer-based models struggle with the sparse reward "validity cliff" and quadratic token-consumption limits. To overcome these bottlenecks, we propose a Geometry-Aware Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) framework. Our approach strictly enforces geometric constraints through incremental updates to the feasible action space. For constraints about collections of collinear points, like those that occur in the classic No-Three-in-Line problem (Max-N3IL), this mechanism reduces the constraint checking complexity from $O(n^3)$ to $O(n^2)$. To improve search efficiency, we exploit geometric symmetries in two ways: canonical pruning during node expansion to reduce the branching factor, and symmetric batch transitions to accelerate the discovery of promising configurations. We perform extensive experiments and establish new best-known computational results on five out of six of the problems that we considered. Notably, for Max-N3IL we find configurations of size roughly $1.8 n$ for grids of size $82 \le n \le 119$. For the Smallest Complete Set problem, we find configurations of size roughly $0.95 n$, providing new upper bounds within the tested grids. This work establishes Geometry-Aware MCTS as a highly adaptable framework for discovering novel configurations in combinatorial geometry.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Geometry-Aware Monte Carlo Tree Search framework to solve hard combinatorial geometry grid problems more efficiently, achieving state-of-the-art configurations on several benchmark problems.
AI.motivation: Classical exact solvers face combinatorial explosion in grid-based extremal combinatorial geometry problems, and common learning-based methods struggle with sparse rewards and sequence-length limits, necessitating a new approach that can exploit geometric structure and constraints directly.
AI.method: The authors design a Geometry-Aware MCTS that incrementally maintains the feasible action space to strictly enforce geometric constraints, particularly for collinearity-based constraints, reducing constraint checking from cubic to quadratic time. They further incorporate geometric symmetries via canonical pruning to shrink the branching factor and use symmetric batch transitions to explore multiple symmetric configurations simultaneously.
AI.result: Their framework sets new best-known computational results on five of six benchmark combinatorial geometry problems. For the No-Three-in-Line problem, they construct configurations of roughly 1.8n points on n×n grids for 82 ≤ n ≤ 119, and for the Smallest Complete Set problem they obtain configurations of size about 0.95n, yielding improved upper bounds on tested grid sizes.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-Aware MCTS effectively leverages geometric constraints and symmetries to search large configuration spaces in combinatorial geometry, providing a versatile and powerful tool that achieves state-of-the-art results and can be applied to a range of similar extremal problems.

title: Extracting Problem and Method Sentence from Scientific Papers: A Context-enhanced Transformer Using Formulaic Expression Desensitization
summary: Billions of scientific papers lead to the need to identify essential parts from the massive text. Scientific research is an activity from putting forward problems to using methods. To learn the main idea from scientific papers, we focus on extracting problem and method sentences. Annotating sentences within scientific papers is labor-intensive, resulting in small-scale datasets that limit the amount of information models can learn. This limited information leads models to rely heavily on specific forms, which in turn reduces their generalization capabilities. This paper addresses the problems caused by small-scale datasets from three perspectives: increasing dataset scale, reducing dependence on specific forms, and enriching the information within sentences. To implement the first two ideas, we introduce the concept of formulaic expression (FE) desensitization and propose FE desensitization-based data augmenters to generate synthetic data and reduce models' reliance on FEs. For the third idea, we propose a context-enhanced transformer that utilizes context to measure the importance of words in target sentences and to reduce noise in the context. Furthermore, this paper conducts experiments using large language model (LLM) based in-context learning (ICL) methods. Quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that our proposed models achieve a higher macro F1 score compared to the baseline models on two scientific paper datasets, with improvements of 3.71% and 2.67%, respectively. The LLM based ICL methods are found to be not suitable for the task of problem and method extraction.

AI.tldr: The paper improves automatic extraction of problem and method sentences from scientific papers by enlarging and enriching training data and reducing models’ reliance on fixed formulaic phrases, achieving better F1 scores than baselines while showing LLM in-context learning is unsuitable for this task.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets for identifying problem and method sentences in scientific papers are small and expensive to annotate, causing models to overfit to surface patterns and limiting generalization. There is a need for scalable ways to capture the core ideas (problems and methods) from the growing volume of scientific literature.
AI.method: The authors (1) introduce formulaic expression (FE) desensitization and design FE-based data augmenters to generate synthetic training data and weaken models’ dependence on common fixed phrases; (2) propose a context-enhanced transformer that leverages surrounding context to estimate word importance in target sentences while filtering contextual noise; and (3) evaluate large language model (LLM) in-context learning approaches on the same task for comparison.
AI.result: On two scientific paper datasets, the proposed models achieve higher macro F1 scores than baseline systems, with improvements of 3.71% and 2.67%. Experiments also show that LLM-based in-context learning methods underperform and are not well-suited for extracting problem and method sentences.
AI.conclusion: Data augmentation via FE desensitization, combined with a context-enhanced transformer architecture, effectively mitigates the limitations of small annotated datasets and improves generalization for problem and method sentence extraction in scientific papers. LLM-based ICL approaches are not effective for this fine-grained extraction task under the tested conditions.

title: When Agents Meet Electric Bus Fleet Operations: Pricing Behavior, Trade-offs, and Policy Implications in an Aggregator Framework
summary: Agentic systems are changing how complex operational tasks are coordinated, introducing a new paradigm for connecting heterogeneous data sources and automating processes. Electric bus fleets provide a relevant test case. Their operation requires continuous coordination between service reliability, battery state-of-charge, charger availability, electricity prices, route-energy uncertainty, and vehicle-to-grid (V2G) opportunities. This paper proposes an agentic aggregator framework that streamlines this decision environment by coupling an optimization-based electric bus scheduling model with supervisory agents for disturbance detection, tariff adaptation, and schedule evaluation. The optimization core enforces physical feasibility across routes, chargers, batteries, and V2G exchanges, while the agentic layer interprets changing operating conditions, triggers real-time re-optimization when needed, and defines how flexibility value is allocated between the aggregator and the public transport operator (PTO). A realistic depot case study evaluates day-ahead and real-time operations under profit-based and operation-based coordination modes, considering service delays, route-energy deviations, electricity price shocks, and combined disturbances. The results show that agentic aggregation can support adaptive fleet-grid coordination by maintaining feasible schedules, activating re-optimization selectively, and improving the use of charging and V2G flexibility. However, they also reveal a critical trade-off: the same agentic capability that reduces operational complexity can extract value from the PTO when configured around profit-oriented pricing. These findings suggest that agentic aggregators can become useful for managing electric bus V2G operations, but their deployment in public-fleet contexts requires transparent coordination modes, auditable tariff-setting, and explicit value-sharing rules.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an agentic aggregator framework that integrates optimization-based scheduling with supervisory agents to manage electric bus fleets with V2G, improving adaptive fleet-grid coordination while highlighting value-sharing and transparency issues.
AI.motivation: Electric bus fleets with vehicle-to-grid capabilities must balance service reliability, battery constraints, charger availability, volatile electricity prices, and route-energy uncertainty. Existing approaches struggle to coordinate these heterogeneous factors in real time, especially when disturbances occur. The motivation is to explore whether emerging agentic systems can streamline this complex decision environment and enable more flexible, adaptive fleet-grid operations, while understanding the economic and governance implications for public transport operators.
AI.method: The authors design an agentic aggregator framework composed of (1) an optimization-based electric bus scheduling core that enforces physical feasibility across routes, chargers, batteries, and V2G exchanges, and (2) a supervisory agent layer responsible for disturbance detection, tariff adaptation, schedule evaluation, and triggering re-optimization. They implement this framework in a realistic electric bus depot case study, running simulations for day-ahead and real-time operations under different coordination modes (profit-based vs operation-based), and subject the system to various disturbances such as service delays, route-energy deviations, electricity price shocks, and their combinations.
AI.result: The framework successfully maintains feasible bus schedules under changing conditions, activates re-optimization selectively rather than continuously, and enhances the utilization of charging and V2G flexibility. The simulations show improved adaptive coordination between the fleet and the grid compared to a static scheduling approach. However, they also demonstrate that when the aggregator’s logic is configured around profit-based pricing, the agentic system can capture more value at the expense of the public transport operator, revealing a tension between operational support and economic fairness.
AI.conclusion: Agentic aggregators are promising tools for managing electric bus fleets with V2G, enabling adaptive, disturbance-aware, and physically feasible coordination between fleets and power systems. Nonetheless, their deployment in public transport contexts must address governance concerns: transparent coordination modes, auditable tariff-setting mechanisms, and explicit value-sharing rules are necessary to ensure that the automation that simplifies operations does not unfairly extract value from public operators. The work highlights both the technical benefits and the institutional requirements for responsible adoption of agentic systems in fleet-grid integration.

title: Utilizing Cognitive Signals Generated during Human Reading to Enhance Keyphrase Extraction from Microblogs
summary: Microblogging platforms generate massive amounts of short, noisy, and dispersed user content, making automatic keyphrase extraction (AKE) an important but challenging task. Prior studies have used eye-tracking signals to improve microblog-based AKE because such signals reflect readers' attention to salient words. However, eye tracking alone is limited by physiological, acquisition, and feature-decoding constraints. To address this issue, we investigate whether electroencephalogram (EEG) signals can complement eye-tracking signals for AKE. Using the ZuCo cognitive language processing corpus, we select 8 EEG features and 17 eye-tracking features and incorporate them into microblog-based AKE models. To reduce possible distortion of cognitive signals by model structures, we inject these features into the input of the soft-attention layer and the query vectors of the self-attention layer. We then evaluate different combinations of cognitive signals across AKE models. The results show that cognitive signals produced during reading consistently improve AKE performance, regardless of feature combinations and model architectures. EEG features bring the largest gains, while combining EEG and eye-tracking features yields performance between the two individual signal types, suggesting partial complementarity but also possible redundancy or noise. These findings indicate that EEG signals provide useful cognitive evidence for microblog-based AKE and that multimodal cognitive signals deserve further investigation.

AI.tldr: The paper explores using EEG and eye-tracking signals to improve automatic keyphrase extraction from noisy microblogs, finding that cognitive signals, especially EEG, consistently boost performance.
AI.motivation: Automatic keyphrase extraction on microblogs is difficult due to short, noisy, and dispersed text. Prior work showed eye-tracking can help by revealing reader attention, but eye-tracking alone has limitations related to physiology, data collection, and feature decoding. The authors are motivated to see whether EEG, another cognitive signal source, can complement eye-tracking to better capture salient information during reading and thus improve keyphrase extraction.
AI.method: The authors use the ZuCo cognitive language processing corpus, which contains both EEG and eye-tracking data recorded during reading. They engineer 8 EEG features and 17 eye-tracking features and integrate them into several microblog-based AKE models. To minimize distortion of cognitive signals by deep model architectures, they inject these features into two specific locations: the input of a soft-attention layer and the query vectors of a self-attention layer. They systematically evaluate different combinations of EEG and eye-tracking features across multiple AKE model architectures to measure their impact on performance.
AI.result: Across all tested models and feature configurations, adding cognitive signals collected during reading improves AKE performance compared with text-only baselines. EEG features yield the largest performance gains. When EEG and eye-tracking features are combined, performance is better than text-only but typically between using EEG alone and eye-tracking alone, indicating that the two modalities are only partially complementary and may introduce redundancy or noise when fused.
AI.conclusion: EEG signals provide valuable cognitive evidence that enhances automatic keyphrase extraction for microblog content. While both EEG and eye-tracking are beneficial, EEG is particularly effective. The mixed results from combining the two modalities suggest that multimodal fusion needs to be handled carefully to avoid redundancy and noise. Overall, the work supports further exploration of multimodal cognitive signals to advance AKE and related NLP tasks.

title: Unbiased Canonical Set-Valued Oracles Via Lattice Theory
summary: A non-agentic "oracle" AI that estimates probabilities of future events faces a self-reference problem: once its answer is learned and acted upon, it can change the very probability it was asked to report. One response, advocated for the Scientist AI programme, is to ask only counterfactual questions, evaluated as if the answer had no influence. We observe that such answers tend to become irrelevant the moment they are learned, precisely because their premise is then false. We therefore explore a self-referential alternative in which the oracle reports not a single probability but a credal set that is simultaneously unbiased and self-consistent with the consequences of being learned. The naive self-consistency requirement is satisfied by too many sets (including the useless answer $[0,1]$), so the problem is to single out a canonical, nontrivial member. We do so with the Knaster--Tarski fixed-point theorem on the complete lattice of closed credal sets, taking the least fixed point of a suitably defined isotone operator; a variant instead reports the least fixed point that contains every self-consistent point estimate. We prove existence, self-consistency, and nonemptiness, show that the construction collapses to the classical point answer for non-performative questions, and that for a binary event the canonical answer is, under a natural hull-factoring assumption, an interval. The development is purely lattice-theoretic and extends unchanged from a binary event $B$ to an arbitrary random variable $X$, with $P(B\mid A,C)$ replaced by the conditional law $\mathcal{L}(X\mid A,C)$. We close with open questions, including whether the interval characterization itself survives that generalization.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a way for non-agentic oracle AIs to answer self-referential, performative questions by returning a set of probabilities (a credal set) that remains correct even after the answer is revealed and acted upon, using fixed-point theory on lattices to define a canonical, nontrivial such set.
AI.motivation: Oracle AIs that estimate probabilities of future events change those probabilities once their answers are known and used, creating a self-reference problem. Existing approaches avoid this by asking purely counterfactual questions that assume the answer has no influence, but these become irrelevant once revealed because the premise becomes false. The authors aim to obtain answers that remain relevant and correct even when the oracle’s influence on the world is taken into account.
AI.method: They replace single probability estimates with credal sets (sets of possible probabilities) and impose a self-consistency requirement: the reported set must be compatible with the probability distribution of outcomes in a world where that very answer is learned and acted upon. Because naive self-consistency is too weak (admitting trivial answers like [0,1]), they use lattice-theoretic tools. On the complete lattice of closed credal sets, they define an isotone operator that maps a credal set to the set implied by self-consistency, and then use the Knaster–Tarski fixed-point theorem to select the least fixed point as the canonical answer. A variant construction chooses the least fixed point that includes all self-consistent point estimates. They analyze the binary-event case, introduce a hull-factoring assumption to show the canonical answer is an interval, and generalize from binary events to arbitrary random variables via conditional laws.
AI.result: They prove that their fixed-point construction always yields at least one nonempty, self-consistent credal set and that this construction collapses to the usual single-number probability for non-performative (non-self-referential) questions. For binary events, under a natural hull-factoring assumption, they show the canonical self-consistent answer is an interval of probabilities. They also demonstrate how the same lattice-theoretic approach extends from binary events to arbitrary random variables by working with conditional laws instead of scalar conditional probabilities.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a mathematically principled way for oracle AIs to answer self-referential, performative probabilistic questions by reporting canonical credal sets defined as least fixed points of an operator on credal sets. This yields unbiased, self-consistent answers that remain valid when the oracle’s influence is taken into account and reduce to classical probabilities in non-performative settings. The work raises further questions, particularly about whether the interval characterization found in the binary case persists for more general random variables.

title: Speaking Numbers to LLMs: Multi-Wavelet Number Embeddings for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are attractive for context-aware time series forecasting because they can integrate heterogeneous textual signals, yet their discrete, language-oriented tokenization and embedding interfaces are misaligned with continuous numerical values, often harming numerical ordering and forecasting reliability. We propose TempoWave, a plug-and-play temporal wavelet digit interface that maps each scalar observation into digit-wise embeddings constructed from multi-wavelet, multi-scale coefficients.
