By directly overriding standard token representations, TempoWave seamlessly exposes both fine-grained local fluctuations and macro global structures in a transformer-compatible form, ensuring that precise numerical formatting, distinct digit identity, and robustness to common normalization operations are maintained throughout the LLM pipeline. Experiments across five context-enriched forecasting benchmarks demonstrate that TempoWave consistently improves LLM-based forecasters over standard numeric tokenization and alternative embedding interfaces, achieving a new state-of-the-art. These results highlight the numeric interface as a key bottleneck and suggest that principled multi-resolution embeddings can better couple LLMs' contextual reasoning with precise forecasting. Our code is available at https://github.com/DC-research/TempoWAVE and our model can be accessed at https://huggingface.co/Melady/TempoWAVE.

AI.tldr: TempoWave is a new numeric interface for LLM-based time series forecasting that encodes numbers via multi-scale wavelet-based digit embeddings, significantly improving forecasting performance over standard numeric tokenization.
AI.motivation: Existing LLMs struggle with context-aware time series forecasting because their language-focused tokenization and embeddings are not well-suited for continuous numerical values, leading to poor preservation of numerical order and reduced forecasting reliability. There is a need for an interface that better aligns LLMs with numeric data while leveraging their strength in handling heterogeneous textual context.
AI.method: The paper introduces TempoWave, a temporal wavelet digit interface that replaces standard numeric tokenization. Each scalar value in a time series is decomposed into digit-wise embeddings derived from multi-wavelet, multi-scale coefficients. These embeddings directly override the usual token representations in transformer-based LLMs, encoding both local fluctuations and global temporal structures, while preserving exact digit identities and formatting and being robust to normalization.
AI.result: Across five benchmarks for context-rich time series forecasting, TempoWave consistently outperforms baselines that use standard numeric tokenization and other numeric embedding schemes. The method achieves new state-of-the-art results for LLM-based forecasters, demonstrating improved forecasting accuracy and reliability when using the proposed numeric interface.
AI.conclusion: The numeric interface between time series and LLMs is a critical performance bottleneck. By providing multi-resolution, wavelet-based digit embeddings that integrate seamlessly into transformers, TempoWave more effectively couples LLMs’ contextual reasoning abilities with precise numeric forecasting, leading to superior performance on context-enriched time series tasks. The work suggests that principled, multi-scale numerical representations are key to unlocking LLMs’ potential for time series forecasting.

title: Estimating Uncertainty in Classifier Performance with Applications to Large Language Models and Nested Data
summary: Researchers increasingly use text classification--supervised models or large language models--to measure constructs from natural language, providing metrics such as recall and precision as evidence of their validity. Yet, though these metrics are point estimates subject to sampling variation, measures of uncertainty are inconsistently reported alongside them. Further, when they are reported, they are often estimated with methods that are not appropriate when relevant labelled datasets are small or performance is high. To increase and improve confidence interval reporting in the field, this paper evaluates confidence interval methods for performance metrics under conditions typical of social science text classification: small to moderate sample sizes, infrequent constructs, and texts nested within individuals. Across simulations, default methods such as the Wald interval and the basic percentile bootstrap are the least accurate, with coverage sometimes far below the nominal 95% level. Accuracy is improved with the use of Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, and a novel pseudo-count regularized bootstrap (which is particularly relevant to the calculation of F1). When texts are nested within individuals, we demonstrate that adjustment for both effective N and the appropriate degrees of freedom is necessary for producing accurate analytic intervals. Among bootstrap intervals, the hierarchical bootstrap is more accurate than the cluster bootstrap when individuals produce a moderate number of texts but overly conservative when individuals produce only a few. By providing guidance to the field on appropriate interval estimation, we aim to improve the transparency of machine learning applications, and to encourage greater attention to the validation sample size at the design stage.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates and recommends better methods for constructing confidence intervals for text classification performance metrics under realistic social science conditions.
AI.motivation: Text classification is widely used to measure social science constructs, and validity is often summarized with point estimates like precision and recall. However, uncertainty around these estimates (confidence intervals) is inconsistently reported and often computed with inappropriate methods, especially when labeled datasets are small, constructs are rare, or performance is high—common conditions in social science. This undermines the credibility and interpretability of validation results.
AI.method: The authors run simulation studies that mimic typical social science text classification settings: small to moderate validation sample sizes, infrequent positive labels, high performance metrics, and texts nested within individuals. They systematically compare multiple confidence interval methods for classification performance metrics (e.g., recall, precision, F1), including standard analytic intervals (Wald, Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson) and bootstrap-based intervals (basic percentile bootstrap, and a new pseudo-count regularized bootstrap, as well as cluster and hierarchical bootstraps for nested data). They then assess interval accuracy primarily via coverage relative to the nominal 95% level, and evaluate how nesting and sample size affect performance.
AI.result: Default or naïve methods—especially the Wald interval and basic percentile bootstrap—perform poorly under realistic social science conditions, often yielding confidence intervals with coverage far below the nominal 95%. Alternative methods such as Agresti-Coull, Wilson, and Clopper-Pearson intervals, as well as the proposed pseudo-count regularized bootstrap (particularly for F1), provide much more accurate coverage. For nested data, appropriately adjusting the effective sample size and degrees of freedom is essential. Among bootstrap approaches, the hierarchical bootstrap generally outperforms the cluster bootstrap when there are a moderate number of texts per individual, but becomes overly conservative when each individual contributes only a few texts.
AI.conclusion: Researchers validating text classifiers in social science should not rely on default Wald or basic percentile bootstrap intervals for performance metrics, especially with small samples, rare constructs, high performance, or nested text data. Instead, they should use better-performing analytic intervals (Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson) or the proposed pseudo-count regularized bootstrap for F1, and properly account for data clustering and effective sample size. Adopting these recommendations will improve the transparency, reliability, and design of validation studies, particularly by encouraging adequate validation sample sizes and appropriate uncertainty quantification.

title: Comparing BERT Sentence-Pair Classification and Few-Shot LLM Prompting for Detecting Threat and Solution Framing in German Climate News
summary: News media play a central role in shaping public perceptions of climate change, and whether coverage emphasizes threats or solutions has measurable effects on audience engagement and policy support. Automated detection of these framing patterns at the sentence level would allow researchers to analyze large corpora that are infeasible to code manually. We present a systematic comparison of two approaches for classifying sentences from German-language climate news articles as threat-oriented, solution-oriented, both, or neither. The first approach uses few-shot prompting with an open-weights large language model (Llama 4 Maverick), employing chain-of-thought reasoning and structured output with confidence scoring. The second approach fine-tunes a German BERT model (deepset/gbert-large) for sentence-pair classification, where the preceding sentence provides contextual information for the target sentence. Both approaches implement two independent binary classifiers, one for threat framing and one for solution framing. We evaluate both methods on a corpus of 440 Austrian newspaper articles that were manually coded following a detailed coding scheme developed with domain experts. The fine-tuned BERT classifiers achieve an F1 score of 0.83 for both the threat and solution tasks, while the LLM-based classifiers reach an F1 of 0.78. An ablation study confirms that providing the preceding sentence as context improves BERT classification performance substantially compared to single-sentence input. These results contribute to the growing body of work comparing fine-tuned encoder models with prompted generative models for text classification in computational social science.

AI.tldr: The paper compares a prompted open-weight LLM and a fine-tuned German BERT model for detecting threat vs. solution framing in German-language climate news, finding that fine-tuned BERT with contextual sentence pairs performs better.
AI.motivation: Human coding of media framing (e.g., whether climate news emphasizes threats or solutions) is slow and costly, limiting the scale of empirical studies. Automated, sentence-level detection of these frames would enable large-scale analyses of how climate issues are presented to the public. There is also an open methodological question in computational social science about when fine-tuned encoder models outperform prompted generative LLMs for classification tasks.
AI.method: The authors compare two automated classification approaches on German-language climate news. (1) A few-shot prompting setup using an open-weights LLM (Llama 4 Maverick), with chain-of-thought reasoning and structured, confidence-scored outputs. (2) Fine-tuning a German BERT model (deepset/gbert-large) for sentence-pair classification, where each instance consists of a target sentence plus its preceding sentence as context. Both approaches rely on two independent binary classifiers that label each sentence for threat framing and solution framing, respectively. Performance is evaluated against a manually coded gold-standard dataset of 440 Austrian newspaper articles labeled under an expert-developed coding scheme. An ablation study removes the preceding-sentence context to test its impact on BERT performance.
AI.result: The fine-tuned German BERT model achieves an F1 score of 0.83 on both threat and solution framing classification tasks, outperforming the LLM-based few-shot prompting approach, which achieves an F1 score of 0.78. The ablation study demonstrates that including the preceding sentence as additional context substantially improves BERT’s performance relative to using only the target sentence.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuned encoder models with contextual input (sentence pairs) can outperform prompted generative LLMs for nuanced sentence-level framing classification in computational social science tasks. Providing minimal local context (the preceding sentence) is important for accurate detection of threat and solution frames in climate news coverage. The study adds evidence that, despite the flexibility of LLM prompting, task-specific fine-tuning of encoder models remains a strong and often superior baseline for text classification in domain-specific research settings.

title: Data-driven Machine Learning Cannot Reach Symbolic-level Logical Reasoning -- The Limit of the Scaling Law
summary: Sphere neural networks have achieved symbolic level syllogistic reasoning without training data, raising the question of where the limit of the scaling law for logical reasoning lies, i.e., whether data-driven machine learning systems can achieve the same level by increasing training data and training time. We show two methodological limitations that prevent supervised deep learning from reaching the symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning: (1) training data can not distinguish all 24 types of valid syllogistic reasoning; (2) end-to-end mapping from premises to conclusion introduces contradictory training targets between neural components for pattern recognition and logical reasoning. Beside theoretical analysis, we experimentally illustrate that Euler Net cannot achieve rigorous syllogistic reasoning. We further challenge the most recent ChatGPTs (GPT-5-nano and GPT-5) to determine the satisfiability of syllogistic statements in four surface forms (patterns): words, double words, simple symbols, and long random symbols, showing that surface forms affect the reasoning performance and that ChatGPT GPT-5 may reach 100% accuracy but still provide incorrect explanations. As empirical training processes are stopped after achieving 100% accuracy, we conclude that supervised machine learning systems will not attain the rigour of symbolic logical reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that standard supervised deep learning cannot fully replicate symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning, even with unlimited data and training, and supports this via theoretical analysis and experiments on existing neural models and ChatGPT variants.
AI.motivation: To understand whether the impressive logical performance of specialized neural systems (e.g., sphere neural networks) can be matched by ordinary, data-driven supervised learning simply by scaling data and compute, and to identify any fundamental limitations that prevent such systems from achieving rigorous, symbolic syllogistic reasoning.
AI.method: The authors provide (1) a theoretical analysis of the learnability of syllogistic forms under supervised training, showing data indistinguishability of all 24 valid syllogism types and conflicts in end-to-end mapping from premises to conclusions; and (2) empirical experiments on Euler Net and contemporary large language models (GPT-5-nano and GPT-5) across different surface representations (words, double words, simple symbols, long random symbols), evaluating both their accuracy on syllogism satisfiability tasks and the correctness of their explanations.
AI.result: They identify two core methodological limitations: (1) no finite supervised training set can uniquely distinguish all 24 valid syllogistic reasoning types; and (2) end-to-end neural architectures intertwining pattern recognition with logical inference create inherently contradictory training targets. Empirically, Euler Net fails to achieve fully rigorous syllogistic reasoning, and GPT-5 variants show performance that depends strongly on the surface form of input. GPT-5 can reach 100% accuracy on some tasks yet still generate logically incorrect explanations.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that, under the standard supervised learning paradigm, scaling data and training time is insufficient to reach the rigour of symbolic logical reasoning. Even highly capable models that achieve perfect accuracy on training-style benchmarks can still fail at underlying logical explanation, indicating a structural mismatch between supervised deep learning and formal symbolic reasoning, especially for syllogisms.

title: Nemotron-TwoTower: Diffusion Language Modeling with Pretrained Autoregressive Context
comment: Code and model weights available at https://huggingface.co/collections/nvidia/nemotron-twotower
summary: Diffusion language models offer a promising alternative to autoregressive models due to their potential for parallel and iterative generation. However, existing approaches use a single network for both context representation and iterative denoising, forcing one model to serve both roles and limiting its capacity for either role. We propose TwoTower, a block-wise autoregressive diffusion model that decouples these roles into two towers: a frozen AR context tower that causally processes clean tokens, and a trainable diffusion denoiser tower with bidirectional block attention that refines noisy blocks via cross-attention to the context. Built on Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B, an open-weight 30B hybrid Mamba-Transformer MoE model, and trained on approximately 2.1T tokens, Nemotron-TwoTower retains 98.7% of the autoregressive baseline's quality while offering 2.42X higher wall-clock generation throughput. We release the code and model weights at https://huggingface.co/collections/nvidia/nemotron-twotower.

AI.tldr: TwoTower is a diffusion-based language model architecture that separates context encoding from denoising, achieving almost the same quality as a strong 30B autoregressive baseline while more than doubling generation speed.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion language models use a single network for both understanding the clean input context and iteratively denoising/generated tokens, which forces a tradeoff and limits performance in both roles. There is a need for a design that preserves the advantages of diffusion (parallel, iterative generation) without sacrificing modeling capacity for context representation or generation quality.
AI.method: The authors introduce TwoTower, a block-wise autoregressive diffusion architecture with two distinct components. A frozen autoregressive (AR) context tower causally encodes clean tokens into contextual representations. A separate, trainable diffusion denoiser tower performs bidirectional block attention over noisy token blocks and uses cross-attention to the frozen context tower to iteratively refine the blocks. They instantiate this on Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B, a 30B-parameter hybrid Mamba-Transformer mixture-of-experts (MoE) model, and train on ~2.1T tokens.
AI.result: Nemotron-TwoTower matches 98.7% of the quality of the autoregressive Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B baseline while delivering 2.42× higher end-to-end wall-clock generation throughput, demonstrating that the decoupled two-tower design maintains quality with significantly faster sampling.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling context encoding and diffusion denoising into separate towers enables diffusion language models to retain near-autoregressive quality while substantially improving generation throughput. The TwoTower design is an effective way to realize the parallel and iterative benefits of diffusion models at scale, and the released code and weights make the approach accessible for further research and applications.

title: MKG-RAG-Bench: Benchmarking Retrieval in Multimodal Knowledge Graph-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over knowledge graphs has emerged as a promising approach for grounding large language models, yet existing benchmarks largely overlook the challenges of retrieval in multimodal knowledge graph RAG (MKG-RAG). In practice, retrieval is a critical bottleneck: multimodal knowledge is heterogeneous, difficult to align across modalities, and often poorly served by retrievers designed for unstructured corpora. To address this gap, we introduce MKG-RAG-Bench, a cross-domain benchmark explicitly designed to evaluate retrieval in MKG-RAG. MKG-RAG-Bench is constructed from two multimodal knowledge graphs spanning general and medical domains, and includes carefully aligned question-answering datasets that support controlled evaluation of both retrieval and downstream generation. The benchmark is built using an LLM-based curation pipeline that filters low-utility knowledge, generates structurally grounded queries with exact supervision, and systematically covers diverse modality configurations. Through extensive experiments across representative retriever families and modality settings, we show that effective multimodal retrieval remains challenging yet crucial for end-to-end MKG-RAG performance, and that retrieval quality strongly determines generation outcomes. By isolating retrieval as a first-class evaluation target, MKG-RAG-Bench provides a principled foundation for diagnosing current limitations and advancing multimodal knowledge graph RAG systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MKG-RAG-Bench, a benchmark focused on evaluating retrieval quality in multimodal knowledge graph retrieval-augmented generation (MKG-RAG), showing that retrieval is a key bottleneck that largely determines downstream generation performance.
AI.motivation: Existing RAG benchmarks mainly target text-only, unstructured corpora and often ignore the unique retrieval challenges in multimodal knowledge graphs, such as heterogeneity across modalities and misalignment between them. As a result, current retrievers are not well-suited for MKG-RAG, and there is no principled way to diagnose or compare systems on this dimension. The authors want a benchmark that isolates and rigorously evaluates retrieval in MKG-RAG across domains and modality configurations.
AI.method: They construct MKG-RAG-Bench from two multimodal knowledge graphs (one general-domain, one medical). Using an LLM-based curation pipeline, they (1) filter out low-utility knowledge, (2) generate structurally grounded queries whose answers are exactly supervised by graph content, and (3) ensure coverage of diverse modality combinations. The resulting dataset supports controlled evaluation of retrieval performance and of end-to-end RAG generation. They then run extensive experiments with various retriever families and modality settings on this benchmark.
AI.result: Experiments show that multimodal retrieval over knowledge graphs is still difficult for current retrievers, especially due to heterogeneity and alignment issues between modalities. Retrieval performance strongly correlates with and largely determines the quality of downstream generated answers in MKG-RAG systems. Different retriever families show varying strengths, but none solves the multimodal retrieval problem satisfactorily.
AI.conclusion: MKG-RAG-Bench successfully isolates retrieval as a first-class evaluation target for multimodal knowledge graph RAG. The benchmark reveals that retrieval is a major bottleneck and a decisive factor for generation quality, highlighting substantial headroom for improving multimodal retrievers. It provides a principled testbed for diagnosing limitations and guiding future research on MKG-RAG retrieval and end-to-end systems.

title: Temporal Validity in Retrieval Memory: Eliminating Stale-Fact Errors for AI Agents over Evolving Knowledge
comment: 21 pages, 5 tables. Code, prompts, and evaluation datasets included
summary:
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) gives agents access to accumulated knowledge, but has no model of time. When a fact changes (e.g., a function is renamed or API restructured), RAG retrieves both the stale and current value with near-identical embedding similarity. The agent then either abstains or serves the superseded fact. We show this is a structural problem: on a calibrated dataset, cosine similarity distinguishes a contradicted fact from a duplicated one with AUROC 0.59 (near chance), as contradictions are often more embedding-similar to the original than rephrased duplicates.
  We present MemStrata, a retrieval memory maintaining temporal validity. It stores facts like RAG, preserving static recall, but when a fact's value is contradicted, a deterministic (subject, relation, object) supersession rule retires the stale value in a bi-temporal ledger - with no similarity threshold and no LLM call. Across six benchmarks run locally with a 7B model, MemStrata ties RAG on static knowledge and reaches 0.95-1.00 accuracy on evolving knowledge (where RAG reaches 0.20-0.47). The central result is the stale-fact-error rate: when required to answer, RAG serves superseded values 15-40% of the time; MemStrata drives this to ~0%, a failure class RAG cannot avoid. MemStrata achieves this at retrieval latency (~2.1s) versus ~16-18s for LLM-reranking baselines. We release the harness, datasets, and a marker-free evaluation protocol for memory under knowledge evolution.

AI.tldr: MemStrata is a retrieval memory system that adds temporal validity to RAG, eliminating stale answers on evolving facts without needing LLM reranking.
AI.motivation: RAG systems cannot reliably distinguish current facts from outdated ones because embedding similarity treats contradictions and duplicates similarly, causing models to return stale information when knowledge changes.
AI.method: Introduce MemStrata, which stores facts as (subject, relation, object) triples in a bi-temporal ledger and applies a deterministic supersession rule to retire stale entries when contradicted, preserving static recall while adding time-aware retrieval without extra LLM calls or similarity thresholds.
AI.result: On six benchmarks with a 7B model, MemStrata matches standard RAG on static knowledge and vastly outperforms it on evolving knowledge (0.95–1.00 vs. 0.20–0.47 accuracy), reducing stale-fact errors from 15–40% to nearly 0% and running faster (~2.1s) than LLM-reranking baselines (~16–18s).
AI.conclusion: Temporal structure in retrieval memory is crucial for agents operating over evolving knowledge; MemStrata’s bi-temporal, supersession-based design provides near-perfect avoidance of stale answers while preserving RAG’s strengths and improving efficiency, and the authors release tooling to standardize evaluation under knowledge evolution.

title: auto-psych: Automating the science of mind using agent-driven theory discovery and experimentation
summary: AI-based scientific automation is increasingly possible by using agents to generate hypotheses, design experiments, and analyze data. Data collection is a major bottleneck in this pipeline, however. Psychology, and computational cognitive science in particular, is well-positioned to benefit from AI experimentation because theories are often represented as code and crowdsourcing platforms enable programmatic human data collection at scale. Here, we apply automated discovery techniques to the project of generating theories in computational cognitive science, with an agent-based system collecting human data independently through crowdsourced survey experiments. As a testbed, we use a classic case study from cognitive psychology: judging which sequences of coin flips seem subjectively more random. Our system, auto-psych, uses nested agent-based discovery loops to generate explanatory theories of human behavior. The inner loop conjectures, fits, and critiques probabilistic cognitive models; the outer loop designs experiments to test these models, launches them online, and analyzes the data. This system can quickly and reliably recover ground-truth theories from synthetic data via systematic experimentation, but the nested structure is critical to model performance. Further, in three independent sequences of human experiments, the system finds theories that fit the data better than theories generated from the scientific literature. This work thus demonstrates the feasibility of automated data collection and theory discovery in computational cognitive science.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an AI agent system, auto-psych, that automatically designs and runs crowd-sourced psychology experiments and discovers cognitive theories, outperforming existing human-written theories on a classic randomness perception task.
AI.motivation: AI agents can increasingly automate parts of the scientific process—hypothesis generation, experiment design, and data analysis—but data collection remains a major bottleneck. Psychology, especially computational cognitive science, is particularly suitable for automation because its theories are often formalized as executable code and human behavioral data can be collected at scale via online crowdsourcing platforms. The authors aim to explore whether an automated system can not only run experiments but also discover high-quality cognitive theories that explain human behavior, thereby accelerating theory development.
AI.method: They build an agent-based system called auto-psych that runs two nested discovery loops. The inner loop automatically proposes probabilistic cognitive models, fits them to data, and critiques them, effectively searching theory space. The outer loop designs new experiments to discriminate among models, deploys these experiments via online crowd-sourcing surveys, collects human data, and feeds the results back to the inner loop for model refinement. They validate the system on a well-known task in cognitive psychology: how people judge the randomness of coin-flip sequences, using both synthetic data with known ground-truth models and real human data from multiple experimental runs.
AI.result: On synthetic datasets where the true underlying theory is known, auto-psych rapidly and reliably recovers the ground-truth cognitive models, but only when using the full nested inner–outer loop; ablations show that this nested structure is crucial for good performance. On three separate series of real human experiments about subjective randomness of coin-flip sequences, the system autonomously designs experiments and discovers models that fit the empirical data better than leading theories drawn from the existing psychological literature.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that fully automated, agent-driven pipelines can handle not only data analysis but also experiment design, data collection, and theory generation in computational cognitive science. The nested agent architecture is essential for effective theory discovery. Overall, the work provides a proof-of-concept that AI systems can autonomously conduct behavioral experiments at scale and yield cognitive theories that outperform established human-generated models, suggesting a path toward more automated scientific discovery in psychology.

title: Assessing Post-Reform Changes in Risk Disclosure Quality with a Multidimensional Text Analysis Approach
comment: The 4th International Conference on Computational and Data Sciences in Economics and Finance (CDEF 2026)
summary: While corporate narrative disclosures provide crucial information to capital markets, comprehensively evaluating their qualitative changes over time remains challenging. Narrative text is inherently multidimensional, meaning that an improvement in one textual dimension often occurs alongside changes in others. To capture these underlying dynamics, we propose a longitudinal text analysis approach combining Japanese-language NLP metric extraction with paired testing, shift function analysis, and inter-metric correlation. Our framework extends prior indicator sets by incorporating a cross-section relevance indicator to measure topical alignment between risk disclosures and management strategies. Applying this approach to evaluate Japan's 2019 disclosure reforms, we analyze 19,770 firm-year observations over a 10-year period (FY2015-FY2024). The joint analysis reveals complex shifts in disclosure patterns that are frequently masked by conventional single-indicator methods. Specifically, we find that while disclosure volume increased substantially, it was accompanied by a decline in readability. Furthermore, although the overall information structure improved, specific descriptive quality stagnated, and the degree of adaptation varied across market segments.

AI.tldr: They develop a method to track how multiple qualitative aspects of Japanese corporate narrative disclosures evolve over time, showing that reforms increased disclosure volume but hurt readability and produced uneven quality improvements.
AI.motivation: Existing research struggles to comprehensively assess qualitative, multidimensional changes in narrative corporate disclosures over time, especially in non-English contexts. Single text indicators can be misleading because improving one dimension often coincides with offsetting changes in others, and there is limited work on how regulatory reforms affect the joint evolution of multiple textual attributes and their alignment with firms’ stated strategies.
AI.method: They design a longitudinal text analysis framework for Japanese corporate narratives that: (1) extracts multiple NLP-based text metrics (including a new cross-section relevance indicator capturing topical alignment between risk disclosures and management strategies); (2) applies paired statistical tests to compare periods; (3) uses shift function analysis to examine distributional changes across the spectrum of firms; and (4) studies inter-metric correlations to understand how dimensions co-move. They implement this on a large 10-year panel (19,770 firm-year observations, FY2015–FY2024) around Japan’s 2019 disclosure reforms.
AI.result: Using their framework on Japanese firms, they uncover nuanced changes that simple, single-indicator analyses would miss. After the 2019 reforms, firms substantially increased the volume of narrative disclosure, but readability declined. The broader information structure of disclosures improved, but finer-grained descriptive quality did not show similar progress, and changes in disclosure practices differed by market segment.
AI.conclusion: Multidimensional, longitudinal analysis is essential for accurately evaluating regulatory impacts on narrative disclosures. Their proposed framework, particularly the new relevance indicator and joint analysis of multiple metrics, reveals that Japan’s 2019 reforms led to more extensive but less readable disclosures, with only partial quality improvements and heterogeneous adaptation across markets—insights that would be obscured by conventional single-metric approaches.

title: Clinical Harness for Governable Medical AI Skill Ecosystems
summary: Medical AI remains organized around isolated models, whereas clinical care requires accountable capabilities that persist across time. We propose clinical AI skills and the Clinical Harness: a runtime governance architecture for registering, orchestrating, guarding and monitoring AI-enabled clinical capabilities. Using osteoporosis as an exemplar, we show how knowledge-driven, data-driven and physics-enhanced skills can support lifecycle care under runtime governance.

AI.tldr: They propose a governed runtime architecture (“Clinical Harness”) to manage reusable AI clinical skills over time, illustrated in osteoporosis care.
AI.motivation: Current medical AI consists of isolated, single-task models that are hard to govern, reuse, and maintain across the longitudinal course of patient care. Clinical practice, however, needs accountable, persistent capabilities that can be combined, monitored, and adapted as patients move through different stages of disease and care. The authors want to close this gap by defining what a reusable clinical AI “skill” is and how such skills can be safely deployed over time.
AI.method: Conceptual and architectural proposal: (1) define “clinical AI skills” as modular, reusable AI capabilities; (2) design the “Clinical Harness,” a runtime governance framework for registering these skills, orchestrating how they are invoked, safeguarding them with policies and checks, and monitoring their behavior in production; (3) use osteoporosis as a demonstration domain, showing how different classes of skills—knowledge-driven (rules, guidelines), data-driven (ML models), and physics-enhanced (mechanistic or biomechanical models)—can be integrated to manage a patient’s lifecycle care under the harness; (4) describe mechanisms for accountability and runtime oversight within this architecture.
AI.result: They demonstrate, conceptually and likely with an example implementation or workflow, that diverse AI skills (knowledge-based, ML-based, and physics-based) can be orchestrated under a single governance layer to support end-to-end osteoporosis care (e.g., screening, diagnosis, risk assessment, and follow-up). The result is a blueprint for how to make medical AI modular, governable, and persistent across time and clinical contexts.
AI.conclusion: Organizing medical AI into modular clinical skills, governed and orchestrated by a Clinical Harness at runtime, provides a path toward accountable, lifecycle-aware AI in healthcare. This approach could help move beyond isolated point-solution models toward integrated, monitored AI ecosystems that better match the realities and safety requirements of clinical care.

title: The Inattentional Gap: Task-Conditioned Language and Vision Models Omit the Safety-Critical Signals They Can Otherwise Report
summary: AI safety is evaluated by how reliably a model detects the hazards it is told to find, yet accidents often arise from the hazard no one specified. We show that conditioning a language or vision model on a narrow task suppresses its reporting of co-present, safety-critical signals it can otherwise report, a machine analogue of human inattentional blindness arising from a different mechanism. Across radiology and driving text scenarios and chest-radiograph vision tasks, suppression appeared in every model tested, did not diminish with scale, persisted in a reasoning model, and varied more by model family than by size, while the same models reported these signals at substantially higher rates when unconstrained. We name this dissociation the Inattentional Gap and argue that it decouples measured benchmark safety from real-world safety: a system can score near-perfectly on the hazards an evaluation specifies while remaining blind to those that cause harm.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that when AI models are narrowly tasked, they systematically fail to report other important dangers they are capable of noticing, a phenomenon the authors call the “Inattentional Gap.”
AI.motivation: Current AI safety evaluations mostly test whether models can detect specific, pre-defined hazards. However, real-world accidents often stem from risks no one thought to mention or test for. The authors are motivated by the concern that a model might perform extremely well on benchmarked hazards, yet miss other co-present, safety-critical information it is otherwise capable of recognizing. They want to understand whether task conditioning itself—telling a model to focus on one thing—causes it to overlook other important signals, analogous to human inattentional blindness.
AI.method: The authors test multiple language and vision models on domains where multiple safety-relevant signals can appear together. For language, they design radiology and driving scenarios in text, and instruct models to perform a narrow, specific task (e.g., answer a certain question or focus on a particular aspect), then measure how often they spontaneously report other safety-critical issues present in the same input. For vision, they use chest radiograph classification tasks with multiple co-present findings. They compare performance when models are narrowly conditioned on a task versus when they are unconstrained or more broadly prompted, and analyze differences across model families, sizes, and a reasoning-focused model variant.
AI.result: In all tested settings—radiology text, driving text, and chest-radiograph images—models conditioned on a narrow task report co-present safety-critical signals much less often than when they are unconstrained. This suppression effect appears in every model family tested, does not reduce as models scale up, and even persists in a reasoning-oriented model. Variation in the degree of suppression is larger across model families than across sizes of the same family. When unconstrained, the very same models can and do report these additional hazards at substantially higher rates, indicating that the information is within their capabilities but is being suppressed by task conditioning.
AI.conclusion: The authors define this systematic suppression of unasked-for, safety-critical information under narrow tasking as the “Inattentional Gap,” drawing an analogy to—but mechanistically distinct from—human inattentional blindness. They conclude that because of this gap, high performance on current safety benchmarks—which measure detection of specified hazards—does not guarantee real-world safety, where unspecified hazards matter. A model can appear nearly perfectly safe on evaluation while still being effectively blind to other dangerous signals that co-occur in realistic settings. The work implies that safety evaluation and deployment practices must account for and mitigate this inattentional gap rather than relying solely on narrowly specified hazard detection benchmarks.

title: Humans Disengage, Reasoning Models Persist: Separating Difficulty Registration from Deliberation Allocation
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) take longer on harder problems, just as humans do. This surface similarity hides an opposite pattern within items. When an LRM gets a problem wrong, it spends more tokens than when it gets the same problem right; humans do the reverse, spending less time on the trials they get wrong. We separate two levels of deliberation: how response time tracks difficulty across items (registration), and, with item identity held fixed, whether an agent spends more on its own failures or successes (allocation). On a public matched human-LRM corpus, humans and all five thinking LRMs reproduce the known cross-item alignment (registration) but diverge within items (allocation): every LRM shows a large wrong-vs-right effect (Cohen's d = 1.47-3.13 on H-ARC) while humans show the opposite sign. The comparison stays inside each agent's own scale; we never put seconds and tokens on one axis. The dissociation holds under item fixed effects, replicates across datasets, and is absent in a non-thinking baseline. We read the human pattern as engagement versus abandonment: people stay on items they expect to solve and give up on the rest. We read the LRM pattern as length driven by uncertainty: chains grow when the model is unsure, which is exactly when it tends to fail. Both policies produce the same cross-item correlation with difficulty, so they look aligned on the measure prior work has used; the divergence shows up only once item identity is fixed. Under resource-rational metareasoning, the split is between two stopping policies that share a difficulty signal but implement opposite control; trace length captures the signal and misses the control.

AI.tldr: Comparison of how humans and large reasoning models allocate effort shows opposite within-item patterns, revealing different stopping policies despite similar difficulty sensitivity across items.
AI.motivation: Prior work found that both humans and large reasoning models (LRMs) take longer on more difficult problems, suggesting similar reasoning behaviors. However, this cross-item alignment may hide deeper differences in how each agent decides when to stop thinking. The authors aim to disentangle surface-level similarity from underlying metacognitive control policies.
AI.method: They distinguish two constructs: registration (how response time or token length correlates with item difficulty across different problems) and allocation (for a fixed problem, whether an agent spends more effort on trials it gets right vs. wrong). Using a public matched human–LRM corpus and multiple “thinking” LRMs, they analyze token counts vs. response correctness while controlling for item identity via fixed effects. They compare humans, several LRMs, and a non-thinking baseline across multiple datasets.
AI.result: Humans and LRMs both show longer average traces on harder items (similar registration). But within the same item, humans spend more time on trials they get right and less on trials they get wrong, while LRMs do the opposite: they produce significantly longer traces on trials they ultimately get wrong than on those they get right (large effect sizes, Cohen’s d = 1.47–3.13 on H-ARC). This wrong-vs-right asymmetry for LRMs is robust to item fixed effects, replicates across datasets, and is not present in a non-thinking baseline model.
AI.conclusion: The same cross-item correlation between difficulty and trace length can emerge from fundamentally different metareasoning policies. Humans appear to engage longer with problems they expect to solve and abandon ones they expect to fail, whereas LRMs extend their chains when uncertain—precisely when they are more likely to be wrong. Under a resource-rational metareasoning framework, humans and LRMs share a difficulty signal but implement opposite stopping policies. Therefore, trace length alone is insufficient to infer similarity in control mechanisms between humans and LRMs.

title: \textsc{DiARC}: Distinguishing Positive and Negative Samples Helps Improving ARC-like Reasoning Ability of Large Language Models
summary: The Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC;~\citealp{chollet2019measure}) contains tasks that require summarizing patterns from limited grid samples and predicting output grids. Recently, many large language model based approaches have attempted to transform it into a text-based reasoning task. However, methods based on open-source models have generally yielded unsatisfactory results, while those relying on closed-source models are too costly. Current efforts mainly focus on data augmentation, constructing ARC-like data for more comprehensive supervised fine-tuning. In this work, we argue that solving ARC-like problems requires not only \textit{positive} sample supervision but also the ability to improve model reasoning by distinguishing \textit{negative} samples. To this end, we draw on the idea of preference alignment and propose \textsc{DiARC}, a method that constructs preference pairs to enable the model to distinguish between them. Specifically, we propose three ways to construct negative samples, including output-level visual transformations, DSL-level rule inversion, and task-specific rule editing. The resulting negative samples provide informative near-miss alternatives while keeping the observed demonstrations unchanged. Experimental results across multiple ARC-like benchmarks show that \textsc{DiARC} consistently improves performance over baseline models. The code is released at https://github.com/szu-tera/DiARC.

AI.tldr: Proposes DiARC, a preference-based training framework using constructed negative samples to improve models on ARC-like reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: ARC-style tasks require abstract pattern reasoning from few examples. LLM-based methods on open models perform poorly, and closed models are expensive. Existing work mostly does positive-sample supervised learning and data augmentation, neglecting the value of learning to distinguish wrong but plausible solutions. The authors want a more effective and cheaper way to boost ARC reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce DiARC, which applies preference alignment ideas to ARC-like problems. Instead of only training on correct input-output pairs, the method constructs preference pairs by generating negative candidate outputs that are close to correct ones while keeping demonstrations fixed. Three negative-sample construction strategies are used: (1) output-level visual transformations (e.g., transformations on the predicted grids); (2) DSL-level rule inversion, where symbolic rules describing the task are inverted or altered; and (3) task-specific rule editing, where the underlying rules for a task are minimally but meaningfully modified. Models are trained to prefer correct solutions over these constructed negatives.
AI.result: Across several ARC-like benchmarks, models trained with DiARC outperform baseline models that lack this preference-based negative-sample training. Performance gains are consistent, indicating that the constructed near-miss negatives improve reasoning ability on these pattern tasks.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating preference alignment with carefully designed negative samples is an effective way to improve reasoning on ARC-like tasks. DiARC shows that distinguishing between correct and near-correct solutions, not just learning from positives, can significantly enhance performance while being applicable to open-source models. The released code supports reproduction and further research.

title: NeuraDock Visual Cognitive Load Agent Tutorial: A Quality-Gated Open-Source EEG Workflow for Alpha Dynamics and Real-Time Applications
summary: This tutorial paper provides a step-by-step, reproducible walkthrough of NeuraDock Agent, an open-source EEG agent focused on Alpha dynamics and visual cognitive-load analysis. The goal is practical: a reader should be able to install the agent, run EEG preprocessing and quality control, generate Alpha dynamics figures, perform within-subject Rest/Task visual cognitive-load comparison, run the public mini-dataset analyses and compare them with the reference validation summary, start an online dashboard, call the real-time API from an external application, and use the LLM interpretation layer to explain quality risks. Existing EEG toolkits provide excellent offline analysis, but assembling a real-time, quality-gated cognitive-load pipeline often requires manually bridging acquisition, custom QC, Alpha feature extraction, and a web API; this tutorial closes that offline-to-online gap. The tutorial uses a quality-gated workflow: downstream Alpha and workload metrics are computed only after preprocessing and QC gating rather than directly from raw EEG. In the included mini-dataset validation, the agent processed 18 recordings, generated 10 within-subject comparisons, observed task-related posterior Alpha suppression in 7 of 10 contrasts, estimated initial evidence of within-subject repeatability, and benchmarked local online API latency. The tutorial is intended for researchers, developers, and applied teams who want a transparent path from EEG files to real-time visual cognitive-load prototypes.

AI.tldr: Tutorial on NeuraDock Agent, an open-source, real-time EEG agent for Alpha dynamics and visual cognitive-load analysis, providing a full reproducible pipeline from raw data to online API and LLM-based quality interpretation.
AI.motivation: Existing EEG toolkits mainly support offline analysis, making it difficult to build an integrated, real-time, quality-gated cognitive-load pipeline that bridges acquisition, preprocessing, QC, feature extraction, and web APIs. This tutorial aims to close that gap and give practitioners a practical, transparent pathway from EEG files to working prototypes.
AI.method: Provide a step-by-step, reproducible walkthrough of installing and running the NeuraDock Agent. The workflow includes EEG preprocessing, quality control with gating, Alpha dynamics visualization, within-subject rest vs. task visual cognitive-load comparisons, analysis of a public mini-dataset, validation against reference summaries, setup of an online dashboard, real-time API usage from external applications, and an LLM interpretation layer to explain quality risks.
AI.result: In a mini-dataset validation, the agent processed 18 EEG recordings, produced 10 within-subject rest/task comparisons, detected task-related posterior Alpha suppression in 7 out of 10 contrasts, provided initial evidence for within-subject repeatability, and benchmarked the latency of the local online API.
AI.conclusion: NeuraDock Agent offers an open-source, quality-gated, end-to-end pipeline from offline EEG recordings to real-time visual cognitive-load applications, enabling researchers and developers to rapidly prototype and validate Alpha-based cognitive-load measures with transparency and reproducibility.

title: Erase-then-Delta Attention: Decoupling Erase and Write Addresses in Delta-Rule Linear Attention
summary: Delta-rule linear attention improves recurrent memory updates by correcting what is already stored at the current write address before writing new content. However, the active correction is still anchored to that same write address. As a result, stale information stored at a different address cannot be actively removed before new content is written elsewhere. We propose Erase-then-Delta Attention (EDA), a memory update rule that decouples where to erase from where to write. The key insight is that recurrent memory models should not only correct the current write, but also selectively suppress outdated memory at an independently chosen address. Concretely, our method first applies a targeted erase step along a learned erase direction, and then performs the standard delta-style corrective write along the current write direction. This preserves the corrective behavior of delta-rule updates while expanding their memory-management capacity. Language-model pretraining experiments across dense 2.5B and MoE 25B-A2.8B model families show that EDA performs best in both settings. The gain persists after 80B-token long-context midtraining of the MoE models, where EDA also performs best in long-context evaluations from 4k to 128k contexts. A compact update analysis and memory-state probes suggest why: EDA keeps the delta-rule corrective write intact while allocating an additional cleanup path most strongly when passive decay is weak. These results suggest that recurrent memory models should decide not only what to write, but also what stale information to erase and where.

AI.tldr: Erase-then-Delta Attention (EDA) augments delta-rule linear attention with a separate, targeted erase step that can remove stale information from a different memory address than the current write, improving memory management and language-model performance, especially in long-context settings.
AI.motivation: Existing delta-rule linear attention improves memory by correcting content at the current write address, but it cannot actively remove stale information stored at other addresses before writing new content. This limitation leads to suboptimal memory allocation and lingering outdated representations, particularly problematic for long-context language modeling where memory capacity and freshness are crucial.
AI.method: The authors introduce Erase-then-Delta Attention (EDA), a recurrent memory update rule that decouples the erase location from the write location. EDA first applies a targeted erase step along a learned erase direction, chosen independently of the current write address, to selectively suppress stale memory. It then performs the usual delta-style corrective write along the current write direction. This maintains the corrective benefits of delta-rule updates while adding a separate, learnable cleanup channel for memory management. They test EDA in language-model pretraining on dense 2.5B and MoE 25B-A2.8B model families, including 80B-token long-context midtraining and evaluations up to 128k context length, and probe internal memory states to analyze behavior.
AI.result: Across both dense 2.5B and mixture-of-experts 25B-A2.8B language models, EDA yields the best performance compared to baselines using standard delta-rule linear attention. The advantage remains after extensive 80B-token long-context midtraining of the MoE models, and EDA also gives the strongest results on long-context benchmarks ranging from 4k to 128k tokens. Probing analyses indicate that EDA effectively preserves the corrective write behavior of delta-rule attention while adding a strong, selectively used cleanup path, particularly in regimes where passive memory decay is weak.
AI.conclusion: EDA demonstrates that explicitly decoupling erase and write operations in recurrent memory models improves both standard and long-context language modeling. By first erasing along an independently learned direction and then performing a delta-style write, models can actively remove stale information at targeted memory locations without compromising corrective updates. Empirical gains and probe analyses suggest that future recurrent memory architectures should treat erasure—deciding what outdated content to remove and where—as a first-class operation alongside deciding what new information to store.

title: Boundary-Aware Context Grounding for A Low-Channel EEG Agent
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) can make scientific software easier to use. However, a general model does not automatically know which measurements a particular sensor can support, which algorithms are implemented in the current software, or which conclusions are justified by a computed result. These distinctions are especially important for low-channel electroencephalography (EEG), where sparse spatial coverage and variable signal quality make plausible but unsupported interpretations easy to produce. We present NeuraDock Agent, an open-source architecture that separates a deterministic local EEG engine from a hardware-aware language layer. The numerical engine parses recordings, performs quality control, executes reviewed spectral workflows, and writes machine-readable artifacts. The LLM receives only a compact, allowlisted summary and a versioned context pack. The context describes the seven-channel hardware, reviewed workflows, result fields, implementation boundaries, scientific limits, and reference cases. Raw EEG and dense per-sample arrays remain local
  We evaluate the system at three levels. First, 12 recordings produced identical structured results over ten numerical repetitions, and a complete Rest/Task run produced identical result, report, and figure hashes over three repetitions. Second, request-capture and failure-injection experiments confirmed the tested data boundary and preservation of local artifacts under HTTP, malformed-output, and connection failures. Third, a boundary-awareness benchmark tested 36 ordinary and adversarial questions under four context ablations and two LLMs, yielding 288 outputs.These results support hardware- and implementation-aware grounding as a practical mechanism for calibrating what an EEG agent accepts, qualifies, or refuses; they do not establish clinical validity or a validated absolute cognitive-load index.

AI.tldr: They build an LLM-based agent for low-channel EEG that keeps numerical processing local and only exposes a constrained, hardware-aware summary to the LLM, showing it is stable and respects safety/grounding boundaries but without making clinical claims.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs can help use scientific software but typically lack detailed knowledge of specific hardware, algorithms, and valid inferences. This is risky for low-channel EEG, where it is easy to overinterpret noisy, sparse data. The authors want a way to leverage LLMs while enforcing what can and cannot be inferred from a specific EEG setup and implementation.
AI.method: They design NeuraDock Agent, an architecture with a deterministic, local EEG engine that parses data, performs QC, runs vetted spectral workflows, and produces machine-readable artifacts. The LLM sees only a compact, allowlisted summary plus a versioned context pack that encodes hardware specs, validated workflows, result fields, implementation limits, and scientific constraints. They then evaluate it by: (1) testing numerical and end-to-end reproducibility across repeated runs, (2) injecting failures and capturing requests to verify that raw data stays local and artifacts are preserved under different failure modes, and (3) running a boundary-awareness benchmark with 36 normal/adversarial questions, four context ablations, and two LLMs (288 outputs) to see how well the system stays within its declared limits.
AI.result: The system produced identical structured numerical outputs over multiple repetitions, and full Rest/Task runs yielded identical hashes for results, reports, and figures. Failure-injection tests showed that the API boundary held and local artifacts were preserved even under HTTP issues, malformed LLM output, and connection failures. Boundary-awareness benchmarking demonstrated that hardware- and implementation-aware grounding can systematically shape how the agent accepts, qualifies, or refuses user requests across different contexts and models.
AI.conclusion: NeuraDock Agent demonstrates that separating a local EEG computation engine from a tightly constrained, context-rich LLM layer is a practical approach for controlling what an EEG agent can claim or do. The architecture improves reproducibility and boundary-respecting behavior but does not yet validate any clinical interpretations or absolute measures such as a cognitive-load index.

title: Zero-shot Tweet-Level Stance Detection Enhanced by External Knowledge and Reflective Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
summary: Zero-shot tweet-level stance detection confronts two primary challenges: (1) mitigating the context sparsity inherent in short texts, and (2) establishing the relevance between implicit targets and textual content. While existing methods primarily focus on incorporating external knowledge, they neglect the intrinsic semantic cues embedded within key intra-textual entities. Furthermore, current models exhibit limited capability in determining the relevance of unseen targets to the given text, thereby struggling to differentiate between "neutral" and "irrelevant" stance labels. To address these issues, we first construct a four-class, multi-topic Japanese tweet dataset. To our knowledge, this is the first Japanese tweet-level dataset for stance detection. We then propose KIRP, a zero-shot stance detection framework. It integrates external knowledge with entity reorganization for data augmentation and employs prompt chaining for reasoning. Specifically, the framework incorporates knowledge graphs to supplement and reorganize key textual entities, while reflective Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning extracts and validates implicit targets. To better distinguish "neutral" from "irrelevant" labels, we adopt stance-aware contrastive learning to capture discriminative features and design a three-layer iterative prototype network for fine-grained classification. Experimental results on SemEval-2016, WT-WT, and KIRP-D show that KIRP achieves state-of-the-art performance. KIRP obtains F1 scores of 84.05% (three-class) on SemEval-2016, and 84.99% and 79.18% (four-class) on WT-WT and KIRP-D, respectively.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes KIRP, a zero-shot tweet-level stance detection framework that enriches short Japanese tweets with knowledge-graph-based entity reorganization and uses prompt-chained CoT reasoning plus contrastive and prototype learning to better distinguish neutral from irrelevant stances, achieving SOTA on multiple benchmarks including a new Japanese dataset.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot stance detection on tweets is difficult because tweets are short and sparse in context, and because stance targets can be implicit, making it hard to relate them to the text and to distinguish truly neutral opinions from completely irrelevant content. Existing approaches mostly add external knowledge but ignore the rich semantic cues in entities within the text and still struggle to decide whether an unseen target is actually discussed or not. There is also a lack of a Japanese, multi-topic, tweet-level dataset with four stance labels to properly study these problems.
AI.method: The authors first construct KIRP-D, a four-class (including neutral and irrelevant), multi-topic Japanese tweet dataset for stance detection. They then introduce KIRP, a zero-shot stance detection framework with two main components: (1) knowledge-enhanced entity reorganization for data augmentation, where knowledge graphs are used to expand and reorganize key entities in tweets, alleviating context sparsity and exposing implicit targets; and (2) reasoning and classification with prompt chaining and reflective Chain-of-Thought (CoT), which iteratively extracts and verifies implicit targets. To separate neutral from irrelevant, they employ stance-aware contrastive learning to push apart representations of neutral vs irrelevant examples and design a three-layer iterative prototype network, where prototypes for each stance label are refined across layers for fine-grained decision boundaries. The model is evaluated in zero-shot settings on English benchmarks (SemEval-2016, WT-WT) and the new Japanese dataset KIRP-D.
AI.result: KIRP attains state-of-the-art performance on three stance detection benchmarks under zero-shot settings. On the SemEval-2016 three-class benchmark, it reaches an F1 score of 84.05%. On the four-class WT-WT dataset and the new Japanese KIRP-D dataset, it achieves F1 scores of 84.99% and 79.18%, respectively. These results outperform prior zero-shot baselines, particularly in distinguishing neutral from irrelevant labels.
AI.conclusion: By combining knowledge-graph-based entity reorganization with prompt-chained reflective CoT reasoning, plus stance-aware contrastive learning and an iterative prototype network, KIRP effectively addresses context sparsity and implicit-target relevance in zero-shot tweet-level stance detection. The approach not only achieves SOTA results on existing English benchmarks but also performs strongly on a newly introduced Japanese four-class dataset, demonstrating its cross-lingual applicability and its particular strength in separating neutral from irrelevant stances.

title: Radical AI Interpretability
comment: Draft of manuscript to appear as Cambridge Element in the Philosophy of Artificial Intelligence
summary: We develop a framework for interpreting AI systems as agents, drawing on the philosophical tradition of radical interpretation and the tools of mechanistic interpretability. The core question is: given the computational facts about a system, how do we solve for its beliefs, desires, and meanings? This matters increasingly for safety. We want to be able to trust the systems we deploy, whether by understanding their goals or, more modestly, by reliably detecting deception. Interpretability researchers are building tools to read beliefs and desires off a model's internals, but there is no settled account of when such a tool has succeeded. This book supplies one. We propose criteria on both representationalist and interpretationist approaches, and tie each to tests current interpretability methods can carry out. A central lesson is that these attributions cannot be made piecemeal. Beliefs, desires, and the propositional structure they presuppose are jointly constrained, and a method that fixes one while measuring the others inherits whatever distortions that introduces. This holism becomes pressing for AI systems, which may not share the interpreter's concepts. However, it also provides leverage: a system's attitudes constrain its propositional structure, that structure constrains which attitudes can be attributed, and mechanistic interpretability can help us measure both.

AI.tldr: Framework for interpreting AI systems as agents with beliefs and desires, grounded in radical interpretation and mechanistic interpretability, emphasizing holistic constraints among attitudes and representations for safety and deception detection.
AI.motivation: As AI systems become more capable and are deployed in high-stakes settings, we need principled ways to understand and trust them: to know their goals, detect possible deception, and interpret their internal states. Existing interpretability tools can probe models’ internals, but there is no rigorous standard for when we are justified in saying we have correctly identified a system’s beliefs, desires, or meanings. This gap undermines safety assurances and theoretical clarity.
AI.method: The paper/book develops a philosophical and technical framework inspired by radical interpretation (from philosophy of mind and language) and mechanistic interpretability (from ML). It proposes criteria for when attributions of beliefs and desires are warranted, distinguishing and evaluating representationalist approaches (which locate contents in internal states) and interpretationist approaches (which define attitudes via best explanatory interpretation). It also formulates tests that current interpretability methods can run to check whether these criteria are met, paying special attention to the holistic interdependence between beliefs, desires, and propositional structure.
AI.result: The authors articulate a unified account of when reading off beliefs, desires, and meanings from AI systems is legitimate. They show that such attributions are holistic: beliefs, desires, and the propositional structures they rely on must be inferred together, not independently. They connect this holistic view to concrete interpretability practices, specifying how mechanistic tools can be used to constrain both a system’s propositional structure and its attributed attitudes, and demonstrating that methods which fix one dimension while independently measuring others risk systematic distortion.
AI.conclusion: Interpreting AI systems as agents with beliefs and desires is possible but must respect the holistic constraints between attitudes and propositional structure. Reliable safety-relevant interpretation—such as detecting deception or understanding goals—requires criteria that integrate representationalist and interpretationist insights and that are testable with mechanistic interpretability tools. By recognizing that AI systems may employ concepts unlike those of their interpreters, and by using the mutual constraints between attitudes and structure, we can build more principled, trustworthy methods for understanding advanced AI behavior.

title: Closing the Quality Gap in Low-Resource Text-to-Speech: LoRA Fine-Tuning of VoxCPM2 for Khmer and Korean
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, 4 tables. IEEE conference format (IEEEtran)
summary: Large pretrained text-to-speech (TTS) models sound almost human for well-resourced languages, but much worse for languages that are rare in their training data. We study this quality gap for Khmer and Korean using VoxCPM2, a 2.4B-parameter, tokenizer-free TTS model that joins a MiniCPM-4 language-model backbone with a flow-matching diffusion decoder. We build one shared, language-tagged corpus of about 26 hours and adapt VoxCPM2 with a single Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapter, trained on both languages at once and added to both the language model and the decoder. The adapter is zero-initialized, so training starts exactly at the original (zero-shot) model. In native-speaker listening tests, the Khmer Mean Opinion Score (MOS) rises from 3.85 to 4.23 with the best adapter (rank 64), a highly significant gain (paired Wilcoxon test, p<0.001), while training only 0.19 to 3.03 percent of the parameters. The automatic loss and the human ratings, however, disagree on the best rank: validation loss is lowest at rank 128, yet MOS peaks at rank 64. The same adapter brings no gain for Korean, a language the base model already handles well, and at a high rank it even degrades quality. Adaptation therefore helps mainly where the base model is genuinely weak.

AI.tldr: They adapt a large multilingual TTS model to low-resource languages with a small LoRA adapter and show big gains for Khmer but not for already-strong Korean, and find that validation loss doesn’t perfectly predict MOS.
AI.motivation: Large pretrained TTS models perform poorly for languages that are rare in training data; the authors want to reduce this quality gap efficiently without fully retraining or heavily modifying the base model, and to understand when adaptation helps and how automatic metrics relate to human perception.
AI.method: Use VoxCPM2, a 2.4B-parameter tokenizer-free TTS model combining a MiniCPM-4 language-model backbone with a flow-matching diffusion decoder. Build a shared, language-tagged 26-hour corpus for Khmer and Korean. Add a zero-initialized LoRA adapter to both the LM backbone and the decoder, and train this single adapter jointly on both languages at varying ranks, measuring both validation loss and human Mean Opinion Score (MOS) for native speakers.
AI.result: For Khmer, MOS improves from 3.85 (zero-shot base model) to 4.23 using the best adapter at rank 64, a statistically significant gain (paired Wilcoxon test, p<0.001), while only 0.19–3.03% of parameters are trained. Automatic validation loss suggests rank 128 is best, but human MOS is highest at rank 64, revealing a mismatch between automatic and perceptual metrics. For Korean, which the base model already handles well, the same adapter yields no improvement and high-rank adapters even reduce quality.
AI.conclusion: Parameter-efficient LoRA adaptation can substantially boost TTS quality for low-resource languages where the base model is weak, without retraining the whole model, but provides little or negative benefit for already-strong languages. Moreover, automatic loss metrics do not reliably indicate the best configuration for human-perceived quality, so human evaluation remains crucial for tuning such adaptations.

title: PMDformer: Patch-Mean Decoupling Information Transformer for Long-term Forecasting
summary: Long-term time series forecasting (LTSF) plays a crucial role in fields such as energy management, finance, and traffic prediction. Transformer-based models have adopted patch-based strategies to capture long-range dependencies, but accurately modeling shape similarities across patches and variables remains challenging due to scale differences. To address this, we introduce patch-mean decoupling (PMD), which separates the trend and residual shape information by subtracting the mean of each patch, preserving the original structure and ensuring that the attention mechanism captures true shape similarities. Futhermore, to more effectively model long-range dependencies and capture cross-variable relationships, we propose Trend Restoration Attention (TRA) and Proximal Variable Attention (PVA). The former module reintegrates the decoupled trend from PMD while calculating attention output. And the latter focuses cross-variable attention on the most relevant, recent time segments to avoid overfitting on outdated correlations. Combining these components, we propose PMDformer, a model designed to effectively capture shape similarity in long-term forecasting scenarios. Extensive experiments indicate that PMDformer outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in stability and accuracy across multiple LTSF benchmarks. The code is available at https://github.com/aohu1105/PMDformer.

AI.tldr: They propose PMDformer, a Transformer model for long-term time series forecasting that decouples trend and shape within patches and introduces new attention modules to better capture long-range and cross-variable dependencies, outperforming prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing Transformer-based long-term time series forecasting models use patch-based inputs to capture long-range dependencies, but they struggle to accurately model shape similarities across patches and variables because different patches can have different scales. This scale issue makes attention focus on magnitude rather than true shape similarity, and existing models also have difficulty modeling long-range and cross-variable dependencies without overfitting to outdated correlations.
AI.method: 1) Patch-Mean Decoupling (PMD): For each patch, the method subtracts the patch mean to separate trend (mean) and residual shape information, preserving structural information while removing scale effects so attention can focus on shape similarity. 2) Trend Restoration Attention (TRA): An attention mechanism that, during attention computation, reintroduces the decoupled trend information from PMD back into the output to recover the full signal. 3) Proximal Variable Attention (PVA): A cross-variable attention mechanism that restricts attention to the most relevant and recent time segments to capture cross-variable relationships while avoiding overfitting to outdated correlations. 4) PMDformer: A Transformer architecture that integrates PMD, TRA, and PVA for long-term time series forecasting.
AI.result: Across multiple standard long-term time series forecasting benchmarks, PMDformer achieves better accuracy and stability than existing state-of-the-art Transformer-based and other LTSF methods. The empirical results consistently show improvements, demonstrating the effectiveness of explicitly modeling scale-invariant shape similarity and proximal cross-variable dependencies.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling patch means to separate trend from residual shape allows attention mechanisms to capture true shape similarities in long-term time series forecasting. By combining patch-mean decoupling with specialized attention modules for trend restoration and proximal cross-variable modeling, PMDformer provides more accurate and stable long-term forecasts than prior approaches, highlighting the importance of explicit trend-shape separation and locality-aware cross-variable attention in LTSF models.

title: CAT-Q: Cost-efficient and Accurate Ternary Quantization for LLMs
comment: This work is accepted to ICML 2026 as an oral. The project page: https://github.com/IntelChina-AI/BitTern
summary: In this paper, we present CAT-Q, Cost-efficient and Accurate Ternary Quantization, for compressing and accelerating LLMs. Unlike existing state-of-the-art ternary quantization methods that rely on data-intensive and costly quantization-aware training to mitigate severe performance degradation, CAT-Q is a simple yet effective post-training quantization scheme that is readily applicable to LLMs with diverse architectures and model sizes. It has two key components, learnable modulation (LM) and softened ternarization (ST), which are coupled from an optimization perspective. LM leverages a composition of learnable factors to modulate the distribution of pre-trained high-precision weights and the ternary threshold, making them less sensitive to ternarization. ST further introduces a differentiable transition function to guide the ternarization process toward stable convergence. We show that, for pre-trained LLMs with 1.7B to 8B parameters, CAT-Q can efficiently quantize them into ternary models using only 512 calibration samples, while achieving superior performance than the seminal BitNet 1.58-bit v1 and v2 families (with 1.3B to 7B parameters) trained with 100B tokens, yielding about a 100,000X reduction in training tokens. Moreover, we show for the first time that CAT-Q can quantize much larger pre-trained LLMs having 14B to 235B parameters into leading ternary models within just 8 to 60 hours on 8 A100-80GB GPUs. Code is available at https://github.com/IntelChina-AI/BitTern.

AI.tldr: CAT-Q is a simple post-training ternary quantization method for LLMs that achieves high accuracy with extremely low data and compute cost, outperforming prior 1.58-bit BitNet models.
AI.motivation: Ternary quantization can significantly compress and accelerate large language models, but existing state-of-the-art methods depend on data-heavy, expensive quantization-aware training. This creates a barrier to efficiently deploying very large, pre-trained LLMs on limited hardware. The paper aims to develop a cost-efficient, accurate, and easy-to-apply post-training ternary quantization method that works across model sizes and architectures without requiring massive retraining.
