AI.method: The paper proposes CAT-Q, a post-training ternary quantization scheme with two tightly coupled components: (1) Learnable Modulation (LM), which applies learnable scaling and modulation factors to reshape the distribution of pre-trained full-precision weights and adjust ternary thresholds, reducing their sensitivity to ternarization; and (2) Softened Ternarization (ST), which introduces a differentiable transition/ternary mapping function so that the ternarization process can be optimized smoothly and converge stably via gradient-based updates. Using a small calibration set (512 samples), they jointly optimize these components to map pretrained weights to ternary values while preserving task performance.
AI.result: On pre-trained LLMs ranging from 1.7B to 8B parameters, CAT-Q produces ternary models that outperform BitNet 1.58-bit v1/v2 (1.3B–7B parameters) despite BitNet being trained with 100B tokens, achieving an effective reduction of about 100,000× in required training tokens. CAT-Q also scales to much larger models (14B–235B parameters), successfully quantizing them into state-of-the-art ternary LLMs in 8–60 hours using 8 A100-80GB GPUs, with only 512 calibration samples required.
AI.conclusion: CAT-Q demonstrates that accurate ternary quantization of large language models can be achieved in a purely post-training manner, with minimal calibration data and modest compute, while matching or surpassing the performance of specialized low-bit models trained from scratch with massive token budgets. The learnable modulation and softened ternarization mechanisms enable stable, effective ternary mapping across a wide range of LLM scales, making CAT-Q a practical and scalable solution for compressing and accelerating large pre-trained LLMs.

title: Explainable Ensemble-Based Machine Learning Models for Detecting the Presence of Cirrhosis in Hepatitis C Patients
summary: Hepatitis C is a liver infection caused by a virus, which results in mild to severe inflammation of the liver. Over many years, hepatitis C gradually damages the liver, often leading to permanent scarring, known as cirrhosis. Patients sometimes have moderate or no symptoms of liver illness for decades before developing cirrhosis. Cirrhosis typically worsens to the point of liver failure. Patients with cirrhosis may also experience brain and nerve system damage, as well as gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Treatment for cirrhosis focuses on preventing further progression of the disease. Detecting cirrhosis earlier is therefore crucial for avoiding complications. Machine learning (ML) has been shown to be effective at providing precise and accurate information for use in diagnosing several diseases. Despite this, no studies have so far used ML to detect cirrhosis in patients with hepatitis C. This study obtained a dataset consisting of 28 attributes of 2038 Egyptian patients from the ML Repository of the University of California at Irvine. Four ML algorithms were trained on the dataset to diagnose cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients: a Random Forest, a Gradient Boosting Machine, an Extreme Gradient Boosting, and an Extra Trees model. The Extra Trees model outperformed the other models achieving an accuracy of 96.92%, a recall of 94.00%, a precision of 99.81%, and an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 96% using only 16 of the 28 features.

AI.tldr: The paper builds and compares machine‑learning models to automatically detect cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients, with Extra Trees achieving the best performance.
AI.motivation: Cirrhosis is a late, often initially silent, but life‑threatening consequence of chronic hepatitis C that leads to liver failure and serious complications. Early detection is clinically critical to slow progression and avoid complications, but existing diagnostic approaches are limited. Machine learning has proven successful in diagnosing various diseases, yet had not been applied specifically to detect cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients. The authors aim to fill this gap by leveraging ML to support earlier, more accurate cirrhosis detection.
AI.method: The authors use a publicly available dataset from the UCI ML Repository containing 28 clinical and laboratory attributes for 2038 Egyptian hepatitis C patients. They frame cirrhosis presence as a supervised classification problem and train four machine‑learning models: Random Forest, Gradient Boosting Machine, Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), and Extra Trees. Feature selection reduces the input space from 28 to 16 variables. Model performance is evaluated using accuracy, recall, precision, and AUC‑ROC to identify the most effective classifier for cirrhosis detection.
AI.result: Among the four evaluated models, the Extra Trees classifier performs best. Using only 16 selected features, it achieves 96.92% accuracy, 94.00% recall, 99.81% precision, and 96% AUC‑ROC for detecting cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients, outperforming Random Forest, Gradient Boosting Machine, and XGBoost on the reported metrics.
AI.conclusion: Machine‑learning models, particularly the Extra Trees classifier, can effectively detect cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients with high accuracy and discrimination using a reduced set of clinical features. This suggests ML‑based tools could support earlier and more reliable cirrhosis diagnosis, potentially aiding clinicians in preventing disease progression and complications. Further work would likely involve clinical validation, generalization to other populations, and integration into decision‑support systems.

title: SocialPersona: Benchmarking Personalized Profiling and Response with Multimodal Social-Media Context
summary: Personalized language-model assistants are often evaluated through a memory lens: can a model recall preferences users have explicitly stated in dialogue? More comprehensive personalization demands a harder capability -- inferring what users care about from the multimodal traces they naturally leave behind. We introduce SocialPersona, a benchmark for evaluating whether multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can recover revealed preferences from longitudinal social-media timelines and use them in dialogue. Built from longitudinal timelines of 171 everyday, non-promotional social-media users, SocialPersona contains text, images, timestamps, and 2,597 human-verified preference tags across seven interest domains, separating stable interests from recent interests. It supports two tasks: constructing structured user profiles from multimodal context and generating responses aligned with inferred profiles. Experiments with proprietary and open-weight MLLMs show that models can identify broad interest domains, yet their performance drops on fine-grained and recent interests and degrades further when inferred profiles must be used to personalize dialogue. Together with evidence that text and images provide complementary preference signals, these results indicate that robust cross-modal, long-horizon user modeling remains a key challenge, and that SocialPersona can help measure and advance progress toward assistants that infer and act on revealed preferences.

AI.tldr: Introduces SocialPersona, a benchmark to test if multimodal LLMs can infer and use users’ revealed preferences from long-term social-media timelines.
AI.motivation: Current personalization research mostly checks whether models can remember explicitly stated preferences in chat, which underestimates the harder, more realistic challenge of inferring what users care about from natural, long-term behavior. There is no standard benchmark that evaluates whether multimodal LLMs can recover users’ revealed preferences from their real social-media activity and use that information to personalize dialogue, especially distinguishing stable vs. recent interests.
AI.method: Build a benchmark (SocialPersona) from longitudinal social-media timelines of 171 everyday users. For each user, collect text posts, images, and timestamps. Human annotators create and verify 2,597 preference tags across seven interest domains, labeling both stable and recent interests. Define two tasks: (1) profile construction, where models must produce structured user profiles from the multimodal context; and (2) profile-conditioned dialogue, where models must generate responses aligned with the inferred profiles. Evaluate both proprietary and open-weight multimodal LLMs on these tasks and analyze their performance across interest types and modalities.
AI.result: MLLMs can reliably recover coarse, high-level interest domains from social-media traces but struggle with fine-grained interests and with identifying recent interests. Performance degrades further when models must actually use the inferred profiles to personalize dialogue. Analysis shows that text and images offer complementary information for preference inference, but current models do not yet integrate these modalities robustly over long time spans.
AI.conclusion: Robust, long-horizon, cross-modal user modeling is still an open challenge for multimodal LLMs. SocialPersona provides a realistic and structured benchmark to measure progress, revealing that current models can handle broad preference inference but fail on fine-grained, temporal, and downstream dialog personalization aspects. The benchmark can guide the development and evaluation of future assistants that better infer and act on users’ revealed preferences from their natural online behavior.

title: EvoOptiGraph: Weakness-Driven Coevolution via Graph-Based Structural Generation for Optimization Modeling
summary: Automating optimization modeling from natural language with large language models (LLMs) faces two key challenges. First, training corpora lack structural diversity. Second, data generation pipelines remain static and decoupled from model learning. To address these challenges, we propose EvoOptiGraph, a novel framework where data and model co-evolve, driven by model weaknesses. EvoOptiGraph represents each mixed-integer linear program (MILP) as an attributed bipartite graph and applies validity-preserving evolutionary operators to generate structurally diverse instances. The evolved graphs are converted into solver code and natural language via deterministic compilation and verified back-translation. Training proceeds in two stages: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on an initial dataset, followed by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), where graph-derived weakness signals guide the generation of new instances targeting the model's failures. This forms a closed loop that continuously updates the training distribution. Empirical results on six public datasets show that EvoOptiGraph significantly outperforms larger generalist models, agentic methods, and specialized baselines in accuracy, executability, and generalization. These results demonstrate that targeted data-model coevolution is an effective strategy for improving LLMs on optimization modeling tasks.

AI.tldr: EvoOptiGraph is a data–model co-evolution framework that uses graph-based evolutionary instance generation plus RL with verifiable rewards to dramatically improve LLMs at translating natural language into optimization (MILP) models.
AI.motivation: Existing attempts to have LLMs write optimization models from natural language struggle because (1) available training corpora of optimization problems lack structural variety, limiting generalization, and (2) synthetic data pipelines are static and do not adapt to what the model is bad at. The authors want a way to systematically create diverse, realistic MILP instances and training data that focus specifically on the model’s weaknesses, so that the LLM becomes more robust and accurate at optimization modeling.
AI.method: They introduce EvoOptiGraph, which encodes each MILP as an attributed bipartite graph and applies specially designed evolutionary operators that preserve feasibility and correctness while changing structure to create diverse instances. These evolved graphs are deterministically compiled into solver code and natural-language descriptions, with a verification step to ensure consistency. Training uses a two-stage pipeline: supervised fine-tuning on an initial dataset, then reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), where signals derived from graph-based weakness analysis determine what new instances to evolve and generate, closing the loop between data generation and model improvement.
AI.result: On six public benchmarks, models trained with EvoOptiGraph achieve higher accuracy in producing correct optimization models, higher code executability, and better generalization to new structures than larger general-purpose LLMs, multi-step agentic approaches, and prior specialized methods. The gains indicate that the co-evolutionary data generation and RLVR training pipeline effectively targets and reduces the model’s specific failure modes in optimization modeling.
AI.conclusion: Data–model co-evolution using a structured graph representation of MILPs and validity-preserving evolutionary operators is a powerful way to improve LLMs on optimization modeling. By continuously generating and verifying new instances tailored to the model’s weaknesses and training with RL and verifiable rewards, EvoOptiGraph surpasses both larger generic LLMs and existing specialized baselines, suggesting that targeted, structure-aware synthetic data pipelines are key for advancing LLM capabilities in formal optimization tasks.

title: Beyond Logical Forms: LLM-Extracted Patterns for Fallacy Classification
summary: In today's fast-paced information era, logical fallacies, defined as defective patterns of reasoning, inevitably contribute to the growth of information disorder. However, often fallacies appear in nuanced forms that complicate automated classification. In this study, we investigate whether merging abstract logical structures with context-level linguistic cues proves beneficial for fallacy classification, developing a framework that inductively extracts such patterns from fallacious examples and their explanations using Large Language Models (LLMs). We evaluate the impact of these patterns across different LLMs and experimental zero- and one-shot configurations, showing statistically significant improvements over zero-shot baselines and outperforming competing approaches. Cross-dataset experiments validate generalization, establishing data-driven pattern extraction as an effective method for generating logical representations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a data-driven framework that combines abstract logical structures with contextual linguistic cues, extracted via LLMs, to improve automatic classification of logical fallacies, achieving significant gains and good cross-dataset generalization.
AI.motivation: In the information era, logical fallacies contribute to information disorder, but they are hard to detect automatically because they often appear in nuanced, context-dependent forms. Existing fallacy classifiers struggle to capture both abstract logical structure and contextual linguistic signals, creating a need for better representations that can be learned from data rather than handcrafted.
AI.method: The authors develop a framework that uses Large Language Models to inductively extract abstract logical patterns and context-level linguistic cues from examples of fallacious reasoning and their textual explanations. These extracted patterns are then used as logical representations for fallacy classification. They test this across different LLMs and in zero-shot and one-shot prompting setups, comparing against standard zero-shot baselines and other competing methods.
AI.result: The proposed pattern-extraction framework yields statistically significant improvements over zero-shot baselines across multiple LLMs and experimental setups. It also outperforms competing approaches for fallacy classification. Cross-dataset experiments indicate that the learned patterns generalize well beyond the training dataset.
AI.conclusion: Data-driven extraction of logical and contextual patterns from fallacious arguments and their explanations is an effective strategy for building logical representations that improve automated fallacy classification. Merging abstract logical structure with surface linguistic cues and leveraging LLMs to induce such patterns leads to better performance and generalization than standard zero-shot or competing methods.

title: A Multi-Level Validation and Traceability Framework for AI-Generated Telescope Scheduling Decisions
summary: With the gradual introduction of AI into telescope scheduling, AI-based decision-making has shown advantages in handling complex multi-constraint problems. However, its outputs often suffer from inconsistent data references, reasoning errors, and non-executable decisions, limiting applicability in high-reliability observational tasks. In this work, we propose a multi-level validation and traceable reasoning framework that performs systematic reliability verification of AI-generated decisions prior to execution, and enables explicit representation of the reasoning process to support traceable decision-making. The framework integrates data reference validation, logical consistency checks, and observational and instrumental constraint verification to filter and correct invalid decisions. It also introduces atomic reasoning units and their dependency relationships, representing scheduling decisions as a sequence of interconnected reasoning steps that support error localization and post hoc analysis. Experiments show that the framework improves executability and reliability of AI scheduling and reduces loss of transient opportunities. In particular, feedback correction and structured validation of reasoning steps enhance the ability to repair and block erroneous decisions, especially in complex scenarios. Compared with pure AI methods, the framework-enhanced approach maintains flexibility while substantially improving reliability and executability. These results demonstrate a feasible and verifiable pathway for applying AI to high-reliability astronomical observation scheduling.

AI.tldr: They propose a reliability and traceability framework that validates and structures AI reasoning for telescope scheduling to ensure executable, high-reliability decisions.
AI.motivation: AI is increasingly used for telescope scheduling because it handles complex, multi-constraint problems well, but its decisions can be unreliable: inconsistent data references, logical errors, and non-executable plans are risky for high-reliability observational tasks, particularly transient events. There is a need for a mechanism to systematically verify and trace AI-generated decisions before real-world execution.
AI.method: They design a multi-level validation and traceable reasoning framework that sits on top of AI schedulers. It performs data reference validation, logical consistency checking, and checks against observational and instrumental constraints. The framework decomposes decisions into atomic reasoning units with explicit dependency relationships, effectively turning AI outputs into sequences of verifiable reasoning steps. It supports feedback correction, structured validation, and error localization, enabling repair or blocking of problematic decisions before execution.
AI.result: Experiments show that integrating this framework with AI scheduling increases the executability and reliability of schedules and reduces missed transient observation opportunities. The feedback and structured validation process is particularly effective in complex scenarios, improving the system’s ability to detect, repair, or block erroneous decisions compared to pure AI methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a practical, verifiable pathway for safely deploying AI in high-reliability astronomical observation scheduling. It preserves the flexibility of AI-based scheduling while significantly enhancing reliability, executability, and traceability through multi-level validation and explicit reasoning structure.

title: ConvMemory v3: A Validity Context Layer for Conversational Memory via Target-Conditioned Relation Verification
summary: Conversational memory retrieval optimizes relevance, yet a retrieved memory can be relevant and simultaneously outdated: a later turn updates, corrects, or supersedes it. ConvMemory v3 adds a validity context layer that detects and surfaces this update evidence through target-conditioned relation verification, sitting after the v1/v2 retrieval path. The core mechanism is a dual-evidence gate that conditions a relation judgment on the specific target proposition, scoring a (target, source) pair through the product of a MiniLM slot head and a DeBERTa-v3 slot head and gating it by conservative event/operation evidence. On a synthetic multi-hop validity benchmark the gate reaches 90.12% +/- 1.73 accuracy; through a real-data feedback loop that mines failure patterns but trains on synthetic pairs only, the verifier transfers to Memora role binding with zero target-side labels, reaching 98.8% +/- 0.9 group-all-correct. The deployed layer preserves retrieval by default: a context mode attaches structured validity metadata while keeping the candidate set and rank order fixed, and a query-conditioned demote mode is an explicit opt-in for dense current-state workloads, where it raises current-active H@1 from a never-demote baseline of 45.1% to 95.7% +/- 1.2 while protecting non-superseded memories at 99.4% recall. Six machine-verifiable safety contracts pin the layer's behavior. Multi-hop graph propagation is validated as a mechanism; fully automatic construction of strict prerequisite edges is characterized as a boundary, since strict necessity requires counterfactual world knowledge. This report extends ConvMemory v1 (arXiv:2605.28062) and v2 (arXiv:2606.10842).

AI.tldr: ConvMemory v3 adds a validity-aware layer on top of existing conversational memory retrieval to detect and handle outdated or superseded memories, using a dual-model relation verifier and conservative evidence gating, achieving strong synthetic and real-world performance while preserving safety and retrieval stability.
AI.motivation: Standard conversational memory retrieval systems focus on relevance only; they can resurface memories that are factually outdated, corrected later in the dialogue, or superseded by new information. This creates errors in long-running conversations and stateful tasks. The paper is motivated by the need to distinguish between still-valid and superseded memories, to support current-state reasoning without losing useful historical context, all while avoiding unsafe or destabilizing behavior in deployed systems.
AI.method: ConvMemory v3 introduces a validity context layer that sits after existing retrieval (v1/v2). It uses a target-conditioned relation verification mechanism: for a given target proposition and a candidate source memory, it evaluates whether the source remains valid, is superseded, or is related in other ways. The core is a dual-evidence gate that computes a relation score as the product of outputs from two slot-filling heads (one on MiniLM, one on DeBERTa-v3), then gates this by conservative event/operation evidence to avoid over-aggressive invalidation. The model is trained and tested on synthetic multi-hop validity tasks, and then transferred to real-world role-binding tasks (Memora) using a feedback loop that mines real failure patterns but only trains on synthetic pairs. The layer offers two deployment modes: a context mode that attaches structured validity metadata without changing candidate sets or ranks, and a query-conditioned demote mode that can lower the rank of memories judged outdated for “current state” queries. Safety is ensured through six machine-verifiable contracts and analysis of multi-hop graph propagation, along with a discussion of limits around automatically learning strict prerequisite relationships.
AI.result: On a synthetic multi-hop validity benchmark, the dual-evidence gate achieves about 90.12% ± 1.73 accuracy. When transferred to a real conversational memory task (Memora role binding) with no target-side labels, the verifier attains 98.8% ± 0.9 group-all-correct, showing strong zero-target-label generalization. In deployed demote mode for dense current-state workloads, the system boosts the rate of retrieving the currently active memory at rank 1 from 45.1% to 95.7% ± 1.2, while maintaining 99.4% recall for memories that have not been superseded. The context mode preserves the original retrieval ranking while enriching it with validity metadata. Multi-hop graph propagation is empirically validated as effective, whereas fully automatic inference of strict prerequisite edges is found to be infeasible without counterfactual world knowledge.
AI.conclusion: ConvMemory v3 successfully augments conversational memory systems with a validity-aware layer that can detect and respond to updates, corrections, and superseding events, improving current-state retrieval without sacrificing historical information. The dual-evidence gate and conservative evidence-based gating offer strong performance on both synthetic and real tasks, with good transfer from synthetic training to real-world deployment. The architecture allows safe, controlled integration into existing retrieval stacks via metadata-only context mode or opt-in demote mode, backed by formal safety contracts. However, the work also delineates limits: fully automatic construction of strict prerequisite relations remains out of reach due to the need for counterfactual world knowledge, marking a boundary for what current graph-based propagation methods can achieve.

title: Content-Based Smart E-Mail Dispatcher Using Large Language Models
summary: Email communication has become an integral part of personal and professional life, but handling its vast volume is still a significant issue for large organisations. Manual perusal of emails and forwarding their contents and attachments to intended recipients using other instant messaging platforms has proved to be error-prone and time-consuming leading to losses in terms of productivity and creating undue stress. The main objective of this paper is to explore an alternative mechanism that is to automate the task of dispatching emails based on their contents to the respective WhatsApp groups of students of various semesters of programs in an engineering college, facilitating a smooth flow of information from one end to another end in an organisation. The dispatcher system is built using agents querying large language models (LLMs) to enable it to analyze the contents of emails and route them to the relevant groups of students for their information and consumption. The system harnesses the capabilities of LLMs in analysing the textual contents for decision-making. With a well-structured agent framework prompt that includes email content as input with instructions and context, the system figures out the relevant groups to which the email message is dispatched, thus providing the required information on time. The proposed system does not rely on labelled datasets and provides several benefits, including enhanced productivity and a reduction in the cognitive load associated with reading emails.

AI.tldr: An LLM-based agent system automatically reads incoming emails in a college and forwards them to the appropriate student WhatsApp groups, reducing manual effort and errors.
AI.motivation: Large organizations face email overload; manually reading, interpreting, and forwarding relevant emails and attachments to the correct recipients via messaging apps is slow, error-prone, and causes productivity loss and stress. The authors want a more efficient and reliable way to route information from staff to specific student cohorts.
AI.method: They design an agent-based dispatcher system that queries large language models. Each agent is prompted with structured instructions, context, and the raw email content. The LLM analyzes the email text, infers which semester/program student groups are relevant, and outputs routing decisions. The framework uses prompt engineering instead of supervised training, avoiding the need for labeled datasets.
AI.result: The system can automatically determine which student WhatsApp groups should receive each email, enabling timely, targeted information delivery. It successfully demonstrates that LLMs can be used in a zero-shot/weakly supervised manner for email triage and routing in an academic setting.
AI.conclusion: An LLM-driven agent dispatcher can automate email-to-WhatsApp routing without labeled data, leading to better productivity and reduced cognitive load from email handling. Prompted LLMs are effective for content-based routing decisions in organizational communication workflows.

title: Evaluation Pitfalls and Challenges in Multimedia Event Extraction
summary: Multimedia event extraction aims to jointly identify events and their arguments across multiple modalities, such as text and images, to support more comprehensive event understanding. While recent work reports steady and substantial progress, the reliability and comparability of these results critically depend on consistent and rigorous evaluation. In this work, we present the first systematic analysis of evaluation pitfalls in multimedia event extraction and identify three major sources of issues: inconsistent data processing, inconsistent task assumptions, and overly relaxed evaluation settings. We demonstrate, through a series of controlled experiments under a strict evaluation framework, that minor evaluation choices can cause large performance variations and lead to overestimation of a model's ability to ground real-world events across modalities. Our findings highlight the need for comparable evaluation standards and encourage a shift toward more rigorous evaluation in multimedia event extraction.

AI.tldr: Paper critiques current evaluation practices in multimedia event extraction and shows small evaluation choices can cause large performance differences.
AI.motivation: Ensure that progress reported in multimedia event extraction is real and comparable by identifying flaws in existing evaluation practices.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of prior work and a series of controlled experiments using a strict, standardized evaluation framework to test how different evaluation choices affect reported performance.
AI.result: Finds three major sources of evaluation issues—inconsistent data processing, inconsistent task assumptions, and overly relaxed evaluation settings—and shows empirically that these factors can significantly inflate reported model performance.
AI.conclusion: Current evaluations in multimedia event extraction often overestimate models’ true ability to ground real-world events across modalities; the field needs stricter, standardized, and more comparable evaluation protocols.

title: LLM-based Models for Detecting Emerging Topics in Service Feedback
summary:
Enhancing the analysis of service feedback is essential for public sector organizations, particularly tax administrations, where trust and compliance depend on fair and effective service delivery. As feedback volumes grow, identifying emerging service quality issues and potential disparities across diverse populations becomes increasingly challenging. Traditional approaches often rely on manual review or static expert-defined indicators, limiting scalability and the ability to capture complex patterns in textual feedback.
  This paper presents a novel methodology that integrates large language models (LLMs), statistical techniques, and human-AI collaboration to improve multilingual customer feedback analysis. The primary objective is to detect emerging service quality topics that may also reveal potential inequities in service delivery. Our framework combines fine-tuned, quantized LLMs with expert oversight to produce accurate, computationally efficient, and context-aware analyses.
  The proposed approach was evaluated using similarity analysis and assessments from experienced tax officers, demonstrating stronger alignment with expert judgments than baseline models. By incorporating a human-in-the-loop framework, the methodology reduces LLM fabrication while improving the reliability and relevance of generated insights.
  The results demonstrate the practicality of combining LLMs with human expertise to support scalable, evidence-based decision-making in public sector organizations. This work contributes to the development of responsible AI systems that enhance service quality, responsiveness, fairness, and public trust through more effective analysis of multilingual customer feedback.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a human-in-the-loop LLM-based framework to automatically detect emerging service quality issues and potential inequities in multilingual customer feedback for tax administrations, showing better alignment with expert judgments than baseline models.
AI.motivation: Public sector organizations, especially tax administrations, rely on trust and perceived fairness, which are influenced by service quality. As they receive large and growing volumes of multilingual feedback, it becomes difficult to manually detect emerging issues and disparities across different population groups. Existing methods depend on manual review or fixed indicators, which do not scale well and may miss nuanced or evolving patterns in text data. There is a need for scalable, accurate, and context-aware analysis methods that can support evidence-based decisions and fairness monitoring.
AI.method: The authors design a framework that integrates fine-tuned and quantized large language models with classical statistical techniques and human expert oversight. LLMs are adapted for multilingual feedback analysis and used to identify and structure emerging topics related to service quality and potential inequities. Quantization improves computational efficiency. A human-in-the-loop setup is used, where tax officers review, validate, and refine the LLM outputs, reducing fabrication and aligning the system with domain expertise. The performance of the approach is evaluated via similarity analysis and expert assessments, and compared with baseline models.
AI.result: The proposed methodology achieves stronger alignment with the judgments of experienced tax officers than baseline models, indicating more accurate and relevant topic detection in multilingual feedback. The human-in-the-loop design reduces LLM hallucinations and improves the reliability of generated insights. The system proves capable of uncovering emerging service quality issues and patterns that may reflect inequities in service delivery, while remaining computationally feasible for large-scale deployment.
AI.conclusion: Integrating fine-tuned, quantized LLMs with human expert oversight offers a practical and responsible way to analyze large-scale multilingual customer feedback in the public sector. The approach enhances the detection of emerging service quality issues and potential inequities, supports scalable and evidence-based decision-making, and strengthens service quality, responsiveness, fairness, and public trust. The work illustrates how human-AI collaboration can enable more trustworthy and context-aware AI systems for public administration applications.

title: OPID: On-Policy Skill Distillation for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Outcome-based reinforcement learning provides a stable optimization backbone for language agents, but its sparse trajectory-level rewards provide little guidance on which intermediate decisions should be reinforced or suppressed. On-policy self-distillation offers dense token-level supervision, yet existing skill-conditioned variants often rely on external skill memories or retrieved privileged context, which are costly to maintain and can be mismatched with the state distribution induced by the current policy in multi-turn interaction. We propose \textbf{OPID} (\textbf{O}n-\textbf{P}olicy Sk\textbf{i}ll \textbf{D}istillation), a framework that extracts skill supervision directly from completed on-policy trajectories. OPID represents trajectory hindsight as hierarchical skills: episode-level skills capture global workflows or failure-avoidance rules, while step-level skills capture local decision knowledge at critical timesteps. A critical-first routing mechanism uses step-level skills when critical decisions are identified and falls back to episode-level skills as default guidance otherwise. The selected skill is injected into the interaction history, allowing the old policy to re-score the same sampled response under both original and skill-augmented contexts. The resulting log-probability shift yields a token-level self-distillation advantage, which is combined with the outcome advantage for policy optimization. OPID thus preserves RL as the primary training objective while introducing dense, distribution-matched hindsight supervision. Experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop and Search-based QA demonstrate that OPID generally improves agent performance, sample efficiency, and robustness over outcome-only RL and existing skill-distillation baselines. Our code is available at https://github.com/jinyangwu/OPID/tree/main.

AI.tldr: OPID is a reinforcement-learning framework for language agents that augments sparse outcome rewards with dense, on-policy skill self-distillation extracted from the agent’s own trajectories, improving performance and sample efficiency.
AI.motivation: Outcome-based RL for language agents provides stable trajectory-level optimization but suffers from sparse, delayed rewards that give little token-level credit assignment. On-policy self-distillation can add dense supervision, but existing skill-conditioned approaches depend on external memories or retrieved privileged context that are expensive and can be distribution-mismatched in multi-turn settings. The paper aims to develop a method that preserves RL as the main driver while adding dense, on-policy, hindsight-based supervision without external skill stores.
AI.method: The authors propose OPID (On-Policy Skill Distillation), which derives skill supervision directly from completed on-policy trajectories. They encode trajectory hindsight into hierarchical skills: (1) episode-level skills that summarize global workflows or failure-avoidance strategies; and (2) step-level skills that capture local decision knowledge at critical timesteps. A critical-first routing mechanism determines when a decision is critical and uses step-level skills there, defaulting to episode-level skills otherwise. The chosen skill is injected into the interaction history, and the old policy re-scores the same sampled response under both the original context and the skill-augmented context. The log-probability difference defines a token-level self-distillation advantage, which is combined with the outcome (return-based) advantage to optimize the policy via RL, keeping RL as the primary objective while adding dense hindsight supervision.
AI.result: On benchmarks including ALFWorld, WebShop, and search-based QA, OPID outperforms outcome-only RL and previous skill-distillation baselines. It yields better final performance, higher sample efficiency, and increased robustness in multi-step language-agent tasks, demonstrating the value of dense, distribution-matched on-policy skill supervision.
AI.conclusion: OPID shows that extracting hierarchical skills directly from on-policy trajectories and using them for critical-first, hindsight-based self-distillation can effectively complement outcome-based RL for language agents. By combining trajectory-level rewards with token-level log-probability shift advantages, the framework provides dense, distribution-aligned guidance without external skill memories, leading to more capable and sample-efficient language agents across several interactive benchmarks.

title: Autoformalization of Agent Instructions into Policy-as-Code
comment: Accepted at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD), ICML 2026
summary: Agent safety in high-stakes domains requires formal policy enforcement, but most existing approaches either rely on probabilistic guardrails (fine-tuned classifiers, prompt-based steering) that offer no formal guarantees, or on hand-coded symbolic enforcement that does not scale to the breadth of real policy specifications. We present an autoformalization pipeline that translates agent prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural language policy documents into formally verified policies using an LLM-based generator-critic loop. The resulting policies are written in the Cedar Policy Language. On the MedAgentBench benchmark, our autoformalized policies cover substantially more of the source natural-language specification than the hand-coded symbolic enforcement in prior work.

AI.tldr: They propose an automated pipeline that converts natural-language safety policies and agent/tool descriptions into formally verified Cedar policies, improving coverage and rigor over prior hand-written enforcement on MedAgentBench.
AI.motivation: Existing safety enforcement for AI agents in sensitive domains either use probabilistic guardrails with no formal guarantees or brittle, hand-coded symbolic rules that don’t scale to rich, real-world policies. There is a need for an automated, scalable way to obtain formally specified, verifiable safety policies from natural-language descriptions.
AI.method: They build an autoformalization pipeline driven by an LLM in a generator-critic loop that takes as input agent prompts, tool specs in the MCP framework, and natural-language policy documents. The LLM iteratively proposes candidate formal policies in the Cedar Policy Language and refines them using feedback from a critic/verifier until they satisfy constraints and align with the source specification.
AI.result: On the MedAgentBench benchmark, the autoformalized Cedar policies produced by their pipeline align with and cover a significantly larger portion of the original natural-language policy specifications than prior work that relied on manually hand-coded symbolic enforcement.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based autoformalization into a language like Cedar is a viable way to obtain formally verifiable safety policies that scale better and capture more of the intended natural-language specifications than traditional manual approaches, improving agent safety in high-stakes domains.

title: From Vajrayana Tara to Bengali Baul: A Computational Study of Lexical Transmission Across Buddhist, Shakta, and Vaishnava Traditions in Bengal
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables. Code and corpus: https://github.com/joyboseroy/bengal-dharma-corpus Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/joyboseroy/bengal-dharma-corpus
summary: We present a computational corpus study of vocabulary relationships across eight tradition layers of Bengali and Sanskrit devotional literature spanning the 8th to 19th centuries, encompassing Buddhist Vajrayana, Shakta Tantra, Vaishnava, and Baul traditions. Using a corpus of 75 texts and TF-IDF character n-gram vectorization with cosine similarity analysis, we address the historically argued but previously unquantified claim that Buddhist Vajrayana vocabulary survived the collapse of the Pala monasteries and was absorbed into the Shakta Tantra tradition of Bengal. The central finding is a specificity result: the Gitagovinda (Vaishnava Sanskrit, 12th century) has zero cosine similarity to Shakta Kali texts, while Bridge Tara texts (Buddhist-Shakta transitional, same century, same language) have cosine similarity 0.54 to Shakta Kali. This 8.5-fold contrast between two Sanskrit traditions from the same century demonstrates that the Buddhist-Shakta vocabulary overlap is not a generic property of Sanskrit devotional literature but is specific to the Buddhist-Shakta transmission chain. Three Brihannilatantra Tara texts show Shakta-to-Buddhist vocabulary ratios of 2.0 to 4.0, constituting measurable evidence of lexical transition within that chain. Ramprasad Sen's 18th-century Bengali Kali songs preserve Buddhist vocabulary residue including 56 occurrences of Tara alongside 103 occurrences of Kali. The Vaishnava Bengali tradition contributes a parallel chain to modern Baul vocabulary (similarity 0.29), slightly weaker than the Buddhist Sahajiya chain via Charyapada (0.31). These results provide the first quantitative multi-tradition corroboration of historically argued Buddhist-Shakta syncretism in Bengal.

AI.tldr: Computational corpus study quantifying how Buddhist Vajrayana vocabulary flowed into Shakta Tantra (and later Bengali Kali and Baul traditions), using n‑gram TF‑IDF and cosine similarity over 75 texts.
AI.motivation: Historians have long argued that when the Pala Buddhist monasteries declined, much of Vajrayana practice and vocabulary survived by being absorbed into Shakta Tantra in Bengal, but this claim has been qualitative and unquantified. The authors want to test this transmission hypothesis rigorously and comparatively across multiple religious-literary traditions and centuries, distinguishing specific transmission chains from generic similarities in Sanskrit or Bengali devotional literature.
AI.method: Build a diachronic corpus of 75 texts across eight tradition layers (Buddhist Vajrayana, Shakta Tantra, Vaishnava, Baul, etc.) from the 8th–19th centuries in Bengali and Sanskrit. Represent each text using TF‑IDF–weighted character n‑gram vectors to capture orthographic/lexical patterns across scripts and spelling variants. Compute pairwise cosine similarities to measure vocabulary overlap between traditions and specific texts, focusing on contrasts between Vaishnava vs Buddhist‑Shakta Sanskrit texts of the same century and later Bengali devotional corpora. Examine ratios of Shakta vs Buddhist vocabulary in transitional Tara texts and track survival of specific lexemes (e.g., ‘Tara’) into later Bengali Kali and Baul songs.
AI.result: Gitagovinda (12th‑c Vaishnava Sanskrit) shows zero cosine similarity with Shakta Kali texts, while 12th‑c Bridge Tara texts (Buddhist‑Shakta transitional, also Sanskrit) show similarity 0.54 to the same Shakta Kali corpus—an 8.5× contrast that rules out generic Sanskrit overlap as an explanation. Three Brihannilatantra Tara texts exhibit Shakta-to-Buddhist vocabulary ratios between 2.0 and 4.0, quantifying a gradual lexical shift along the transmission chain. In 18th‑c Bengali, Ramprasad Sen’s Kali songs retain clear Buddhist residues: 56 occurrences of ‘Tara’ alongside 103 of ‘Kali’. For modern Baul vocabulary, similarity to the Vaishnava Bengali chain is 0.29, slightly less than similarity 0.31 via the Buddhist Sahajiya–Charyapada chain. Overall, measured similarities and lexeme counts map distinct transmission paths from Buddhist Vajrayana into later Shakta and Baul traditions.
AI.conclusion: Lexical similarity patterns, measured via TF‑IDF n‑gram cosine similarity over a multi‑tradition corpus, substantiate the historical hypothesis that Buddhist Vajrayana vocabulary did not simply disappear with the fall of the Pala monasteries but was specifically transmitted into Shakta Tantra and further into Bengali Kali and Baul traditions. The strong contrast between Buddhist‑Shakta transitional texts and contemporaneous Vaishnava Sanskrit, along with staged vocabulary ratios in Tara corpora and persistent Buddhist lexemes in later Bengali, demonstrates that the overlap is not a generic feature of devotional Sanskrit/Bengali but evidence of a particular syncretic Buddhist‑Shakta transmission chain in Bengal.

title: SKILL-DISCO: Distilling and Compiling Agent Traces into Reusable Procedural Skills
summary: Agents often repeatedly solve similar task instances from scratch, leading to unnecessary reasoning cost and long execution traces. Prior work has explored workflow reuse and executable skill induction, but it remains unclear which task scenarios admit procedural skills and how the shared procedural structure should be represented across successful traces. We study this problem in FSM-defined scenarios, where successful traces can be viewed as paths in an unknown transition graph, and formulate procedural skills as reusable parameterized control-flow subgraphs. Based on this view, we introduce SkillDisCo, a distillation-and-compilation framework that distills reusable PFSM subgraphs from successful traces and compiles them into callable, executable, and verifiable procedural skills. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebArena show that SkillDisCo improves success rates and reduces agent turns across benchmarks and model scales, demonstrating the benefits of representing shared experience as reusable execution structures.

AI.tldr: They propose SkillDisCo, a framework to extract reusable procedural skills from successful execution traces, improving performance and efficiency of agents on complex tasks.
AI.motivation: Agents repeatedly solving similar tasks from scratch incur high reasoning costs and long execution traces. Existing approaches on workflow reuse and skill induction don’t clearly characterize when and how procedural skills should be extracted and represented. There is a need for a principled way to identify and encode reusable procedural structures from prior successful experiences.
AI.method: Model problem scenarios as finite state machines (FSM) where each successful trace is a path in an unknown transition graph. Define procedural skills as reusable parameterized control-flow subgraphs of this graph. Introduce SkillDisCo, a distillation-and-compilation pipeline that (1) distills shared PFSM (probabilistic FSM) subgraphs from collections of successful traces and (2) compiles these subgraphs into callable, executable, and verifiable procedural skills that agents can invoke in future tasks.
AI.result: On benchmarks ALFWorld and WebArena, integrating SkillDisCo into agents leads to higher task success rates and fewer interaction turns across different model sizes, indicating better sample efficiency and more effective reuse of prior execution structures.
AI.conclusion: Representing shared agent experience as reusable PFSM-based procedural skills is effective: distilled and compiled subgraphs can be invoked as skills, improving both effectiveness and efficiency of agents across tasks and scales. SkillDisCo provides a concrete framework to automatically discover, formalize, and reuse such procedural structures in FSM-defined scenarios.

title: FBK's Long-form SpeechLLMs for IWSLT 2026 Instruction Following
summary: This paper describes our submission to the IWSLT 2026 Instruction Following shared task. SpeechLLMs are developed for both short-form and long-form speech instruction following under constrained settings. For the short track, strong performance is achieved on MCIF, with a SIFS score of 2.0708. For the long track, three speech segmentation methods are explored, and the HIFS score is introduced to account for unstable long-form generation. Experimental results show that fixed 30-second segmentation provides the most robust long-form performance, achieving the highest HIFS score of 2.0663. Further analysis shows that hallucination mainly manifests as repetitive insertions in generated outputs, substantially affecting ASR and SSUM, while short-form capabilities are largely retained after long-form extension.

AI.tldr: Paper presents SpeechLLMs for short- and long-form speech instruction following in IWSLT 2026, with new metrics (SIFS, HIFS), segmentation strategies, and analysis of hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Instruction-following for spoken input and long-form generation is challenging due to unstable outputs, hallucinations, and segmentation issues; this paper aims to build robust SpeechLLMs and evaluation metrics under constrained conditions for a shared task.
AI.method: Develop SpeechLLMs tailored to short-form and long-form speech instruction following; for short-form, optimize for MCIF and evaluate with SIFS; for long-form, design and compare three speech segmentation strategies (including fixed 30s windows) and introduce a new HIFS metric to better capture instability in long-form generation; conduct experiments under the IWSLT shared-task constraints and analyze hallucination patterns in the outputs.
AI.result: Short track system attains strong performance on the MCIF benchmark with a SIFS score of 2.0708. In the long track, among three segmentation methods, fixed 30-second segmentation yields the most stable performance and best long-form HIFS score of 2.0663. Analysis finds that hallucinations appear mainly as repetitive insertions, severely impacting ASR and summarization metrics, while short-form skills are preserved in long-form models.
AI.conclusion: Fixed-length segmentation (30s) is an effective strategy for robust long-form speech instruction following with SpeechLLMs, as captured by the proposed HIFS metric. Despite hallucination issues—largely repetitive insertions that hurt downstream ASR/summary scores—core short-form instruction-following capabilities are maintained when extending to long-form. The proposed models and metrics advance evaluation and design of instruction-following SpeechLLMs in shared-task settings.

title: NebulaExp-8B: An Empirical Post-Training Pipeline via Full-Scale Ablation Research
summary: Post-training alignment determines the reasoning and human preference following capabilities of large language models, yet most existing works withhold detailed data construction, filtering rules and training recipes, which hinders community reproducibility and lightweight model optimization. This work presents NebulaExp, a fully transparent, ablation-driven post-training pipeline built on Qwen3-8B-base, covering two orthogonal model branches: general instruct model and complex reasoning-specialized model. We curate a raw corpus of 3.84M multi-source SFT samples and a 200K verifiable RL candidate pool, and design an end-to-end data processing stack including response distillation, multi-dimensional cross-verification filtering, fine-grained difficulty grading, task classification and diversity-aware sampling. For the Instruct branch, our three-stage optimized supervised fine-tuning approach NebulaExp-Ins-SFT improves the average benchmark score from the 55.01 baseline of Qwen3-8B-nothink to 60.99. GRPO reinforcement learning then further elevates the average score to 61.85. For the Reasoning branch, medium-difficulty GRPO RL improves average reasoning score from 73.88 to 75.17. To address RL's dependency on task verifiers, we systematically investigate single-teacher and multi-teacher OPD (MOPD): utilizing merely 4K instruction-following samples and outperforms RL baseline by 3.26 points on IFEval with +4.43 average overall gain; MOPD fuses four domain-specialist teachers with merely 10K samples, lifting average performance by 4.18 over the base model. This report provides a fully reproducible empirical post-training recipe for 8B-scale LLMs, and comprehensively dissects the capability trade-offs among instruction adherence, mathematical reasoning, code generation and general knowledge.

AI.tldr: NebulaExp introduces a transparent, ablation-driven post‑training pipeline for an 8B LLM (Qwen3‑8B‑base), delivering two aligned variants: a general instruction-following model and a complex reasoning–specialized model, with fully disclosed data, filters, and training recipes.
AI.motivation: Post‑training alignment strongly affects an LLM’s reasoning quality and alignment with human preferences, but most prior work hides key details of data construction, filtering, and training, limiting reproducibility and preventing smaller labs from optimizing 8B‑scale models effectively. The authors aim to provide an open, fully reproducible pipeline and to clarify how different post‑training choices trade off capabilities such as instruction following, reasoning, coding, and knowledge.
AI.method: They build NebulaExp on Qwen3‑8B‑base with two branches (Instruct and Reasoning). They curate 3.84M multi‑source SFT samples plus a 200K RL candidate pool and design an end‑to‑end processing stack: response distillation from stronger models, multi-dimensional cross-verification filters, fine-grained difficulty grading, task classification, and diversity-aware sampling. For the Instruct branch, they use a three-stage optimized supervised fine-tuning scheme, then apply GRPO reinforcement learning. For the Reasoning branch, they apply GRPO RL focused on medium-difficulty reasoning data. To reduce dependence on expensive/verifier-based RL, they study Operational Preference Distillation (OPD), including a multi-teacher variant (MOPD) that distills from several domain-specialist teacher models using small instruction-following datasets (4K–10K examples).
AI.result: For the Instruct branch, NebulaExp-Ins-SFT lifts the average benchmark score from 55.01 (Qwen3‑8B‑nothink) to 60.99; subsequent GRPO RL further raises it to 61.85. For the Reasoning branch, medium-difficulty GRPO RL improves the average reasoning score from 73.88 to 75.17. Single-teacher OPD, using only 4K instruction-following samples, beats the RL baseline on IFEval by 3.26 points and delivers a +4.43 average overall gain. MOPD, combining four specialist teachers with 10K samples, yields a +4.18 average performance boost over the base model.
AI.conclusion: NebulaExp offers a fully transparent and reproducible post‑training recipe for 8B‑scale LLMs, demonstrating that careful SFT data curation, staged fine-tuning, GRPO RL, and lightweight OPD/MOPD can significantly improve instruction following and reasoning with modest data sizes. The work also clarifies capability trade-offs across instruction adherence, math reasoning, coding, and general knowledge, providing a practical blueprint for community-driven alignment and specialization of mid‑sized LLMs.

title: Cascaded Multi-Granularity Pruning for On-Device LLM Inference in Industrial IoT
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE Internet of Things Journal for possible publication
summary: Deploying large language models (LLMs) on Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) edge devices demands extreme compression, yet existing structured pruning methods collapse at high compression ratios due to one-shot importance estimation, and their cross-architecture behavior remains unpredictable. This article presents a cascaded multi-granularity pruning framework that removes layers, attention heads, and feed-forward channels in coarse-to-fine order, with lightweight low-rank recovery between stages to re-estimate component importance. An information-theoretic analysis motivates this ordering, and the Structural Independence Assumption (SIA) is formalized as a checkable condition predicting whether per-component pruning criteria are reliable for a given architecture: Multi-Head Attention (MHA)+GELU designs satisfy the SIA, whereas Grouped Query Attention (GQA)+SwiGLU designs violate it. On bearing fault diagnosis spanning 88M to 6.25B-parameter models, the framework extends achievable compression to 13.8 times on MHA+GELU architectures with 83.82% accuracy (+3.70 percentage points (pp) over the strongest baseline), while exposing a ~74pp accuracy collapse on GQA+SwiGLU architectures that violate the SIA. Deployed on an industrial slewing bearing fault diagnosis platform with NVIDIA DGX Spark, compressed models reduce inference latency by up to 67.2% and peak memory by 62.5%, demonstrating viability for IIoT edge inference.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a cascaded multi-granularity pruning framework for compressing LLMs on IIoT edge devices, achieving much higher compression ratios and better accuracy than existing methods, and introduces an information-theoretic condition (SIA) to predict when structured pruning will be reliable across architectures.
AI.motivation: Running large language models on IIoT edge devices requires extreme compression due to limited compute and memory, but current structured pruning methods fail at high compression ratios and behave unpredictably across different model architectures. The authors aim to develop a pruning approach that remains effective and predictable across architectures, and to provide a theoretical understanding of when per-component importance scores are trustworthy.
AI.method: They design a cascaded multi-granularity pruning framework that prunes components in a coarse-to-fine order: first entire layers, then attention heads, then feed-forward channels. Between pruning stages, they apply lightweight low-rank recovery to partially restore model capacity and re-estimate the importance of remaining components. They conduct an information-theoretic analysis to justify this ordering and formalize the Structural Independence Assumption (SIA) as a condition under which per-component pruning criteria are reliable. They then analyze which transformer variants satisfy or violate SIA, focusing on MHA+GELU versus GQA+SwiGLU architectures.
AI.result: On bearing fault diagnosis tasks using LLMs ranging from 88M to 6.25B parameters, their framework achieves up to 13.8× compression on MHA+GELU models with 83.82% accuracy, which is 3.70 percentage points better than the strongest baseline at similar compression. For architectures that violate SIA, such as GQA+SwiGLU, the same strategy leads to about a 74 percentage-point accuracy collapse, empirically validating their theoretical prediction about unreliability. In a real industrial slewing bearing fault diagnosis platform using NVIDIA DGX Spark, the compressed models reduce inference latency by up to 67.2% and peak memory by 62.5%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed cascaded multi-granularity pruning with interleaved low-rank recovery substantially extends the achievable compression of LLMs for IIoT edge deployment on architectures satisfying SIA, providing both practical performance gains and a theoretical lens (SIA) for predicting pruning reliability. However, the effectiveness is architecture-dependent: designs that violate SIA, such as GQA+SwiGLU, can suffer severe accuracy degradation under aggressive structured pruning, highlighting the need to consider architectural compatibility when compressing LLMs for edge scenarios.

title: Do Safety Guardrails Need to Reason? LeanGuard: A Fast and Light Approach for Robust Moderation
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, 3 tables. Project page: https://ndb796.github.io/LeanGuard ; code and models: https://github.com/ndb796/LeanGuard
summary: In order to screen a prompt or a response, the recent guardrail methods generate a chain-of-thought (CoT) before they issue a verdict. This design follows a common belief that step-by-step reasoning improves a decision. However, CoT also makes the guard heavy and slow, because the model must generate many tokens before it decides. This may not match how guardrails are actually deployed. A guardrail sometimes should not be heavy and slow, and it often runs on-device, for example on an embodied robot. In this paper, we pose a question whether a safety guardrail really needs to reason. To answer this question, we train a lightweight bidirectional encoder and a reasoning guard on the same corpus, and we then remove only the reasoning while we keep everything else fixed. With this controlled same-base comparison, we show that the chain does not improve moderation accuracy. We name the resulting guard LeanGuard. A 395M label-only encoder reaches an average F1 of 82.90 $\pm$ 0.26 over public benchmarks. It matches a reasoning guard that is built on a much larger decoder, while it uses only a single forward pass over an input of at most 512 tokens. This is about a ~100x reduction in inference compute. We further show that this label-only encoder stays robust under training-label noise and retains far more recall at a strict false-positive rate than the reasoning guard, so a heavier reasoning guard is not the more robust choice either. Our finding suggests that the current guardrail benchmarks may not be hard enough to reward reasoning, and that the necessity of CoT for moderation is still not proven. We release all source codes and models including LeanGuard at https://github.com/ndb796/LeanGuard.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LeanGuard, a lightweight label-only encoder-based safety guardrail that avoids chain-of-thought reasoning yet matches or exceeds the accuracy and robustness of heavier reasoning-based guardrails, with ~100x less inference compute.
AI.motivation: Existing guardrail methods for moderating prompts and responses often rely on chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning before issuing a safety verdict, under the belief that step-by-step reasoning improves decisions. However, CoT-based guards are slow, computationally heavy, and poorly suited for real-world deployments like on-device or robotic systems where latency and compute are constrained. The authors question whether such explicit reasoning is actually necessary for effective safety moderation and seek to empirically test this assumption in a controlled way.
AI.method: The authors train two types of guardrails on the same corpus: (1) a lightweight bidirectional label-only encoder (LeanGuard) and (2) a reasoning guard that uses a larger decoder with chain-of-thought generation. They carefully control the comparison by removing only the reasoning component while keeping the underlying data and other training conditions fixed. They then evaluate both systems on public moderation benchmarks, measure F1 scores, inference compute, and robustness under training-label noise, and analyze performance at strict false-positive rates to assess recall behavior.
AI.result: LeanGuard, a 395M-parameter encoder, achieves an average F1 of 82.90 ± 0.26 across public moderation benchmarks, matching the performance of a much larger decoder-based reasoning guard. It operates with a single forward pass over up to 512 input tokens, yielding about a 100x reduction in inference compute. Additionally, the label-only encoder exhibits stronger robustness to noisy training labels and maintains significantly higher recall at strict false-positive-rate thresholds compared to the reasoning guard.
AI.conclusion: The study finds no empirical benefit of explicit chain-of-thought reasoning for current guardrail moderation tasks: the label-only encoder LeanGuard matches or surpasses reasoning-based guards in accuracy, robustness, and efficiency. This challenges the assumption that reasoning is necessary for safety guardrails and suggests that existing benchmarks may be too easy to meaningfully reward CoT reasoning. The authors conclude that the necessity of CoT for moderation remains unproven and release all code and models, including LeanGuard, for further investigation.

title: Information-Aware KV Cache Compression for Long Reasoning
summary: Reasoning capability has advanced rapidly in large language models (LLMs), leading to an increasing size of key-value (KV) cache in both prefilling and decoding stages. Existing KV cache compression methods mainly rely on attention weights to estimate token importance. While attention effectively captures contextual relevance, it overlooks complementary information-theoretic signals related to predictive uncertainty and token informativeness. In this paper, we revisit token importance from a forward-looking perspective and introduce \textit{Forward Influence}, a metric that measures how compressed tokens affect future contexts. Our analysis reveals that tokens selected by attention scores mainly influence nearby contexts, whereas tokens associated with high predictive uncertainty exhibit substantially stronger influence on distant future contexts. Based on the observation, we propose \textbf{InfoKV}, an entropy-aware KV cache compression framework that incorporates information-theoretic signals. It combines token-level predictive uncertainty with layer-wise representation evolution and integrates the resulting entropy scores with attention scores during reasoning. Experiments on long-context reasoning benchmarks with Llama-3.1, Llama-3.2, and DeepSeek-R1 demonstrate that InfoKV consistently outperforms existing attention-based KV compression methods in both long prefilling and decoding scenarios.

AI.tldr: They propose InfoKV, an entropy-aware KV cache compression method for LLMs that goes beyond attention scores by incorporating information-theoretic measures of token importance, improving long-context reasoning efficiency and performance.
AI.motivation: KV caches in LLMs grow large in both prefilling and decoding, making inference expensive. Existing compression approaches mainly use attention weights to judge token importance, but attention only reflects contextual relevance and tends to favor nearby tokens, neglecting information about predictive uncertainty and long-range influence. There is a need to better identify which past tokens truly matter for future predictions, especially over long contexts.
AI.method: They define a new notion of token importance called Forward Influence, which quantifies how keeping or compressing a token affects future contexts and predictions. They analyze how attention-selected tokens versus uncertainty-based tokens influence near vs. distant future tokens. Building on this, they design InfoKV, a KV cache compression framework that computes token-level predictive uncertainty (entropy) and tracks layer-wise representation evolution, combining these entropy-based scores with standard attention scores during reasoning to decide which KV entries to retain or compress.
AI.result: On long-context reasoning benchmarks using Llama-3.1, Llama-3.2, and DeepSeek-R1, InfoKV consistently surpasses prior attention-based KV cache compression methods in both long prefilling and decoding regimes, indicating better trade-offs between compression and model performance.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating information-theoretic signals—specifically predictive uncertainty and Forward Influence—into KV cache compression yields more effective identification of influential tokens than relying on attention alone. InfoKV provides a practical, entropy-aware framework that improves long-context reasoning performance while reducing KV cache size, and highlights the importance of forward-looking, uncertainty-based metrics for managing memory in LLMs.

title: Kalman Prototypical Networks for Few-shot Fault Detection in Combined Cycle Gas Turbines
summary: Combined-cycle gas turbines (CCGTs) play a key role in modern power generation, offering both high efficiency and reduced environmental impact. However, their complex thermo-fluid and mechanical interactions complicate fault detection, particularly when labeled fault data are scarce. In this paper, we introduce the Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN), a metric-based few-shot learning (FSL) framework specifically tailored for CCGT fault diagnosis. We model the evolution of class prototypes as latent stochastic states in a dynamic system to reduce episodic variance and improve robustness in embedding representation. Synthetic data sets generated with a high-fidelity Modelica-based dynamic simulation of an offshore CCGT system were used, simulating both normal operation and progressive leak faults under transient conditions. Application of the proposed framework on simulated leak fault detection tasks demonstrate that KPN outperforms conventional FSL methods such as Matching Networks, Relation Networks, and MAML in both accuracy and stability under varying support and query configurations. The proposed framework significantly improves training convergence and generalization by stabilizing class representations, making it well-suited for real-world CCGT fault detection where labeled data is limited.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN), a few-shot learning framework that models class prototypes as latent dynamic states for improved fault diagnosis in combined-cycle gas turbines with scarce labeled data.
AI.motivation: Fault detection in combined-cycle gas turbines is challenging due to complex thermo-fluid-mechanical interactions and the scarcity of labeled fault data. Conventional data-driven methods struggle when faults are poorly represented in the training set, especially under transient operating conditions. There is a need for a robust few-shot learning approach that can generalize from limited fault samples while handling dynamic behavior in the system.
AI.method: The authors design a Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN), a metric-based few-shot learning model. They treat class prototypes as latent stochastic states in a state-space (dynamic) model and use a Kalman filtering mechanism to update and stabilize these prototypes over episodes. A high-fidelity Modelica-based dynamic simulation of an offshore CCGT system is used to generate synthetic time-series datasets, including normal and progressive leak-fault conditions under transients. KPN is then trained and evaluated on few-shot classification tasks, and its performance is compared against standard FSL methods like Matching Networks, Relation Networks, and MAML across different support and query set configurations.
AI.result: On simulated leak-fault detection tasks, KPN achieves higher accuracy and more stable performance than conventional few-shot learning methods. It exhibits better robustness to variability in support/query configurations and shows improved training convergence behavior. The stabilized prototype representations lead to improved generalization to unseen episodes compared to baseline FSL approaches.
AI.conclusion: Modeling class prototypes as dynamic latent states with Kalman-style updates provides a more stable and robust embedding space for few-shot fault diagnosis in CCGTs. The proposed KPN framework outperforms existing FSL methods in both accuracy and stability on synthetic leak-fault detection benchmarks and improves convergence and generalization, indicating strong potential for real-world deployment where labeled fault data are limited.

title: Heterogeneous Neural Predictivity from Language Models During Naturalistic Comprehension
summary: Language-model representations provide structured, high-dimensional annotations of naturalistic language stimuli and can serve as informative neural predictors during comprehension. We analyzed locked derived data from Brain Treebank, MEG-MASC, and Podcast ECoG with eight frozen language models, blocked encoding models, and matched temporal, nuisance, and representation-capacity controls. Positive held-out prediction and gains over low-level baselines were widespread in source-level summaries. Across Brain Treebank and Podcast ECoG, 67 of 432 evaluable rows met a controlled predictive-only criterion, and model-side feature ablations changed prediction scores in most evaluable source rows. Brain-derived, timing-linked, acoustic, and implanted-signal controls confirmed component-level sensitivity of the analysis pipeline. These findings show that language-model-derived quantities can annotate neural activity during natural speech and text comprehension. Participant-level matched-control advantages were localized rather than uniform, response-profile and feature-specificity contrasts bounded representational or computational interpretations, and complete co-indexed integrated interpretation will require future jointly indexed coverage. Together, the analyses identify language-model features as useful neural predictors and separate predictive usefulness from claims about shared neural organization or language-processing computations.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well different frozen language models predict neural responses (MEG, ECoG, etc.) during natural speech/text comprehension, using carefully controlled encoding analyses.
AI.motivation: To rigorously test whether features from modern language models meaningfully align with brain activity during natural language comprehension, beyond what can be explained by low-level acoustic/visual or generic statistical properties, and to disentangle predictive usefulness from stronger claims about shared computation or representation.
AI.method: The authors use eight frozen language models to generate feature representations for naturalistic language stimuli used in three neural datasets (Brain Treebank, MEG-MASC, Podcast ECoG). They train blocked encoding models to predict neural signals from these features, and compare them against multiple control models: temporal controls, nuisance controls, representation-capacity controls, brain-derived/timing-linked/acoustic/implanted-signal controls. They assess prediction on held-out data, perform feature ablations on the model side, and summarize results across source-level and participant-level analyses.
AI.result: Language-model features provide positive held-out prediction and outperform low-level baselines widely at the source level. In Brain Treebank and Podcast ECoG, 67 of 432 evaluable rows pass a strict predictive-only criterion, and feature ablations significantly affect prediction scores in most evaluable source rows. Control analyses validate that the pipeline is sensitive to component-level changes rather than artifacts of timing or signal properties.
AI.conclusion: Language-model-derived features are useful predictors of neural activity during natural language comprehension, but their predictive success does not in itself imply that brains and models share the same internal organization or computations. The advantages are localized, not uniform across participants; feature and response-profile specificity place limits on representational/computational interpretations, and a full integrated account will require richer, jointly indexed datasets.

title: LithoDreamer: A Physics-Informed World Model for Multi-Stage Computational Lithography
comment: Correspondence to: Qi Sun <qisunchn \at zju \dot edu \dot cn>
summary: As semiconductor technology nodes scale, computational lithography is essential for ensuring yield and performance. However, lithography is a continuous physical process involving mask optimization, optical imaging, resist exposure, and development, which existing models fail to capture. To overcome this limitation, we present LithoDreamer, the first physics-informed World Model (WM) framework for computational lithography, which formulates the ``Layout-Mask-Resist Image-After Development Image (ADI)'' pipeline as a decision-driven multi-step evolution system. LithoDreamer captures feature changes between adjacent states to model stage-specific physics-informed latent spaces, in which it controls process intervention exploration and drives subsequent state transitions. To achieve interpretable intervention optimization without continuous supervision, we propose a contrastive variational optimization paradigm that contrasts the latent differences between intervention paths with variational evolution constraints, guiding the model to generate evolutions consistent with real lithography physics. Experiments show LithoDreamer achieves state-of-the-art performance in forward evolution and inverse planning. Our lithography dataset is publicly available at GitHub (https://github.com/7jiangyq/lithodreamer.git).

AI.tldr: LithoDreamer is a physics-informed world model for computational lithography that models the full layout-to-ADI process as a multi-step decision-driven evolution, enabling accurate forward prediction and inverse mask/process optimization.
