AI.motivation: Existing computational lithography methods struggle to fully capture the continuous, multi-stage physical process (mask, optics, resist, development). This leads to a gap between models and real lithography physics, limiting both prediction accuracy and the ability to do efficient, interpretable process/mask optimization. The authors want a unified framework that can model the sequential physical evolution and support optimization (intervention) across stages.
AI.method: They design LithoDreamer, a physics-informed World Model that treats the layout→mask→resist image→after-development-image pipeline as a multi-step decision process. The model learns stage-specific latent spaces that encode physical feature changes between adjacent states. Within these latents, the system explores and applies process interventions that drive transitions to the next state. To enable unsupervised yet interpretable optimization, they propose a contrastive variational optimization scheme, which contrasts latent differences along different intervention paths while imposing variational evolution constraints. This encourages the learned evolutions to respect real lithography physics and enables controllable, decision-driven evolution across the pipeline.
AI.result: On lithography benchmarks, LithoDreamer achieves state-of-the-art performance in both forward evolution (predicting downstream states such as ADI from upstream conditions) and inverse planning (finding masks or process settings that yield desired outputs). They release a lithography dataset on GitHub for reproducibility and further research.
AI.conclusion: Modeling computational lithography as a physics-informed world model over the full layout-to-ADI pipeline, with stage-specific latent spaces and contrastive variational optimization, yields more accurate and controllable lithography prediction and optimization than prior methods. LithoDreamer provides a unified, interpretable framework and a public dataset, potentially serving as a foundation for future lithography modeling and process optimization research.

title: SamaVaani: Auditing and Debiasing Multilingual Clinical ASR for Indian Languages
summary: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) is increasingly used to document clinical encounters, yet its reliability in multilingual and demographically diverse Indian healthcare context remains largely unknown. In this study, we first conduct the systematic audit of ASR performance on real-world psychiatric interview data spanning Kannada, Hindi and Indian English, comparing eight state-of-the-art models including IndicWhisper, WhisperLargeV3, Sarvam, GoogleS2T, Gemma3n, OmniLingual, Vaani, and Gemini. Our results reveal substantial variability across models and languages, with some systems performing competitively in Indian English but failing in regional speech. We further fine-tune two of the best performing opensource models, i.e., Gemma3n and OmniLingual, using various methods. With this, we uncover systematic performance gaps tied to speaker role and gender, raising concerns about equitable deployment in clinical settings, which are further mitigated by fairness-aware fine-tuning. To this end, we propose SamaVaani, a unified debiasing technique that simultaneously improves ASR performance and improves fairness across demographic groups.

AI.tldr: Analysis of ASR systems on multilingual Indian psychiatric interviews; introduction of SamaVaani debiasing method to improve accuracy and fairness across demographics.
AI.motivation: ASR is being used to document clinical encounters, but its reliability and fairness in multilingual and demographically diverse Indian healthcare settings, especially in psychiatric interviews across Kannada, Hindi, and Indian English, are not well understood.
AI.method: Systematically audit eight state-of-the-art ASR models (IndicWhisper, WhisperLargeV3, Sarvam, GoogleS2T, Gemma3n, OmniLingual, Vaani, and Gemini) on real-world multilingual psychiatric interview data; then fine-tune two top-performing open-source models (Gemma3n and OmniLingual) with various strategies, including fairness-aware fine-tuning, and propose SamaVaani, a unified debiasing technique.
AI.result: Observed large variability in ASR performance across models and languages, with strong performance in Indian English but poor performance on regional languages; identified systematic performance gaps by speaker role and gender; fairness-aware fine-tuning with the proposed SamaVaani technique improved overall ASR performance and reduced demographic performance gaps.
AI.conclusion: Current ASR systems are unreliable and potentially inequitable for multilingual Indian clinical use, particularly in regional languages. Fairness-aware training such as SamaVaani can both boost accuracy and mitigate demographic biases, making ASR more suitable for equitable clinical deployment.

title: A Latent ODE Approach to Spatiotemporal Modeling of Cine Cardiac MRI
summary: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR) captures rich spatiotemporal information about ventricular structure and motion, but conventional risk models use only a few image-derived indices from selected cardiac phases. We present a latent dynamical model that encodes bi-ventricular anatomy and full-cycle cine motion as a continuous latent trajectory, using heart-rate-aware neural ordinary differential equation (ODE) dynamics and a graph-based mesh autoencoder to reconstruct anatomically consistent 3D+t ventricular motion. A covariate-conditioned prior defines the expected end-diastolic latent state, and a Cox proportional hazards model tests whether deviations from this prior predict incident heart failure. We studied 72,386 UK Biobank participants without baseline cardiovascular disease, including 367 incident heart failure events. In a held-out evaluation subset, adding the latent score to refitted pooled cohort equations improved the stratified C-index from 0.704 to 0.785, compared with 0.764 for seven established cardiac markers. Compared with non-graph and non-ODE approaches, the proposed model gave the best trade-off between reconstruction fidelity, generative realism, and downstream prognostic performance. These results suggest that continuous full-cycle modeling of ventricular motion provides informative cardiac phenotypes beyond conventional CMR summaries, while external validation in more representative patient cohorts is required before clinical risk-prediction use.

AI.tldr: They build a neural latent dynamical model that encodes full-cycle 3D ventricular motion from CMR into a trajectory, and show that deviations in this latent space predict future heart failure better than standard CMR indices.
AI.motivation: Conventional cardiac risk models reduce rich 3D+t CMR data into a few static indices (e.g., volumes, ejection fraction), discarding detailed motion patterns that may carry prognostic information. There is a need for models that can exploit the entire spatiotemporal structure of ventricular motion for risk prediction, and for understanding whether these latent motion phenotypes improve prediction of incident heart failure beyond established markers.
AI.method: They design a latent dynamical system for bi-ventricular 3D+t CMR: a graph-based mesh autoencoder encodes ventricular anatomy and motion into a low-dimensional latent space; neural ODE dynamics, conditioned on heart rate, govern a continuous latent trajectory over the cardiac cycle; a covariate-conditioned prior specifies the expected end-diastolic latent state. The decoder reconstructs anatomically consistent full-cycle ventricular motion from latent trajectories. On UK Biobank CMR data (72,386 participants), they infer subject-specific latent trajectories, summarize deviations from the prior as a latent score, and feed this into a Cox proportional hazards model for incident heart failure, comparing performance with standard risk scores and alternative deep learning baselines (non-graph and non-ODE models).
AI.result: On 72,386 UK Biobank participants free of baseline cardiovascular disease, there were 367 heart failure events. In a held-out subset, augmenting refitted pooled cohort equations with the learned latent score increased the stratified C-index from 0.704 to 0.785. Using seven conventional cardiac markers yielded a C-index of 0.764, so the latent score provided additional prognostic value. Among model variants, the proposed heart-rate-aware graph-based neural ODE model achieved the best balance of reconstruction quality, realistic generative motion, and downstream heart failure risk prediction performance.
AI.conclusion: Modeling full-cycle bi-ventricular motion as a continuous trajectory in a learned latent space captures prognostically relevant cardiac phenotypes that are missed by conventional CMR summaries and standard risk markers. The proposed heart-rate-aware neural ODE plus graph-mesh autoencoder framework improves incident heart failure risk prediction in a large population cohort and produces realistic 3D+t reconstructions. However, the authors emphasize that external validation in more diverse and clinically representative cohorts is needed before translating this approach into routine clinical risk prediction.

title: GAVEL: Grounded Caption Error Verification and Localization
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) often produce hallucinated or inconsistent outputs, where text and images are not properly aligned. Addressing this issue requires not only detecting misalignment but also explaining the discrepancy and localizing its visual evidence. We introduce GAVEL (Grounded Caption Error Verification and Localization), a task that jointly addresses verification, explanation, and localization for image-text pairs. To support systematic evaluation, we also present a corresponding dataset and benchmark. We further train a supervised baseline on the human-annotated training split to assess whether GAVEL provides learnable supervision for these abilities. Experiments show that even strong closed-source models struggle on GAVEL, while the supervised baseline yields consistent improvements across grounding and explanation metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GAVEL, a task, dataset, and benchmark for detecting, explaining, and localizing vision-language misalignments, and shows that a supervised baseline can learn these skills better than existing strong models.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models often hallucinate or generate outputs where the image and text are misaligned, but existing work typically focuses on detection alone and lacks systematic tools to also explain and localize the errors. There is a need for a benchmark and supervision signal that jointly targets verification, explanation, and grounding of such misalignments.
AI.method: The authors define a new task called GAVEL (Grounded Caption Error Verification and Localization) that requires models to verify whether an image-text pair is aligned, explain any discrepancies, and localize visual evidence of the errors. They build a dataset and benchmark with human-annotated training data and then train a supervised baseline model on this data to evaluate whether these abilities can be learned in a unified way.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that even strong closed-source vision-language models perform poorly on the GAVEL benchmark across verification, explanation, and localization metrics, whereas the supervised baseline trained on GAVEL’s annotations achieves consistent improvements, particularly on grounding and explanation measures.
AI.conclusion: GAVEL provides a challenging and informative benchmark for assessing and training vision-language models to detect, explain, and localize hallucinations and misalignments, and the positive results from the supervised baseline indicate that the task offers useful supervision for developing more reliable and interpretable multimodal systems.

title: Socratic agents for autonomous scientific discovery in high-dimensional physical systems
summary: The automation of scientific discovery has reached an inflection point. While AI systems now operate instruments, optimize parameters and generate hypotheses, most remain procedural: they execute workflows fixed by human designers. True autonomous science demands epistemic autonomy--the capacity to construct, challenge and revise physical explanations in response to evidence. Here we introduce AHOIS, a multi-agent AI scientist that embeds Socratic midwifery into closed-loop experimentation. A physics-critic agent interrogates hypotheses through causal questioning, constraint checking, counterexample generation and falsification-criteria formulation. We evaluate AHOIS on a real multimode-fibre optical platform, a high-dimensional system with complex wave transformations, indirect detection, environmental drift and multi-modal acquisition. Without prior encoding schemes, classifiers or speckle models, the system autonomously proposed and validated a random-interference encoding hypothesis, discovered task-adaptive sparse-measurement strategies, diagnosed distinct failure modes (encoding instability, fluorescence contamination and detector noise) and translated a published imaging protocol into an executable workflow on a non-original configuration. The discovered encoding yielded 16x16 measurements with effective rank 56.9 and classification accuracies of 76.97% on MNIST and 83.17% on Fashion-MNIST. Ablations show that Socratic interrogation improves physical consistency, hypothesis completeness, uncertainty calibration and experimental-plan validity. These results establish a route from workflow automation towards evidence-grounded, self-correcting autonomous discovery in complex physical environments.

AI.tldr: The paper presents AHOIS, a multi-agent AI scientist that goes beyond fixed experimental workflows to autonomously generate, critique, and refine physical hypotheses through Socratic-style questioning, demonstrating self-correcting scientific discovery on a complex optical system.
AI.motivation: Most current AI systems in labs automate procedures but lack epistemic autonomy: they cannot independently form, challenge, and revise scientific explanations. The authors aim to bridge the gap between mere workflow automation and genuinely autonomous scientific discovery that is grounded in evidence and capable of self-correction, especially in complex physical environments where models are not pre-specified.
AI.method: The authors design AHOIS, a multi-agent AI system that embeds Socratic midwifery into closed-loop experimentation. A dedicated physics-critic agent interrogates candidate hypotheses using causal questioning, constraint checking, counterexample generation, and explicit formulation of falsification criteria. This system is deployed on a real multimode-fibre optical platform characterized by high-dimensional, complex wave transformations and environmental drift, without supplying prior encoding schemes, classifiers, or speckle models. The framework autonomously proposes hypotheses, designs experiments, evaluates outcomes, and iteratively refines explanations and measurement strategies. Ablation studies are conducted to assess the contribution of the Socratic interrogation components.
AI.result: On the multimode-fibre optical system, AHOIS autonomously hypothesized and validated a random-interference encoding mechanism, discovered sparse, task-adaptive measurement strategies, identified distinct experimental failure modes (encoding instability, fluorescence contamination, detector noise), and translated a published imaging protocol to a new hardware configuration. The discovered encoding achieved 16x16 measurements with an effective rank of 56.9 and produced classification accuracies of 76.97% on MNIST and 83.17% on Fashion-MNIST. Ablation experiments show that removing Socratic interrogation degrades physical consistency, hypothesis completeness, uncertainty calibration, and the validity of experimental plans.
AI.conclusion: Embedding Socratic-style, critical hypothesis interrogation into a multi-agent AI scientist enables a transition from rigid workflow automation to evidence-driven, self-correcting autonomous discovery. AHOIS demonstrates that AI systems can independently construct and refine physical explanations, adapt measurement strategies, and robustly diagnose failure modes in complex optical experiments, suggesting a viable route toward more generally autonomous scientific discovery in real-world physical laboratories.

title: Term-Centric Hierarchy Induction from Heterogeneous Corpora
summary: Organizing knowledge from diverse text sources into interpretable hierarchies is crucial for tasks such as policy analysis, innovation monitoring, and exploratory domain mapping. Existing taxonomy induction methods typically rely on document-level representations that capture entire documents rather than the specific domain concepts relevant for knowledge organization, limiting their ability to generalize across heterogeneous sources. We propose a term-centric framework for inducing hierarchical taxonomies from heterogeneous corpora that scales to massive document collections. Our approach maps documents from diverse sources into a shared representation space using automatic term extraction, enabling robust cross-source alignment. Based on these representations, we construct interpretable hierarchies that integrate domain priors with datadriven clustering. Experiments on a novel English and German multi-source benchmark of over one million documents demonstrate that our method improves cross-source coherence and hierarchy quality over text- and summarybased baselines. A case study on German regional innovation analysis further demonstrates its practical utility for technology landscape mapping.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a scalable, term-centric framework for inducing hierarchical taxonomies from large, heterogeneous text corpora, improving cross-source coherence and hierarchy quality over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing taxonomy induction methods work mainly with document-level representations, which capture whole documents instead of fine-grained domain concepts. This limits their ability to generalize and align knowledge across heterogeneous sources, which is crucial for tasks like policy analysis, innovation monitoring, and domain mapping. There is a need for a method that organizes knowledge at the term/concept level and scales to massive, multi-source corpora while producing interpretable hierarchies.
AI.method: The authors introduce a term-centric framework that first performs automatic term extraction on documents from heterogeneous sources and maps them into a shared representation space. This shared, term-based representation allows robust cross-source alignment of concepts. Using these representations, the method builds hierarchical taxonomies by combining domain priors with data-driven clustering to derive interpretable concept hierarchies. They evaluate the approach on a new multi-source benchmark (English and German, >1M documents) and conduct a case study in German regional innovation analysis.
AI.result: On the newly constructed multi-source benchmark, the proposed method outperforms text-based and summary-based baselines in terms of cross-source coherence and overall hierarchy quality. The experiments confirm that using term-centric representations and integrating domain priors with data-driven clustering yields more consistent and meaningful taxonomies across heterogeneous corpora.
AI.conclusion: The term-centric framework enables scalable, interpretable taxonomy induction from large, heterogeneous document collections. By aligning documents via automatically extracted terms and constructing hierarchies that blend domain knowledge with clustering, the method improves cross-source coherence and taxonomy quality. The successful application to German regional innovation analysis indicates its practical value for technology landscape mapping and similar analytical tasks involving diverse text sources.

title: Scientific discovery as meta-optimization: a combinatorial optimization case study
summary: Scientific discovery is fundamentally an optimization problem, defined by a vast "state space" of theories and experiments, and an evaluation criterion based on quality, novelty, and validity. Large language models (LLMs) have enabled automated exploration of this space, but we argue that simultaneous modification of the evaluation criteria is equally important. Here, we propose formalizing research as meta-optimization, where the optimization objective itself is also being optimized. Our key contribution is "consensus objective aggregation," where LLM-generated objective functions are combined via correlation-weighted voting, yielding a stable, self-correcting evaluation criterion that evolves as understanding deepens. We apply this framework to algorithm discovery for 3-SAT problems based on digital MemComputing machines, reducing the baseline scaling with problem size $N$ from $\sim N^{2.51}$ to $\sim N^{1.33}$ and delivering a $\sim 67\times$ speedup on the largest instances tested. As a problem-agnostic framework, we hope this approach will considerably aid scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a meta-optimization framework for scientific discovery where both candidate solutions and the evaluation objective are optimized, using LLMs to generate and aggregate evolving objective functions, and demonstrates its power by discovering much faster MemComputing-based algorithms for 3-SAT.
AI.motivation: Scientific discovery operates over an enormous space of hypotheses, models, and experiments, and is guided by evaluation criteria like quality, novelty, and validity. Current uses of LLMs mainly automate the search over this state space while taking the evaluation objective as fixed (e.g., accuracy, runtime). The authors argue that progress is limited if the objective itself cannot adapt as understanding improves; hence, there is a need to formalize research as meta-optimization, where the objective guiding discovery is also learned and refined. They aim to create a stable yet evolving evaluation criterion that better captures scientific value and can accelerate discovery across domains.
AI.method: 1) Formalize research as a meta-optimization problem in which not only candidate solutions (e.g., algorithms) but also the evaluation objectives are optimized. 2) Use LLMs to generate multiple candidate objective functions that quantify desiderata like performance, robustness, novelty, and plausibility. 3) Introduce "consensus objective aggregation," a scheme that combines these LLM-generated objective functions via correlation-weighted voting: objectives that are more mutually consistent and predictive receive higher weight. 4) Iteratively update the objective as new results are obtained, enabling a self-correcting, evolving evaluation criterion. 5) Apply this framework to algorithm discovery for solving 3-SAT (using digital MemComputing machines), letting the meta-optimization search over algorithmic variants guided by the aggregated objective. 6) Empirically measure the scaling of the discovered algorithm with problem size N and compare it to a baseline.
AI.result: Using the proposed meta-optimization and consensus objective aggregation framework, the authors discover improved algorithms for solving 3-SAT with digital MemComputing machines. The new algorithms reduce the scaling of computational cost from approximately N^{2.51} to approximately N^{1.33}, where N is the problem size. On the largest tested problem instances, this translates into about a 67-fold speedup compared to the baseline MemComputing implementation. The aggregated objective remains stable and self-correcting as the search progresses, providing a robust guide for further optimization.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that treating scientific discovery as a meta-optimization problem—where both candidate solutions and the evaluation objective are co-optimized—can significantly accelerate progress. Their consensus objective aggregation method yields a stable, evolving evaluation criterion that leverages LLMs to propose and refine what "good" research outcomes look like. The successful application to 3-SAT algorithm discovery, with substantial improvements in scaling and runtime, suggests that this framework is problem-agnostic and has the potential to broadly aid scientific discovery across different scientific and engineering domains.

title: RedVox: Safety and Fairness Gaps in Speech Models Across Languages
summary: Speech-capable models are increasingly deployed in real-world applications across languages. Yet their safety and fairness beyond English settings and under naturalistic conditions remain understudied. We survey safety reporting practices across state-of-the-art speech model releases, finding that only 8% document any multilingual analysis. To address this gap, we introduce RedVox, a multilingual safety and fairness benchmark for audio and speech built on real voices, covering unsafe and unfair stereotypical requests across five languages (English, French, Italian, Spanish, and German). Evaluating eight state-of-the-art models, we find that vulnerabilities persist even under non-adversarial conditions, worsen in non-English languages, and are amplified when the request comes from a spoken input. Finally, by surveying the participants who contributed to RedVox, we document the unique personal and privacy challenges of collecting speech data with human participants, pointing to broader sociotechnical challenges in naturalistic speech safety research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RedVox, a multilingual safety and fairness benchmark for speech models, and shows that current systems exhibit significant vulnerabilities, especially in non-English and spoken-input scenarios.
AI.motivation: Speech models are being widely deployed across languages, but their safety and fairness, particularly outside English and under naturalistic, real-world conditions, are poorly understood and rarely documented. Existing model releases almost never include multilingual safety analysis, leaving a critical gap in evaluating and improving these systems.
AI.method: The authors first survey safety reporting practices for state-of-the-art speech model releases to quantify how often multilingual safety is evaluated. They then build RedVox, a multilingual benchmark using real human voices and unsafe/unfair stereotypical prompts spanning five languages (English, French, Italian, Spanish, German). Using this benchmark, they systematically evaluate eight state-of-the-art models, comparing behavior across languages, input modalities (spoken vs. non-spoken), and adversarial vs. non-adversarial conditions. They also conduct a survey of participants who contributed their speech to RedVox to understand the personal, ethical, and privacy issues involved in collecting such data.
AI.result: Only 8% of surveyed state-of-the-art speech model releases reported any multilingual safety analysis. On the RedVox benchmark, all eight evaluated models showed persistent safety and fairness vulnerabilities even when the inputs were not adversarial. These problems became more severe in non-English languages and were further exacerbated when the unsafe/unfair requests were provided via spoken audio rather than text or other modalities. The participant survey revealed that contributors experienced notable personal and privacy concerns in providing their real voice data for these sensitive scenarios.
AI.conclusion: RedVox demonstrates that current speech-capable models are not robustly safe or fair across languages or modalities: their weaknesses intensify in multilingual and speech-input settings. The work highlights the necessity of systematic multilingual safety evaluation for speech models and underscores that collecting realistic speech data raises significant sociotechnical and privacy challenges that must be addressed in future research and deployment practices.

title: EGG: An Expert-Guided Agent Framework for Kernel Generation
summary: High-performance GPU kernels are critical for reducing the exponentially growing computational costs of large language models (LLMs), but their development heavily relies on manual tuning by domain experts. While recent advances in LLM-based approaches show promise for automating kernel generation, they still struggle to achieve both correctness and high performance. This limitation primarily arises from the lack of domain-specific optimization guidance, hindering effective exploration of the optimization space. We propose EGG, an Expert-Guided Agent Framework for Kernel Generation, which incorporates expert optimization principles to guide LLMs' decisions. Inspired by expert workflows, we decompose kernel generation into two hierarchical stages: 1) algorithmic structure design, which establishes a high-quality computational structure foundation; 2) hardware-specific tuning, which performs targeted adjustments through parallel mapping, tensor tiling, and memory optimization. This staged decomposition defines explicit optimization objectives, structuring the design space to achieve progressive refinement. To this end, a stage-aware multi-agent collaboration mechanism is designed for inter and intra-stage context management, ensuring stable optimization trajectories. Experiments on KernelBench and real-world workloads show that EGG achieves a 2.13x average speedup over PyTorch, outperforming existing agent-based and RL-based approaches.

AI.tldr: EGG is an expert-guided multi-agent framework that uses LLMs to automatically generate high-performance and correct GPU kernels through two hierarchical stages and structured optimization objectives, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Developing high-performance GPU kernels for LLMs is expensive and requires intensive manual tuning by experts. Existing LLM-based automatic kernel generation methods often fail to simultaneously guarantee correctness and high performance due to insufficient domain-specific optimization guidance and poor exploration of the large optimization space. There is a need for a system that embeds expert optimization principles to better guide LLMs in generating efficient and correct kernels.
AI.method: The paper proposes EGG, an Expert-Guided Agent Framework for Kernel Generation, which integrates domain expert optimization strategies into an LLM-driven multi-agent system. EGG decomposes kernel generation into two hierarchical stages: (1) algorithmic structure design, where the core computational structure is designed to be high quality; and (2) hardware-specific tuning, where optimizations like parallel mapping, tensor tiling, and memory optimization are applied. The framework uses stage-aware multi-agent collaboration with explicit optimization objectives and context management across and within stages to maintain stable and progressive optimization trajectories.
AI.result: On KernelBench and a set of real-world workloads, EGG achieves an average 2.13x speedup over PyTorch baselines and surpasses existing agent-based and reinforcement learning-based approaches for kernel generation in both performance and stability.
AI.conclusion: By embedding expert optimization principles into a hierarchically staged, multi-agent LLM framework, EGG can effectively guide the kernel generation process to achieve both correctness and high performance. The structured decomposition of the design space and stage-aware collaboration enables more efficient exploration and refinement, leading to significant speedups over standard frameworks and prior automated kernel generation methods.

title: Auditing Framing-Sensitive Behavioral Instability in Large Language Models for Mental Health Interactions
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being integrated into mental health support tools and other psychologically sensitive conversational applications. In such settings, behavioral stability and consistency are important for trustworthy human-AI interaction. However, semantically similar concerns can be presented through different contextual framings, potentially eliciting different model responses. Such framing-sensitive variability may challenge user expectations regarding system behavior and complicate the assessment of AI reliability. While prior studies have primarily examined such effects at the behavioral level, less is known about how framing-related variation is reflected in the internal representations of aligned language models. In this work, we investigate these effects using controlled matched prompts spanning multiple contextual framing conditions across several instruction-tuned model families. Across architectures, framing systematically alters interpretive response tendencies. Layer-wise probing analyses show that behavior-associated information remains decodable throughout transformer depth, with architecture-dependent variation in decoding strength. Moreover, held-out framing probes remained consistently above chance across architectures despite strong lexical baselines. Activation steering experiments further suggest that framing-associated representational directions can partially modulate downstream behavioral outcomes. Finally, these findings indicate that robustness to contextual variation may represent an important consideration when evaluating the consistency and trustworthiness of conversational AI systems deployed in mental-health-oriented interactions.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how different contextual framings of similar mental‑health-related queries change both the behavior and internal representations of instruction‑tuned LLMs, and shows that robustness to such framing is key for trustworthy use in mental health settings.
AI.motivation: LLMs are used in mental health support and other psychologically sensitive chat applications, where users expect stable, consistent behavior. However, semantically similar concerns can be expressed with different contextual framings, which may cause models to respond differently and undermine perceived reliability and safety. Existing work has mostly looked at framing effects on observable outputs, not on how these differences are encoded inside the models’ representations. The authors want to understand whether and how contextual framing systematically shapes internal activations, and what that implies for behavioral robustness and trustworthiness in mental‑health-oriented deployments.
AI.method: The authors construct controlled, matched prompts that express similar mental‑health-related concerns under multiple contextual framing conditions. They run these prompts across several instruction‑tuned LLM families with different architectures. First, they compare the models’ generated responses to quantify framing-sensitive behavioral differences. Then they conduct layer-wise probing: training classifiers on internal activations at each transformer layer to decode behavior-associated and framing-related information, including tests with held-out framing probes and lexical baselines to ensure the probes are not just picking up on surface wording. Finally, they perform activation steering experiments, identifying representation directions associated with specific framings and injecting or modifying these directions during inference to see whether they can systematically shift downstream model behavior.
AI.result: Across different architectures, the contextual framing of a mental-health-related query systematically changes the models’ interpretive tendencies and response styles. Probing shows that information predictive of these framing-dependent behaviors is decodable throughout the depth of the transformer, although the strength and distribution of this signal differ by architecture. Probes trained on one set of framing conditions generalize to held-out framing probes and remain above chance even when controlling for strong lexical baselines, indicating that the models encode framing beyond mere word choice. Activation steering along framing-associated representational directions can partially control or modulate the resulting behavior, demonstrating a causal link between these internal representations and output variability, though the control is not complete.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that contextual framing exerts a systematic and persistent influence on both internal representations and observable behavior of instruction-tuned LLMs in mental-health-relevant dialogue. This framing sensitivity challenges assumptions of behavioral stability and may complicate reliability assessments for conversational AI in sensitive psychological settings. At the same time, the existence of robust, decodable framing representations and the partial success of activation steering suggest that representation-level interventions could help manage or reduce undesirable framing effects. Overall, the authors argue that robustness to contextual variation should be a core criterion when designing, aligning, and evaluating LLMs intended for mental-health-oriented or otherwise sensitive human-AI interactions.

title: ResilPhase: Plug-and-Play Phase Mapping and Noise-Resilient Macro-Trajectory Extrapolation for Diffusion Acceleration
summary: The adoption of powerful diffusion models is hindered by their significant inference latency. Recent ``cache-then-forecast'' schemes alleviate this issue by accelerating DiTs using derivative-based polynomials, but they suffer from severe quality degradation at high acceleration ratios. Our analysis reveals its root cause: the discrete extrapolation performed on representations that are misaligned with the continuous diffusion trajectory and are numerically unstable. Thus, accelerated DiTs suffer from accumulated spatial errors, noisy derivative amplification, and high-order instability. We therefore reformulate accelerated inference as stable macro-trajectory extrapolation in ordinary differential equation (ODE) space. Instead of predicting intermediate features, we align forecasting with the model's Global Drift (GD), i.e., the end-to-end state evolution, thereby eliminating feature inconsistency and memory overhead. However, even this smooth macro-trajectory remains vulnerable to the derivative fallacy: its higher-order temporal derivatives are intrinsically noisy. Thus, we introduce a derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolator to effectively bypass derivative instability and approximation error. We further propose a bounded Phase Mapping that regularizes the extrapolation domain, suppressing oscillatory error growth. These elements collectively constitute ResilPhase, a noise-resilient acceleration framework. Experiments on FLUX.1-dev and HunyuanVideo demonstrate state-of-the-art fidelity under aggressive acceleration ratios.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ResilPhase, a noise-resilient acceleration framework for diffusion Transformers (DiTs) that performs stable, derivative-free macro-trajectory extrapolation in ODE space to achieve state-of-the-art image/video quality under aggressive acceleration ratios.
AI.motivation: Powerful diffusion models, especially diffusion Transformers, suffer from high inference latency, which limits their practical adoption. Existing cache-then-forecast acceleration methods use derivative-based polynomial extrapolation of intermediate features, but at high acceleration ratios they cause severe quality degradation due to numerical instability and trajectory misalignment. The paper is motivated by understanding and fixing the root causes of these failures so that diffusion models can be accelerated aggressively without losing fidelity.
AI.method: The authors first analyze why existing derivative-based, feature-space extrapolation methods fail, identifying misalignment with the true continuous diffusion trajectory and numerical instability (spatial error accumulation, noisy derivative amplification, and high-order instability). They then reformulate accelerated inference as stable macro-trajectory extrapolation in ODE space, focusing on the model’s Global Drift (GD), i.e., end-to-end state evolution, rather than intermediate features. To avoid the intrinsic noise in higher-order temporal derivatives (the "derivative fallacy"), they introduce a derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolator to forecast future states from past GD samples. Additionally, they propose a bounded Phase Mapping that regularizes the extrapolation domain, reducing oscillatory error growth. These components are integrated into the ResilPhase framework for accelerating DiTs.
AI.result: ResilPhase achieves state-of-the-art fidelity for diffusion Transformer acceleration at aggressive acceleration ratios, outperforming prior cache-then-forecast and derivative-based acceleration methods. Empirical evaluations on FLUX.1-dev and HunyuanVideo show that ResilPhase can significantly reduce inference time while preserving perceptual quality and stability of generated content better than competing approaches.
AI.conclusion: By reframing diffusion model acceleration as stable macro-trajectory extrapolation in ODE space and using derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolation with bounded phase mapping, ResilPhase overcomes the instability and quality loss of previous derivative-based feature extrapolation schemes. The work demonstrates that aligning extrapolation with the model’s global drift and avoiding noisy higher-order derivatives enables aggressive acceleration of diffusion Transformers with minimal degradation, providing a practical path toward faster, high-fidelity diffusion inference.

title: ReaORE: Reasoning-Guided Progressive Open Relation Extraction Empowered by Large Reasoning Models
summary: Open Relation Extraction (OpenRE) requires a model to extract unseen relations between head and tail entities from unstructured text for real-world applications. The core challenge of OpenRE lies in achieving reliable generalization to unseen relation types. Current OpenRE approaches either employ clustering techniques, which cannot generate relation labels and suffer from poor generalization, or rely on direct relation label generation via Large Language Models (LLMs), which lack sufficient discriminative capacity to distinguish easily confused relations. To address these limitations, we propose Reasoning-guided progressive OpenRE (ReaORE), a framework for performing relation extraction through coarse-to-fine relation reasoning. Specifically, ReaORE consists of two key stages: (i) relation filtering, which reasons over multiple aspects to understand relations and instances, yielding an initial relation set, and further supplements and filters relations via embedding-based similarity to ensure the target relation is included; (ii) relation prediction, which aims to predict the target relations from the above set via fine-grained comparative reasoning to better distinguish easily confused relations. Extensive experiments on two widely used OpenRE datasets demonstrate that ReaORE outperforms existing baselines.

AI.tldr: They introduce ReaORE, a reasoning-guided, coarse-to-fine framework for open relation extraction that improves generalization to unseen relation types and outperforms prior methods.
AI.motivation: Open Relation Extraction must handle unseen relation types in real-world text, but existing methods struggle. Clustering-based approaches cannot output explicit relation labels and generalize poorly, while LLM-based direct label generation lacks fine discriminative power for similar, easily confused relations. There is a need for a method that both generates explicit labels and reliably distinguishes fine-grained, novel relations.
AI.method: ReaORE is a two-stage, reasoning-guided framework. (1) Relation filtering: it performs multi-aspect reasoning over relations and instances to form an initial candidate relation set, then refines this set via embedding-based similarity to ensure the true target relation remains included. (2) Relation prediction: given this candidate set, it conducts fine-grained comparative reasoning to decide which candidate is the correct relation, explicitly focusing on distinguishing between closely related or easily confused relations. The whole pipeline works in a coarse-to-fine manner from broad filtering to precise prediction.
AI.result: On two standard OpenRE benchmarks, ReaORE achieves better performance than existing baselines, indicating improved generalization to unseen relation types and better discrimination among similar relations.
AI.conclusion: A reasoning-guided, progressive (coarse-to-fine) approach effectively addresses the main challenges of OpenRE. By combining multi-aspect reasoning, embedding-based candidate refinement, and fine-grained comparative reasoning, ReaORE can generate and select accurate relation labels for unseen relations, outperforming prior OpenRE methods on public datasets.

title: Memory Depth, Not Memory Access: Selective Parametric Consolidation for Long-Running Language Agents
comment: Main paper with supplementary material included as ancillary file
summary: Long-running language agents need more than memory access. Retrieval systems can fetch past facts at query time, but they do not decide which experiences should continue to shape behavior after the working context is unloaded. We study this separate problem as memory depth: durable goal-conditioned tendencies written into a small parametric store. We introduce the loop-drift protocol, a controlled stress test in which the retrieval index remains intact while working context is unloaded and goal-conditioned behavior must persist under long-loop interference. We evaluate EVAF, a surprise- and valence-gated LoRA consolidation mechanism. Across GPT-2 and TinyLlama, retrieval is strongest on shallow factual recall (short-fact accuracy 0.956--0.973), while EVAF is strongest on goal persistence and post-unload recovery (0.812--0.904) with only 2--3 parametric writes per 200 events. Mechanism controls show that selective consolidation factorizes into two controllable dimensions: selection and actuation. Matched random gates isolate selection beyond sparse writing; fixed-inner controls across GPT-2, TinyLlama, and Mistral-7B show that inner-loop write strength is model-dependent; and a Mistral-7B matched-gate inversion reveals asymmetric selection-actuation coupling under miscalibrated actuation. Public Memora event streams serve as an external diagnostic, exposing stale-memory invalidation as an unresolved boundary. Within this probe, selective parametric consolidation supplies memory depth distinct from and complementary to retrieval access.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and evaluates mechanisms for "memory depth" in long-running language agents, focusing on durable, goal-conditioned tendencies beyond simple retrieval.
AI.motivation: While retrieval systems let language models access past facts, they do not determine which experiences should leave lasting influence after the working context is gone. The authors want to understand and engineer how agents can maintain persistent, goal-directed behavior (memory depth) under long interactions and interference, which retrieval alone cannot provide.
AI.method: They define "memory depth" as durable, goal-conditioned behaviors stored in a small parametric memory, separate from retrieval indexes. They introduce the loop-drift protocol, a stress-test where retrieval remains intact but the working context is repeatedly unloaded, forcing agents to preserve goals over many loops of interference. They propose EVAF, a LoRA-based consolidation mechanism gated by surprise and valence signals, which selectively writes to a small set of parameters. Experiments compare retrieval vs EVAF across models (GPT-2, TinyLlama, Mistral-7B), measure short-fact recall, goal persistence, and post-unload recovery, and use several mechanism controls (matched random gates, fixed inner loops, gate inversion) to factorize selection vs actuation and explore model dependence of write strength.
AI.result: Retrieval-based memory excels at shallow factual recall (short-fact accuracy 0.956–0.973), but does not provide strong, persistent goal behavior after context unloads. EVAF, with only 2–3 parametric writes per 200 events, achieves much higher performance on maintaining and recovering goals after unloads (0.812–0.904). Control experiments show that selective consolidation can be decomposed into selection (which events to write) and actuation (how strongly they change parameters). Random-gate baselines demonstrate benefits beyond sparsity; fixed-inner controls reveal that optimal write strengths vary by model; and a gate-inversion experiment on Mistral-7B exposes asymmetric coupling between selection and actuation when actuation is miscalibrated. External tests on Memora event streams highlight unresolved issues with invalidating outdated memories.
AI.conclusion: Selective parametric consolidation via EVAF provides a form of "memory depth" that is different from and complementary to retrieval-based memory. The loop-drift protocol demonstrates that durable, goal-conditioned tendencies can be written into small parametric stores, supporting long-run goal persistence even when working context is repeatedly cleared. However, challenges remain, such as handling stale or outdated memories, and the interaction between selection and actuation is nuanced and model-dependent.

title: Where Do Models Find Happiness? Emotion Vectors in Open-Source LLMs
summary: Recent work identified emotion vectors in Claude Sonnet 4.5, which are internal representations that encode emotion concepts, causally influence behavior, and exhibit geometry mirroring human psychological structure. We test the generality of these findings in two open-weight models, Apertus-8B-Instruct-2509 and Gemma-4-E4B-it, extracting emotion contrast vectors across all layers, using two model-generated corpora. We recover valence geometry for both models, with peak PC1--valence correlations of $r = 0.76$ and $r = 0.83$, approaching the $r = 0.81$ reported for Claude.Beyond replication, we observe notable differences in how valence representations emerge across model depth. In Gemma-4-E4B-it, valence is strongly encoded in early layers but collapses towards later layers, whereas Apertus-8B-Instruct-2509 exhibits the opposite pattern, with valence representations absent in early layers, but emerging at mid depths. Arousal encoding, in contrast, is sensitive to the extraction corpus: both models show stronger PC2--arousal alignment with Gemma-generated stories ($r$ up to $0.45$) than Apertus-generated ones ($r \leq 0.21$), suggesting arousal-relevant cues are unevenly distributed across generated corpora. We open-source our experiment code and dataset for reproducible investigation of emotion representations across language model architectures.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether emotion representations (like valence and arousal) found in one proprietary model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) also appear in two open-weight language models, and how these representations vary across layers and data sources.
AI.motivation: Prior work showed that a proprietary LLM, Claude Sonnet 4.5, has internal directions (emotion vectors) that align with human emotion dimensions and causally affect behavior, but it is unclear if these are idiosyncratic to that model or a general property of LLMs. The authors want to test generality across different open-weight architectures and understand where in the network these emotion concepts emerge and how stable they are across corpora.
AI.method: They take two open-weight LLMs (Apertus-8B-Instruct-2509 and Gemma-4-E4B-it), generate two text corpora using the models themselves, and extract emotion contrast vectors layer-wise. They then perform PCA on these representations and correlate the principal components—especially PC1 and PC2—with standard emotion dimensions, valence and arousal, computed from labels or lexicons. They compare correlation strengths across layers, models, and corpora to characterize how emotion geometry emerges and varies.
AI.result: Both models exhibit a clear valence dimension in their internal representations, with maximum correlation between PC1 and valence of r=0.76 (Apertus) and r=0.83 (Gemma), close to the r=0.81 previously observed in Claude Sonnet 4.5. However, the layerwise profiles differ: Gemma strongly encodes valence in early layers but this signal collapses in later layers, while Apertus lacks valence in early layers but develops it at mid depths. Arousal is encoded less consistently and is highly dependent on the generation corpus: both models show higher PC2–arousal alignment with Gemma-generated stories (up to r=0.45) than with Apertus-generated ones (≤0.21), implying corpus-specific distribution of arousal cues.
AI.conclusion: Emotion-like internal structure, especially valence, appears to be a robust and general feature across different LLM architectures, but the depth at which it emerges can differ substantially between models. Arousal is more fragile and data-dependent, suggesting that not all emotion dimensions are equally well represented. By open-sourcing their code and datasets, the authors facilitate further reproducible research on how emotion concepts are internally represented and how they vary across architectures and training data.

title: KARLA: Knowledge-base Augmented Retrieval for Language Models
summary: We propose a new method that allows an LLM to automatically pull in factual knowledge from a knowledge base during token generation. This means that (1)~factual knowledge in the LLM output can be updated without retraining the LLM, (2)~facts in the LLM output can be traced to the knowledge base for transparency and explainability, and (3)~smaller models can achieve the same factual accuracy as larger models. Our core idea is to train the model to produce special tokens that trigger a query to the knowledge base. Our experiments show that our method improves factual grounding in both short and long-form generation, and allows factual revisions to take effect through KB edits rather than parameter updates.

AI.tldr: A method to let LLMs dynamically query a knowledge base during generation, improving factual accuracy, updatability, and traceability without retraining.
AI.motivation: Standard LLMs store factual knowledge in parameters, making it hard to update facts, trace information sources, or get high factual accuracy without scaling model size. There is a need for a mechanism where LLMs can access external knowledge bases on the fly so that facts can be updated easily, transparently traced, and used by smaller models.
AI.method: Train the LLM to emit special control tokens during token generation. When such a token appears, it triggers a query to an external knowledge base (KB). The retrieved factual information is then incorporated into the ongoing generation. The training objective encourages the model to learn when and how to use these special tokens to request KB facts appropriately.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that the method improves factual grounding in both short and long-form text generation. The system can incorporate factual revisions simply by editing the knowledge base instead of updating model parameters, and smaller models enhanced with this mechanism reach factual accuracy levels comparable to larger baseline models.
AI.conclusion: Integrating explicit knowledge-base queries into the token generation process enables LLMs to be more accurate, updatable, and transparent. This approach decouples factual knowledge from model parameters, supports traceability of facts to KB entries, and allows smaller models to close the factual accuracy gap with larger ones via targeted KB access.

title: MinGram: A Minimalist Unigram Tokenizer with High Compression and Competitive Morphological Alignment
summary: The Unigram tokenizer uses an elegant representation which makes it straightforward to edit vocabularies, but its training is comparatively heavy and complex. We introduce MinGram (Minimalist Unigram), which keeps the token-list representation but simplifies training using a BPE-derived seed vocabulary, Hard EM on a minimum-token path, and a single flat score-pruning step. This removes the suffix array, the forward-backward pass, and the iterative prune loop, leaving a procedure that requires little beyond tokenizer inference itself. By making token count the primary objective and using a Unigram score only as a tiebreak, MinGram keeps the compression of pure token-count methods while retaining much of the morphological alignment and downstream quality of probabilistic ones. Across six languages, MinGram compresses better than both BPE and standard Unigram, and a compression-oriented variant matches the strongest token-count compressors while retaining substantially higher morphological alignment. In controlled downstream language-model training, Unigram-family tokenizers, with MinGram among the best, consistently beat BPE in bits-per-byte.

AI.tldr: MinGram is a simplified Unigram-style tokenizer that keeps Unigram’s flexible token-list representation but uses a lightweight, BPE-seeded training procedure, achieving better compression and strong downstream performance across languages.
AI.motivation: Standard Unigram tokenizers have an elegant representation that makes vocabulary editing easy, but they require complex, computationally heavy training with suffix arrays, forward-backward EM, and iterative pruning. There is a need for a tokenizer that preserves Unigram’s advantages while reducing training complexity and cost, and ideally improving compression and downstream model performance compared to both BPE and traditional Unigram.
AI.method: The authors propose MinGram, a minimalist Unigram-family tokenizer. It keeps the Unigram-style token-list representation but simplifies training via: (1) initializing from a BPE-derived seed vocabulary; (2) using Hard EM focused on minimum-token segmentation paths instead of full probabilistic EM; and (3) applying a single flat score-pruning step rather than iterative prune loops. This design removes the need for a suffix array, the forward–backward pass, and repeated pruning, so training is close in complexity to tokenizer inference. The objective is primarily to minimize token count, using a Unigram score only as a tiebreaker, attempting to combine the compression strength of token-count-based methods with the morphological alignment and quality of probabilistic methods.
AI.result: On experiments across six languages, MinGram achieves better compression than both BPE and standard Unigram tokenizers. A variant specifically tuned for compression matches the best token-count-based compressors while still maintaining substantially better morphological alignment. In controlled language-model training comparisons, tokenizers from the Unigram family—including MinGram as one of the top performers—consistently outperform BPE in bits-per-byte, indicating more efficient representations for downstream models.
AI.conclusion: MinGram demonstrates that it is possible to retain the structural and practical advantages of Unigram tokenizers while radically simplifying training. By using a BPE-seeded vocabulary, Hard EM on minimum-token paths, and a single pruning pass with token-count-focused objectives, MinGram both improves compression and supports better downstream language modeling than BPE, offering a strong, efficient alternative within the Unigram tokenizer family.

title: Computational Analysis of Heart Rate Variability in Healthy Adults
summary: Heart Rate Variability (HRV) analysis is a key indicator of cardiac physiological state and aids in disease diagnosis. However, research on HRV parameters in healthy individuals remains limited, and no gold standard exists. This study evaluates HRV indices in 40 healthy adults (20 men, 20 women, aged 30-50) to improve HRV's clinical utility. Using computational methods for signal processing and data analysis, time, frequency, and nonlinear indices were analyzed to address five questions: (1) normality, (2) stability, (3) correlation, (4) reproducibility, and (5) consistency. Key findings: (1) Time-domain and nonlinear indices, particularly global and LF (low frequency), follow normal distributions, with gender differences noted. (2) Most indices are stable except HF (high frequency)-related ones. (3) High correlations in HF-related indices suggest redundancy, indicating only one is necessary in studies. (4) Comparisons with the Fantasia database revealed less than 10% error for most indices, except SD2 and SDNN in women (greater than 15%). (5) Time-domain and nonlinear indices show low inter-study variability, while frequency-domain indices exhibit high variability, limiting cross-study comparisons. The selected indices-ApEn and IRRR (global variability), HRVi and SD2 (LF), and MADRR or rMSSD (HF)-are best suited for accurately representing HRV components and enhancing its clinical and research relevance.

AI.tldr: The paper characterizes heart rate variability (HRV) indices in healthy adults and recommends a compact set of robust, clinically useful metrics.
AI.motivation: HRV is widely used as an indicator of cardiac autonomic function and disease risk, but normative data in healthy adults and consensus on which indices are most reliable and informative are lacking. This limits HRV’s clinical adoption and makes it hard to compare results across studies. The paper aims to define normal ranges, assess reliability, and identify the most useful HRV indices in healthy individuals.
AI.method: The authors recorded HRV data from 40 healthy adults (balanced by gender, aged 30–50). Using computational signal processing, they computed standard time‑domain, frequency‑domain, and nonlinear HRV indices. They then systematically tested each index for distribution shape (normality), short‑term stability, inter‑index correlations (redundancy), reproducibility against the Fantasia reference database, and consistency across different studies (inter‑study variability).
AI.result: They found that many time‑domain and nonlinear indices—especially global and low‑frequency (LF) measures—are approximately normally distributed and show some gender differences. Most indices are stable over time except those related to high‑frequency (HF) components. HF‑related indices are strongly correlated with each other, indicating redundancy. When compared with the Fantasia database, most indices showed <10% error, but SD2 and SDNN in women deviated by >15%. Time‑domain and nonlinear indices had low inter‑study variability, whereas frequency‑domain indices varied substantially across studies, reducing their comparability.
AI.conclusion: Time‑domain and nonlinear HRV indices are generally more stable, normally distributed, and consistent across studies than frequency‑domain measures, making them more suitable for clinical and research use. Because HF indices are redundant, a single representative metric is sufficient. The authors recommend a practical core set of indices—ApEn and IRRR for global variability, HRVi and SD2 for LF, and MADRR or rMSSD for HF—to capture the main HRV components and to standardize HRV analysis in healthy adults.

title: Improving General Role-Playing Agents via Psychology-Grounded Reasoning and Role-Aware Policy Optimization
summary: Building general-purpose role-playing agents that faithfully portray any character from a natural-language profile remains challenging. The dominant paradigm -- supervised fine-tuning -- encourages behavioral mimicry without deep, human-like internal thought processes, resulting in poor out-of-distribution generalization. Therefore, we propose \textbf{Psy-CoT}, a psychology-grounded chain-of-thought framework that decomposes pre-response reasoning into three role-specific steps -- \emph{Interaction Perception}, \emph{Psychological Empathy}, and \emph{Logical Construction} -- so that the model \emph{thinks dynamically} from the profile rather than merely mimicking surface patterns. While structured reasoning provides a foundation, it alone is insufficient; reinforcement learning is essential to further align the model with character fidelity. However, we observe that under LLM-based reward models, both generic phrases that hack the reward model and genuinely role-specific phrases receive identical gradient signals -- this hacking accumulates over training, misleading the model into treating both as equally optimal choices. To address this, we propose \textbf{Role-Aware Policy Optimization (RAPO)}, which uses profile--token mutual information to weight gradients asymmetrically -- amplifying role-specific tokens under positive advantage while attenuating them under negative advantage. Experiments on CoSER, CharacterBench, and CharacterEval demonstrate that Psy-CoT outperforms existing role-playing CoT methods, and RAPO consistently surpasses GRPO across multiple model scales.

AI.tldr: They introduce Psy-CoT, a psychology-inspired chain-of-thought framework plus a new reinforcement learning algorithm (RAPO) to build more faithful, generalizable role-playing agents from natural-language character profiles.
AI.motivation: Existing role-playing agents trained with supervised fine-tuning mostly learn to mimic surface behaviors from data instead of developing dynamic, human-like internal reasoning from character profiles. This leads to poor generalization, especially out-of-distribution, and limited character fidelity. Moreover, when using LLM-based reward models in RL, generic “reward-hacking” phrases and genuinely role-specific behavior get similar positive gradients, so models learn to exploit reward models instead of truly embodying roles.
AI.method: They propose Psy-CoT, a psychology-grounded chain-of-thought decomposition for role-playing agents with three explicit reasoning stages: (1) Interaction Perception (understanding the current conversational context), (2) Psychological Empathy (inferring the character’s internal states, motivations, and emotions consistent with the profile), and (3) Logical Construction (deriving the final response from these in-character mental states). To further align behavior, they introduce Role-Aware Policy Optimization (RAPO), a reinforcement learning algorithm that uses mutual information between the character profile and generated tokens to modulate policy gradients: role-specific tokens get larger gradient magnitude under positive advantage and are down-weighted under negative advantage, while generic tokens are less amplified, reducing reward hacking. They train and evaluate models with these components on standard role-playing benchmarks.
AI.result: On three benchmarks—CoSER, CharacterBench, and CharacterEval—the Psy-CoT framework yields better role-playing quality than existing chain-of-thought or reasoning-based role-playing approaches. Additionally, RAPO consistently outperforms a strong baseline RL method, GRPO, across various model sizes, indicating more faithful and robust character adherence with less reward hacking.
AI.conclusion: Psychology-grounded, structured reasoning (Psy-CoT) combined with role-aware reinforcement learning (RAPO) more effectively teaches LLMs to dynamically think and act from character profiles, rather than merely copying surface patterns. This leads to better character fidelity and generalization in role-playing tasks, and mitigates reward-model exploitation by preferentially reinforcing truly role-specific behavior.

title: The Capability Frontier: Benchmarks Miss 82% of Model Performance
summary: Existing benchmarks typically report accuracy for a single model on a single run. This systematically understates real-world LLM capabilities, particularly under heterogeneous data distributions: (i) different models get different questions correct according to their specializations, and (ii) given a budget, multiple generations can be sampled and selectively retained. To quantify this gap, we introduce the Capability Frontier: a Pareto frontier over a set of models that characterizes the best achievable performance at each cost level under optimal selection across models and generations (i.e., via an oracle). Our construction corrects for two opposing biases: underestimation from single-model evaluation and overestimation from taking maxima over noisy samples. We study 21 LLMs across 16 widely used benchmarks spanning coding, reasoning, medicine, factuality, instruction following, and agentic tasks, comparing Capability Frontier performance at matched cost to each benchmark's top-performing model. Correcting for single-model evaluation yields a 54% error rate reduction; additionally correcting for single runs yields an 82% improvement, with SOTA accuracy matched at 85% cost reduction. Complementing these empirical results, we use controlled probabilistic simulations to show that higher query topic entropy produces a near-monotonic increase in the performance gap between oracle routing and the best single model. Our findings suggest collective LLM capabilities are substantially underestimated, with implications for evaluation and deployment in data-heterogeneous, multi-domain settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Capability Frontier, a way to measure the best possible performance achievable by optimally combining multiple LLMs and multiple generations at different cost levels, showing that current single-model, single-run benchmarks significantly underestimate real-world LLM capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks evaluate one model with one run, which misses two major real-world factors: different models excel on different kinds of questions, and practitioners can sample multiple generations and keep only the best. This leads to systematic underestimation of what is achievable in practice, especially when data is heterogeneous and multi-domain. The authors want a principled way to quantify the realistic upper bound of performance across costs when using a pool of models and generations.
AI.method: They define the Capability Frontier as a Pareto frontier over a pool of LLMs and generation samples, where an oracle selects the best answer at each query for a given cost. This corrects for underestimation from single-model evaluation and overestimation from naïvely taking maxima over noisy samples. Empirically, they compute this frontier across 21 LLMs and 16 popular benchmarks spanning several task families (coding, reasoning, medicine, factuality, instruction following, agent tasks). They then compare Capability Frontier performance, at matched cost, against the strongest single model on each benchmark. Additionally, they run controlled probabilistic simulations varying query topic entropy to study how heterogeneity in query distribution affects the advantage of oracle routing over the best single model.
AI.result: Across the 16 benchmarks, adjusting for single-model evaluation alone yields a 54% reduction in error rate relative to standard reports. When they also account for multiple generations and optimal selection, the improvement rises to 82%. They further show that state-of-the-art benchmark accuracy can be matched while cutting cost by 85% when optimally combining models and samples. Simulations demonstrate that as query topic entropy (heterogeneity) increases, the performance gap between oracle routing and the best single model grows in a near-monotonic fashion.
AI.conclusion: Collective capabilities of LLM ecosystems—using multiple models and multiple samples with smart selection—are far higher than current benchmark numbers suggest. Standard single-model, single-run evaluations substantially underestimate what is achievable, especially on heterogeneous, multi-domain workloads. Evaluations and deployment strategies should consider multi-model routing and multi-sample selection, and future benchmarks should reflect these more realistic usage patterns by incorporating Capability Frontier-type analyses.

title: NuclearQAv2: A Structured Benchmark for Evaluating Domain-Science Competence in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of tasks, but ensuring their reliability in highly technical domains remains a significant challenge. In nuclear engineering, problem solving often requires not only factual knowledge but also quantitative reasoning and conceptual understanding. To address the need for systematic evaluation in this domain, we introduce NuclearQAv2, a benchmark for assessing LLMs on nuclear engineering knowledge. The benchmark comprises approximately 1,240 question-answer pairs spanning three categories: boolean, numeric, and verbal. NuclearQAv2 is constructed using a hybrid pipeline that combines expert-authored questions, existing datasets, and LLM-assisted generation from domain-specific technical corpora. By leveraging structured prompting for both automated question generation and response evaluation, the proposed framework enables scalable benchmark construction and evaluation. We evaluate a diverse set of LLMs using NuclearQAv2 and observe substantial performance differences across task types. While the models generally perform well on factual questions, quantitative reasoning and conceptual understanding remain considerably more challenging. These results highlight the importance of multi-faceted evaluation frameworks and establish NuclearQAv2 as a scalable benchmark for assessing LLM capabilities in technical domains.

AI.tldr: The paper presents NuclearQAv2, a benchmark of ~1,240 questions to systematically evaluate large language models on nuclear engineering knowledge, highlighting that LLMs handle factual queries better than quantitative and conceptual ones.
AI.motivation: Despite strong general performance, LLMs’ reliability in highly technical, safety‑critical fields like nuclear engineering is unclear. Existing evaluations underemphasize the combination of factual recall, quantitative reasoning, and deep conceptual understanding needed in this domain. The authors aim to create a systematic, scalable way to measure LLM capabilities specifically for nuclear engineering tasks.
AI.method: The authors build NuclearQAv2, a benchmark of about 1,240 question‑answer pairs across three types: boolean (true/false-style), numeric (quantitative problems), and verbal (explanatory or conceptual questions). They use a hybrid construction pipeline that mixes (1) expert-authored questions, (2) questions from prior datasets, and (3) LLM-assisted generation from domain-specific technical corpora. Structured prompting is applied both to generate candidate questions and to automate parts of the response evaluation, making the process scalable.
AI.result: Using NuclearQAv2, the authors test a variety of LLMs and find large performance gaps across question types. Models perform relatively well on factual, boolean-style questions but their accuracy drops significantly on numeric reasoning and deeper conceptual tasks, indicating that current LLMs struggle with the more technical and quantitative aspects of nuclear engineering.
AI.conclusion: NuclearQAv2 provides a scalable, multi-faceted benchmark for evaluating LLMs in nuclear engineering, revealing that present models are much stronger at surface-level factual recall than at quantitative reasoning and conceptual understanding. The work underscores the need for richer evaluation frameworks and suggests that further methodological advances are required before LLMs can be relied on for complex technical problem solving in domains like nuclear engineering.

title: Context-Aware Synthesis of Optimization Pipelines for Warehouse Optimization
summary:
Order fulfillment in manual picker-to-goods warehouses involves interconnected decisions such as item assignment, order batching, and picker routing. While integrated models capture interactions between these decisions, practical warehouse systems often require decomposed approaches due to organizational boundaries, differing responsibilities, or limited data availability. Existing studies primarily evaluate algorithms for isolated subproblems or fixed subproblem combinations for specific warehouse settings, but lack a general mechanism to determine applicable algorithm configurations, compose them into valid solution pipelines, and assess their performance.
  With Context-Aware Synthesis of Optimization Pipelines (CASOP), we propose a framework for constructing and evaluating context-specific optimization pipelines and apply these to order fulfillment. The framework comprises: (1) a modular repository of algorithms for common order fulfillment problems; (2) semantic data and algorithm cards to describe warehouse context and algorithm requirements; (3) a taxonomy that structures order fulfillment problems into relevant subproblems; (4) a pipeline synthesizer that identifies applicable algorithms for a given warehouse context and composes all valid optimization pipelines; and (5) a pipeline evaluator that assesses all resulting pipelines. We demonstrate the framework on 7 benchmark instance sets covering four problem classes, resulting in 1,063,044 valid pipelines. The framework supports researchers and practitioners in designing, automatically synthesizing, and selecting valid, high-performing algorithmic pipelines for warehouse operations. The software is open-source and available at https://github.com/kit-dsm/ware_ops_pipes and https://github.com/kit-dsm/ware_ops_algos.
  Keywords: Warehouse optimization, Algorithm selection, Pipeline synthesis, Order fulfillment

AI.tldr: Framework to automatically build and evaluate algorithmic pipelines for warehouse order fulfillment, based on context and modular algorithm components.
AI.motivation: Manual warehouses face several interconnected optimization decisions (item assignment, order batching, routing). Integrated models are often impractical due to organizational constraints and data silos, while existing research focuses on isolated subproblems without guidance on how to combine algorithms or configure them for specific contexts. There is no generic mechanism to compose and evaluate end-to-end solution pipelines tailored to a particular warehouse.
AI.method: Propose CASOP, a context-aware synthesis framework. It includes: (1) a modular repository of algorithms for typical order-fulfillment subproblems, (2) semantic data and algorithm cards encoding warehouse characteristics and algorithm preconditions/requirements, (3) a taxonomy decomposing order fulfillment into subproblems, (4) a pipeline synthesizer that matches context with algorithm requirements and enumerates all valid algorithm pipelines, and (5) a pipeline evaluator that benchmarks and compares all synthesized pipelines. They implement CASOP and apply it to multiple benchmark instance sets.
AI.result: On 7 benchmark sets across four order-fulfillment problem classes, CASOP generates 1,063,044 valid pipelines. This demonstrates that the framework can systematically construct many feasible algorithm combinations and identify high-performing configurations for different warehouse contexts.
AI.conclusion: CASOP enables automated, context-aware construction and assessment of optimization pipelines for warehouse order fulfillment. It gives researchers and practitioners a structured way to select and combine algorithms, moving from isolated subproblem studies to end-to-end, warehouse-specific solution pipelines.
