Open-source software supports adoption and further research.

title: Towards Explainable Adjudicative Variance: Quantifying Judicial Discretion via Gated Multi-Task Learning
comment: 17 pages (8 pages main text), 5 figures, 9 tables. Accepted to the AI for Law Workshop at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026), Seoul, South Korea
summary: Legal outcome prediction must disentangle objective case facts from adjudicative context. Merit-based rulings rely on factual evidence while technical disposals may hinge on judicial discretion. We propose a Judge-Aware Gated Multi-Task Learning architecture that explicitly models this distinction. We introduce a fine-grained outcome taxonomy to supervise the encoder, enforcing a structural regularization that disentangles distinct semantic pathways. This granular legal curriculum enables our Gated Fusion mechanism to dynamically modulate reliance on judge identity. We evaluate our approach on 13,937 UK Employment Tribunal decisions. We benchmark our design against supervised fine-tuning (SFT) of a Gemma-4 26B-A4B backbone, in which judge identity and the taxonomy are injected as prompt tokens or autoregressive output targets. The two contextual signals compose only weakly when forced through a single autoregressive channel. In contrast, coupling a LoRA-adapted Gemma-4 encoder with our gated architecture defines a new state of the art on this benchmark while requiring an order of magnitude fewer trainable parameters than the generative SFT baselines, with gains concentrated on the most ambiguous and rarest outcome classes. Beyond accuracy, the architecture is interpretable; learned judge embeddings and calibration profiles localize the cases where adjudicative context drives the prediction. These results indicate that, for identity-conditioned classification of legal outcomes, the choice of conditioning interface dominates scale: differentiable structured composition yields more accurate, more parameter-efficient models than prompt-based composition over a substantially larger backbone.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a judge-aware, gated multi-task learning architecture for legal outcome prediction that disentangles factual merits from adjudicative context (judge identity), achieving state-of-the-art performance on UK Employment Tribunal decisions with far fewer trainable parameters than large generative SFT baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing legal outcome prediction models tend to conflate factual case information with adjudicative context, such as judge-specific behavior, and often rely on prompt-based conditioning within a single autoregressive channel, which weakly composes multiple contextual signals and is parameter-inefficient. There is a need for models that can explicitly separate merit-based reasoning from technical or judge-driven factors, better handle rare and ambiguous outcomes, and provide interpretable insight into when and how judge identity influences decisions.
AI.method: The authors design a Judge-Aware Gated Multi-Task Learning architecture built on a LoRA-adapted Gemma-4 encoder. They introduce a fine-grained legal outcome taxonomy that supervises the encoder via multi-task learning, imposing structural regularization to disentangle semantic pathways related to different outcome types. A Gated Fusion mechanism then dynamically adjusts how much the model relies on judge identity for each prediction. They compare this to supervised fine-tuning of a larger Gemma-4 26B-A4B generative backbone where judge identity and taxonomy labels are only provided through prompts or output targets in a single autoregressive channel.
AI.result: On a dataset of 13,937 UK Employment Tribunal decisions, the proposed gated architecture establishes a new state of the art in legal outcome prediction, particularly improving performance on ambiguous and rare outcome classes. It uses roughly an order of magnitude fewer trainable parameters than the generative SFT baselines, while outperforming them. Experiments show that when judge identity and outcome taxonomy are forced through a single autoregressive channel via prompting, their compositional benefits are weak, whereas the proposed structured, differentiable conditioning composes these signals effectively.
AI.conclusion: For identity-conditioned legal outcome classification, architectural choices about how contextual signals are integrated matter more than simply scaling model size. A discriminative, judge-aware gated multi-task architecture with structured supervision and explicit conditioning on judge identity can outperform larger generative, prompt-conditioned models in both accuracy and parameter efficiency. Additionally, the approach yields interpretable judge embeddings and calibration profiles that highlight where adjudicative context, rather than purely factual merits, drives predictions.

title: LCAi: Life Cycle Assessment with big data fusion and retrieval-augmented generation-assisted interpretation
comment: 23 pages, 14 figures, 6 tables. Includes Supplementary Information
summary: The interpretation phase of life cycle assessment often lacks structured mechanisms for translating quantified improvement opportunities addressing environmental hotspots into actionable strategic pathways under technological, social, and policy uncertainty. To overcome this limitation, this study introduces a perspective-conditioned retrieval-augmented generation framework for LCA interpretation, where a multi-perspective retrieval and controlled synthesis is incorporated in the artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted LCA. To operationalise large language models in LCA interpretation, a perspective fusion RAG architecture was developed, covering academic, industry, public discourse, and European union (EU) funding datasets. Our approach comprises three steps: (1) a scenario anchor defining system boundaries and decarbonization targets, (2) a set of perspective-specific micro-queries with constrained retrieval, and (3) a neutral synthesis step integrating only ledger-stored outputs without further retrieval. The framework is demonstrated through a hydrogen-enabled diesel reduction use case in an Italian apple production facility using GPT-5 nano as the reasoning model. Overall, the structured retrieval and constrained synthesis are designed to mitigate the risk of hallucination while preserving cross-domain diversity. The approach presented can support more disciplined translation of impact results into strategic pathways and opens up new avenues for the use of advanced AI tools in LCA studies, particularly those focused on technologies that could be deployed at scale. This proof-of-concept demonstrates how AI-assisted, evidence-grounded interpretation can support implementation-oriented decision-making beyond conventional LCA studies.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a structured, AI-assisted framework to turn LCA hotspot findings into concrete strategic decarbonization pathways using a multi-perspective retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approach.
AI.motivation: In life cycle assessment, the interpretation phase struggles to move from identified environmental hotspots and quantified improvement potentials to concrete, actionable strategies, especially under technological, social, and policy uncertainty. Existing approaches lack structured mechanisms and often do not leverage diverse knowledge sources or advanced AI tools in a controlled way, leading to risks such as hallucination and weak linkage between LCA results and real-world implementation decisions.
AI.method: The authors design a perspective-conditioned retrieval-augmented generation framework for LCA interpretation. They develop a perspective fusion RAG architecture that operates over multiple datasets representing different viewpoints: academic literature, industry sources, public discourse, and EU funding information. The workflow has three main steps: (1) define a scenario anchor that sets system boundaries and decarbonization targets; (2) issue multiple micro-queries, each constrained to a specific perspective and dataset for focused retrieval; and (3) perform a neutral synthesis step that integrates only the previously retrieved and ledger-stored outputs, without any additional retrieval, to generate strategic pathways. GPT-5 nano is used as the core reasoning model. The framework is demonstrated in a case study on hydrogen-enabled diesel reduction in an Italian apple production facility.
AI.result: The framework successfully generates interpretation outputs that translate LCA results into strategic decarbonization pathways grounded in evidence from diverse domains. The structured retrieval and constrained synthesis steps reduce hallucination risk while retaining cross-domain diversity in the recommendations. In the hydrogen-enabled diesel reduction case, the method yields implementation-oriented insights that connect LCA findings to practical options, showing the feasibility of perspective-conditioned RAG for LCA interpretation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework shows that AI-assisted, evidence-grounded interpretation can make LCA more implementation-oriented by systematically converting impact results into strategic pathways, even under uncertainty. By using multi-perspective RAG with controlled retrieval and synthesis, the approach improves discipline, transparency, and relevance in the interpretation phase of LCA. This proof-of-concept suggests that advanced AI tools, when carefully constrained, can enhance LCA studies, particularly those assessing scalable technologies and supporting real-world decision-making.

title: The Riddle Riddle: Testing Flexible Reasoning in Large Language Models and Humans
summary: Humans flexibly adapt their reasoning strategies to the requirements of a given problem. Large language models (LLMs) have performed well on many cognitive tasks, however, it is unclear whether this accuracy is a result of pattern matching from training data or flexible reasoning. Here, we introduce a novel paradigm to test this question: the riddle riddle paradigm. Riddle riddles are word problems written to mimic popular riddles, but altered so their answers only require literal interpretations. Identifying correct answers requires looking past the structure of each question and flexibly apply different reasoning strategies based on the content. If LLMs respond to surface features, such as form, a riddle-like structure should cause models to use an inventive reasoning strategy even when a literal interpretation suffices. Alternatively, if LLMs reason based on content, they should flexibly switch strategies when appropriate. Across two experiments with nine state-of-the-art LLMs and 100 human participants, we show humans and LLMs fail on this paradigm in opposite directions. LLMs were far more accurate on genuine riddles than on riddle riddles (84.9% vs. 50.7%); whereas humans showed the reverse effect (50.5% vs. 80.5%). Error analysis shows that 90.8% of LLM errors on riddle riddles (the condition where they show diminished performance) were due to inappropriate use of inventive reasoning while only 57.6% of human errors on genuine riddles were due to overextending literal reasoning. Thus, while both groups make mistakes, reasoning mistakes are made more often by LLMs than by humans. Overall, LLMs' strong performance on genuine riddles may reflect memory retrieval rather than flexible strategy selection, and without stimuli designed to elicit this contrast, it becomes easy to conflate LLM-generated outputs that look like reasoning with genuine reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a 'riddle riddle' paradigm to test whether LLMs rely on pattern matching or flexible reasoning, finding that LLMs and humans show opposite performance patterns, suggesting LLMs often overuse inventive reasoning and may rely on memory retrieval rather than genuine flexible strategy selection.
AI.motivation: To determine whether large language models’ high accuracy on cognitive tasks reflects genuine flexible reasoning or mere pattern matching and memory of training data, and to develop a paradigm that can clearly dissociate these possibilities.
AI.method: The authors design 'riddle riddles'—problems that look like familiar creative riddles but are solvable via straightforward, literal reasoning. They compare performance on genuine riddles vs. riddle riddles across two experiments involving nine state-of-the-art LLMs and 100 human participants, then conduct detailed error analyses to classify mistakes as overuse of inventive vs. literal reasoning.
AI.result: LLMs perform much better on genuine riddles (84.9% correct) than on riddle riddles (50.7% correct), while humans show the opposite pattern (50.5% vs. 80.5%). For LLMs, 90.8% of errors on riddle riddles are due to inappropriate inventive reasoning; for humans, only 57.6% of errors on genuine riddles stem from overextending literal reasoning. This indicates a systematic divergence in strategy use between LLMs and humans.
AI.conclusion: LLMs’ success on genuine riddles likely reflects pattern-based memory retrieval and an over-tendency toward inventive, nonliteral reasoning when surface form suggests it, rather than flexible, content-based strategy selection. Consequently, outputs that appear to involve reasoning may not reliably indicate genuine reasoning competence, and specially designed paradigms like riddle riddles are needed to reveal this difference.

title: AgentX: Towards Agent-Driven Self-Iteration of Industrial Recommender Systems
comment: Authors are listed alphabetically by their first name
summary:
Recommendation algorithm iteration is moving from an artisanal, engineer-bound process toward an industrialized research loop, but this transition remains blocked by a structural execution bottleneck: the idea-to-launch cycle still depends on human engineers to generate hypotheses, modify production code, launch A/B experiments, and attribute online results. Innovation therefore scales linearly with headcount rather than compounding with evidence, compute, and accumulated experimental knowledge. We present AgentX, a production-deployed multi-agent system that fundamentally restructures this production function. AgentX operates as a self-evolving development engine: it autonomously generates, implements, evaluates, and learns from recommendation experiments at a scale and pace that no manual workflow can sustain.
  The system orchestrates four tightly coupled stages in a closed loop. A Brainstorm Agent synthesizes evidence from historical experiments, system architecture, data analysis, and external research into ranked, executable proposals. A Developing Agent translates each proposal into production-ready code through repository-grounded generation and multi-dimensional reliability verification. An Evaluation Agent conducts safe online rollout with guardrail-vetoed A/B judgment, converting both successes and failures into structured knowledge assets. A Harness Evolution layer (SGPO) then distills execution trajectories into semantic-gradient updates that continuously sharpen the agents themselves -- making the system not merely automated, but self-improving.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AgentX, a production multi-agent system that automates and self-improves the entire lifecycle of recommendation algorithm experiments, from hypothesis generation to deployment and learning from results.
AI.motivation: Current recommendation algorithm development is constrained by a slow, engineer-intensive idea-to-launch cycle where every step—hypothesis generation, code changes, A/B tests, and analysis—requires human intervention. This causes innovation to scale only with headcount instead of leveraging data, compute, and accumulated experimental knowledge. The authors are motivated to remove this structural bottleneck by re-architecting the process so that experimentation and improvement can proceed autonomously and at much larger scale and speed.
AI.method: They design AgentX, a multi-agent system operating in a closed loop with four main components: (1) a Brainstorm Agent that mines historical experiments, system design, data analysis, and external research to propose and rank executable recommendation ideas; (2) a Developing Agent that converts these ideas into production-ready code using repository-grounded code generation plus multiple reliability checks; (3) an Evaluation Agent that runs safe online rollouts and A/B tests with guardrails that can veto harmful changes, and structures the learnings from both positive and negative results; and (4) a Harness Evolution layer (SGPO) that encodes whole execution trajectories into “semantic-gradient” updates, continuously improving the agents’ own capabilities so the system becomes self-improving over time.
AI.result: The abstract claims that AgentX is deployed in production and can autonomously generate, implement, and evaluate recommendation experiments at a scale and speed unattainable by manual workflows, indicating significant gains in throughput and automation of the experimentation lifecycle. Specific quantitative metrics are not provided in the abstract but the implication is that the system effectively removes the human-in-the-loop bottleneck for most steps.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that AgentX restructures the recommendation R&D pipeline from a human-engineer-bottlenecked process into a self-evolving, industrialized experimentation engine. By tightly coupling idea generation, development, evaluation, and agent-level learning via SGPO, the system turns recommendation improvement into a compounding, evidence-driven loop rather than a linearly scaling manual effort.

title: Forecasting With LLMs: Improved Generalization Through Feature Steering
summary: Successful forecasting involves identifying patterns between historical and future states of the world which generalize to future observations. We apply LLMs to a variety of forecasting tasks and inspect their internal states using sparse autoencoders to understand whether they appear to rely on time-specific pieces of knowledge versus generalizable patterns. Our analyses identify features associated with both time-aware reasoning and look-ahead-biased reasoning. We then apply the LLMs to an entirely different domain and intervene on these features. We find that amplifying time-awareness features substantially reduces look-ahead bias on forecasting prompts while preserving general reasoning performance. In contrast, steering the candidate look-ahead-bias features does not produce an effect. These results suggest that interpretable temporal features can be used to causally shift LLMs toward more historically grounded reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how large language models (LLMs) perform forecasting, distinguishes time-specific knowledge from general patterns in their reasoning, and shows that manipulating temporal features can reduce look-ahead bias while preserving overall performance.
AI.motivation: Forecasting with LLMs is vulnerable to look-ahead bias because models are trained on future information relative to a forecasting date. The authors want to know whether LLMs actually learn generalizable temporal reasoning patterns or just memorize time-specific facts, and whether interpretable internal features can be used to make LLMs more historically grounded and less biased by future information.
AI.method: They apply LLMs to several forecasting tasks and use sparse autoencoders for mechanistic interpretability, identifying neurons/features in the model’s internal activations that correlate with time-aware reasoning and with look-ahead-biased reasoning. They then perform causal interventions by amplifying or steering these features, and evaluate the effects on forecasting performance and general reasoning on out-of-domain tasks.
AI.result: They find distinct interpretable features associated with temporal reasoning and with look-ahead bias. Causally amplifying time-awareness features significantly lowers look-ahead bias on forecasting prompts while maintaining general reasoning capabilities. However, directly steering the candidate look-ahead-bias features does not meaningfully change model behavior.
AI.conclusion: Interpretable temporal features in LLMs can be leveraged to causally shift models toward more historically grounded, less look-ahead-biased forecasting without degrading general performance. This suggests that mechanistic interpretability and feature-level interventions are a promising path for debiasing LLM-based forecasting systems.

title: TAVR-VLM: Risk-Conditioned Causal Grounding for Hallucination-Resistant Report Generation
summary: Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement (TAVR) planning requires meticulous multimodal reasoning. However, adapting Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to this high-stakes domain is severely impeded by diagnostic hallucinations, where generated text lacks anatomical grounding. To address this, TAVR-VLM is introduced: a novel framework featuring Risk-Conditioned Causal Grounding Attention (R-CGA) that instantiates a model-internal ``Risk $\rightarrow$ Region $\rightarrow$ Word'' structural grounding pathway. R-CGA compresses multimodal inputs into a causal risk bottleneck, purifying dense visual features into a global risk mask. During autoregressive generation, a support-projected causal consistency objective constrains token-level grounding within the risk-defined support mask. Evaluated on $\text{M}^3\text{TAVR}$, a comprehensive 1,482-patient cohort, TAVR-VLM establishes a new state-of-the-art. It achieves an AUROC of 0.896, boosts CIDEr to 0.936, and drastically reduces the hallucination rate to 8.1\%, thereby improving interpretability for evidence-based surgical AI.

AI.tldr: Paper introduces TAVR-VLM, a multimodal model for TAVR planning that reduces hallucinations via risk-conditioned causal grounding attention.
AI.motivation: TAVR planning is a high-stakes clinical task requiring accurate multimodal reasoning. Existing multimodal LLMs suffer from diagnostic hallucinations—text that is not grounded in actual patient anatomy—making them unsafe and unreliable for surgical use.
AI.method: Proposes TAVR-VLM with Risk-Conditioned Causal Grounding Attention (R-CGA). The method builds an internal structural pathway from predicted procedural risk to anatomical regions to generated words ("Risk → Region → Word"). It compresses visual and other multimodal inputs into a compact causal risk bottleneck, yielding a global risk mask over anatomy. During text generation, a support-projected causal consistency objective forces each token to stay grounded within the risk-defined support mask, mitigating hallucinations.
AI.result: On the M^3TAVR dataset of 1,482 patients, TAVR-VLM sets new SOTA: AUROC 0.896 for risk prediction, CIDEr 0.936 for text quality, and hallucination rate reduced to 8.1%, outperforming prior MLLMs on both accuracy and faithfulness.
AI.conclusion: Risk-conditioned causal grounding can substantially reduce diagnostic hallucinations in surgical AI while preserving or improving predictive performance and report quality. TAVR-VLM demonstrates that embedding an explicit "Risk → Region → Word" causal structure in multimodal transformers improves interpretability and trustworthiness for TAVR planning and could generalize to other high-stakes medical domains.

title: Syntactic Belief Update as the Driver of Garden Path Processing Difficulty
summary:
Garden path sentences present a processing difficulty for humans -- the sentence prefix leads the listener towards one interpretation, until the listener hears a critical word that shows that the initial interpretation was wrong. Lexical surprisal, a measure that usually predicts sentence processing difficulty quite well, fails to provide good predictions for garden path sentences.
  We propose an alternative that actively predicts a probability distribution over syntactic trees (its syntactic belief) and updates that distribution after each new word. If a processor is led down a garden path, syntactic beliefs will be wrong and will require a large update at the critical word. The magnitude of the update is measured with a generalized Rényi divergence. Crucially, this metric is dependent on lexical items, but is fully independent of the probability of lexical items. This Syntactic Belief Update provides a better fit to the human reading time data on garden path sentences. This suggests a new research direction examining purely non-lexical alternatives to surprisal for psycholinguistics.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Syntactic Belief Update, a non-lexical metric based on belief changes over syntactic structures, which better predicts processing difficulty for garden path sentences than lexical surprisal.
AI.motivation: Lexical surprisal is successful at predicting human sentence processing difficulty in many contexts, but it fails for garden path sentences where syntactic reanalysis is required. This gap motivates a model that explicitly tracks and updates syntactic expectations rather than relying solely on word-level probabilities.
AI.method: The authors define a processor that maintains a probability distribution (syntactic belief) over possible syntactic trees and updates this distribution incrementally with each word. When the input leads the processor down a garden path, the eventual disconfirmation of the preferred parse triggers a large belief revision. They quantify the size of this revision using a generalized Rényi divergence between prior and posterior syntactic beliefs, constructing a Syntactic Belief Update metric that is independent of lexical probabilities but still conditioned on the lexical items observed.
AI.result: Using human reading time data on garden path sentences, the Syntactic Belief Update metric shows a stronger correlation with processing difficulty than standard lexical surprisal. In particular, critical disambiguating words in garden path sentences induce large syntactic belief updates that align well with observed reading slowdowns, where surprisal alone underestimates the difficulty.
AI.conclusion: The findings indicate that processing difficulty in garden path sentences is better captured by changes in syntactic beliefs than by lexical surprisal. This supports the view that non-lexical, structure-based metrics—such as Syntactic Belief Update—are crucial for explaining certain psycholinguistic phenomena and opens a line of research into purely syntactic or structural predictors of comprehension difficulty.

title: A Pipeline for Generating Longitudinal Synthetic Clinical Notes Using Large Language Models
summary: Synthetic data is increasingly used to enable the development and evaluation of AI systems in domains where access to real-world data is restricted. In healthcare, clinical documentation presents particular challenges due to its sensitivity. This work introduces a synthetic clinical notes pipeline and dataset designed to support the development of clinical AI tools while avoiding the privacy risks associated with real patient data. The dataset is generated using a modular pipeline that combines structured patient generation, semi-structured patient journey simulation, and unstructured clinical note generation using large language models. The pipeline is designed to prioritise internal consistency across longitudinal patient records, while also capturing variation in writing style, note structure, and clinical detail. Additional mechanisms, including LLM-based validation and augmentation steps, are used to improve faithfulness, realism, and diversity of the generated notes. We release a dataset of 70 synthetic patients, each associated with 20-50 clinical notes spanning a full hospital journey. The dataset is provided at multiple levels of validation, enabling users to balance realism and scalability depending on their use case. This dataset supports the development, testing, and evaluation of clinical AI systems, including summarisation tools, coding models, and decision support systems, without reliance on real patient data.

AI.tldr: They propose and release a synthetic clinical notes dataset generated with a modular LLM-based pipeline that preserves longitudinal consistency and realism while avoiding patient privacy risks.
AI.motivation: Access to real clinical notes in healthcare is heavily restricted due to privacy and sensitivity, which limits the development, testing, and evaluation of clinical AI systems that need such text data. There is a need for realistic but privacy-safe clinical notes that capture longitudinal patient journeys and diverse documentation styles.
AI.method: They build a modular synthetic data pipeline with three main stages: (1) structured patient generation to define synthetic patient profiles; (2) semi-structured patient journey simulation to create longitudinal hospital trajectories and events; and (3) unstructured clinical note generation using large language models conditioned on the structured data. The pipeline is designed to ensure internal consistency over time, while promoting diversity in style and content. Additional LLM-based validation and augmentation steps check and refine the generated notes for faithfulness, realism, and diversity. They then use the pipeline to generate 70 synthetic patients, each with 20–50 notes over a full hospital journey, and release multiple dataset variants with different validation levels.
AI.result: The outcome is a publicly released dataset of 70 synthetic patients with longitudinal clinical documentation (20–50 notes per patient) that is internally consistent, stylistically varied, and tunable in terms of validation rigor. The pipeline successfully produces synthetic notes that can support a range of clinical NLP tasks while not containing real patient information.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pipeline offers a practical way to create privacy-preserving, realistic synthetic clinical notes that can stand in for restricted real-world data. By providing a validated, multi-level synthetic dataset, the work enables broader development and evaluation of clinical AI tools—such as summarization, coding, and decision support systems—without direct reliance on sensitive patient records.

title: Paved with True Intents: Intent-Aware Training Improves LLM Safety Classification Across Training Regimes
summary: We argue that safety classifiers should model user intent as an explicit signal between the prompt and the final label. To study this, we introduce AIMS, a human-annotated dataset of 1,724 difficult safety prompts, each paired with an intent description and harm label. We use AIMS to evaluate intent-aware training across supervised fine-tuning, preference learning, reasoning distillation, and reinforcement learning. Despite its size, AIMS enables competitive safety classifiers across training regimes: DPO from model-generated intent errors improves over SFT, and intent-conditioned distillation outperforms reasoning-only distillation in most teacher-student pairs. Most notably, directly rewarding intent faithfulness with GRPO yields the strongest average performance across five external safety benchmarks, while our intent-aware models form the inference latency-F1 Pareto frontier. These results show that faithful intent modeling is a compact, high-quality supervision signal for more robust safety classifiers.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that explicitly modeling user intent as an intermediate signal significantly improves AI safety classifiers, using a new dataset and multiple training paradigms.
AI.motivation: Existing safety classifiers often rely only on surface-level prompts and final labels, which can miss the underlying user intent and lead to misclassification, especially for hard, ambiguous, or adversarial prompts. The authors want a more faithful and robust way to detect harmful requests by explicitly capturing intent.
AI.method: They introduce AIMS, a human-annotated dataset of 1,724 challenging safety prompts, each with a natural-language intent description and a harm label. Using AIMS, they test several intent-aware training strategies: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on intent+label, preference learning via DPO using model-generated intent errors, intent-conditioned reasoning distillation versus reasoning-only distillation, and reinforcement learning (GRPO) that directly rewards fidelity between the predicted intent and the ground-truth intent. They then evaluate these models on external safety benchmarks and measure performance and inference latency.
AI.result: Even though AIMS is relatively small, models trained with it perform competitively or better than baselines across multiple training regimes. DPO using intent-error data does better than plain SFT, and intent-conditioned distillation generally beats reasoning-only distillation for most teacher–student configurations. The strongest gains come from GRPO that rewards intent faithfulness, which achieves the best average performance on five external safety benchmarks. The intent-aware models also lie on the Pareto frontier of F1 versus inference latency, indicating they are both accurate and efficient.
AI.conclusion: Making user intent an explicit, faithfully-modeled signal during training yields more robust and efficient safety classifiers than approaches that rely only on final labels or generic reasoning. AIMS provides compact but high-quality supervision that allows a range of training methods to benefit from intent information, and GRPO-style reward shaping around intent faithfulness appears particularly effective.

title: Generative Retrieval via Diffusion Transformer with Metric-Ordered Sequence Training and Hybrid-Policy Preference Optimization
summary: Embedding-based retrieval ranks items by their similarity to a query in a shared vector space and usually aims to return the highest-scoring items. In many production settings this is not what is wanted: given a seed set that expresses a fine-grained pattern, one needs more items that both satisfy a target attribute and stay within that pattern. We formalize this as pattern-preserving attribute retrieval. The two goals pull against each other: averaging the seeds preserves the pattern but stays in a low-attribute region, while global attribute retrieval drifts to unrelated patterns. We approach the task with continuous generative retrieval, where a model reads a sequence of item embeddings and generates query embeddings for nearest-neighbor search. We propose MO-DiT+HPPO, a staged framework with raw-sequence pretraining, multi-domain metric-ordered continuation pretraining, tail-centroid fine-tuning, and HPPO. Metric-ordered training turns sparse online retrieval labels into in-pattern trajectories ordered from low to high predicted attribute density, teaching one model the metric-improvement direction across domains. HPPO aligns the generated query distribution with the true online objective by labeling a hybrid candidate pool with the online intersection metric and applying reference-anchored preference optimization. A Pareto pair filter keeps only winner pairs that do not lower same-pattern purity, raising the attribute metric without sacrificing the pattern. Across four attribute domains under item- and pattern-holdout protocols, metric-ordered DiT improves the intersection metric over a pretrained generative retriever, and HPPO improves it further, with significant gains on seven of eight domain-split cells and a marginal tie on the hardest split. Metric-predictor validation, order ablations, CPT/SFT comparisons, and a candidate-policy ablation show where the gains come from.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new retrieval setting—pattern-preserving attribute retrieval—and a generative retrieval framework (MO-DiT+HPPO) that better balances keeping fine-grained patterns with improving a target attribute in embedding-based retrieval systems.
AI.motivation: Standard embedding-based retrieval returns items most similar to a query, which is often misaligned with practical needs where one wants more items like a seed set that both preserve a nuanced pattern and improve on a specific attribute. Existing approaches either preserve the pattern but fail to improve the attribute, or improve the attribute but drift away from the pattern. There is a need to formalize and solve this trade-off in a principled way for production retrieval systems.
AI.method: They formalize “pattern-preserving attribute retrieval” and propose a continuous generative retrieval approach: a model reads sequences of item embeddings and generates new query embeddings for nearest-neighbor search. Their framework, MO-DiT+HPPO, has several stages: (1) raw-sequence pretraining of a DiT-style model; (2) multi-domain metric-ordered continuation pretraining that turns sparse online labels into ordered trajectories from low to high attribute density to teach the model an improvement direction that generalizes across domains; (3) tail-centroid fine-tuning to better target high-attribute regions; and (4) HPPO, a preference-optimization stage that aligns generated queries with the true online objective by labeling a hybrid candidate pool with the online intersection metric and applying reference-anchored preference optimization with a Pareto pair filter that keeps only improvements that do not reduce same-pattern purity.
AI.result: On four attribute domains and under both item-holdout and pattern-holdout protocols, their metric-ordered DiT model improves the intersection metric (jointly measuring attribute and pattern preservation) over a strong pretrained generative retriever baseline. Adding HPPO provides further gains, achieving significant improvements in seven out of eight domain-split conditions and a marginal tie in the hardest case. Additional analyses—metric-predictor validation, ablations on ordering strategy, comparisons of continual pretraining vs. supervised fine-tuning, and candidate-policy ablations—support that the gains stem from the proposed training stages and design choices.
AI.conclusion: The work shows that pattern-preserving attribute retrieval is a useful formalization for many real-world retrieval tasks and that continuous generative retrieval with MO-DiT+HPPO can effectively balance attribute improvement with pattern preservation. Metric-ordered training plus preference optimization aligned with an online objective significantly improve retrieval quality over prior generative retrieval methods without sacrificing in-pattern purity.

title: Compositionality and the lexicon in evolutionary semantics
summary: Formal semantics has shown that sentence meanings arise by recursively composing lexical meanings, yet much of the literature on semantic universals models either lexicons with fixed signal structures or holistic composition without interpretable lexical parts. We introduce a framework that integrates this fundamental insight of formal semantics in evolutionary modeling, by allowing lexical meanings and a composition function to co-evolve under pressures for conceptual simplicity and communicative accuracy. We apply this framework to the evolution of quantificational meaning. Analyzing the Pareto frontier, we find that the most well-known semantic universal, conservativity, emerges as an efficient system-wide abstraction. The account is sensitive to syntactic structure and helps reconcile tensions between empirical evidence on quantifier learnability and prior evolutionary models. More broadly, the results demonstrate that the picture of sentential meaning developed in formal semantics can be productively combined with evolutionary modeling. The framework offers a template for studying universals that involve global compression within a grammatical category, semantic specialization of syntactic arguments, and the co-evolution of lexical and compositional meaning.

AI.tldr: They build an evolutionary model where both lexicon and composition co-evolve and show that conservativity of quantifiers emerges as an efficient solution.
AI.motivation: Most work on semantic universals either assumes a fixed signal structure or treats sentence meanings holistically, ignoring the recursive composition of formal semantics. The authors want a model where interpretable lexical items and a composition function can evolve together, to better explain why certain semantic patterns like quantifier conservativity are universal and how they relate to learnability and communicative efficiency.
AI.method: They define a formal framework in which (i) a set of lexical meanings and (ii) a compositional function jointly generate sentence meanings. These two components are allowed to co-evolve under pressures for conceptual simplicity (compression) and communicative accuracy. They then instantiate this for the domain of quantificational meanings, explore the resulting space of possible systems, and analyze the Pareto frontier of simplicity vs. accuracy, paying attention to syntactic structure and the distribution of quantifier types that arise.
AI.result: Along the efficiency Pareto frontier, systems where quantifiers satisfy conservativity dominate: conservativity emerges not as a hardwired constraint but as a globally efficient abstraction at the system level. The model is sensitive to syntactic structure and provides a better fit to empirical data on how quantifiers are learned than previous evolutionary models with fixed or non-compositional semantics.
AI.conclusion: Combining the compositional view of meaning from formal semantics with evolutionary modeling is both feasible and insightful. The proposed framework explains the prevalence of conservative quantifiers as an emergent consequence of trade-offs between simplicity and communicative success and clarifies how syntactic structure and semantic universals interact. More generally, it provides a template for studying semantic universals that involve global compression within categories, specialized roles for syntactic arguments, and the co-evolution of lexical items with their mode of composition.

title: Learning to Recover Task Experts from a Multi-Task Merged Model
summary: Multi-task model merging aims to consolidate several task-specific experts into a unified model, yet static merging consistently suffers from parameter interference. While dynamic merging models aim to bridge this gap, many works rely on the costly storage and loading of redundant expert components at inference. In this work, from the perspective of task expert, we view parameter interference as parameter perturbation introduced to each expert during merging process. We show that such parameter perturbations can be modeled as affine transformation, which can be approximated as additive offsets. Motivated by these, we propose Recover Task eXpert (ReTeX), a framework that predicts those offsets, in order to undo parameter interference and recover task-expert performance from a single merged checkpoint. To recover the appropriate expert when task identity is unknown, we introduce a router-free task identifier based on SVD subspace signatures computed offline before inference. At inference, the identifier selects the task whose subspace yields the smallest projection residual for a given input. As a result, ReTeX recovers over 95% of individual-expert performance in both vision and NLP domains, while significantly improving generalization to unseen tasks. Crucially, we also show that the parameter offset prediction leads to emergent adaptive interpolation of expert knowledge for out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks. ReTeX adaptively interpolates seen expert knowledge to handle unseen tasks. Our code is available at https://github.com/BAIKLAB/ReTeX

AI.tldr: ReTeX is a framework for recovering task-specific expert performance from a single merged multi-task model by predicting parameter offsets to undo interference and identifying tasks without explicit routing.
AI.motivation: Static multi-task model merging causes parameter interference that harms each task expert’s performance, while dynamic approaches need storing/loading many expert components at inference, which is costly. There is a need for an efficient way to merge experts into one checkpoint without losing per-task performance or requiring heavy routing infrastructure.
AI.method: Treat the interference caused by merging as parameter perturbations to each expert, modeling these as affine transformations that can be approximated as additive offsets. Train a Recover Task eXpert (ReTeX) module to predict these offsets that restore each expert’s parameters from the single merged model. To handle unknown task identity at inference, compute SVD-based subspace signatures for each task offline and use a router-free identifier that chooses the task whose subspace yields the lowest projection residual for a given input.
AI.result: ReTeX recovers more than 95% of the original individual-expert performance in both vision and NLP benchmarks from a single merged checkpoint, while also improving generalization to unseen/OOD tasks thanks to adaptive interpolation of expert knowledge via the learned offsets.
AI.conclusion: By modeling merging-induced interference as learnable parameter offsets and using SVD-based task identification, ReTeX enables efficient multi-task model merging that retains almost all expert performance, avoids costly dynamic expert loading, and exhibits emergent adaptive interpolation that benefits unseen tasks.

title: CARVE: Content-Aware Recurrent with Value Efficiency for Chunk-Parallel Linear Attention
comment: 27 pages, 2 figures, multiple tables. Submitted to arXiv. Primary category: cs.LG; cross-list: cs.CL
summary:
Recurrent models must forget in order to remember, yet the state of the art decides what to erase without consulting what is stored -- the gate sees only the arriving token, not the memory it is about to modify. This memory-blind gating is one of three coupled defects in the leading delta-rule architecture (GDN-2): the value-axis erase mask wastes parameters at the scale of the value projection, and -- as we prove -- mathematically prevents the WY-form triangular chunk solver that makes recurrent training competitive with Transformers.
  We introduce CARVE (Content-Aware Recurrent with Value Efficiency), which resolves all three problems through one principle: erase only on the key axis. This is provably necessary and sufficient for the WY-form solver to remain valid. Within it, CARVE reuses the recurrent output tensor -- already written to GPU memory -- as a free content signal for the erase gate, and replaces the per-value write-gate projection with a single scalar per head. At initialisation CARVE is bit-identical to GDN-2; any quality difference emerges from what the content gate learns.
  At 1.3B parameters trained on 100B tokens, CARVE achieves WikiText perplexity 15.72 (minus 0.18 vs. GDN-2, a 4.5-sigma effect), leads every recurrent baseline on nine common-sense reasoning benchmarks, and sets state of the art on every RULER retrieval probe -- at 0.4% throughput overhead, 13% lower peak memory, and 19% fewer parameters. Six formal theorems cover memory capacity, Lyapunov stability, gradient flow, expressivity separation, Pareto-optimal chunk size, and hybrid optimality.

AI.tldr: They propose CARVE, a new recurrent architecture that uses content-aware, key-axis-only erasure to fix theoretical and practical flaws in GDN-2, enabling an efficient WY-form solver and improving performance and efficiency over prior recurrent models.
AI.motivation: Existing state-of-the-art recurrent models (like GDN-2) use memory-blind gating: the erase gate decides what to forget based only on the incoming token, not on the current memory content. This causes theoretical issues (blocks the WY-form triangular chunk solver), wastes parameters via value-axis erase masks, and limits expressivity and training efficiency. The authors want a recurrent architecture that is both theoretically well-founded and competitive with Transformers in performance and efficiency.
AI.method: They analyze GDN-2 and prove that its value-axis erase masking prevents the use of the WY-form chunked triangular solver. From this analysis, they derive a necessary and sufficient condition for the WY-form solver to be valid: erasures must operate only on the key axis, not on values. Based on this principle, they design CARVE, a recurrent architecture that: (1) performs erasure only on the key axis; (2) reuses the recurrent output tensor as a free content signal for the erase gate, making gating content-aware without extra memory reads; and (3) replaces per-value write-gate projections with one scalar per head, improving parameter efficiency. CARVE is initialized to be bit-identical to GDN-2 so that any changes in behavior are due only to learned content-aware gating. They also provide six formal theorems about memory capacity, stability, gradient flow, expressivity separation from GDN-2, optimal chunk size for the solver, and hybrid optimality of their design.
AI.result: On a 1.3B-parameter model trained on 100B tokens, CARVE achieves WikiText perplexity 15.72, beating GDN-2 by 0.18 perplexity points (a reported 4.5-sigma improvement). It outperforms all recurrent baselines on nine common-sense reasoning benchmarks and sets state of the art on all RULER retrieval probes. These gains come with only 0.4% throughput overhead while reducing peak memory by 13% and parameter count by 19%. Theoretically, their six theorems establish better-understood and provably sound properties regarding memory, stability, gradients, expressivity, and algorithmic efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Content-aware, key-axis-only erasure is the right inductive bias for modern recurrent architectures that use delta-rule updates and WY-form solvers. CARVE fixes key deficiencies of GDN-2, yields better language modeling and reasoning performance, improves retrieval capabilities, and does so with fewer parameters and lower memory usage. Their theoretical results justify the design and show that the architecture is stable, expressive, and compatible with efficient training via the WY-form chunk solver.

title: Diagnosing Task Insensitivity in Language Agents
summary: Large language models can serve as capable long-horizon agents, but their out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization remains weak. We identify a key source of this failure as task insensitivity: when faced with similar but distinct tasks, models might apply patterns learned during training and fail to solve the task at hand. We show that models often continue with actions aligned with the original task even when the instruction is semantically corrupted and cannot be directly answered. We further find that, when we replace the task description in a trained prompt with another similar but distinct task, the model may still output the same action. This behavior is accompanied by a consistent training-time attention drift away from task tokens and toward local observations, suggesting an optimization bias toward shortcuts. To mitigate this problem, we propose Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization, a lightweight contrastive regularizer that explicitly encourages action dependence on the task instruction. Extensive evaluations show that our intervention improves task sensitivity and OOD generalization while preserving more stable attention to task tokens.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why large language models fail to generalize to slightly different tasks and proposes a regularization method that makes models’ actions more sensitive to task instructions, improving out-of-distribution generalization.
AI.motivation: Large language models can act as long-horizon agents, but they often fail when deployed on tasks that are similar, yet not identical, to those seen in training. The authors suspect that a major cause is that models ignore or underweight task descriptions, instead relying on learned shortcuts from training, leading to incorrect behavior when tasks change slightly.
AI.method: The authors empirically analyze model behavior by corrupting task instructions and by swapping task descriptions in trained prompts to see whether actions change. They also inspect training-time attention patterns to determine where the model focuses (task tokens vs. local observations). Based on these findings, they introduce Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization, a contrastive regularization technique that perturbs task instructions and encourages the model’s action distribution to depend on the correct task description rather than on spurious cues.
AI.result: They observe that models often follow the original task even when instructions are corrupted or swapped, and attention during training drifts away from task tokens toward local observations, indicating shortcut learning. With Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization, models show increased dependence on task instructions, better task sensitivity, and improved out-of-distribution generalization, while maintaining more stable attention on task tokens.
AI.conclusion: Task insensitivity is a key failure mode for LLM agents in out-of-distribution settings, driven by optimization biases that favor shortcuts and reduce attention to task descriptions. By explicitly regularizing models to condition actions on task tokens via Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization, it is possible to significantly improve task sensitivity, stabilize attention to instructions, and enhance OOD generalization for long-horizon LLM agents.

title: Bridging Talk and Thought: Understanding Dialogue Dynamics Across Collaborative Problem-Solving Contexts
summary: We present a conceptual framework for analyzing dialogue in collaborative problem-solving contexts, with an emphasis on the emerging dynamics of human-AI and multi-agent collaboration. As intelligent systems become active agents capable of autonomous reasoning and strategic cooperation, understanding the dialogic interaction during collaborative problem solving is increasingly important for optimizing and evaluating such partnerships. Our framework addresses key limitations in current analytical approaches through a hierarchical two-layer coding scheme that integrates cognitive and non-cognitive problem solving with metacognitive regulatory mechanisms. We demonstrate its effectiveness and generalizability across nine datasets spanning multiple domains, and provide insights into how humans and agents coordinate their knowledge, skills, and efforts to solve complex problems, showing in particular that metacognitive regulation can be an essential discriminator of deeper collaboration.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework to analyze dialogue in collaborative problem solving, especially for human-AI and multi-agent teams.
AI.motivation: Existing dialogue analysis methods do not adequately capture the intertwined cognitive, non-cognitive, and metacognitive processes that occur in collaborative problem solving, particularly as AI systems become autonomous collaborators rather than passive tools.
AI.method: The authors design a hierarchical two-layer coding scheme that jointly represents (1) cognitive and non-cognitive problem-solving processes and (2) metacognitive regulatory mechanisms. They apply this coding framework to nine diverse datasets covering different domains of collaborative problem solving, focusing on human-AI and multi-agent interactions.
AI.result: The framework can be successfully applied across all nine datasets, suggesting it is both effective and generalizable. Using the codes, the authors extract patterns of how humans and agents coordinate knowledge, skills, and effort. They find that metacognitive regulation patterns differentiate superficial from deeper forms of collaboration.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical dialogue coding framework offers a robust way to analyze and evaluate collaborative problem solving in human-AI and multi-agent contexts. Metacognitive regulation emerges as a key marker of higher-quality, deeper collaboration, indicating that future collaborative systems should support and leverage such regulatory processes.

title: Where Do CoT Training Gains Land in LLM based Agents?
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is widely used in language-model agents, but prior work has shown that verbalized CoT is not always faithful and may instead reflect post-hoc reasoning, which means the model already knows the answer before reasoning. We therefore ask what CoT training is actually improving: is the model getting better at changing its action through generated reasoning, or is it getting better at predicting the action directly from the prompt? We study this question by comparing \emph{prompt actions} (predicting action without CoT) with CoT actions (predicting action with CoT). Across checkpoints, prompt-action quality improves substantially. While interacting with the environment, the relative advantage of CoT actions over prompt actions remains similar, showing that CoT training does not widen the advantage of CoT reasoning, and it helps to improve the quality of prompt actions. We further find that later checkpoints are less likely to revise the action in response to CoT, suggesting greater reliance on the prompt. Motivated by these patterns, we selectively mask action-token supervision on a fraction of training examples. This intervention improves out-of-domain generalization.

AI.tldr: The paper studies what chain-of-thought (CoT) training actually improves in language-model agents and finds that it mainly enhances direct (prompt-based) action prediction rather than the benefit gained from explicit CoT reasoning, and that masking some action supervision improves out-of-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: Although CoT reasoning is widely used, prior work shows that the generated explanations are often not faithful and may reflect post-hoc justifications. This raises the question of what exactly improves when models are trained with CoT: does CoT help models genuinely revise and improve actions via explicit reasoning, or does it simply improve their ability to pick good actions directly from the prompt without relying on CoT? Clarifying this is important for understanding and improving reasoning-style training methods.
AI.method: The authors compare two types of behaviors across training checkpoints of language-model agents: (1) "prompt actions," where the model predicts actions directly from the prompt without generating CoT, and (2) "CoT actions," where the model first generates a chain-of-thought and then predicts the action. They measure performance differences between the two across training progress and during interaction with environments. They also analyze how often later checkpoints revise their initial actions after CoT. Finally, they perform an intervention that selectively masks (removes) supervision on the action tokens for a subset of training examples and evaluate its effect on out-of-domain generalization.
AI.result: They find that: (a) the quality of prompt actions improves substantially across checkpoints; (b) the performance gap between CoT actions and prompt actions stays roughly constant, indicating that CoT training does not significantly increase the unique advantage of using explicit CoT; (c) later checkpoints show a reduced tendency to revise their actions in response to CoT reasoning, implying a growing reliance on direct prompt-to-action mapping; and (d) masking action-token supervision on a portion of training data leads to improved out-of-domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: CoT-style training primarily enhances the model’s ability to choose good actions directly from the prompt rather than deepening its dependence on explicit CoT-based reasoning. Over training, models rely more on prompt information and are less likely to modify their decisions after generating CoT. Moreover, carefully reducing explicit action supervision (masking some action tokens) can improve generalization to out-of-domain tasks, suggesting that how CoT and action signals are combined in training strongly shapes the model’s reasoning behavior and robustness.

title: LMs as Task-Specific Knowledge Bases: An Interpretability Analysis
summary: Language models (LMs) capture large amounts of factual knowledge applicable to a wide range of tasks, motivating the view of their parameters as a knowledge base. An important property of knowledge bases is that different queries for the same fact return consistent results, drawing on a single source of truth. We investigate whether LMs satisfy this property through behavioral and mechanistic analyses. Our results suggest that they encode knowledge in a task-specific manner. Behaviorally, facts acquired on one task frequently fail to co-emerge on others during training. Parameter localization experiments suggest a mechanistic explanation, revealing distinct parameter subsets underlying different tasks for the same fact. Finally, we show that chain-of-thought reasoning draws part of its effectiveness from engaging task-specific parameters beyond those tied to the evaluation task. Our findings suggest that what the model knows and how it is asked are intertwined in parameter space, undermining the "knowledge base" analogy and carrying implications for the reliability and controllability of factual knowledge in LMs.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that language models do not store facts as a single, unified knowledge base: how a question is asked and which task is used strongly affect which parameters and facts are used.
AI.motivation: Language models are often treated as if their parameters form a unified knowledge base where a fact, once learned, is consistently available across tasks and phrasings. In traditional knowledge bases, different queries about the same fact access a single source of truth. The authors want to test whether this analogy holds for LMs and whether factual knowledge is shared consistently across tasks and prompts.
AI.method: The authors run both behavioral and mechanistic analyses. Behaviorally, they track how and when specific facts are learned across different tasks during training, checking if learning in one task transfers to others. Mechanistically, they conduct parameter localization experiments to identify which subsets of parameters are responsible for responding to different tasks about the same fact. They also analyze chain-of-thought reasoning to see which parameter subsets are activated and how that affects performance.
AI.result: They find that facts learned in one task often do not appear in other tasks during training, indicating poor cross-task consistency. Mechanistic localization shows that different tasks querying the same fact tend to rely on different subsets of parameters, instead of a shared unified representation. Chain-of-thought prompts improve performance partly by recruiting additional, task-specific parameter subsets that are not primarily used for the straightforward evaluation task.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that language models encode factual knowledge in a task-specific, disentangled way rather than as a single coherent knowledge base. The mapping between “what is known” and “how it is asked” is intertwined in the model’s parameter space. This undermines the analogy between LMs and conventional knowledge bases and raises concerns about the consistency, reliability, and controllability of factual knowledge in LMs, especially across different tasks and prompting styles.

title: Look-Before-Move: Narrative-Grounded World Visual Attention in Dynamic 3D Story Worlds
summary: As embodied AI and world models increasingly operate in dynamic 3D environments, visual perception must move beyond passively interpreting given observations toward actively deciding what to observe. We study this problem through camera planning in dynamic 3D story worlds, where the camera must not only generate smooth motion, but also decide what visual evidence should be acquired before it moves. We formulate this capability as Narrative-Grounded World Visual Attention, where the camera acts as an embodied observer that determines what to observe, how to compose the observation, and how to shift attention over time under narrative intent and physical 3D constraints. To realize this capability, we propose Look-Before-Move, a camera planning framework that separates observation specification from motion execution. It first builds a Semantic Observation Contract to convert directorial intent into executable visual constraints, then performs Monte Carlo Viewpoint Search to find narrative-compliant and geometrically feasible viewpoints, and finally applies Semantic Trajectory Grounding to connect selected viewpoints into continuous, collision-aware, and temporally coherent camera motion. We further construct a dynamic 3D Story World Benchmark based on StoryBlender, covering 50 stories, 457 scenes, and 1585 shots with animated characters, semantic scene configurations, and executable 3D environments. Experiments show that our framework improves subject perception, intent consistency, and trajectory quality over representative baselines, demonstrating the importance of organizing visual attention before generating camera motion.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework for active camera planning in dynamic 3D story worlds, enabling an AI-controlled camera to decide what to look at and how to move based on narrative intent and physical constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing visual perception in embodied AI and world models is mostly passive, focusing on interpreting given images rather than actively deciding what to observe next, especially in dynamic 3D narrative environments. There is a need for cameras that can reason about narrative goals, choose informative viewpoints, and generate smooth, feasible motion while respecting the story context and 3D physics.
AI.method: The authors define Narrative-Grounded World Visual Attention, where a camera acts as an embodied observer that plans its observations under narrative and 3D constraints. They propose the Look-Before-Move framework, which decouples observation planning from motion generation. It: (1) builds a Semantic Observation Contract translating directorial/narrative intent into explicit visual constraints; (2) uses Monte Carlo Viewpoint Search to sample and evaluate candidate viewpoints that satisfy those constraints and are geometrically feasible; and (3) applies Semantic Trajectory Grounding to link chosen viewpoints into continuous, collision-free, and temporally coherent camera trajectories.
AI.result: They develop a dynamic 3D Story World Benchmark based on StoryBlender with 50 stories, 457 scenes, and 1585 shots, including animated characters and executable environments. Experiments on this benchmark show that Look-Before-Move outperforms baseline methods in subject perception (how well key subjects are seen), consistency with narrative/directorial intent, and camera trajectory quality.
AI.conclusion: Actively organizing visual attention via explicit observation planning before generating camera motion significantly improves camera behavior in dynamic 3D story worlds. Separating observation specification from motion execution and grounding camera planning in narrative constraints leads to better perception and more coherent, high-quality trajectories, which is important for embodied AI and world-model-based systems operating in 3D environments.

title: How Surprising Is Historical Italian to Language Models? Tokenization Tax, Comprehension Tax, and a Simple Mitigation
comment: The 22nd Conference on Information and Research Science Connecting to Digital and Library Science
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly critical to digital library workflows, yet their ability to process historical language remains poorly understood. Historical difficulty is typically treated as a monolithic barrier, conflating orthographic variation, linguistic distance, and pretraining exposure. In this paper, we propose a diagnostic framework that decomposes this difficulty into four distinct dimensions: tokenization cost, predictive uncertainty (surprisal), semantic robustness, and context sensitivity.
  We evaluate this framework on three datasets spanning three centuries: (1) a newly curated corpus of 17th-century Italian texts (1610-1689) digitized from original page images; (2) canonical 19th-century Italian "I Promessi Sposi" serving as a high-exposure control; and (3) 18th-century Russian civil print books as a contrastive orthographic stress test.
  Our results reveal a distinct dissociation between encoding cost and comprehension. While Russian and early modern Italian incur comparable tokenization penalties (25-30% inflation), their predictive difficulty diverges sharply. 17th-century Italian is on average 2.4 times more surprising than its modern equivalent - with academic prose reaching 3.2 times - whereas Russian shows only a modest increase. But predictive uncertainty does not imply representational degradation: embedding similarity remains robust (> 0.85) across all datasets, confirming that models can represent historical meaning even when generation is unstable.
  Finally, we demonstrate that a minimal temporal context prompt reduces historical surprisal by approximately 60%, offering a simple, model-agnostic mitigation. These findings suggest that while historical text imposes a consistent encoding tax, digital libraries can safely deploy LLMs for semantic retrieval tasks, provided that generative applications are carefully adapted.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework to understand why large language models struggle with historical texts, decomposing difficulty into four dimensions and showing that models can still represent meaning well even when generation is unstable, and that a simple temporal context prompt greatly improves performance.
AI.motivation: Existing work treats historical language difficulty for LLMs as a single, opaque problem, conflating spelling variation, linguistic change, and training data scarcity. Digital libraries increasingly rely on LLMs, but it’s unclear whether these models can reliably handle historical texts. The authors aim to provide a principled, fine-grained way to diagnose where and how LLMs fail on historical language so that libraries can deploy them more safely and effectively.
AI.method: They propose a four-part diagnostic framework for historical text processing by LLMs: (1) tokenization cost (how many tokens historical vs modern text requires), (2) predictive uncertainty / surprisal (how unexpected tokens are to the model), (3) semantic robustness (how similar embeddings are between historical and modern language), and (4) context sensitivity (how additional temporal context affects performance). They apply this framework to three datasets: a new corpus of 17th-century Italian texts, a well-known 19th-century Italian novel as a high-exposure baseline, and 18th-century Russian civil print books as an orthographic stress test, then compare metrics across them.
AI.result: They find that historical texts consistently inflate token counts (a 25–30% tokenization penalty) for both Russian and early modern Italian, but this does not neatly track with actual predictive difficulty. Early modern Italian is much harder for the model to predict (2.4x surprisal on average, up to 3.2x for academic prose), whereas Russian only shows a modest surprisal increase despite similar tokenization penalties. Despite higher surprisal, semantic representations remain strong: embedding similarities stay above 0.85 for all datasets, indicating preserved meaning representations even when next-token prediction is unstable. They also show that adding a minimal temporal context prompt (e.g., information about time period/language variety) cuts surprisal on historical texts by about 60%.
AI.conclusion: Historical language imposes a fairly uniform “encoding tax” in terms of tokenization, but predictive difficulty and semantic understanding are dissociable: LLMs may find historical text hard to generate but can still encode its meaning reliably. A small amount of temporal context in the prompt dramatically improves predictability. Thus, digital libraries can confidently use LLMs for semantic retrieval and representation over historical texts, while generative tasks (e.g., rewriting, summarization) require targeted adaptation and prompting to handle elevated surprisal and instability.

title: Einstein World Models
summary: Does intelligence require the ability to reason about phenomena beyond direct experience? It is natural to suspect that some complex thought cannot be captured through language alone. However, of particular concern to this work, is whether visualising counterfactual events can complement language as a mechanism for complex thought. We ask whether LLMs can be trained to utilise such visualisation mechanisms, in a way that benefits their reasoning abilities. Motivated by this question, we propose Einstein World Models. EWMs are a blueprint for LLM-based reasoning systems that place visual-temporal rollouts inside the reasoning trace, allowing them to reason in ways that text alone may not support well. In an EWM, the LLM calls a world-module (not to be confused with a world model), to produce short rollouts of scenes under consideration. The returned rollout is treated not as the answer, but as an inspectable hypothesis that can support later reasoning. Einstein World Models extend the capability of LLMs for tool calling (such as web search or code execution), into the domain of visual thought experiments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Einstein World Models (EWMs), a framework where an LLM interleaves its reasoning with short, simulated visual-temporal rollouts to support complex, counterfactual reasoning beyond text-only thinking.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether intelligence, especially complex and counterfactual reasoning, benefits from mechanisms beyond language alone, and to test if LLMs can leverage internally visualized world rollouts as a cognitive tool to improve reasoning.
AI.method: The authors design Einstein World Models, in which an LLM can call an external world-module that generates short visual-temporal rollouts of hypothetical scenes. These rollouts are not treated as final answers but as intermediate, inspectable hypotheses that the LLM can reference and reason over within its chain of thought, extending existing tool-use paradigms (like web search or code execution) into visual thought experiments.
AI.result: The abstract indicates a conceptual and architectural contribution: a blueprint showing how to embed visual-temporal rollouts in LLM reasoning traces via a world-module interface, demonstrating that such rollouts can serve as structured hypotheses that inform later reasoning steps. (Specific empirical results are not detailed in the abstract.)
AI.conclusion: Einstein World Models offer a way to augment LLMs with visual thought-experiment capabilities, suggesting that integrating visual-temporal rollouts as tools within reasoning traces can enable forms of complex, counterfactual reasoning that are difficult to achieve with text-only approaches.

title: Multilingual Reasoning Cascades Need More Context
summary: Translation cascades for reasoning translate the query from another language to English, reason in English, and translate the answer back to the original language. This is a competitive approach to multilingual reasoning, but structurally lossy, since each stage discards information later stages may need, including cues for cultural grounding, register, and disambiguation. We examine the benefits of a simple and training-free intervention: a context-aware translation cascade, which additionally provides the original question, the English translated question, and the reasoning trace to the context of the final translation module. We evaluate gains across nine multilingual benchmarks including various task types, three backbone models, and 285 high-, mid-, and low-resource languages, and demonstrate strong gains for open-ended generation across models and resource regimes. We show that the original language question carries most of the beneficial context. Our study emphasizes the need to better design information flow in machine translation cascades for mitigating error propagation, and provides a simple and actionable default strategy: preserve the original user question until the end of the pipeline.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a context-aware translation cascade for multilingual reasoning that preserves the original-language question and intermediate English reasoning trace all the way to the final translation, significantly improving open-ended multilingual generation across models and languages.
AI.motivation: Standard translation cascades for multilingual reasoning translate a non-English query into English, perform reasoning in English, and then translate the English answer back. Each stage typically only passes forward its own output, discarding the original question and intermediate reasoning. This is structurally lossy: it removes culturally grounded cues, style/register information, and disambiguating context that could help the final answer translation. The authors are motivated to reduce this information loss and mitigate error propagation through the cascade without requiring model retraining or complex architecture changes.
AI.method: They introduce a context-aware translation cascade: instead of only feeding the English answer into the final translation step, they also supply the original-language question, its English translation, and the full English reasoning trace in the context for the final translation module. This is a training-free intervention that only changes how information is passed between modules. They then systematically evaluate this modified cascade on nine multilingual benchmarks covering varied task types, using three different backbone models and 285 languages spanning high-, mid-, and low-resource settings. They analyze which contextual elements contribute most to performance gains.
AI.result: Across benchmarks, models, and language resource levels, the context-aware cascade yields strong improvements for open-ended generation tasks. Providing the original-language question to the final translation step delivers most of the benefit, with additional gains from including the English question and reasoning trace. The improvements are consistent across three backbone models and are observed in high-, mid-, and especially low-resource languages over nine multilingual benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Careful design of information flow in translation-based reasoning pipelines can significantly reduce error propagation without retraining models. The key design principle is to preserve and expose the original user question—and, when helpful, intermediate English representations and reasoning traces—to the final translation component. The authors recommend this simple context-aware cascade as a practical default for multilingual reasoning systems that rely on English-centric reasoning, highlighting that structural information loss in traditional cascades is avoidable with minimal engineering changes.

title: Adaptive Utility driven Resource Orchestration for Resilient AI (AURORA-AI)
comment: Accepted at IEEE Research and Technologies for Society and Industry 2026 conference
