summary: Modern AI systems are increasingly deployed under non-stationary computational, demographic, and operational conditions in which static resource allocation strategies degrade both predictive performance and human-centric properties such as fairness and explainability. This paper presents AURORA-AI, an Adaptive Utility-driven Resource Orchestration framework for Resilient AI that unifies Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman feedback control, Lyapunov-based stability monitoring, and a fairness-aware composite utility into a single closed-loop policy.The framework continuously redistributes computational budget across a population of heterogeneous AI models so that the global utility, defined jointly over predictive performance, demographic parity, cost, latency, robustness, and interpretability, remains maximised under disruption. The framework is evaluated in a stress-rich discrete-time simulation that concurrently injects demographic bias shocks, gradual concept drift, and abrupt black-swan disruptions, and is compared against five established controllers including Static, Round Robin, Greedy, LinUCB, and a deep reinforcement-learning agent based on Proximal Policy Optimisation. AURORA-AI achieves immediate recovery from the black-swan event compared to eighty-eight time steps for the Static baseline and twenty-two for Proximal Policy Optimisation, lifts the alpha-quantile and the super-quantile by twenty-nine and twenty-five percent respectively, simultaneously reduces the mean and maximum demographic parity gap, and increases the fraction of Lyapunov-stable operating steps. These results indicate that fairness-aware adaptive orchestration grounded in stability theory is a practical and theoretically motivated path toward resilient human-centric AI deployment.

AI.tldr: AURORA-AI is a control-theoretic framework that dynamically reallocates compute across multiple AI models to keep overall utility (performance, fairness, robustness, cost, latency, interpretability) high under changing and disruptive conditions.
AI.motivation: Static or naive resource-allocation strategies for AI systems break down under non-stationary environments (concept drift, demographic shifts, disruptions), harming both accuracy and human-centric properties like fairness and explainability. There is a need for a principled, adaptive orchestration mechanism that can guarantee resilience and stability while explicitly accounting for multi-objective trade-offs.
AI.method: The authors design AURORA-AI, a closed-loop resource orchestration controller that integrates: (1) Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman (HJB) feedback control to compute optimal adaptive policies over time; (2) Lyapunov-based stability monitoring to track and enforce system stability; and (3) a fairness-aware composite utility that jointly scores predictive performance, demographic parity, cost, latency, robustness, and interpretability. In each discrete time step, the controller reallocates computational budget among heterogeneous models based on observed state, estimated utility gradients, and stability metrics. They then implement this in a stress-test simulation with injected demographic bias shocks, gradual concept drift, and abrupt “black-swan” disruptions, and compare against standard baselines including Static, Round Robin, Greedy, LinUCB, and PPO-based deep RL.
AI.result: In simulations, AURORA-AI recovers from sudden black-swan disruptions almost immediately, whereas the Static baseline needs 88 time steps and PPO needs 22. It improves tail-performance metrics by increasing the alpha-quantile and super-quantile of global utility by 29% and 25%, respectively. It simultaneously lowers both mean and maximum demographic parity gaps and increases the proportion of time steps where the system remains Lyapunov-stable, outperforming all baseline controllers on both performance and fairness-oriented metrics.
AI.conclusion: AURORA-AI shows that combining control theory (HJB, Lyapunov stability) with a fairness-aware multi-objective utility leads to an effective and resilient orchestration policy for AI systems under non-stationary and disruptive conditions. The framework provides a theoretically grounded and practically effective way to maintain performance, fairness, and stability in real-time deployments, suggesting a promising path for human-centric, robust AI resource management.

title: Beyond Surface Forms: A Comprehensive, Mechanism-Oriented Taxonomy of Indirect Linguistic Encoding for LLM-Based Coded Language Detection
comment: Submitted for review in ARR for EMNLP 2026
summary: To avoid moderation and surveillance on social media, some users routinely invent indirect linguistic expressions (ILE) that camouflage sensitive meanings. Such expressions surface as algospeak, euphemisms, and adversarial obfuscation, depending on intent and context, and they involve recurring encoding mechanisms. We propose a comprehensive, mechanism-oriented taxonomy of ILE that abstracts away from communicative goals and instead categorizes the underlying operations through which meaning is encoded and recovered. We evaluate the taxonomy by incorporating it into LLM prompts and comparing it with four existing taxonomies and a no-taxonomy baseline, using 2,000 manually annotated TikTok and Bluesky posts. The proposed taxonomy attains the strongest document- and span-level performance across the three LLMs, achieving an improvement of 4.7% in accuracy and 5.4% in F1 over the best-performing benchmark. The empirical results reveal the importance of a comprehensive, mechanism-oriented taxonomy as a stable scaffold for detecting emerging coded language and a useful input to content moderation. Disclaimer: This paper contains content that may be profane, vulgar, or offensive.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a mechanism-oriented taxonomy for indirect linguistic expressions (ILE) used to evade social media moderation and shows it improves LLM detection of such coded language.
AI.motivation: Users on social media increasingly use indirect linguistic expressions (ILE), such as algospeak and euphemisms, to bypass moderation and surveillance, making it difficult for automated systems to detect sensitive or harmful content. Existing taxonomies focus on communicative goals or specific domains and may not generalize across contexts. The authors aim to build a comprehensive, mechanism-based taxonomy that captures how meanings are systematically encoded and decoded, providing a more stable scaffold for detecting emerging coded language.
AI.method: The authors design a taxonomy that categorizes ILE by the underlying encoding mechanisms rather than by intent or topic. They then embed this taxonomy into prompts for large language models and compare its effectiveness to four existing taxonomies and to a baseline without any taxonomy guidance. The evaluation uses 2,000 manually annotated posts from TikTok and Bluesky, measuring document-level and span-level performance for detecting ILE across three different LLMs.
AI.result: Across all three LLMs tested, the proposed taxonomy yields the best performance at both document and span levels. It improves accuracy by 4.7% and F1 score by 5.4% relative to the strongest existing taxonomy benchmark, demonstrating that mechanism-oriented categorization helps LLMs better identify and interpret indirect linguistic expressions in social media posts.
AI.conclusion: A comprehensive, mechanism-oriented taxonomy of indirect linguistic expressions provides a robust and generalizable framework for modeling how users encode sensitive meanings in social media. Incorporating this taxonomy into LLM prompts significantly enhances automated detection of coded language, making it a valuable tool for content moderation and for tracking emerging linguistic obfuscation strategies.

title: Semantic Early-Stopping for Iterative LLM Agent Loops
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. Open implementation, machine-checked theory, and reproducible harness: github.com/SahilShrivastava-Dev/semantic-halting-problem
summary: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) loops, for example a Writer that drafts and a Critic that revises, are almost always terminated by a fixed iteration cap (max_iterations). This is a syntactic kill-switch: it is blind to whether the answer is still improving, so it over-spends tokens on easy inputs and truncates hard ones. We study semantic early-stopping: the loop halts when consecutive draft embeddings stop changing in meaning (cosine distance with a patience window) and the answer's measured quality stops improving. Our work makes three contributions. First, an honest theoretical footing: we prove deterministic termination and well-definedness and machine-check these claims, while treating the convergence of the distance sequence as an empirically tested conjecture rather than a (previously over-claimed) Banach contraction. Second, a judge-efficient evaluation protocol: we generate each question's full trajectory once, replay every stopping policy over the identical drafts, and cache every LLM-judge call, yielding a strictly paired efficiency-versus-quality comparison at low cost; we further separate operational tokens (charged to a policy) from evaluation tokens (a measurement instrument). Third, an empirical study on multi-hop retrieval-augmented question answering (HotpotQA). On the 60-question test split, a judge-free semantic stopper reduces operational tokens by 38% relative to max_iterations at parity quality (Delta-IS = -0.004, p = 0.81), whereas the full quality-gated variant is counter-productive because its per-round judging dominates cost. An oracle that selects the best round attains +0.115 Information Score over every practical policy (p ~ 4e-11), reframing the problem from "when to stop" (easy) to "which round is best" (open).

AI.tldr: They propose semantic early stopping criteria for multi-agent LLM loops to save tokens while preserving answer quality, and evaluate it theoretically and empirically.
AI.motivation: Fixed iteration caps in multi-agent LLM loops are inefficient because they ignore whether answers are still improving, wasting tokens on easy problems and truncating hard ones. The paper aims to design a semantically informed stopping rule that is both theoretically sound and practically efficient.
AI.method: They define semantic early-stopping based on changes in embeddings between consecutive drafts (cosine distance with a patience window) and on measured answer quality. They prove deterministic termination and well-definedness, formalize assumptions around convergence without overstating them, and machine-check the proofs. For evaluation, they pre-generate full trajectories of drafts per question, then replay multiple stopping policies over these trajectories while caching LLM-judge outputs and separating operational vs evaluation tokens. Finally, they test on multi-hop retrieval-augmented QA (HotpotQA) with writer-critic loops.
AI.result: A judge-free semantic stopping rule reduces operational token usage by 38% compared with a fixed max_iterations policy while maintaining essentially identical answer quality (Delta Information Score around -0.004, statistically non-significant). A quality-gated variant that uses an LLM judge each round is not beneficial because judge calls dominate costs. An oracle that picks the best round per question significantly outperforms all practical policies by a large margin in Information Score, revealing a gap between achievable and attained performance.
AI.conclusion: Semantic early stopping based purely on embedding-change signals can substantially cut the cost of multi-agent LLM loops without hurting quality, and its behavior is theoretically well-founded. However, dynamically judging quality each round is too expensive, and there remains a large performance gap to an oracle that can always pick the best intermediate draft, shifting focus from stopping criteria to round selection strategies.

title: LLM-Based Examination of Eligibility Criteria from Securities Prospectuses at the German Central Bank
summary: Verifying the eligibility of securities as collateral is a key responsibility of the German Central Bank. However, manually verifying these assets against legal and financial criteria within lengthy, semi-structured, and often bilingual prospectuses is a resource-intensive task. While previous efforts utilized traditional Named Entity Recognition (NER) for information extraction, these methods can struggle with OCR noise, linguistic variance, and rigid span-based constraints, and the need for manually annotated training data for each relevant annotation type. In this paper, we present the first case study applying Large Language Models (LLMs) to the eligibility examination process, shifting the paradigm toward a generative Information Extraction pipeline. Our approach decomposes the task into extraction, normalization, and interpretation, allowing for greater flexibility in handling noisy text and interleaved German-English content. We further introduce a value-based evaluation methodology using LLM-as-a-judge, which offers a more semantic assessment than location-based metrics. Our results demonstrate that LLM-based systems achieve high precision (up to 91%) in document-level eligibility, exhibiting a conservative operating profile that minimizes false acceptance.

AI.tldr: The paper explores using Large Language Models (LLMs) instead of traditional NER to automatically check whether securities are eligible as collateral for the German Central Bank, achieving high-precision, conservative decisions on noisy, bilingual prospectus data via a generative IE pipeline and LLM-as-a-judge evaluation.
AI.motivation: Manually verifying securities’ eligibility as collateral is labor-intensive because it requires experts to read long, semi-structured, often bilingual (German-English) prospectuses and apply complex legal and financial rules. Prior automated approaches based on traditional NER struggle with OCR noise, linguistic variability, rigid span-based extraction, and the constant need for manually annotated training data per label type. The authors are motivated to find a more robust, scalable, and semantically aware method to support or partially automate this critical central bank task.
AI.method: The authors design a generative Information Extraction pipeline using Large Language Models to support the eligibility examination of securities. They decompose the task into three stages: (1) extraction of relevant information from noisy, semi-structured, possibly bilingual prospectus text, (2) normalization of the extracted content into structured, comparable formats, and (3) interpretation of this normalized data to determine document-level eligibility. To evaluate, they move away from purely span-based, location-oriented metrics and instead introduce a value-based evaluation framework, employing an LLM-as-a-judge to semantically assess the correctness of the extracted and interpreted values relative to ground truth requirements.
AI.result: The LLM-based pipeline achieves high precision in determining document-level eligibility of securities, with precision scores reported up to 91%. The system shows a conservative behavior, favoring the avoidance of false acceptances (i.e., wrongly classifying ineligible securities as eligible), which is desirable in the central bank’s risk-sensitive context. This demonstrates that LLMs can handle noisy, bilingual prospectuses and complex eligibility criteria more flexibly than traditional NER-based systems.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that Large Language Models, used within a generative IE pipeline, are a promising solution for automating or assisting eligibility checks for securities at the German Central Bank. By decomposing the process into extraction, normalization, and interpretation and by adopting value-based evaluation with LLM-as-a-judge, the approach overcomes several limitations of traditional NER. The conservative, high-precision performance indicates that LLM-based systems can reliably support high-stakes financial decision processes, especially where minimizing false acceptances is critical.

title: How to evaluate clustering with ground truth?
comment: Preprint of a book chapter to appear: P. Fränti, "How to evaluate clustering with ground truth?", In Center-based clustering, Springer Nature, 2026
summary: External indexes can be used for cluster evaluation when ground truth is available. We review the most common external validity indexes focusing on set-matching-based measures. We recommend centroid index (CI), because it is an intuitive cluster-level measure with an explainable result. If we need a more fine-tuned, point-level measure, there are more choices. Pair-set index (PSI) provides a normalized score which is not biased by cluster sizes. If all points should matter equally, then clustering accuracy (ACC) or any other set-matching measure is suitable.

AI.tldr: The paper reviews external validity indexes for cluster evaluation and recommends specific measures for different needs.
AI.motivation: To guide practitioners and researchers in choosing appropriate external validity indexes for clustering evaluation when ground truth labels are available, especially among set-matching-based measures.
AI.method: The authors review and compare common external cluster validity indexes with a focus on set-matching-based measures, analyzing their properties such as interpretability, bias with respect to cluster sizes, and the granularity of evaluation (cluster-level vs. point-level).
AI.result: They characterize several measures: Centroid Index (CI) as an intuitive, explainable cluster-level measure; Pair-Set Index (PSI) as a normalized, cluster-size-unbiased point-level measure; and Clustering Accuracy (ACC) and similar set-matching measures as suitable when equal weighting of all points is desired.
AI.conclusion: The authors recommend using CI for intuitive cluster-level evaluation, PSI for detailed and size-unbiased point-level assessment, and ACC or other set-matching measures when every point should contribute equally to the evaluation score.

title: Empowering GUI Agents via Autonomous Experience Exploration and Hindsight Experience Utilization for Task Planning
summary: Multimodal web agents can assist humans in operating repetitive GUI tasks, where effective task planning is essential for decomposing complex tasks into executable actions. While small open source MLLMs are cost efficient and privacy preserving compared with commercial large models, they suffer from weak planning and limited cross website generalization. To address these limitations, we introduce the planning experience exploration and utilization (PEEU) method, which autonomously explores environments to discover experiences and utilizes hindsight experience to synthesize strictly aligned, high level training data. To quantitatively analyze the generalization behaviors driving this performance, we propose the task decomposition hierarchical analysis framework (TDHAF) to systematically study compositional generalization across three task granularities: low, middle and high levels. Our analysis reveals that mastering low level atomic skills does not guarantee high level planning competence, while high level task training yields stronger OOD generalization. Experiments on real world benchmarks demonstrate PEEU's superior effectiveness: our 7B model achieves 30.6% accuracy, outperforming the much larger Qwen2.5-VL-32B model. These demonstrate constructing hindsight high level tasks and leveraging experiences is crucial for OOD planning abilities of small MLLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes PEEU, a method that lets small multimodal web agents autonomously explore GUIs and use hindsight experience to create high-level training data, leading to much better cross-website task planning and out-of-distribution generalization, analyzed through a new hierarchical task decomposition framework.
AI.motivation: Small open-source multimodal LLMs are attractive for cost and privacy, but they are poor at planning complex GUI tasks and generalizing across different websites. Existing training often focuses on low-level atomic actions and does not clearly explain what kind of experience or supervision is needed to improve high-level planning and compositional generalization. The paper is motivated by the need to systematically improve and understand the planning ability and OOD generalization of small web agents.
AI.method: They propose PEEU (Planning Experience Exploration and Utilization). First, a multimodal web agent autonomously explores environments and collects rich interaction experiences. Then they apply hindsight-based processing to those experiences to synthesize strictly aligned, high-level task demonstrations from the raw logs. These curated experiences are used to train small MLLMs on high-level planning rather than just low-level skills. To study generalization, they introduce TDHAF (Task Decomposition Hierarchical Analysis Framework), which decomposes tasks into three granularities—low-level atomic actions, mid-level operations, and high-level tasks—and measures compositional generalization at each level.
AI.result: Using the generated hindsight, high-level training data, they train a 7B multimodal model as a web agent. On real-world web benchmarks, this model achieves 30.6% task accuracy, which surpasses the much larger Qwen2.5-VL-32B model. Analysis under TDHAF shows distinct behaviors at low, middle, and high levels and how training on high-level tasks improves out-of-distribution planning.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that simply learning low-level atomic skills is insufficient for strong high-level planning and cross-website generalization. Instead, constructing hindsight-based, high-level task experiences and leveraging them during training is key to boosting OOD planning capabilities of small multimodal web agents. High-level task supervision provides better generalization than just extensive low-level training, enabling small open-source models to rival or surpass much larger systems on complex web tasks.

title: Joint Learning of Experiential Rules and Policies for Large Language Model Agents
summary: For LLM agents in multi-step interactive environments, a key challenge is to make effective use of accumulated interaction experience. Existing work has typically separated two uses of such experience: keeping it outside the model as natural-language rules for later prompting, or using trajectories and feedback to update the model parameters. The former is easy to interpret but can fall out of sync with the evolving policy; the latter improves the policy more broadly but provides only limited correction for local mistakes in sparse-reward settings. We present Joint Learning of Experiential Rules and Policies for LLM Agents (JERP), which updates a long-term experiential-rule pool and the policy from the same interaction trajectories. At decision time, JERP retrieves task-relevant rules and conditions the agent on them together with the interaction history. After each episode, it uses the collected trajectories both to optimize the policy and to revise the rule pool by comparing current rollouts with reference successful trajectories. This coupling keeps the rule pool aligned with the evolving policy while allowing stable and effective behaviors to be gradually absorbed into the model itself. Experiments on AlfWorld and WebShop show that JERP yields consistent gains in decision performance for complex interactive tasks.

AI.tldr: JERP is a framework that jointly learns natural-language experiential rules and policy parameters from the same interaction trajectories to improve LLM agents in multi-step environments.
AI.motivation: LLM agents in multi-step interactive environments struggle to effectively leverage accumulated experience. Existing approaches either store experience as external natural-language rules—interpretable but prone to becoming inconsistent with the evolving policy—or use it only to update model parameters, which helps globally but is inefficient for localized corrections in sparse-reward settings. There is a need for a method that integrates both forms of learning to maintain alignment and effectively exploit experiential knowledge.
AI.method: JERP maintains a long-term pool of experiential rules and a learnable policy. During decision making, it retrieves task-relevant rules and conditions the LLM agent on these rules together with the current interaction history. After each interaction episode, JERP uses the same collected trajectories in two ways: (1) to optimize the policy parameters, and (2) to revise the experiential rule pool by comparing current rollouts with reference successful trajectories, updating or adding rules accordingly. This joint optimization keeps rules and policy co-evolving.
AI.result: On two complex interactive benchmarks, AlfWorld and WebShop, JERP achieves consistently improved decision performance over baselines that either rely solely on external rules or purely on policy learning, demonstrating better use of accumulated interaction experience in long-horizon tasks.
AI.conclusion: Jointly learning and updating both experiential rules and policy parameters from the same trajectories allows LLM agents to better exploit interaction history, maintain alignment between rules and policy, and achieve more stable and effective behavior in complex multi-step environments. JERP offers a practical and empirically validated approach for combining interpretable rule-based guidance with policy-level learning.

title: Mapping Political-Elite Networks in Europe with a Multilingual Joint Entity-Relation Extraction Pipeline
summary: Whether political elites organise into rent-seeking coalitions that capture public resources or civic networks that sustain governance is a central question in comparative politics. Yet observing these complex, informal, and adversarial ties at scale has historically required intensive manual coding, while automated text-as-data methods have largely been limited to simple co-occurrence. Recent large language model (LLM) approaches offer a path forward but often rely on proprietary APIs, lack cross-lingual capability, and struggle with scalable entity resolution. We present a modular, fully open-weight pipeline for multilingual joint entity-relation extraction that builds signed, temporal knowledge graphs from massive unstructured news corpora. It combines span-based named-entity recognition (NER) with a three-stage linking cascade mapping mentions to language-independent Wikidata identifiers; a high-throughput, ontology-constrained mixture-of-experts model then uses guided decoding to extract directed, signed relationships grounded in a domain ontology. A full-coverage spot-check against a 3491-relation gold standard shows high textual correctness (68.2% strict to 93.7% lenient). Two large-scale case studies validate the pipeline against the public record. In Austria, it reconstructs a political party's complete lifecycle, dating internal fractures and tracking personnel into successor factions and court convictions. In a Polish corpus, it uncovers the overlapping economic and governance networks of state-enterprise patronage, alongside the structurally balanced, signed conflict network of the polarized Civic Platform (Platforma Obywatelska, PO)--Law and Justice (Prawo i Sprawiedliwość, PiS) duopoly. By bridging raw multilingual text and structured relational data, our framework provides a robust, replicable foundation for cross-national empirical computational social science.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an open, multilingual pipeline that converts large news corpora into signed, temporal political knowledge graphs, enabling scalable study of elite networks and governance.
AI.motivation: Understanding whether political elites form rent-seeking coalitions or civic networks requires mapping complex, informal, adversarial relationships at scale. Traditional approaches depend on labor-intensive manual coding, while existing automated text-as-data methods largely rely on simplistic co-occurrence and cannot adequately capture direction, sign, and temporal dynamics of relations. New LLM-based methods are promising but often depend on proprietary systems, lack multilingual robustness, and have difficulty with large-scale entity resolution. The authors aim to create a scalable, open, and replicable way to transform multilingual news into structured data suitable for comparative political analysis.
AI.method: The authors design a modular, fully open-weight pipeline for multilingual joint entity and relation extraction from large, unstructured news corpora. First, span-based named entity recognition (NER) identifies mentions, which are linked through a three-stage cascading process to language-independent Wikidata identifiers. Next, a high-throughput, ontology-constrained mixture-of-experts model performs relation extraction, using guided decoding to identify directed, signed relationships grounded in a domain-specific ontology. The output is a signed, temporal knowledge graph representing political and economic actors and their interactions across languages and time.
AI.result: In a comprehensive spot-check against a 3,491-relation gold standard, the system achieves high textual correctness, with 68.2% strict and 93.7% lenient accuracy. Applying the pipeline to real-world corpora, the authors present two case studies. For Austria, the system reconstructs the full lifecycle of a political party, including internal fractures, successor factions, personnel flows, and court convictions. For Poland, it reveals overlapping economic and governance networks tied to state-enterprise patronage and constructs a structurally balanced, signed conflict network capturing the polarized competition between Civic Platform (PO) and Law and Justice (PiS).
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that their open, multilingual, modular pipeline effectively bridges raw text and structured relational data, producing accurate, signed, temporal knowledge graphs from large news corpora. This provides a robust and replicable infrastructure for cross-national computational social science, enabling systematic empirical analysis of political and economic elite networks, conflict, and governance across different countries and languages.

title: OpenRCA 2.0: From Outcome Labels to Causal Process Supervision
summary: Root cause analysis (RCA) poses a holistic test of LLM agentic capabilities, such as long-context understanding, multi-step reasoning, and tool use. However, existing datasets suffer from a fundamental gap: they label only the root cause, not the propagation path connecting it to the observed symptom, which largely simplifies the task to naive pattern matching. To support rigorous evaluation, we introduce PAVE, a step-wise labeling protocol that leverages known interventions from fault injection to reconstruct causal propagation paths. The mechanism is forward verification: reasoning from cause to effect rather than inferring backward from symptoms. Applying PAVE yields OpenRCA 2.0 (500 instances), the first cross-system RCA benchmark with step-wise causal annotations for LLM agents. Across 11 frontier LLMs, recovering the exact root-cause set succeeds in only 20.7% of cases on average. To locate where this difficulty lies, we relax the criterion and find what we call the ungrounded diagnosis: agents identify at least one correct root-cause service in 76.0% of cases, but ground that service in a verified causal propagation path to the observed symptom in only 61.5%. Outcome-only evaluation hides this failure mode; step-wise causal ground truth is the missing piece for trustworthy LLM-based RCA agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PAVE, a step-wise labeling protocol that reconstructs full causal propagation paths in root cause analysis (RCA) tasks, and uses it to build OpenRCA 2.0, a benchmark for evaluating LLM agents’ RCA capabilities beyond simple root-cause matching.
AI.motivation: Existing RCA datasets only label final root causes without the intermediate propagation path from cause to symptom, which allows models to succeed via shallow pattern matching instead of demonstrating genuine causal, multi-step reasoning and tool-using capabilities. The authors want a benchmark that tests whether LLM agents can reason causally and step-wise in realistic fault scenarios.
AI.method: They propose PAVE, a step-wise labeling protocol that exploits known interventions from fault injection experiments to reconstruct verified causal propagation paths from injected faults (causes) to observed symptoms (effects). This uses forward verification—reasoning from cause to effect—to build detailed step-wise causal annotations. Applying PAVE to multiple systems yields OpenRCA 2.0, a cross-system RCA dataset with 500 instances, each annotated with step-wise causal paths for use in evaluating LLM agents. They then benchmark 11 frontier LLMs on this dataset and analyze performance under strict and relaxed evaluation criteria.
AI.result: On OpenRCA 2.0, the evaluated LLMs correctly recover the exact set of root causes in only 20.7% of cases on average. Under a relaxed evaluation (the "ungrounded diagnosis" criterion), LLMs manage to include at least one correct root-cause service in their diagnoses in 76.0% of cases, but can justify that service with a fully verified causal propagation path to the observed symptom in only 61.5% of cases. This gap reveals that models often guess plausible root causes without grounding them in correct causal reasoning steps.
AI.conclusion: Outcome-only RCA evaluation, which checks only whether the root cause is mentioned, masks important failure modes in LLM-based RCA agents. Step-wise causal ground truth—like that provided by PAVE and OpenRCA 2.0—is essential for rigorously assessing and improving LLMs’ true causal reasoning and agentic capabilities in root cause analysis tasks.

title: TOPS: First-Principles Visual Token Pruning via Constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets for Efficient MLLM Inference
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved strong multimodal reasoning capabilities, but their efficiency is limited by the large number of visual tokens, which introduces substantial computational overhead. Visual token pruning offers a natural solution, yet existing methods are imperfect: attention-based criteria tend to retain redundant tokens, while diversity-based criteria are often agnostic to user instructions. Even methods that combine multiple criteria still lack a principled formulation of the intrinsic objective of token pruning. In this paper, we revisit visual token pruning from a first-principles perspective and formulate it as constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets. Through a top-down information-theoretic analysis, we identify three fundamental principles for effective token selection: Task Relevance, Information Coverage, and Semantic Diversity. Based on these principles, we propose TOPS, a training-free and model-agnostic pruning module that can be applied to various MLLMs. Extensive experiments on 7 MLLM backbones and 14 benchmarks demonstrate that TOPS outperforms prior methods under diverse pruning settings. Notably, on LLaVA-NeXT, TOPS removes 77.8% of visual tokens while preserving 100.0% and 100.6% performance on its 7B and 13B models, respectively, suggesting that pruning redundant visual tokens can sometimes mitigate hallucination and inspire future lightweight MLLM design.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TOPS, a training-free, model-agnostic visual token pruning method for multimodal LLMs, grounded in information-theoretic principles, that greatly reduces visual tokens while maintaining or slightly improving performance.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs are computationally expensive because they process many visual tokens, most of which are redundant for a given user instruction. Existing pruning methods are heuristic: attention-based ones often keep redundant tokens, and diversity-based ones ignore task instructions. Even combined approaches lack a principled objective for which tokens should be preserved. The authors want a theoretically grounded, instruction-aware, and efficient token pruning framework that maintains performance while reducing computation.
AI.method: The authors reframe visual token pruning as constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets from a first-principles, information-theoretic standpoint. They derive three core principles for token selection—Task Relevance, Information Coverage, and Semantic Diversity—and use them to design TOPS, a pruning module that is training-free and model-agnostic. TOPS selects a subset of visual tokens that are most relevant to the task, collectively cover the needed information content, and remain semantically diverse. It can be plugged into different multimodal LLM backbones without additional training.
AI.result: Across 7 different MLLM backbones and 14 multimodal benchmarks, TOPS consistently outperforms existing pruning methods under various pruning ratios. On LLaVA-NeXT, TOPS can prune 77.8% of the visual tokens while preserving 100.0% of the performance on the 7B model and even achieving 100.6% of the original performance on the 13B model, indicating that aggressive but principled pruning can avoid or reduce performance loss and sometimes slightly improve it.
AI.conclusion: Principled, information-theoretic token selection enables aggressive pruning of visual tokens in multimodal LLMs with negligible or no performance loss, and occasionally performance gains. The proposed TOPS module, guided by Task Relevance, Information Coverage, and Semantic Diversity, offers a training-free, model-agnostic solution that improves efficiency and can reduce hallucinations. This suggests that many visual tokens are redundant and that carefully designed pruning can be an important direction for building lighter, more reliable multimodal models.

title: A Process Harness for Uplifting Legacy Workflows to Agentic BPM: Design and Realization in CUGA FLO
summary: We introduce the process harness, a new mechanism for uplifting legacy workflows into Agentic Business Process Management (Agentic BPM) without replacing the underlying workflow engine. A process harness places a policy-governed agentic layer around a deterministic workflow engine, intercepting designated control points to contribute reasoning, adaptation, and oversight while the engine retains structural authority over the process. To define the process harness rigorously, we develop the Task-Decision-Flow (TDF) model, specifying both its data schema and its execution semantics. TDF decomposes LLM reasoning across three policy-governed agent types: a TaskAgent for knowledge-intensive task execution, a DecisionAgent for per-case gateway routing, and a FlowAgent that governs runtime flow adaptation through a principled hook mechanism. Each agent reasons within an explicit policy drawn from the process FRAME, the aggregate policy set governing all LLM calls in the system. We then present CUGA FLO as the design and implementation realization of the TDF model, and demonstrate it on a loan approval workflow that exercises all three agent types and hook-driven regulatory override. The process harness uniquely reconciles imperative requirements, realized through deterministic workflow execution that enforces structural compliance, with normative requirements, realized through policy-framed agentic autonomy invoked at designated control points wherever the process demands it.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a 'process harness' that adds an agentic, LLM-based layer around existing deterministic workflow engines, enabling reasoning, adaptation, and policy-governed oversight without replacing legacy systems.
AI.motivation: Existing business process management systems are deterministic and good at enforcing structural compliance, but they lack the flexibility and reasoning capabilities of LLM-based agents. Organizations want to benefit from agentic/LLM capabilities while preserving their invested legacy workflow engines and maintaining strong compliance and control. There is also a need for a rigorous model that integrates LLM reasoning with process control points under explicit policies.
AI.method: The authors define a rigorous model called Task-Decision-Flow (TDF), specifying its data schema and execution semantics. TDF decomposes LLM reasoning into three policy-governed agent types: (1) TaskAgent for knowledge-intensive tasks, (2) DecisionAgent for per-case gateway routing decisions, and (3) FlowAgent for runtime flow adaptation via hooks. All agents operate under policies collected in the process FRAME, which governs all LLM calls. They then design and implement CUGA FLO as a concrete realization of TDF, and demonstrate it on a loan approval workflow showcasing all three agents and regulatory override through hooks.
AI.result: They produce: (1) the formal TDF model with schema and semantics, (2) the concept and design of the process harness that wraps legacy workflow engines with an agentic layer, (3) the FRAME policy structure that governs agents, and (4) the implementation CUGA FLO. In the demonstration, the loan approval workflow shows that the system can exercise all three agent types and support hook-based regulatory overrides while keeping the underlying deterministic engine in control of structure.
AI.conclusion: The process harness approach successfully integrates agentic, LLM-based reasoning into existing deterministic workflow engines, reconciling imperative (structural, compliant execution) with normative (policy-driven, context-sensitive autonomy) requirements. It offers a principled, policy-governed way to add adaptable, intelligent behavior at selected control points, without discarding legacy BPM infrastructure.

title: Vulnerability of Natural Language Classifiers to Evolutionary Generated Adversarial Text
summary: Deep learning models have achieved impressive performance across various fields but remain vulnerable to adversarial inputs, particularly in NLP, where such attacks can have significant real-world consequences. Adversarial attacks often involve small, semantically similar token replacements to fool NLP models, and recent methods have become more precise by targeting specific vulnerable words, often by exploiting some level of access to the model's internal structure. This paper proposes GAversary, a hybrid Genetic Algorithm (GA) to generate adversarial attacks on natural language models. The GA is able to treat the target model as a black box, requiring only the logit value output by the model to guide the search. GAversary differs from GAs previously proposed for this problem by using GloVe embeddings to propose word replacements (the mutation operator) to improve the semantic similarity of the adversarial examples. GAversary is applied to several benchmark data sets and well-known target models. GAversary is able to substantially reduce the target model's accuracy on test data compared to the BAE and A2T attacks compared against (in the best case, reducing a 76.8% accuracy to 5.8%, compared to BAE's 27.6%). The trade-off is that GAversary perturbs just under twice as many words as the other two methods, with a slightly lower semantic similarity to the original text and around a 5% increase in run-time.

AI.tldr: Proposes GAversary, a genetic algorithm–based black-box adversarial attack for NLP models that uses GloVe embeddings for semantically similar word substitutions, achieving stronger attacks than BAE and A2T at the cost of more word changes, slightly lower semantic similarity, and modestly higher runtime.
AI.motivation: Deep learning NLP models are highly vulnerable to adversarial examples created via small token substitutions, which is problematic because such models are widely deployed in real-world applications. Existing word-level attacks often need some model access and may not optimize well for semantic similarity or fully exploit black-box settings. The authors aim to design a more effective black-box adversarial attack that maintains semantic similarity while significantly degrading model performance.
AI.method: Introduce GAversary, a hybrid genetic algorithm for generating adversarial text examples in a black-box setting. The GA treats the target model as a black box and uses only its output logits to guide the evolutionary search. Candidate adversarial texts are evolved via selection, crossover, and a mutation operator that proposes word replacements using GloVe word embeddings to preserve semantic similarity. The method is evaluated on several standard NLP benchmark datasets and target models, comparing performance to existing attacks such as BAE and A2T.
AI.result: On multiple benchmark datasets and models, GAversary substantially lowers the target models' test accuracy, outperforming competing attack methods BAE and A2T in terms of attack success rate. In the best reported case, model accuracy drops from 76.8% to 5.8% under GAversary, compared to 27.6% under BAE. However, GAversary generally modifies nearly twice as many words as the baselines, results in marginally lower semantic similarity to the original sentences, and incurs about a 5% increase in runtime.
AI.conclusion: GAversary demonstrates that a genetic algorithm with embedding-based mutation can be an effective black-box adversarial attack on NLP models, achieving stronger attacks than existing methods while requiring only logit outputs. The approach trades off increased perturbation level, slight semantic degradation, and modest computational overhead for significantly higher attack success. This highlights both the vulnerability of current NLP systems to black-box adversarial attacks and the potential of evolutionary search with semantic-aware operators for crafting such attacks.

title: Ask, Don't Judge: Binary Questions for Interpretable LLM Evaluation and Self-Improvement
comment: Acceepted to the Second Workshop on Compositional Learning at ICML 2026, Seoul, South Korea
summary: Evaluating LLM outputs remains a major bottleneck in NLP: human evaluation is expensive and slow, lexical metrics correlate poorly with human judgments on open-ended generation, and holistic LLM judges often produce opaque scores that are hard to debug. We propose BINEVAL, a framework that decomposes evaluation criteria into atomic binary questions and aggregates the resulting verdicts into interpretable, multi-dimensional scores. Given a task prompt, a meta-prompt generates fine-grained evaluation questions, and an LLM answers them independently for each output, yielding transparent question-level feedback together with calibrated overall scores. This decomposition makes evaluation easier to inspect, easier to diagnose, and directly usable for prompt improvement. Across SummEval, Topical-Chat, and QAGS, BINEVAL matches or outperforms strong baselines including UniEval and G-Eval, with especially strong results on factual consistency benchmarks such as QAGS. Beyond competitive correlation with human judgments, BINEVAL better matches human score distributions and avoids the ceiling effects common in prior LLM judges, leading to better discrimination between borderline and clearly flawed outputs. We further show that the same question-level feedback supports iterative prompt optimization, improving evaluator prompts on summarization and generation prompts on IFBench under both self-update and cross-model update settings. Overall, BINEVAL provides a task-agnostic, training-free, and interpretable evaluation framework that combines strong empirical performance with practical diagnostic and optimization value.

AI.tldr: BINEVAL is an interpretable LLM-evaluation framework that breaks complex criteria into binary questions, producing transparent, multi-dimensional scores that match or beat existing automatic evaluators and are directly useful for diagnosis and prompt optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for evaluating LLM outputs are problematic: human evaluation is costly and slow; traditional lexical metrics like BLEU or ROUGE correlate poorly with human judgments for open-ended text generation; and recent holistic LLM-based evaluators often output single opaque scores that are hard to interpret, debug, or use for improving prompts. There is a need for an evaluation framework that is accurate, interpretable, granular, and practically useful for both diagnosis and iterative improvement.
AI.method: The authors propose BINEVAL, a task-agnostic, training-free evaluation framework. For a given task prompt, they use a meta-prompt to automatically generate a set of fine-grained, atomic, binary evaluation questions that decompose complex criteria into small checks. For each candidate output, an LLM independently answers these binary questions. BINEVAL aggregates these question-level verdicts into multi-dimensional, calibrated scores and provides transparent, question-level feedback. They evaluate BINEVAL on multiple benchmarks (SummEval, Topical-Chat, QAGS), comparing it to strong LLM-judge baselines like UniEval and G-Eval. They also use the question-level feedback to iteratively optimize prompts in both evaluator and task-generation settings, including summarization and IFBench, under self-update and cross-model update regimes.
AI.result: On SummEval, Topical-Chat, and especially QAGS (which focuses on factual consistency), BINEVAL matches or outperforms strong baselines such as UniEval and G-Eval in correlation with human judgments. It better reproduces human score distributions, avoiding the ceiling effects common in earlier LLM judges and offering finer discrimination between borderline and clearly flawed outputs. The question-level feedback is shown to support effective iterative prompt optimization: evaluator prompts for summarization and generation prompts on IFBench are improved in both self-update and cross-model update settings using BINEVAL’s feedback.
AI.conclusion: BINEVAL offers a practical, interpretable alternative to opaque LLM-judge scores by decomposing evaluation into binary questions and aggregating them into multi-dimensional metrics. It is task-agnostic and does not require training, yet achieves strong or superior empirical performance compared to existing automatic evaluators while better matching human scoring behavior. Its transparent, question-level feedback is directly useful for diagnosing model errors and for systematically improving both evaluation and generation prompts, making it a valuable tool for LLM evaluation and prompt engineering workflows.

title: EO-WM: A Physically Informed World Model for Probabilistic Earth Observation Forecasting
summary: Earth Observation (EO) forecasting aims to predict future Earth surface dynamics from satellite observations under changing meteorological conditions. In this paper, we view this task as a partially observed, weather-driven world modeling problem, in which weather acts as a conditioning signal, while forecasting remains uncertain due to sparse observations and unobserved land-surface states. However, existing methods do not fully capture this setting: deterministic models collapse uncertainty into a single future prediction, while diffusion-based methods typically treat weather variables as undifferentiated conditioning signals, and existing benchmarks focus mainly on reconstruction accuracy rather than whether forecasts respond correctly to changed weather forcing.We introduce EO-WM, a video diffusion transformer for multispectral EO forecasting. EO-WM incorporates a physically informed conditioning framework that represents meteorological forcing through a climatological baseline, weather anomalies, and cumulative physical stress signals. Specifically, it separates baseline and anomaly through distinct conditioning pathways, and accumulates anomalous forcing over time to capture sustained heat and drought stress. To evaluate weather-response behavior beyond standard metrics, we introduce two diagnostic benchmarks: an Extreme Summer Benchmark for severity-aware prediction of vegetation degradation under extreme weather, and a Seasonal Matched-Pair Benchmark for testing response fidelity under changed weather forcing. Experiments show that EO-WM reduces the error in predicted Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) decline amplitude by a relative 5.63% and improves directional hit rate by a relative 7.80%, while remaining competitive on standard pixel-level metrics. The benchmarks and model will be made open-source at https://github.com/Luo-Z13/EO-WM.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes EO-WM, a video diffusion transformer with physically informed weather conditioning to better forecast Earth surface dynamics (e.g., vegetation changes) from satellite data under changing weather, and introduces new benchmarks to test whether forecasts respond correctly to extreme and altered weather conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing EO forecasting models are either deterministic, collapsing uncertainty into a single prediction, or diffusion-based models that treat weather inputs naively as generic conditioning signals. Moreover, current benchmarks emphasize reconstruction accuracy rather than whether model forecasts change appropriately when the weather forcing changes (e.g., extreme heat or drought). This limits the usefulness of EO models for climate-sensitive applications like vegetation monitoring and impact assessment under extreme events.
AI.method: The authors frame EO forecasting as a partially observed, weather-driven world model, where weather is a structured conditioning signal and land surface states are only partially observed. They develop EO-WM, a multispectral video diffusion transformer. Its core design is a physically informed conditioning framework that decomposes meteorological forcing into: (1) a climatological baseline, (2) weather anomalies, and (3) cumulative physical stress signals. Baseline and anomalies are injected via distinct conditioning pathways, while anomalies are temporally accumulated to capture sustained stress such as prolonged heat or drought. The model is trained as a diffusion-based video generative model over multispectral satellite sequences.
AI.result: EO-WM is evaluated on two new diagnostic benchmarks: (1) an Extreme Summer Benchmark for predicting vegetation degradation severity (NDVI decline) under extreme weather, and (2) a Seasonal Matched-Pair Benchmark to test whether forecasted vegetation responds correctly when weather conditions are changed but other factors are controlled. Experiments demonstrate that EO-WM reduces error in predicted NDVI decline amplitude by 5.63% relative and improves directional hit rate (correctly predicting whether NDVI will increase or decrease) by 7.80% relative, while maintaining competitive performance on conventional pixel-level reconstruction metrics.
AI.conclusion: Structuring weather as physically meaningful conditioning signals and explicitly modeling cumulative stress within a diffusion-based video transformer leads to EO forecasts that are not only accurate in reconstruction metrics but also more faithful in their response to changing and extreme weather. The proposed EO-WM model and new diagnostic benchmarks provide a more appropriate framework for evaluating and improving weather-responsive EO forecasting models and will be released open-source for the community.

title: Simulation-based inference for rapid Bayesian parameter estimation in epidemiological models: a comparison with MCMC
summary: Mechanistic epidemiological models are widely used to support infectious disease forecasting and public-health decision making. Bayesian calibration of such models is commonly performed using Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), which can become computationally expensive for high-dimensional nonlinear systems and repeated near-real-time analyses. Here, we investigate simulation-based inference (SBI) using neural posterior estimation as a scalable alternative for Bayesian calibration of a mechanistic SECIR epidemiological model using COVID-19 intensive care unit (ICU) occupancy data from Germany during 2020. We compared SBI and MCMC across multiple epidemic phases using both 31-day inference windows and a substantially more challenging 201-day reconstruction problem involving multiple transmission change points. Posterior agreement was evaluated quantitatively using Wasserstein distances and Kullback-Leibler divergences together with posterior predictive checks. Across the 31-day windows, SBI recovered posterior distributions in strong agreement with MCMC while accurately reproducing observed ICU trajectories. In the 201-day setting, SBI preserved the dominant posterior structure despite increased uncertainty. SBI, by combining CPU and GPU resources, substantially reduced computational runtime compared with MCMC, which was restricted to running on CPUs. Whereas MCMC required approximately 1000 seconds for the 31-day inference problems, SBI achieved comparable posterior and predictive performance in approximately 60-70 seconds on a single GPU. For the 201-day inference problem, SBI required an average of 157 seconds, while the MCMC runs took over 19,000 seconds. Our results demonstrate that SBI provides a rapid and computationally efficient framework for Bayesian calibration of mechanistic epidemiological models, supporting repeated near-real-time inference and rapid outbreak analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates simulation-based inference (SBI) with neural posterior estimation as a fast, scalable alternative to MCMC for Bayesian calibration of a mechanistic COVID-19 ICU occupancy model, showing similar posterior accuracy with much lower runtime.
AI.motivation: Mechanistic epidemiological models need Bayesian calibration for forecasting and public-health decisions, but standard MCMC becomes too slow and computationally expensive, especially for high-dimensional nonlinear systems and for repeated, near-real-time analyses during outbreaks. A faster yet accurate alternative is needed.
AI.method: The authors use simulation-based inference (SBI) with neural posterior estimation to calibrate a mechanistic SECIR model to COVID-19 ICU occupancy data from Germany in 2020. They compare SBI against traditional MCMC across multiple epidemic phases using sliding 31-day windows and a more complex 201-day reconstruction with multiple transmission change points. Agreement between methods is assessed using Wasserstein distance, Kullback-Leibler divergence, and posterior predictive checks. They also benchmark computational times using CPU-only for MCMC and CPU+GPU for SBI.
AI.result: For 31-day windows, SBI recovers posterior distributions that closely match MCMC and accurately reproduces observed ICU time series. For the 201-day reconstruction, SBI maintains the main posterior structure but with higher uncertainty, still capturing key epidemiological patterns. Runtime is drastically reduced: ~60–70 seconds for SBI vs ~1000 seconds for MCMC on the 31-day tasks, and ~157 seconds vs >19,000 seconds on the 201-day task.
AI.conclusion: Simulation-based inference with neural posterior estimation offers a rapid, computationally efficient alternative to MCMC for Bayesian calibration of mechanistic epidemiological models. It can deliver comparable posterior and predictive performance at a fraction of the runtime, making it suitable for repeated near-real-time inference and rapid outbreak analysis in practice.

title: Prompt Injection in Automated Résumé Screening with Large Language Models: Single and Multi-Injection Settings
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to screen and rank job applicants, creating incentives for candidates to strategically manipulate algorithmic hiring systems. We study prompt injection in automated résumé screening, defined as subtle self-promotional text that introduces no new qualifications but is designed to influence LLM evaluations. Using controlled experiments, we show that prompt injection reliably improves applicant rankings when résumé quality is homogeneous and few candidates inject. However, its effectiveness rapidly diminishes as more candidates inject, collapsing when manipulation becomes widespread. When candidate quality is heterogeneous, prompt injection is less effective on average, but can occasionally allow lower-quality candidates to outrank higher-quality ones, raising fairness concerns. Overall, LLM-based screening is most vulnerable when manipulation is rare and candidate quality differences are small. Code and resources are publicly available at: https://github.com/preetb1199/Prompt_Injection_ACL26

AI.tldr: The paper examines how strategically crafted self-promotional text (prompt injection) in résumés affects rankings by LLM-based hiring systems.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in automated hiring, applicants may try to game these systems, potentially undermining fairness and reliability. The paper aims to understand how effective such manipulation is and under what conditions it poses the greatest risk.
AI.method: The authors run controlled experiments with LLM-based résumé screening, systematically varying résumé quality homogeneity/heterogeneity and the prevalence of prompt injection among candidates to measure its effect on applicant rankings.
AI.result: Prompt injection significantly boosts rankings when résumés are of similar quality and only a few candidates use it, but its benefit declines quickly as more applicants inject, disappearing when manipulation is common. With heterogeneous résumé quality, injection is on average less helpful but can let weaker candidates outrank stronger ones, introducing fairness concerns.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based résumé screening is most vulnerable to prompt injection in scenarios with small quality differences and low manipulation prevalence, and it can create fairness issues by enabling lower-quality candidates to surpass better-qualified ones. Designers of such systems must account for these vulnerabilities when deploying LLMs in hiring workflows.

title: When Does Combining Language Models Help? A Co-Failure Ceiling on Routing, Voting, and Mixture-of-Agents Across 67 Frontier Models
summary:
Multi-model LLM systems such as routing, voting, cascades, fusion, and mixture-of-agents are used to beat single-model accuracy. We show that their gain is capped by a quantity the field rarely reports. For any policy whose output is one member model answer, accuracy cannot exceed one minus beta, where beta is the rate at which every model is wrong on the same query. In contrast, the usual diagnostic, average pairwise error correlation rho, cannot identify beta: error laws with identical marginals and pairwise correlations can have different all-wrong rates. A Clopper-Pearson bound on beta gives a finite-sample certificate on the largest gain any router, vote, or cascade could deliver before training a router.
  Across 67 models from 21 providers, a tetrachoric-calibrated single-factor model still underprices the all-wrong tail: on open-ended mathematics, observed beta is 0.052 versus 0.023 under the full 67-model Gaussian copula, about 2.5 times underpricing, with 90 percent CI 1.7 to 3.4 and k equals 17. The effect recurs on execution-graded code, where beta is 0.079. Re-asking the same GPQA-Diamond questions in free-response rather than multiple-choice form reopens the tail, with beta 0.127 and a five-judge panel with kappa 0.73 to 0.92, locating co-failure in answer format rather than subject. At matched quality, low-rho heterogeneous ensembles beat high-rho Self-MoA, but on checkable tasks in our pool, combining models rarely beats the single best model without a strong query-level routing signal. Gains come from models failing on different questions, not from adding more models.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why ensembles of large language models (LLMs) often give limited gains over the best single model, and shows that the key limiter is how often all models fail on exactly the same query.
AI.motivation: Many recent systems combine multiple LLMs (via routing, voting, cascades, mixtures-of-agents, etc.) to improve accuracy over a single model. Practitioners usually look at average pairwise error correlation between models to assess potential ensemble gains. However, ensembles frequently underperform expectations, and there is no clear, theoretically grounded quantity that directly characterizes the maximum possible benefit from combining models.
AI.method: The authors introduce and analyze the quantity beta, defined as the rate at which all models in a pool are simultaneously wrong on the same query. They show that any ensemble policy that selects one of the base model answers (e.g., routing, voting, cascades) has accuracy at most 1 - beta. They contrast this with the commonly used average pairwise error correlation rho and demonstrate that rho does not determine beta: one can construct error distributions with the same marginals and rho but different all-wrong rates. They provide a Clopper-Pearson confidence bound on beta using finite samples, giving a certified upper bound on the potential gain of any such ensemble before training routers. Empirically, they estimate beta across 67 models from 21 providers on several benchmarks, and fit a tetrachoric single-factor Gaussian copula model to compare predicted vs. observed all-wrong rates.
AI.result: On open-ended math tasks, the empirical all-wrong rate beta across 67 models is 0.052, while the Gaussian copula model calibrated with tetrachoric correlations predicts only 0.023, underestimating the tail by roughly 2.5x (90% CI 1.7–3.4) with effective dimension k=17. A similar underpricing of the all-wrong tail appears on execution-graded coding tasks, where beta is 0.079. When GPQA-Diamond questions are switched from multiple-choice to free-response, the all-wrong rate jumps to 0.127, and a panel of five human judges with high agreement (kappa 0.73–0.92) shows that these co-failures are due to answer format rather than topic domain. Empirical comparisons indicate that, for ensembles with matched individual-model quality, heterogeneous low-correlation ensembles outperform highly correlated self-mixture-of-agents, but on the evaluated checkable tasks, combining models rarely surpasses the single best model unless a strong per-query routing signal exists.
AI.conclusion: The main conclusion is that the achievable benefit of multi-model LLM systems is fundamentally capped by the all-wrong rate beta, not by pairwise error correlations. Average error correlation is an inadequate diagnostic because it does not determine the tail behavior where all models fail together. Practical ensemble gains derive primarily from having models that fail on different queries rather than from the sheer number of models. As a result, without strong query-level routing signals or deliberately diversified model errors, ensembles of current LLMs will often offer little improvement over the best individual model. The paper advocates directly estimating and reporting beta (with confidence intervals) as a standard metric to realistically characterize and certify the potential value of LLM ensembles.

title: Language-Based Digital Twins for Elderly Cognitive Assistance
comment: Accepted and published in the Proceedings of the ACM International Conference on PErvasive Technologies Related to Assistive Environments (PETRA 2026). The final published version is available through the ACM Digital Library
summary: Digital twins have emerged as a promising paradigm for personalized healthcare, enabling modeling of individual behavior and health trajectories. In cognitive health, early detection of Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) remains challenging, where language and conversational patterns serve as non-invasive biomarkers. In this work, we propose a language-based digital twin framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) to mimic the conversational behavior of elderly individuals by incorporating stylometric cues and contextual metadata. To evaluate fidelity and cognitive consistency, we introduce a multi-head conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) that jointly measures reconstruction quality and predicts cognitive scores. Experiments on the I-CONECT dataset show that the digital twin preserves identity-specific characteristics and achieves reconstruction and MoCA prediction errors comparable to real data, while outperforming baseline GPT-generated responses. These results highlight the potential of language-based digital twins as a scalable and non-invasive approach for personalized and continuous cognitive health monitoring.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a language-based digital twin framework using LLMs and a multi-head conditional VAE to mimic elderly conversational behavior for early Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) detection, achieving realistic identity-preserving conversations and accurate cognitive score prediction on the I-CONECT dataset.
AI.motivation: Early detection of Mild Cognitive Impairment is difficult, yet language and conversational patterns are promising non-invasive biomarkers. Existing methods lack personalized, scalable tools to model individual conversational behavior over time. The authors are motivated to use digital twins and LLMs to create individualized models that can support continuous cognitive health monitoring.
AI.method: They design a language-based digital twin framework where large language models generate conversations mimicking elderly individuals by conditioning on stylometric cues and contextual metadata. To quantitatively evaluate how faithful and cognitively consistent these digital twins are, they introduce a multi-head conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) that simultaneously optimizes for reconstruction quality of language features and prediction of cognitive scores (e.g., MoCA). The system is trained and tested on the I-CONECT conversational dataset of older adults.
AI.result: On the I-CONECT dataset, the generated digital twin conversations preserve identity-specific linguistic characteristics and yield reconstruction errors and MoCA (cognitive) score prediction errors that are on par with those obtained using real conversational data. Additionally, their approach outperforms baseline responses generated directly by GPT-like models without the proposed digital twin and cVAE framework.
AI.conclusion: Language-based digital twins built with LLMs and evaluated via a multi-head cVAE can realistically emulate individual elderly conversational behavior while retaining cognitively relevant signals, making them a promising, scalable, and non-invasive tool for personalized and continuous monitoring of cognitive health and early MCI detection.
